One major external obstacle to keeping IMF funding on track may have been removed: Sunday's election ouster of Hungarian Premier Viktor Orban is expected to pave the way for 90 ÔÇőbillion euros of European Union funding to Kyiv, a precondition for the IMF programme.
However, Ukraine also still needs to pursue an ambitious domestic reform ÔÇőprogramme ranging from raising revenue to tackling corruption and greater exchange rate flexibility.
"The key question domestically is if we see more, Ôüáand we do need to see more, efforts by the Ukrainians to pass these pieces of reform, and they seem like they're going there," Roger Mark ÔÇőat asset manager Ninety One said.
The West African nation's economic outlook is still murky due to the discovery of billions in undisclosed debt, which prompted the IMF to halt ÔÇőa $1.8 billion loan programme in 2024.
Talks on a new programme are ongoing, but correcting the debt burden would likely require a painful and lengthy fiscal consolidation , which may not be easy for local authorities.
"Without the large fiscal effort, the Fund may ask Senegal for some sort of debt treatment, which has been firmly rejected by the authorities," JPMorgan analysts said in a ÔÇőnote, adding they expected talks between Dakar and the IMF to continue. "Our base case of a new IMF arrangement remains tricky, with a muddle-through scenario as ÔÇőanother option."
Mozambique has been in talks with the IMF since mid-2025 over a new lending programme. The country has said it wants to restructure its debt, though it has yet to ÔÇîoutline any Ôüádetails. In a surprise move, it also repaid dues to the IMF early in March - though that was interpreted as a sign it wanted to secure fresh funds down the line.
"Mozambique's recent payment is a bold attempt to acquire further credit from the IMF," Christian Franken at Oxford Economics said. "Indeed, we expect such a loan agreement to be finalised in Q2 2026."
Gabon's authorities formally requested a Fund programme in March to support their reform agenda. Years of political instability have left Gabon, the ÔÇősecond-largest CEMAC economy, in an economic crunch with dwindling reserves.
Talks on ÔÇőthe contours of a programme are Ôüáexpected to continue during the Spring meetings.
In the past two years, Egypt has unlocked billions in IMF funding and foreign direct investment from Gulf states. But heavy exposure to energy imports, GCC remittances and tourism makes Egypt vulnerable to the fallout ÔÇőfrom the Iran war.
Some expect Egypt to ask for an augmentation of its IMF programme which includes an $8ÔÇ» billion ÔÇőExtended Fund Facility and Ôüáa $1.3 billion Resilience and Sustainability Facility. The Fund is due to review the programme for the next financing tranche in June, but analysts expect delays could push that to later in the year.
Venezuela's $5 billion in special drawing rights - the IMF's own currency - has been frozen since 2021 as its government was not recognised by a majority of IMF ÔüáBoard Member countries - a ÔÇőstatus that has yet to change under the leadership of President Delcy Rodriguez.
But with the ÔÇőFund consulting on the matter, this could soon change. A data blackout, which has stymied engagement with multilateral lenders, is also being faded out, with Caracas releasing batches of macroeconomic data in recent weeks. Some analysts ÔÇősay this could be seen as a precursor to a formal IMF mission visiting Caracas in coming months.

item.title: Hungarian election winner Magyar outlines his party's plans, views
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 13 - Hungarian election winner Peter Magyar vowed on Monday to change the constitution in a drive to restore democratic standards, following a landslide victory he said showed the country wanted to be anchored in Europe after 16 years of nationalist rule.
Hungary's veteran Prime Minister Viktor Orban ÔÇőand his nationalist Fidesz party lost power to the upstart centre-right Tisza party in Sunday's election, marking a setback ÔÇîfor Orban's allies in Russia and U.S. President Donald Trump's White House.
Following is a rundown of some key quotes from Magyar's news conference in Budapest.
"According to the rules, the result of the election becomes final by May 4 the latest, but this could happen earlier and the President... must convene the first session ÔÇőof the new parliament 30 days after the election at the latest."
"I call on President Tamas Sulyok to convene the inaugural ÔÇősession as soon as possible, and not wait until May 12."
"The Hungarian people yesterday, exactly 23 years Ôüáafter the referendum on our EU membership, confirmed Hungary's place in Europe."
"I would like to see a strong Central Europe within a strong ÔÇőEU."
"We will have a lot of tasks... the first, to adopt the functional measures, to start Hungary's accession to the European Public Prosecutor's ÔÇőOffice... to amend the fundamental law (constitution), and to write into it that in the future in Hungary anyone can only serve as prime minister for two terms, which is eight years."
"We will fundamentally restore, we will do everything to restore the rule of law, plural democracy, and the system of checks and balances."
"I ÔÇőwould like to underline that we will not use anti-democratic measures to restore the rule of law, but luckily our two-thirds mandate allows ÔÇőus to do a lot."
"A government's job is to ensure the independence of the judiciary, the independence of the investigative authorities, to provide all possible financial ÔÇîand other Ôüáresources to carry out their tasks."
"We don't need this president," Magyar said, referring to Sulyok, saying he was only signing off on all papers that were proposed to him.
"I call on him to leave, if he doesn't, we will find a solution."
Sulyok became Hungary's ceremonial head of state with the backing of Orban's Fidesz party.
"Hungary is in a difficult financial and economic situation. The National Bank is primarily responsible for ÔÇőensuring exchange rate stability. I myself ÔÇőbelieve... Governor Mihaly Varga is Ôüádoing his job in line with the central bank law and I think that in a country which is in a difficult economic situation, cooperation with the national bank is important... of course while respecting the ÔÇőindependence of the central bank.
"So I will try to work this way, and if we see that ÔÇőthe central bank Ôüágovernor and the bank itself work in line with their legal mandate and are not, for example, trying to obstruct the financial policy of the new government... then we will be able to work together. We do not need yet more chaos... to harm investors' confidence in Hungary."
"I Ôüáhave clearly ÔÇőstated several times already last year that everyone has the right to assemble in ÔÇőHungary.
"We have clearly stated that, according to Tisza and the many millions of Hungarians who support Tisza, everyone can live with, and love, whomever they want, as long as ÔÇőthey do not violate the laws and do not harm others."

item.title: Nigeria plans July start for gas deliveries to Abuja with AKK pipeline
item.description:
LAGOS, April 13 (Reuters) - Nigeria hopes to begin delivering natural gas to the capital Abuja by July through its long-delayed AjaokutaÔÇôKadunaÔÇôKano (AKK) gas ÔÇőpipeline , the upstream oil regulator said, marking a key milestone ÔÇîfor the country's gas development plans.
"We're hoping that by July, gas will be delivered to Abuja through the AKK gas pipeline," a spokesperson for the ÔÇőNigerian Upstream Petroleum Regulatory Commission told the regulator's in-house magazine.
The ÔÇő614-kilometre (382-mile) pipeline is designed to deliver more than 2.2 billion Ôüácubic feet of gas per day and is a core ÔÇőpart of Nigeria's strategy to shift its energy mix towards gas, supply ÔÇőpower plants and industries in the north, and reduce reliance on diesel and fuel oil.
Nigeria holds Africa's largest gas reserves, estimated at over 210 trillion cubic ÔÇőfeet, but much of the country's gas infrastructure remains underdeveloped, ÔÇőmaking the AKK pipeline a critical test of its gas-led growth ambitions.
The $2.8 billion project, ÔÇîfirst Ôüáconceived in 2008, has missed several delivery targets, including earlier deadlines of 2023 and the final quarter of 2025.
Construction began in 2020 but was slowed by funding pressures and engineering challenges, most notably the ÔÇőcrossing of the ÔÇőRiver Niger. That Ôüásection, widely regarded as the project's most technically demanding, required drilling beneath the riverbed using horizontal directional ÔÇődrilling, often compared to a scaled-down version of ÔÇőthe Eurotunnel.
An Ôüáenergy lawyer involved in the project told Reuters that work was now at an advanced stage, with the pipeline more than 90% complete.
Gas Ôüátransported ÔÇőthrough the AKK pipeline will be sourced ÔÇőfrom Nigeria's southern producing areas largely through its interconnection with the East-West Obiafu-Obrikom-Oben (OB3) gas ÔÇőpipeline, according to industry officials.

item.title: Botswana signs energy and mineral exploration deals with Oman
item.description:
GABORONE, April 13 (Reuters) - Botswana's president has clinched multiple ÔÇőagreements with Oman, including on mineral exploration, oil storage infrastructure and renewable ÔÇîpower, during a visit to the Gulf nation designed to strengthen economic ties, the presidency said on Monday.
Botswana has been seeking to engage Gulf states as part of its strategy to diversify beyond ÔÇődiamond mining and into critical minerals such as copper, gold, graphite, and ÔÇőiron ore.
President Duma Boko, who is on a three-day visit, presided over Ôüáthe signing of deals with the Sultan of Oman, Haitham bin Tariq, the ÔÇőpresidency said.
They included a joint mineral exploration deal under which Botswana will seek to increase mining ÔÇőexploration , targeting unexplored areas covering roughly 70% of its territory.
Revenue from diamond sales, which typically account for about a third of Botswana's national revenue, has fallen as the global diamond market has been ÔÇőweakened by economic uncertainty and the rise in popularity of lab-grown stones.
Another deal was for the development of a 500-megawatt solar power plant that ÔÇîfollows Ôüáon from an announcement on March 20 that NAQAA Sustainable Energy LLC, a subsidiary of Oman's state-owned O-Green, had been selected to design, finance, construct, and operate the plant in northwest Botswana.
It will have a minimum operational life of 25 years as Botswana ÔÇőseeks to increase ÔÇőthe contribution of renewable Ôüáenergy in its power supply from 8% now to 50% by 2030.
The presidency's statement said the plant would position Botswana "to secure ÔÇőits energy future and unlock long-term economic value".
It did not provide ÔÇőany financial Ôüádetails.
State-owned Botswana Oil Limited also signed a joint development agreement with Oman's energy investment company, OQ S.A.O.C to build oil storage infrastructure in Walvis Bay, Namibia, and in Botswana.
Oman Ôüáis also ÔÇőtrying to diversify its economy, which is dependent ÔÇőon oil, although its revenues have increased as a result of the oil price surge linked to the Middle Eastern ÔÇőconflict that began at the end of February.

item.title: Colombia to control hippo population through euthanasia
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Colombia will launch a plan in the second half of 2026 to control its invasive hippopotamus ÔÇőpopulation, including euthanizing an initial 80 animals, Environment Minister ÔÇîIrene Velez said on Monday.
The South American country is home to some 200 hippos in the central region near the Magdalena River. If ÔÇőcontrol measures are not adopted, the population could increase ÔÇőto as many as 1,000 by 2035, Velez said.
"We Ôüámust act to reduce the hippopotamus population. These actions ÔÇőare essential to protect our ecosystems and our native species," Velez ÔÇőtold journalists, noting that population growth threatens species like river turtles and manatees and causes water pollution. "We believe there may be approximately 80 individuals ÔÇőthat could be subject to the measure (of euthanasia)."
Colombia's first ÔÇőfour hippos were illegally imported in the 1980s by the late drug trafficker ÔÇîPablo ÔüáEscobar, who established a zoo on one of his properties. The population has since grown largely unchecked, despite some efforts by provincial environmental authorities.
The program announced on Monday has a ÔÇőbudget of 7.2 ÔÇőbillion pesos ($1.98 Ôüámillion) and includes measures like confinement and relocation. Though Colombia began talks months ago with eight ÔÇőgovernments including India, Mexico, Philippines, Ecuador, Peru ÔÇőand South ÔüáAfrica to possibly transfer some animals to zoos or sanctuaries in those countries, the necessary authorizations have not yet been obtained, ÔüáVelez ÔÇősaid.
The Colombia hippos have genetic defects ÔÇődue to inbreeding, which has reduced some institutionsÔÇÖ interest in them.

item.title: Dominican Republic rains force 30,000 to evacuate, 3 reported dead
item.description:
SANTO DOMINGO, April 13 (Reuters) - Some 30,000 people have evacuated their homes in the Dominican Republic over recent days due to torrential rains ÔÇőfrom weather systems that have hovered over the Caribbean ÔÇînation hurling rain onto much of its northern region for more than a month.
Local media reported three people had died, including a seven-year-old girl who ÔÇőtried to cross a river, a 19-year-old man who ÔÇőtried to cross on horseback, and an infant girl Ôüáin the capital, where the wall of her house collapsed due ÔÇőto the rains.
"The government's priority at this time is to save ÔÇőlives, protect property and preserve infrastructure," President Luis Abinader's office said in a statement on Monday, urging people to stay away from vulnerable areas.
Abinader declared ÔÇőa national emergency in five provinces as well as in ÔÇőthe capital.
A day earlier, the country's Emergency Operations Center (COE) Director Juan Manuel Mendez ÔÇîtold Ôüáa press conference 30,500 people had been evacuated, 14 communities were left isolated, 6,100 homes were flooded and highways and bridges were damaged.
"The rains will continue," meteorological institute chief Gloria Ceballos said on ÔÇőMonday, noting these ÔÇőshould ease Ôüáslightly before intensifying again on the weekend.
"We have a frontal system that remains stationary in the northeast," ÔÇőshe said. "The soil is already saturated. We have ÔÇőhad Ôüápractically 45 days of it raining every day in some parts of the territory."
In the nearby U.S. territory of Puerto Rico, authorities warned Ôüáthat ÔÇőheavy rain and thunderstorms could trigger urban ÔÇőflooding later on Monday.

item.title: Opposition candidate concedes defeat to Wadagni in Benin presidential race
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - The sole ÔÇőopposition candidate in Benin's presidential election, Paul Hounkpe, has conceded defeat to Finance ÔÇîMinister Romuald Wadagni following the vote in the West African country, state television reported on Monday.
A 49-year-old former Deloitte executive, Wadagni is the chosen successor of outgoing President Patrice Talon and had the backing of the ruling ÔÇőcoalition in Sunday's election, which took place four months after the government was nearly ÔÇőtoppled in a coup attempt .
The opposition candidate, Paul Hounkpe of the Cowry Forces Ôüáfor an Emerging Benin party, acknowledged his defeat in a statement on Monday before provisional ÔÇőresults were announced, according to a social media post by the state television network.
Wadagni was ÔÇőthe overwhelming favourite after the main opposition party, The Democrats, failed to get enough lawmakers to sponsor a candidate of its own and did not back Hounkpe.
Wadagni will come under ÔÇőpressure to bolster the country's defences in the north, which faces a growing threat from jihadist ÔÇőgroups based in the central Sahel.
The disgruntled soldiers who staged the December coup attempt said they were ÔÇîpartly Ôüámotivated by deteriorating security conditions there.
"A stable transition in Benin is important not only for the country itself but also for the broader West Africa region, given the political instability caused by successive coups in the Sahel," said Heni Nsaibia, senior West Africa analyst for Armed Conflict Location & ÔÇőEvent Data (ACLED), a conflict ÔÇőmonitoring group.
"It is Ôüátherefore especially necessary to focus efforts on stabilizing the north, preventing further upheaval, and demonstrating a model of stability for the broader region."
Wadagni ÔÇőhas also vowed to deliver on bread-and-butter issues like expanding access to ÔÇőclean water, Ôüáexpanding social security programmes and guaranteeing emergency healthcare regardless of ability to pay.
On the campaign trail, Hounkpe said high-profile tourism projects and impressive GDP growth under Talon and Wadagni had not improved Ôüáthe lives ÔÇőof most people.
He has also decried what he described ÔÇőas a climate of fear as political space for the opposition shrinks, with the ruling coalition holding every seat in ÔÇőthe National Assembly.

item.title: Former Brazil intelligence chief arrested by ICE in the US, Brazilian media says
item.description: SAO PAULO, April 13 (Reuters) - Former ÔÇőBrazilian intelligence ÔÇîchief Alexandre Ramagem has ÔÇőbeen ÔÇőarrested by ICE Ôüáin the ÔÇőUnited States, ÔÇőthe Brazilian media reported on ÔÇőMonday ÔÇőciting Brazil's federal police, ÔÇîafter Ôüáfleeing Brazil in September following ÔÇőhis ÔÇőconviction Ôüáfor plotting ÔÇőa coup ÔÇőwith Ôüáformer President Jair ÔüáBolsonaro.

item.title: Goldman Sachs nears another precarious peak
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Climbing the mountain is one thing. For David Solomon, staying on top might be the real challenge. On Monday, the boss of ÔÇőGoldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab announced that revenue , opens new tab for the first three months of 2026 had reached within 3% of ÔÇîa quarterly peak set five years ago. Back then, an ill-advised foray into retail banking and a drop-off in initial public offerings led to a quick descent. This time around, rising global conflict and uncertainty, paired with increasing risk in trading and lending, could arrest the Wall ÔÇőStreet titanÔÇÖs rise.
For now, many of the firmÔÇÖs businesses are riding high. Revenue from dispensing deal advice rose ÔÇő89% year-over-year, as mergers and acquisitions stage a massive comeback . Equity traders turned in the best Ôüáquarter in GoldmanÔÇÖs history. All told, the bankÔÇÖs revenue hit $17.2 billion, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion ÔÇőin the first quarter of 2021. That was right before rising inflation halted the Federal ReserveÔÇÖs easy-money policy, and with ÔÇőit, the frenetic pace of initial public offerings and buyouts. SolomonÔÇÖs push into retail banking also proved a misstep, from which he has since retreated.
Profitability still lags: earnings rose 19% from a year ago, to $5.6 billion, but still well below a $6.8 billion peak. That has to ÔÇődo with mix. Five years ago, investment banking, generally Goldman's most profitable business, generated $3.6 billion in sales versus $2.8 billion now.
A ÔÇőlower reliance on investment banking ÔÇö notoriously vulnerable to market whipsaws ÔÇö could be a good thing. Indeed, Goldman's share price has more than doubled ÔÇîover Ôüáthe past half-decade. Executives repeat the common Wall Street refrain that the deal pipeline remains strong. In a nod to variability, though, they added that raging Gulf conflict is weighing on corporate confidence, a necessary ingredient for massive transactions.
These days, Goldman generates more revenue from asset management, which rose to $3.3 billion from $735 million five years ago, and lending. The firm says ÔÇőthese are more "durable" than investment banking. ÔÇőThat could be the Ôüácase, but they are still far from risk-free.
GoldmanÔÇÖs reckoning of how much it could lose from its market activities on an average day rose 40% from a year ago. Provisions ÔÇőfor loan losses remain small, at just 2% of overall revenue, but nearly tripled in ÔÇőits corporate and Ôüáinvestment bank. History suggests that lending is as subject to booms and busts as anything else. The view from the summit, of course, tends to make any problems look small.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: IEA is ready to further tap global oil reserves if needed, chief says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The head of the International Energy Agency, ÔÇîFatih Birol, said on Monday he hopes another oil stockpile release is not needed but "we stand ready to act" if ÔÇőthe energy shock resulting from the war ÔÇőwith Iran requires it.
The 32-member IEA agreed last Ôüámonth to release 400 million barrels of oil ÔÇőfrom reserves, the largest coordinated release ever, in a ÔÇőbid to calm oil markets. The U.S., the world's largest oil and gas producer, agreed to release 172 million barrels from ÔÇőits Strategic Petroleum Reserve.
"I hope, very much hope, ÔÇőwe don't need to do it but if it is ÔÇîneeded Ôüáwe are ready to act," Birol said.
Birol reiterated at an Atlantic Council event that the war has resulted in the worst global energy disruption ever and ÔÇősaid that more ÔÇőthan 80 Ôüáoil and gas facilities including production, terminals and refineries across the Middle East have been ÔÇődamaged by war with Iran. Benchmark oil ÔÇőprices Ôüáare trading near $100 a barrel.
Due to the vast extent of the production shut-ins and closure of the Strait Ôüáof ÔÇőHormuz, the oil releases are "not ÔÇőa solution," Birol said, "it's just reducing the pain."

item.title: RussiaÔÇÖs Rosneft diverts oil to Tuapse refinery from drone-hit Novorossiysk port, sources say
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Rosneft is diverting oil supplies to the Tuapse refinery from the Black Sea port of Novorossiysk after the terminal, which handles roughly 14% of Russia's crude exports, was heavily ÔÇődamaged in a drone attack, three sources said on Monday.
A Ukrainian drone attack ÔÇîsparked a large fire at the Sheskharis oil terminal in the Novorossiysk port last week, damaging two of its main berths and forcing oil exports from the port to halt, according to the sources, who ÔÇőwere familiar with the matter but were not authorised to speak to the ÔÇőmedia.
The development underscores Russia's challenges in exporting oil as the rising number of ÔüáUkrainian drone attacks deals damage to its energy infrastructure, putting additional pressure on the ÔÇőRussian pipeline system and increasing the risk of disruptions to crude oil output.
Russian oil producer Rosneft, ÔÇőone of the country's largest exporters shipping crude via Novorossiysk, diverted a significant volume of oil to its Tuapse refinery, two of the sources said.
Runs at the refinery, which is located close to the Novorossiysk ÔÇőport, are expected to increase to nearly 800,000 metric tons this month, they added, from ÔÇőusual 500,000-600,000 tons per month.
Tuapse processed about 500,000 tons in March, the sources said. The refinery is focused ÔÇîon fuel Ôüáexports and ramping up its capacity makes sense amid higher margins, they added.
Rosneft did not respond to a request for comment.
Other exporters transporting oil via the port have begun diverting crude volumes to alternative destinations, including other export routes and domestic refineries, the sources added, saying ÔÇőthey do not expect ÔÇőa quick resolution.
They Ôüáare likely to redirect oil to Baltic ports that have resumed loadings after the attacks or also to the domestic market, the sources ÔÇősaid.
Novorossiysk, which can export about 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) of crude, resumed ÔÇőlimited oil Ôüáloadings last week, but traders said the flows were minimal and the port was not operating anywhere near full capacity.
The loadings resumed for berth number two, which accommodates smaller tankers, while the Ôüátwo main ÔÇőoil export berths remained shut.
Moscow ramped up oil loadings in ÔÇőearly April and exported more crude on average than in March, Reuters reported last week, helping to offset part ÔÇőof the losses caused by disruptions in the Black Sea.

item.title: EU launches operations of critical minerals procurement platform
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Union on Monday launched the critical minerals section of its energy and materials procurement platform, which aims ÔÇőto give more power to regional buyers and cut dependence on ÔÇîdominant producer China by aggregating their purchases.
The platform is part of the bloc's RESourceEU strategy , announced in December to develop its supply chains for rare earths and other ÔÇőstrategic minerals needed for the energy transition and defence applications. China ÔÇőcontrols up to 90% of output in that sector.
The EU's Energy and Raw Materials Platform , opens new tab opened to submissions from buyers on ÔÇőMonday in the first phase of a multi-month process, an EU spokesperson ÔÇőtold Reuters.
The platform connects buyers with suppliers, but leaves the two sides to finalise trades.
The first round of connections between buyers and sellers will start on April 13, ÔÇőthe spokesperson said, and will focus on "immediately and soon-to-be-available rare earths, battery ÔÇőand defence raw materials". The results will be announced in September.
Last year, the EU chose ÔÇîPriceWaterhouseCoopers Ôüáand a Slovak software company to develop its 9 million euro platform, which has separate mechanisms for strategic raw materials, hydrogen, and energy products such as natural gas and biomethane.
The platform kicked off with hydrogen, which started submissions ÔÇőin November and announced ÔÇőresults , opens new tab on April Ôüá1, with 273 match-ups.
The REsourceEU plan aims to accelerate the bloc's Critical Raw Materials Act, passed in 2023. ÔÇőThe EU wants to speed up the development of ÔÇőits own Ôüásupply chains so as not to rely on any single country for more than 65% of its demand.
Under the CRMA, the bloc has set ambitious Ôüá2030 targets ÔÇőfor domestic production of critical minerals required ÔÇőfor its green transition - 10% of annual needs mined, 25% recycled and 40% processed domestically ÔÇőby the end of the decade.

item.title: South Africa agrees new climate loan, critical minerals cooperation on Germany visit
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Germany has agreed to give South Africa ÔÇîa new 200 million euro ($234 million) concessional climate loan, and the two countries will deepen cooperation in ÔÇőother areas like critical minerals, South Africa's ÔÇőforeign minister said on Monday.
The loan will Ôüásupport investment in South Africa's power grid ÔÇőand renewable energy capacity, Minister Ronald Lamola said ÔÇőafter talks with German counterpart Johann Wadephul in Berlin.
He said German and European Union funding for green hydrogen and ÔÇőbattery value chain cooperation had also been ÔÇőextended by more than 270 million euros.
Lamola thanked Wadephul ÔÇîfor ÔüáGerman support despite South Africa's strained relationship with the United States during President Donald Trump's second term in office.
Trump has excluded South Africa from meetings ÔÇőof the ÔÇőGroup of Ôüá20 nations this year and criticised its foreign policy and domestic ÔÇőrace laws. He boycotted a G20 summit in ÔÇőJohannesburg Ôüáin November.
Lamola said: "We feel we are part of it (the G20) because of the support that we Ôüáhave ÔÇőreceived from Germany and from ÔÇőother G20 members."
($1 = 0.8547 euros)

item.title: Hezbollah chief urges Lebanese government to cancel Washington talks with Israel
item.description:
BEIRUT, April 13 (Reuters) - Hezbollah chief Naim ÔÇőQassem on Monday ÔÇîcalled on the Lebanese government to ÔÇőcancel a ÔÇőTuesday meeting between Ôüáthe Lebanese and ÔÇőIsraeli ambassadors ÔÇőin Washington, describing the talks as pointless.
In ÔÇőa televised ÔÇőspeech, Qassem said the ÔÇîarmed Ôüágroup will continue to confront Israeli attacks on ÔÇőLebanon.
Lebanon's ÔÇőforeign Ôüáminister said Beirut would ÔÇőuse Tuesday's face-to-face ÔÇőnegotiations Ôüáto press for a ceasefire Ôüáin ÔÇőthe war.

item.title: US government agrees to return rainbow pride flag to New York's Stonewall Monument
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's administration has agreed ÔÇőto restore the rainbow pride flag ÔÇîto the Stonewall National Monument, which marks the birthplace of the modern gay rights ÔÇőmovement in New York City, ÔÇőaccording to a proposal settlement filed in Ôüácourt on Monday.
The National Park ÔÇőService, the federal agency overseeing U.S. national ÔÇőmonuments, had removed the flag without warning in February, saying the flag was not an expression ÔÇőof the Trump administration's "official sentiments."
Some New ÔÇőYorkers sued the Trump administration, which has sought ÔÇîto Ôüálimit the rights of some LGBT people and vilified transgender people in particular, in order to restore the flag.
Under ÔÇőthe proposed ÔÇőjoint Ôüásettlement filed for U.S. District Judge Jennifer Rochon's approval in ÔÇőthe Manhattan federal court, the ÔÇőNational ÔüáPark Service agreed to hang the pride flag alongside the U.S. flag within Ôüáseven ÔÇődays and to not ÔÇőremove it "save for maintenance or other practical purposes."

item.title: Turkey's TPAO to begin acquiring shares in foreign companies, minister says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 13 (Reuters) - Turkey's state-owned upstream oil and gas company TPAO will start acquiring ÔÇőshares in foreign companies as part ÔÇîof Ankara's plans to develop international partnerships to boost its energy exploration, Energy Minister Alparslan ÔÇőBayraktar said on Monday.
Earlier, French oil ÔÇőmajor TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab said it signed a preliminary Ôüáagreement with TPAO to explore Turkey's Black ÔÇőSea waters for hydrocarbons, marking the fifth ÔÇősimilar accord signed by the Turkish firm with oil majors this year.
"Soon, we will turn a ÔÇőnew page too. And that is ÔÇőthis: as TPAO, we will enter yet another ÔÇînew Ôüáarea by buying shares of companies abroad," Bayraktar said without elaborating.
He said the agreement with TotalEnergies would allow the two ÔÇőcompanies to ÔÇőcarry out Ôüáexploration in Angola, adding Ankara also aimed to increase its ÔÇőoperations in Libya and Pakistan in ÔÇőcoming Ôüámonths.
Turkey has been pushing to develop its domestic resources via international partnerships, including with U.S. major ÔüáExxon , ÔÇőto boost security of ÔÇősupply and become a regional exporter.

item.title: EU plans more fuel subsidies to tackle Iran war price spikes
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 13 (Reuters) - The European Commission wants to let countries unleash more public money to help businesses with fuel and fertiliser bills, ÔÇőit said on Monday, as governments race to offset the ÔÇîeconomic shock from soaring prices triggered by the Iran war.
Oil prices jumped about 6% to more than $100 a barrel on Monday after the U.S. military ÔÇősaid it will blockade ships leaving Iran's ports, raising fears ÔÇőof prolonged disruptions to oil and gas shipments.
As part Ôüáof a broader package of measures Brussels is preparing to respond ÔÇőto the energy price spike, the Commission on Monday proposed changing ÔÇőEU state aid rules to allow more public spending for industries hit acutely by fuel price increases, including agriculture, road transport and shipping within Europe.
The changes ÔÇőwould let governments cover part of the price increase companies have ÔÇőpaid for their fuel or fertilisers, versus prices before the U.S.-Israeli war with ÔÇîIran Ôüábegan on February 28.
The draft EU plan would also increase, to above 50%, the maximum share of aid that energy-intensive industries can receive to help pay their power bills.
European governments including Germany , Italy, Poland ÔÇőand Hungary have already ÔÇőintroduced a Ôüáraft of funding measures including fuel price caps and tax cuts, to try to contain the Iran ÔÇőwar's economic fallout.
Governments will give feedback on the ÔÇőEU proposals, Ôüábefore the Commission plans to adopt a final version by the end of the month.
The proposed changes would be temporary, introduced specifically to Ôüáaddress ÔÇőthe energy fallout of the Iran war.
The ÔÇőCommission vets national governments' state aid to check that it does not distort competition ÔÇőin the EU single market.

item.title: Morgan Stanley maintains oil price forecasts and predicts slow recovery in supply
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - Morgan Stanley left its Brent crude oil price forecasts unchanged on Monday, at $110 a barrel for ÔÇőthe second quarter of 2026 and $100 a ÔÇîbarrel in the third quarter, falling to $80 a barrel in 2027.
The bank said it expected oil supply chains to take ÔÇőmonths to normalise even if a reopening of the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz can be achieved.
Under its Ôüábase-case scenario, exports through the Strait stay at low ÔÇőlevels in April, recover about 70% of lost volumes ÔÇőbetween May and July, and return to steady-state levels by October.
Oil prices climbed back above $100 per barrel on Monday, as ÔÇőthe U.S. Navy prepared to block ships to ÔÇőand from Iran via the Strait of Hormuz, potentially restricting Iranian ÔÇîoil Ôüáexports, after Washington and Tehran failed to reach a deal to end the war .
Brent crude futures were at $102.23 a barrel by 0810 GMT and U.S. West Texas ÔÇőIntermediate traded at $103.88.
Meanwhile, ÔÇőMiddle Eastern Ôüáproducers including Kuwait and Iraq have lifted Asia-bound official prices sharply for May.
Saudi Arabia ÔÇőset the price of its Arab Light ÔÇőcrude Ôüáto Asia at a record premium of $19.50 a barrel to the Oman/Dubai average.
Analysts expect the hit to global oil Ôüáproduction ÔÇőto flip the oil market into ÔÇőa supply deficit this year, versus pre-conflict estimates of a comfortable oversupply.

item.title: Exclusive: US, Iran leave door open to dialogue after tense Islamabad talks
item.description:
ISLAMABAD/DUBAI/WASHINGTON April 13 (Reuters) - After a sleepless and at times tense night in Islamabad, Iranian and U.S. officials ended their highest-level talks in decades without a breakthrough, but 11 sources familiar with the negotiations said dialogue was still alive.
The weekend meeting to resolve the conflict between the U.S. and Iran , held four days after last Tuesday's ceasefire announcement, was the first direct encounter between U.S. and Iranian officials ÔÇőin more than a decade and the most senior engagement since Iran's 1979 Islamic Revolution.
Inside Islamabad's luxury Serena Hotel, the talks unfolded across two separate wings and one common area ÔÇö one for the U.S. side, one for the Iranians and one for trilateral meetings ÔÇîinvolving Pakistani mediators, operational staff told Reuters.
Among the slew of issues at stake was the Strait of Hormuz, a major transit point for global energy supplies that Iran has effectively blocked but the U.S. has vowed to reopen, as well as Iran's nuclear programme and international sanctions on Tehran.
Phones were not allowed in the main room, forcing delegates, including U.S. Vice President JD Vance and Iranian Parliamentary Speaker Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf, to step out during breaks to relay messages back home, two of the sources said.
"There was a strong hope in the middle of the talks that there would be a breakthrough and the two sides would reach an agreement. However, things changed within no time," a Pakistani government source said.
Another source involved in the talks said ÔÇőthe parties came "very close" to an agreement and were "80% there", before running into decisions that could not be settled on the spot.
Two senior Iranian sources described the atmosphere as heavy and unfriendly, adding that while Pakistan tried to soften the mood, neither side showed any willingness to ease tensions.
Nevertheless the two Iranian sources said that by early Sunday morning the atmosphere had shown some improvement, and the possibility of a one-day extension began to take shape.
However, differences persisted. A U.S. Ôüásource said the Iranians did not properly understand that the core U.S. aim was to have a deal that ensured Iran would never obtain a nuclear weapon. Among Iran's concerns was a distrust of U.S. intentions.
This account, based on sources who spoke on condition of anonymity due to the sensitivity of the matter, offers ÔÇőa first account of the internal dynamics of the meeting, how the mood in the room shifted, how talks ended after signs the meeting might be extended, and how further dialogue remains on the cards.
There was no immediate response from the Iranian government to a request for comment on the issues reported in this ÔÇőstory.
On Monday U.S. President Donald Trump said Iran had "called this morning" and that "they'd like to work a deal." Reuters could not immediately verify the assertion.
A U.S. official, referring to Trump's comment, said there was continued engagement between the U.S. and Iran and forward motion on trying to get to an agreement.
Asked for comment, White House spokeswoman Olivia Wales said the U.S. position had never shifted in the Islamabad meeting.
"Iran can never have a nuclear weapon, and President TrumpÔÇÖs negotiating team stuck to this red line and many others. Engagement continues toward an agreement,ÔÇŁ she said.
A Middle East-based diplomat said conversations between mediators and the Americans have continued since Vance left Islamabad, while the source involved in the talks said Pakistan was ÔÇőstill passing messages between Tehran and Washington.
"I want to tell you that a full effort is still on to resolve the issues," Pakistan's Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif said on Monday.
Despite numerous obstacles to peace, both sides appear to have strong reasons to consider de-escalation.
The U.S. strikes appear unpopular at home and look unlikely ÔÇőto topple Iran's theocratic ruling system, while Tehran's strangling of energy supplies is hurting the global economy and pushing up inflation months before U.S. midterm elections.
Also, war damage to Iran's ailing economy risks leaving the authorities there weaker internally just weeks after protests they were able to put down only with mass killings.
In Islamabad, the longtime foes had gathered ÔÇîto try to chart Ôüáa path to a long-term settlement, after a Pakistan-brokered ceasefire paused six weeks of war that has killed thousands of people and disrupted the world's energy supplies.
Central to the dispute is a belief among Western countries and Israel that Iran wants a nuclear bomb. Iran denies seeking nuclear weapons.
A White House official said the U.S. wanted Iran to end all uranium enrichment, dismantle all major nuclear enrichment facilities, turn over its highly enriched uranium, accept a broader peace, agree a security framework that includes regional allies, end funding for regional proxies and fully open Hormuz, charging no tolls.
IranÔÇÖs demands included a guaranteed permanent ceasefire, assurances of no future strikes on Iran and its allies in the region, lifting of primary and secondary sanctions, unfreezing of all assets, recognition of its right to enrichment and continued control of Hormuz, Iranian sources have said.
Four of the 11 sources said that at times the dialogue appeared close to producing at least a framework understanding, but unraveled over Iran's ÔÇőnuclear programme, the Hormuz Strait and the amount of frozen assets Tehran wants access ÔÇőto.
The Iranian sources said most of the substantive exchanges in Islamabad Ôüáwere between Vance, Qalibaf and Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi.
"There were ups and downs. There were tense moments. People left the room, and then came back," the security source said.
Pakistani representatives, including Army Chief Asim Munir, and Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, moved between the sides through the night to keep things on track, five Pakistani sources said.
The talks stretched for more than 20 hours with on-duty hotel staff eating, sleeping and working on site after undergoing expedited ÔÇőbackground checks, they said.
When discussions turned to guarantees, both non-aggression assurances and sanctions relief, the tone of the normally mild-mannered Araqchi grew sharper, the two Iranian sources said.
The sources quoted him as saying: ÔÇťHow can we trust you when, ÔÇőin the last Geneva meeting, you said the ÔüáU.S. would not attack while diplomacy was under way?ÔÇŁ
The U.S.-Israeli attack on Iran began two days after the two sides held a previous round of talks in Geneva.
In addition to differences over Hormuz, sanctions and other topics, the two sides also disagreed over the scope of any deal. While Washington focused on the nuclear file and Hormuz, Tehran wanted a broader understanding, according to two of the sources.
During one tense moment, raised voices could be heard outside the negotiating room before Munir and Dar called a tea break and moved the two sides back into separate rooms, the government source said.
Toward the final stages of the discussions, which spilled into Sunday Ôüámorning, the U.S. delegates ÔÇőwere moving between the negotiating room and their private floor far more often than the Iranians, the senior Pakistani official said.
A U.S. source said the vice president came to the talks ÔÇőwith the aim of making a deal and reaching a mutual understanding. The U.S. side has been wary of protracted negotiations with Iran, believing the Iranians are adept at delaying tactics and refusing to make concessions, the source said.
Despite the deadlock, when Vance appeared in front of reporters later to announce the talks had ended, his remarks suggested more exchanges of some kind might be in ÔÇőprospect.
"We leave here with a very simple proposal, a method of understanding that is our final and best offer," he said. "We'll see if the Iranians accept it."

item.title: Minnesota county probes federal arrest as possible kidnapping
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - A Minnesota county is investigating an arrest by federal agents who allegedly broke into a St. Paul home with guns drawn, handcuffed ÔÇőa man and forced him outside in the snow in his underpants ÔÇîand sandals, only to discover they had the wrong man.
Ramsey County officials said in a statement on Monday that federal agents "may have committed the crimes of kidnapping, burglary, and false imprisonment" ÔÇőin the January incident.
The man mistakenly detained, ChongLy ÔÇťScottÔÇŁ Thao, has said the ÔÇőofficers had no warrant.
The incident has drawn further scrutiny to how federal Ôüáagents have operated in Minneapolis, a city at the center of debates over ÔÇőlaw enforcement tactics in President Donald Trump's immigration enforcement actions.
Minneapolis and St. Paul, situated ÔÇőside by side, are known as the Twin Cities. Ramsey County officials said they were seeking information from the Department of Homeland Security.
County Attorney John Choi and Sheriff Bob Fletcher said in ÔÇőthe statement that Thao was handcuffed, removed from his home without consent and ÔÇődriven around and questioned while in custody for about an hour before being returned home.
The two "criminal ÔÇîillegal Ôüáaliens" who DHS said it was seeking in that raid turned out to be elsewhere, one of them behind bars in a Minneapolis-area prison serving a four-year sentence.
Asked about the investigation, DHS said in a statement that U.S. Immigration and Customs ÔÇőEnforcement does not kidnap ÔÇőpeople.
It said its Ôüálaw enforcement officers were executing a warrant and concluded through surveillance and intelligence that suspected sexual predators had ties to the ÔÇőproperty, adding that a U.S. citizen at the house refused ÔÇőto be Ôüáfingerprinted or identified facially.
DHS added: "As with any law enforcement agency, it is standard protocol to hold all individuals in a house of an operation for safety of the public Ôüáand ÔÇőlaw enforcement.ÔÇŁ
Trump earlier this year deployed thousands of ÔÇőarmed agents in and around Minneapolis to detain and deport migrants in an operation in which agents fatally ÔÇőshot two American citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti .

item.title: TRADING DAY US stocks gain, dollar dips on hopes for Iran war negotiations
item.description: NEW YORK, April 13 (Reuters) - Wall Street's main indexes rallied to close sharply higher on Monday as hopes for de-escalation in the Middle East helped investors look past the failed U.S.-Iran talks and focus on the start of first-quarter earnings season.

item.title: North Korea tests cruise and anti-ship missiles from naval destroyer
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - North Korea conducted another test-fire of strategic cruise missiles ÔÇőand anti-warship missiles on Sunday as part ÔÇîof operational efficiency trials of its destroyer Choe Hyon, state media KCNA said on Tuesday.
Leader Kim Jong Un ÔÇőobserved the test alongside senior defence officials ÔÇőand naval commanders, the report said.
Two strategic cruise Ôüámissiles and three anti-warship missiles were fired ÔÇőto check the warship's integrated weapons command system, train ÔÇőcrews in missile-launch procedures and verify the accuracy and anti-jamming performance of upgraded navigation systems, KCNA said.
The cruise missiles ÔÇőflew for about 7,869 to 7,920 seconds and ÔÇőthe anti-warship missiles for about 1,960 to 1,973 seconds over ÔÇîwaters Ôüáoff the country's western coast, striking their targets with what the report described as ultra-precision accuracy.
KCNA said Kim was briefed the same day on ÔÇőweapons system plans ÔÇőfor two Ôüáadditional destroyers under construction.
Kim said strengthening what he called the country's nuclear ÔÇőwar deterrent remained a top priority, and ÔÇőcalled Ôüáfor improving strategic and tactical strike capabilities and rapid-response readiness, the report said.
Pyongyang first test-fired weapons on Ôüáthe 5,000-ton ÔÇőChoe Hyon-class warship in April ÔÇő2025, and Kim personally oversaw a missile test from the ship ÔÇőlast March.

item.title: Bloom Energy to supply up to 2.8 GW of fuel cells under expanded Oracle deal
item.description: April 13 (Reuters) - Bloom Energy (BE.N) , opens new tab said on Monday it would supply Oracle (ORCL.N) , opens new tab with up to 2.8 ÔÇőgigawatts of fuel cell capacity under ÔÇîan expanded deal as rising artificial intelligence use drives up power consumption.

item.title: Peak oil price likely to come 'in next few weeks,' US Energy secretary says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Oil prices are likely to ÔÇőhit their peak "in the next few ÔÇîweeks" once ship traffic resumes through the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. Department of Energy ÔÇőSecretary Chris Wright said on ÔÇőMonday.
Prices are expected to continue rising until "meaningful" Ôüáship traffic resumes through the ÔÇőstrait, Wright told the Semafor World Economy ÔÇőForum in Washington, despite previous comments he made that oil prices would likely come down ÔÇősoon.
"We're going to see energy prices ÔÇőhigh - and maybe even rising - until we get ÔÇîmeaningful Ôüáship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz," Wright said. "That'll probably hit the peak oil price at that time. That's ÔÇőprobably sometime ÔÇőin Ôüáthe next few weeks."
U.S. President Donald Trump said on Sunday ÔÇőthat the price of oil ÔÇőand Ôüágasoline may remain high until November's midterm elections, a rare admission of the Ôüápotential ÔÇőpolitical fallout from his ÔÇődecision to attack Iran six weeks ago.

item.title: IMF, World Bank, IEA urge countries to stop hoarding energy supplies, imposing export controls
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The International Monetary Fund, World Bank and International Energy Agency on ÔÇőMonday urged countries to avoid hoarding energy supplies and imposing export controls that could worsen what they called the biggest ÔÇîshock ever to the global energy market.
IEA chief Fatih Birol told reporters after a meeting with IMF and World Bank leaders that several countries were holding onto stocks and imposing export restrictions, and appealed to all countries to let energy stocks flow to the markets. He did not name the countries.
"Do no harm," said IMF Managing Director ÔÇőKristalina Georgieva, noting that she was meeting with countries that were being hit hard in Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa and some South ÔÇőPacific islands that were worried about supplies.
"The first principle should be: don't impose export restrictions that are only making Ôüáthe disequilibrium worse," she said, adding that the war would have a more severe impact on growth and inflation if it continued for ÔÇőa prolonged period.
The U.S. military on Monday began a blockade of ships leaving Iran's ports and Tehran threatened to retaliate against its Gulf neighbors' ports after ÔÇőweekend talks in Islamabad on ending the war broke down. Oil prices jumped back over $100 per barrel, with no sign of a swift reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, which carries 20% of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas.
Birol told an Atlantic Council event earlier that the conflict had triggered the worst global energy disruption ever, ÔÇőwith more than 80 oil and gas facilities across the Middle East damaged to date. He said the situation was bad in March, when ÔÇősome cargoes had been loaded, but could worsen this month.
"The scale of the problem is huge, and countries will suffer under this, some more than others, but ÔÇîI can Ôüátell you... no country is immune," Birol said.
The leaders of the three institutions vowed to keep coordinating their responses to the conflict in the Middle East , which has sent oil prices up by 50% since it began on February 28. The shock has also driven gas and fertilizer prices higher, triggering concerns about food security and job losses.
"We recognize that when we act together, the impact of our action is higher. We are more efficient, ÔÇőwe help the membership the most," ÔÇőGeorgieva said.
The statement Ôüánoted that the situation remained very uncertain, and even after a resumption of regular shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz, it would take time for global supplies of key commodities to move back toward pre-conflict ÔÇőlevels.
The IMF and World Bank have said they expect to downgrade their growth forecasts and lift their inflation numbers ÔÇőas a result Ôüáof the war. The IMF will release new forecasts on Tuesday, and the IEA is due to release a new monthly oil market report. The war has cast a deep shadow over the spring meetings of the IMF and World Bank, being held in Washington this week.
Birol said the IEA had already Ôüáreleased some ÔÇő400 million barrels of oil from its reserves and was prepared to take further action ÔÇőif additional releases were deemed necessary.
"The 400 million is only 20% of our reserves. We have still 80% in our pocket," he said. "We are assessing the situation, and if and ÔÇőwhen we decided it is the time, we are ready to act and act immediately."

item.title: Vance says US made a lot of progress in talks with Iran
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The United States made a lot of progress ÔÇőin talks ÔÇőwith Iran, U.S. Vice President JD ÔüáVance said in ÔÇőan interview on ÔÇőFox News' Special Report with Bret Baier on Monday.
Vance, ÔÇőasked whether more ÔÇőtalks were coming, said the ÔÇîball Ôüáwas in Iran's court.
He added that the U.S. expects Iran ÔÇőwill ÔÇőmake Ôüáprogress on opening the Strait ÔÇőof Hormuz, warning ÔÇőthat Ôüáthe negotiation would change if Tehran does Ôüánot.

item.title: US crude futures fall over $1 as traders weigh supply risks
item.description:
BENGALURU, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. crude futures fell more ÔÇőthan $1 in early ÔÇőAsian trade on Tuesday Ôüáas traders balanced ÔÇőheightened supply risks linked ÔÇőto a U.S. blockade of Iranian shipping in the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz ÔÇőwith reports that Washington and Tehran ÔÇîwere Ôüástill open to talks after a tense meeting in Islamabad.
U.S. ÔÇőWest ÔÇőTexas ÔüáIntermediate was at $97.76 a barrel, down $1.32, ÔÇőor 1.3% ÔÇőby Ôüá2205 GMT, following a 1.5% gain in Ôüáthe ÔÇőprevious session.
(This story has been corrected to say U.S. West Texas Intermediate was down $1.32 a barrel, not up, in paragraph 2)

item.title: Chevron agrees to asset swap in Venezuela to focus on heavy oil projects
item.description:
CARACAS/HOUSTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab signed two key agreements on Monday to expand ÔÇîoperations at Venezuela's vast Orinoco Belt, including an asset swap adding an extra heavy crude area to its main project while returning an offshore gas field and a small crude area, executives and officials said at an event.
The agreements are among the first big expansion deals since the U.S. launched ÔÇőa $100 billion reconstruction plan for Venezuela's energy sector after capturing President Nicolas Maduro , and a sweeping reform of ÔÇőthe country's main oil law was approved in January, encouraging foreign investment.
The pacts, expected to allow Ôüáthe U.S. major to boost crude output and participation at the OPEC country's main oil region, were signed ÔÇőby company executives led by Javier La Rosa, head of Chevron's Base Assets and Emerging Countries, and officials from state-owned ÔÇőcompany PDVSA in the presence of acting President Delcy Rodriguez.
The deals include the increase of Chevron's stake at one of its joint ventures with PDVSA in the Orinoco, Petroindependencia, to 49% from a previous 35.8%.
The company also agreed to relinquish two gas blocks that include the ÔÇőcoveted Loran offshore field and its stake at a small oil project in western Venezuela, while receiving a new ÔÇőoil area, Ayacucho 8, as part of its existing Petropiar project also in the Orinoco, the company's largest.
The deals give Chevron, PDVSA's main ÔÇîjoint Ôüáventure partner, a strong foot to expand heavy oil projects in the country amid expected increased competition with foreign companies.
Chevron's asset swap with PDVSA and its subsidiaries is "a mutually beneficial agreement, which will consolidate all parties' focus on strategic assets in the country," the company said in a release after the event.
Chevron executives said in January the firm could increase ÔÇőoutput in Venezuela by about ÔÇő50% in the next Ôüátwo years within its existing footprint. The company's joint ventures with PDVSA are producing 260,000 barrels per day of crude, about a fourth of the country's total output.
The agreements will ÔÇőallow Venezuela and Chevron "to progress to increase output and secure revenue for the benefit ÔÇőof the people," ÔüáRodriguez said during the broadcast event.
Another energy major progressing to rapidly expand in Venezuela, Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , is expected to sign a separate deal later this week to develop the Loran gas field, which extends into Trinidad and Tobago, as a single project Ôüáwith its ÔÇőManatee field, sources said.
The possible reassignment of the area would provide ÔÇőthe fastest alternative to start gas output at the 7.3 trillion-cubic-foot field, whose gas Trinidad needs to produce and export liquefied natural gas.

item.title: Petrobras in initial talks to buy back Brazil refinery from Mubadala, sources say
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, April 13 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil firm Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab ÔÇőis in initial direct negotiations with Abu Dhabi's ÔÇîMubadala sovereign wealth fund to repurchase the Mataripe refinery in Brazil, two sources with knowledge of the matter told Reuters on ÔÇőMonday.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva said last ÔÇőmonth that Petrobras would repurchase the refinery, which had Ôüábeen sold during the administration of his predecessor ÔÇőJair Bolsonaro. After Lula's remarks, Petrobras had said it would ÔÇőanalyze a potential deal.
A deal could be inked by the end of this year, the sources said. Mataripe is Brazil's second largest ÔÇőrefinery, but is operating at only around 60% ÔÇőof capacity, while Petrobras' plants are operating at their limit in ÔÇîorder Ôüáto boost local production, one source said.
Petrobras' plans to increase refining capacity have gained fresh urgency after the U.S.-Israel conflict with Iran caused global diesel prices to ÔÇősurge, impacting Brazilian ÔÇőconsumers due Ôüáto the country's dependency on foreign diesel. Brazil imports about a quarter of its ÔÇőtotal diesel needs.
The rise in fuel prices ÔÇőhas Ôüábecome a major concern for Lula ahead of a presidential election in October, where he will seek a fourth Ôüánon-consecutive ÔÇőterm.
Petrobras did not immediately comment. Mubadala ÔÇődeclined to comment.

item.title: Benin's Wadagni wins presidency in landslide with over 94% of votes
item.description:
COTONOU, April 13 (Reuters) - Benin's ÔÇőFinance Minister Romuald ÔÇîWadagni secured a landslide victory in the West ÔÇőAfrican nation's ÔÇőApril 12 presidential election, garnering Ôüáover 94% of ÔÇővotes, provisional results ÔÇőfrom the electoral commission showed on Monday.
Sacca Lafia, head ÔÇőof the country's ÔÇőindependent electoral commission, announced on ÔÇînational Ôüátelevision that the result was based on more than 90% ÔÇőof ÔÇővotes Ôüácounted, highlighting Wadagni's insurmountable lead.
Voter ÔÇőturnout for the ÔÇőelection Ôüástood at 58.78%, Lafia added.
Opposition candidate Paul ÔüáHounkpe conceded ÔÇődefeat earlier ÔÇőon Monday.

item.title: Man charged after Molotov cocktail attack on OpenAI CEO Sam Altman's home
item.description:
April 13 (Reuters) - A Texas man was charged with hurling a Molotov cocktail at the home of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and attempting to set fire to ÔÇőthe AI firm's headquarters.
Daniel Moreno-Gama, 20, was captured on surveillance video ÔÇîthrowing an incendiary device outside Altman's San Francisco residence, according to a Federal Bureau of Investigation affidavit filed in federal court on Monday.
They said Moreno-Gama was carrying a ÔÇőself-authored "anti-AI" document when the San Francisco Police Department arrested him.
According to ÔÇőthe FBI, the document Moreno-Gama wrote - which was cited as Ôüáevidence of motive - contained threats directed at Altman.
"We are only at the ÔÇőbeginning of this investigation, but if the evidence shows that Mr. Moreno-Gama executed ÔÇőthese attacks to change public policy or to coerce government and other officials, we will treat this as an act of domestic terrorism and together with our law enforcement ÔÇőpartners prosecute him to the fullest extent allowed by law," U.S. Attorney ÔÇőCraig Missakian said in a statement.
OpenAI faces growing scrutiny for its role in national security, ÔÇîincluding Ôüácriticism of a proposed deal with the U.S. government to allow its artificial intelligence technology to be used in classified military operations.
Moreno-Gama is charged with attempted damage and destruction of property by means of explosives and possession of ÔÇőan unregistered firearm.
Police ÔÇősaid he traveled Ôüáfrom Texas to California before carrying out the attacks and they recovered multiple incendiary devices, kerosene and a lighter ÔÇőat the time of his arrest.
Moreno-Gama told security personnel ÔÇőat the Ôüácompany's headquarters that he intended to burn the building and harm the people inside, according to court records.
If convicted, he faces a mandatory minimum sentence of Ôüáfive years ÔÇőand up to 20 years in prison ÔÇőon the explosives charge, and up to 10 years in prison on the unregistered firearm charge, ÔÇőcourt filings showed.

item.title: US kills two men in strike in Eastern Pacific
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - The U.S. ÔÇőmilitary said on ÔÇîMonday that it had ÔÇőcarried out ÔÇőa strike in the ÔüáEastern ÔÇőPacific that killed ÔÇőtwo men.
The U.S. Southern Command wrote ÔÇőon ÔÇőX: "Intelligence confirmed the vessel ÔÇîwas Ôüátransiting along known narco-trafficking routes in ÔÇőthe ÔÇőEastern ÔüáPacific and was ÔÇőengaged in ÔÇőnarco-trafficking Ôüáoperations."

item.title: The commodities supercycle is here. How might investors participate?
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - Commodities supercycles can reshape markets for years, if not decades. We appear to be in one again, as the Iran war has amplified several long-term bullish trends. The question for many investors has shifted from whether to engage with the supercycle to how.
Following the initial U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran on ÔÇőFebruary 28, oil recorded its largest monthly gain in history. Combine that with copper and gold's substantial gains since mid-2025 ÔÇô as well as the ongoing race for energy, metals and minerals to fuel the artificial intelligence arms ÔÇîrace ÔÇô and a commodities supercycle appears to be underway.
Commodities supercycles are long, powerful waves driven by major structural shifts. Just think of the oil shocks of the 1970s and China's urbanisation boom in the early 2000s.
The question, as always, is how investors can participate.
Commodities are often treated as a single asset class, but a bull market is not necessarily a synchronous move across the entire complex. Instead, leadership tends to shift. So rotation within the asset class can be as important as its overall direction.
For example, a geopolitical shock may lift oil prices immediately, yet copper or gold may lag ÔÇőor even fall as investors cut crowded positions or reassess growth risks.
This occurred in 2022 when energy prices surged after Russia's invasion of Ukraine , while some industrial metals retraced as recession concerns mounted.
WeÔÇÖre seeing such divergence again today.
Oil prices spiked 64% ÔÇőin March after the Iran war began as energy supply was squeezed, functioning as a good portfolio hedge against equity market volatility. Yet gold fell by 12%, recording its worst month since Ôüá2008 , largely because it entered the shock inflated by months of heavy speculative buying and was one of the few liquid assets investors could sell to meet margin calls.
Given these uneven shifts ÔÇô and the massive geopolitical uncertainty ÔÇô what are some of the ÔÇőkey points that investors should keep in mind as they consider riding the latest commodity wave?
First, investors should remember that small shifts in commodity allocations can have a massive impact on price.
Commodities are enormously important to the real economy, but as ÔÇőinvestable markets they are small relative to stocks and bonds. Within the S&P 500, energy and materials together account for only about 6% of the index, compared with more than 30% for the "Magnificent Seven" tech giants.
Size matters because price is set at the margin. If even a modest amount of capital rotates out of broad equities or bonds and into commodities ÔÇô whether through physical exposure or listed derivatives ÔÇô the impact on prices could be outsized. A relatively small market can absorb only so much new capital before price adjusts.
Interconnectedness is another key issue: one commodity's move can reshape the ÔÇőoutlook for others.
Many commodities are linked through potential demand substitution or as raw materials for each other. For example, high natural gas prices can lead to more oil consumption. Rising energy prices feed into fertiliser, freight and food prices. Copper and aluminium ÔÇőcan substitute for each other in some industrial applications.
Another point to consider is inventories. While firms may prize cost-efficiency when supply is unconstrained, geopolitical shocks can spur a move towards higher inventory levels.
The Iran conflict is a case in point. TehranÔÇÖs ability to disrupt roughly one-fifth of ÔÇîthe worldÔÇÖs energy Ôüásupply by blocking the narrow Strait of Hormuz has underscored how vulnerable energy and other goods are to chokepoints.
Moving forward, governments and companies are likely to prioritise security of supply of many goods, producing higher inventory levels across a wide set of strategic materials. That could create a broader structural premium across the complex.
The next decision is whether to own the underlying commodities or commodities-related equities.
While physical commodities primarily respond to current and near-term supply-demand imbalances, commodity-related equities are impacted by a host of other factors, including each companyÔÇÖs hedging policy, capital allocation decisions, project pipelines, position in the commodity value chain, location and even geological issues.
Consider the energy industry. When crude prices spike, upstream energy firms ÔÇô those involved in exploration and production ÔÇô tend to benefit, while refiners and petrochemical firms ÔÇőmay suffer from higher input costs.
Not all upstream producers benefit ÔÇőequally, of course.
During the current conflict, U.S. shale production has Ôüábeen insulated from direct damage. These producers also have more flexibility to ramp up or down than most of their Middle Eastern counterparts, due to differing geological profiles and production methods.
Moreover, many U.S. oil companies were well adapted to the pre-Iran war price regime of roughly $70 per barrel, generating healthy free cash flow at those levels. At current prices of around $100, the windfall could be substantial.
Of course, ÔÇőenergy companies ÔÇô no matter where theyÔÇÖre located ÔÇô also come with their own bespoke risks related to management, debt issues, long-term strategic concerns and other issues ÔÇô something one doesnÔÇÖt have to ÔÇőconsider when investing in the commodity itself.
Finally, investors should not underestimate one additional feature of commodities' return profile: positive convexity. Price upside is often much larger than downside.
This is rooted in commoditiesÔÇÖ physical nature and direct links to productive activities that are necessary to keep economies humming.
Investors can delay purchases of financial assets or short sell them when prices rise.
The same logic rarely applies to physical commodities, where consumption is hard to postpone or reduce quickly. Demand destruction may eventually emerge as prices increase, but often not fast enough to prevent a sharp squeeze first.
That asymmetry is one Ôüáreason commodity cycles, ÔÇőonce they take hold, can prove more forceful than many investors expect. Understanding this can be the difference between being well positioned for a cycle ÔÇőand being run over by it.
(The views expressed here are those of the author. Taosha Wang is a portfolio manager and creator of the ÔÇťThematically ThinkingÔÇŁ newsletter at Fidelity International.)
