And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , ÔÇőor the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: OPEC lowers second-quarter global oil demand forecast on Iran war
item.description:
LONDON, April 13 (Reuters) - OPEC on Monday lowered its forecast for world oil demand in the second quarter by 500,000 barrels per day, the producer group's monthly oil report showed in its first public assessment of the Iran war's impact ÔÇőon the market.
OPEC sees a smaller hit to oil demand from the war this year ÔÇőthan some other forecasters, such as the U.S. government's Energy Information Administration . OPEC also made Ôüáno change to its full-year outlook as it sees consumption rebounding in later months.
The war has effectively ÔÇőclosed the Strait of Hormuz, the worldÔÇÖs most important oil route, shutting in millions of barrels of ÔÇőMiddle East production and sending fuel prices soaring. The price surge is pressuring consumers and businesses across the globe, and triggering government action to conserve supplies.
Global oil demand is projected to average 105.07 million bpd in the second quarter, OPEC's ÔÇőreport said, down from the 105.57 million bpd forecast in last month's report.
"The demand growth for the ÔÇősecond quarter of 2026 is revised down for both the OECD and non-OECD, driven mainly by slight transitory weakness ÔÇîin oil Ôüádemand growth, given ongoing developments in the Middle East," OPEC said, referring to countries inside or outside the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development grouping of industrialised nations.
OPEC kept unchanged its forecast that world oil demand will rise by 1.38 million bpd in 2026, in contrast to ÔÇőthe EIA which halved ÔÇőits prediction in an ÔüáApril 7 report.
OPEC+, which includes the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries plus other producers such as Russia, had agreed to resume oil production hikes as ÔÇőof April, although the report highlighted how steeply output has fallen since the ÔÇőIran conflict Ôüábegan at the end of February.
Crude oil output by OPEC+ averaged 35.06 million bpd in March, down 7.70 million bpd from February, citing secondary sources, the report showed, with Iraq and Saudi Arabia making the biggest cuts.
OPEC+ agreed Ôüáon ÔÇőApril 5 to raise its oil output quotas by 206,000 bpd ÔÇőfor May, a modest rise that will largely exist on paper as long as key members are unable to raise production ÔÇődue to the effective Hormuz blockage.

item.title: Dozens detained in New York City protest over US arms sales to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - Dozens of protesters were detained by police in New York City on Monday during demonstrations calling for the blocking ÔÇőof arms sales to Israel and an end to U.S. ÔÇîmilitary support for its ally.
Demonstrators included antiwar group Jewish Voice for Peace, which said around 90 people were detained. Among those detained was whistleblower Chelsea Manning , a former ÔÇőU.S. Army soldier and WikiLeaks source.
The New York City Police Department ÔÇősaid there were "multiple" arrests but did not provide a number.
Clips Ôüáfrom the protests showed a crowd gathering near the offices of ÔÇőU.S. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer and his Democratic colleague, Senator Kirsten ÔÇőGillibrand.
Protesters chanted slogans like "stop the bombs," "end the killings" and "free Palestine" while expressing opposition to the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran , Israel's attacks in Lebanon and Israel's assault on Gaza .
The protesters also ÔÇőchanted "let Gaza live," "let Iran live" and "let Lebanon live."
The U.S. and Israel ÔÇőattacked Iran on February 28. Iran responded with its own strikes on Israel and ÔÇîGulf Ôüástates that host U.S. bases. U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran and Israeli attacks in Lebanon have killed thousands and displaced millions .
President Donald Trump's administration has cracked down on protests by attempting to deport foreign students , threatening to freeze funding for universities where ÔÇőprotests were held and ÔÇőordering screening of Ôüáimmigrants' online comments . The crackdown has faced judicial obstacles.
New York City was at the heart of pro-Palestinian protests ÔÇőin 2024.
U.S. military support for Israel has faced particular ÔÇőscrutiny from Ôüárights groups during Israel's war in Gaza that killed tens of thousands , caused a hunger crisis, internally displaced Gaza's entire population and led to assessments of genocide Ôüáfrom scholars ÔÇőand a United Nations inquiry .
Israel calls its actions self-defense ÔÇőafter Hamas-led militants killed 1,200 people and took more than 250 hostages in an October 2023 attack .

item.title: Prince Harry and Meghan arrive in Australia to a muted welcome
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain's Prince Harry and wife Meghan landed in Australia on Tuesday for a ÔÇőfour-day visit with engagements covering sport, mental health and veterans' affairs.
The Duke and ÔÇîDuchess of Sussex will begin their trip with a visit to a children's hospital in Melbourne, before Meghan attends a women's domestic violence shelter, their office said in a statement.
"Their programme will focus on ÔÇőmental health, community resilience and support for veterans and their families, alongside private ÔÇőmeetings and special projects," the statement said.
The couple will travel to the Ôüácapital, Canberra, on Wednesday to meet military veterans, attend a mental health summit in ÔÇőMelbourne on Thursday and round off the trip with sailing and rugby events in Sydney ÔÇőon Friday.
The Sussexes stepped down as working members of the British royal family and moved to the U.S. in 2020, citing a desire to be financially independent and to escape what they characterised as ÔÇőmedia intrusion into their private lives.
They last visited Australia in 2018 while still working ÔÇőroyals, announcing Meghan's first pregnancy hours after arriving in Sydney.
Their latest visit has captured public attention in ÔÇîAustralia, where ÔüáBritain's King Charles is the head of state, though a sizeable minority supports becoming a republic.
But there was little sign of the crowds and official reception that greeted them on their previous trip. Television networks aired footage that they said showed the couple arriving ÔÇőin Melbourne on a ÔÇőcommercial flight from ÔüáLos Angeles, before being taken from the tarmac in a vehicle convoy.
The couple's travel is being privately funded, though local media reported some ÔÇőpolicing costs associated with the visit would be paid by Australian ÔÇőtaxpayers, sparking Ôüáa protest petition signed by more than 45,000 people.
In contrast to their previous visit, the Sussexes will also undertake commercial activities while in Australia, with Meghan remaining in the country to Ôüáhost ÔÇőa wellness retreat at a luxury beachside hotel in ÔÇőSydney over the weekend.
Tickets for the event, which includes yoga, manifestation and sound healing, begin at A$2,699 ($1,912) per person.
($1 = ÔÇő1.4114 Australian dollars)

item.title: Colombia's Petro walks back 100% tariffs on Ecuadorean goods
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 13 (Reuters) - Colombian President Gustavo Petro on Monday said his country would not impose 100% tariffs on imports from Ecuador, reversing an announcement made last week ÔÇőby the trade ministry.
Instead, Petro said Colombia would introduce subsidies and what ÔÇîhe described as "smart" tariffs, without providing further details.
"There are no 100% tariffs, trade minister, we are not that stupid," Petro said during a televised cabinet meeting. "Everything that is necessary for Colombia, 0%, ÔÇőit comes in."
Last week, Ecuador said it would raise tariffs on imports from ÔÇőColombia to 100% starting in May, citing what it called Colombia's Ôüáfailure to implement concrete and effective security measures along their shared border.
Following the ÔÇőannouncement, Colombia's Ministry of Trade, Industry and Tourism said it would raise tariffs on ÔÇőimports from Ecuador to 100% from 30% at present, amid mounting diplomatic tensions between the two countries.
"Everything that is produced in Colombia but was being imported from Ecuador should be produced in ÔÇőColombia, with subsidies from the Agriculture Ministry so it can be cheaper," Petro said. "Everything ÔÇőproduced in Colombia that cannot be exported to Ecuador because of the tariff should be exported ÔÇîto ÔüáVenezuela, where it is needed."
The trade dispute between the two countries began in January, when the government of Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa imposed a 30% tariff on imports from Colombia. Petro's government responded with a similar measure and also suspended electricity sales ÔÇőto the country.
Noboa ÔÇőjustified the successive Ôüátariff increases by pointing to Ecuador's trade deficit with Colombia and what he called a lack of cooperation from Bogota in ÔÇőcombating drug trafficking along the shared border, where illegal armed groups ÔÇőoperate, including Ôüáleftist guerrillas involved in drug trafficking and illegal gold mining.
Petro rejected the accusations, saying Colombia has maintained operations against drug trafficking and illegal armed groups along the border, Ôüáas shown ÔÇőby cocaine seizures.
Colombia posted a trade surplus of $1.016 ÔÇőbillion with Ecuador in 2025, with exports of $1.847 billion and imports of $830.1 million, according to DANE, Colombia's statistics ÔÇőagency.

item.title: Bahamas police release Michigan man after his wife vanishes at sea
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 13 (Reuters) - A 59-year-old Michigan man was released by police following the disappearance of his wife while ÔÇősailing in The Bahamas, Bahamian Police Commissioner Shanta Knowles ÔÇîtold Reuters on Monday.
Brian Hooker's release follows his arrest by Bahamian police on April 8 after he was initially questioned as a witness ÔÇőin the disappearance.
His lawyer, Terrel Butler, has denied any ÔÇőwrongdoing on his part. Butler said Hooker was transferred Ôüáfrom Abaco, where he was questioned by police, to ÔÇőGrand Bahama, the archipelago's second most populous island.
Hooker's wife, Lynette, ÔÇő55, went missing while the Michigan couple were traveling from Hope Town, Abaco, to Elbow Cay on April 4, police said in an initial ÔÇőreport on the incident.
The pair were sailing on an ÔÇő8-foot (2.4 m) hard-bottom dinghy, called "Soulmate," when Lynette fell overboard with the boat's ÔÇîkeys, Ôüáaccording to police. Police said Brian paddled to shore after losing sight of his wife.
After arriving on shore, according to the report, he informed an individual of the incident and ÔÇőthe police were ÔÇőalerted.
In the Ôüádays that followed, police took Hooker by boat to search the Soulmate, his lawyer said, adding ÔÇőhe remained handcuffed "despite the choppy and dangerous ÔÇősea conditions."
Hooker Ôüáwas injured during the search after he "lost his footing and fell overboard," Butler said. She said Hooker sustained an injury Ôüáto his ÔÇőknee and an abrasion.
He was taken ÔÇőto a Grand Bahama hospital where he received treatment, Butler said.

item.title: US-sanctioned Chinese tanker passes Strait of Hormuz despite US blockade, data shows
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 14 (Reuters) - A Chinese tanker sanctioned by the United States passed ÔÇőthrough the Strait of Hormuz ÔÇîon Tuesday despite a U.S. blockade on the chokepoint, shipping data showed.
The Rich ÔÇőStarry would be the first ÔÇőto make it through the strait Ôüáand to exit the Gulf since ÔÇőthe blockade began, data from LSEG, ÔÇőMarineTraffic and Kpler showed.
The tanker and its owner Shanghai Xuanrun Shipping Co Ltd were ÔÇősanctioned by the United States for ÔÇődealing with Iran. The company could not be ÔÇîimmediately Ôüáreached for comment.
Rich Starry is a medium-range tanker that is carrying about 250,000 barrels of methanol on ÔÇőboard, according ÔÇőto the Ôüádata. It loaded the cargo at its last port ÔÇőof call, the United Arab ÔÇőEmirates' ÔüáHamriyah, the data showed.
The Chinese-owned tanker has Chinese crew on board, the Ôüádata ÔÇőshowed.

item.title: Rising fuel prices weigh on UK consumers, surveys show
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Surging fuel prices caused by the conflict in Iran weighed on British consumers last month, dampening household spending as travel plans ÔÇőwere shelved, surveys showed on Tuesday.
Barclays said consumer morale remained ÔÇîresilient despite falling confidence in the economic outlook.
A separate survey from the British Retail Consortium (BRC) showed a boost in retail sales related to the timing of the ÔÇőEaster holidays, but also showed a hit to spending on ÔÇőtravel-related items.
Barclays said:
ÔÇó Consumer spending grew by 0.9% in annual Ôüáterms in March after a 1.0% rise in February
ÔÇó Travel spending ÔÇőfell by 3.3% year-on-year - the first such drop since March 2021, during ÔÇőthe COVID-19 pandemic
ÔÇó Travel agents and airlines were the biggest contributors to the drop
ÔÇó One in seven consumers surveyed by Barclays said they were delaying major purchases ÔÇőin response to the uncertainty in the Middle East, while the ÔÇősame proportion said they were building up a savings buffer
ÔÇó "Shoppers delaying major purchases and ÔÇîbuilding Ôüáup a savings buffer in response to the shock from the Middle East reinforces our view that activity will be muted in the coming months," said Barclays economist Jack Meaning
ÔÇó Barclays' survey covered February 20 - ÔÇőMarch 26
The BRC ÔÇősaid:
ÔÇó Total Ôüáretail sales increased by 3.6% in annual terms in March, against growth of 1.1% in February
ÔÇó Food sales ÔÇőincreased sharply, related to the early timing of the ÔÇőEaster holidays
ÔÇó "Non-food Ôüáperformance was more uneven: demand was robust for computers, toys, and homeware, but clothing and footwear continued to struggle. The disruption to international travel Ôüácaused ÔÇőby the Middle East conflict also hit ÔÇősales of travel-related good," said BRC chief executive Helen Dickinson
ÔÇó The BRC survey covered the ÔÇőfive weeks to April 4

item.title: Japan's greenhouse gas emissions fell to record low in FY2024
item.description:
TOKYO, April 14 (Reuters) - Japan's greenhouse gas emissions fell by 1.9% in fiscal 2024, which ended in March 2025, government data showed on ÔÇőTuesday, the third straight annual fall and the lowest since ÔÇîrecords began on lower manufacturing energy use and greater use of renewable and nuclear power.
Japan's GHG emissions totalled 1.046 billion metric tons of carbon dioxide equivalent in fiscal ÔÇő2024, down from 1.067 billion tons a year earlier, environment ÔÇőministry data showed.
The figure marks the lowest level since records Ôüábegan in 1990/91 and the third consecutive annual decline.
By sector, carbon ÔÇődioxide (CO2) emissions from industry fell 2.5%, while the commercial and services sector ÔÇősaw a 0.2% increase. Household emissions declined 0.7%, and the transportation sector recorded a 1.6% drop.
Japan aims to cut GHG emissions by 46% from 2013 levels by 2030. ÔÇőThe fiscal 2024 figure represents a 24.9% reduction from 2013.
GHG absorption ÔÇőby forests and other sinks fell 2.9% to 52.3 million tons, the ministry said, ÔÇîadding Ôüáthat Japan's total GHG emissions, after accounting for absorption by sinks such as forests, stood at 994 million tons, down 1.9% from a year earlier.
Emissions surged after the 2011 Fukushima nuclear disaster, which led to widespread ÔÇőnuclear reactor shutdowns ÔÇőand greater reliance Ôüáon fossil fuels. They peaked at 1.394 billion tons in fiscal 2013 before trending downward, supported by increased use ÔÇőof renewable energy and the gradual restart of nuclear ÔÇőreactors.
In Ôüáfiscal 2024, renewable energy accounted for 23.1% of the 991.1 billion kilowatt-hours of electricity generated, up 0.2 percentage points from a year earlier. Nuclear energy Ôüácontributed ÔÇő9.4%, up 0.9 percentage points.
Thermal power made up ÔÇő67.5% of total electricity generation, down 1.1 percentage points year-on-year, consisting of 28.1% coal, 32.2% ÔÇőnatural gas, and 7.2% oil.

item.title: India forecasts sub-par monsoon after two years of above-average rains
item.description:
NEW DELHI, April 13 (Reuters) - India is likely to see below-average monsoon rains for the first time in three years in 2026, the government said on Monday, stoking concerns over farm ÔÇőoutput and growth in Asia's third-largest economy as it battles inflation driven by the Iran war .
The monsoon is the lifeblood ÔÇîof India's nearly $4 trillion economy, delivering almost 70% of the rainfall needed to water farms and replenish aquifers and reservoirs.
The monsoon, which typically arrives over the southern state of Kerala around June 1 and retreats by mid-September, is expected to reach 92% of the long-period average (LPA) this year, M. Ravichandran, ÔÇősecretary in the Ministry of Earth Sciences, told a news conference.
The India Meteorological Department defines normal rainfall as between ÔÇő96% and 104% of a 50-year average of 87 cm (35 inches) for the four-month season.
"Currently weak ÔüáLa Nina-like conditions are transitioning to neutral conditions. But after June it's very likely that El Nino will develop," Mrutyunjay Mohapatra, ÔÇődirector-general of the India Meteorological Department, said.
El Nino is a weather phenomenon that occurs when ocean temperatures in the central and eastern ÔÇőPacific Ocean rise above normal, typically resulting in hot and dry weather in Southeast Asia and other parts of the world.
In the past, India has experienced below-average rainfall during most El Nino years, sometimes leading to severe droughts that destroyed crops and forced authorities to limit the export of some ÔÇőgrains.
"But in the latter part of the monsoon season, positive Indian Ocean Dipole (IOD) conditions are likely to develop and that will ÔÇőhelp the monsoon," Mohapatra said.
The IOD is a climate pattern marked by differences in sea-surface temperatures between the western and eastern Indian Ocean.
A positive ÔÇîIOD, Ôüádefined by warmer-than-normal waters in the western Indian Ocean, often supports stronger monsoon rainfall in India.
The IMD's first forecast of rainfall at 92% of the LPA is the lowest in nearly three decades. It will issue an updated outlook for the season in the last week of May.
"This, along with the impending impact of the ongoing crisis in (the Middle East), poses downside risks to India's GDP ÔÇőgrowth in financial year 2026-27," ÔÇősaid Aditi Nayar, chief economist Ôüáat rating agency ICRA.
The government has forecast India's economy will grow by between 6.8% and 7.2% in the fiscal year that started on April 1.
Lower rainfall forecasts also pose material upside risks ÔÇőto the retail inflation trajectory this fiscal year, with average inflation "could well exceed 4.5%," Nayar said. Inflation ÔÇőstood at 3.4% Ôüáin March, data showed on Monday.
India is the world's biggest exporter of rice and onions, and the second-biggest producer of sugar.
The world's largest importer of edible oils, India currently fulfils nearly two-thirds of its demand through overseas purchases of palm oil, soyoil and sunflower oil, Ôüáprimarily from ÔÇőIndonesia, Malaysia, Argentina, Brazil, Russia, and Ukraine.
"Lower rainfall is likely to increase India's ÔÇőedible oil imports and eliminate the possibility of sugar exports in the next season," said a Mumbai-based dealer with a global trade house.

item.title: Surging petrol prices drive record EV sales in Europe in March
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - High petrol prices in Europe steered car buyers towards EVs in a record-beating March, and led to the first month of global ÔÇőEV sales growth this year, data by consultancy Benchmark Mineral ÔÇîIntelligence showed on Tuesday.
Governments worldwide have capped fuel prices to shield motorists from soaring costs after the war in Iran , which erupted on Feb. 28, disrupted a key shipping route carrying ÔÇőabout 20% of global oil supplies.
BMI said registrations, a proxy for ÔÇősales, of new battery-electric and plug-in hybrid cars rose 3% Ôüáyear on year globally to over 1.7 million cars, with a 37% jump ÔÇőin Europe to a monthly all-time high of almost 540,000 EVs sold.
While car ÔÇőregistrations lag sales, "there is a good portion of this that you can put down to the rise in petrol prices", BMI data manager Charles Lester said.
Growth was strongest in ÔÇőcountries that saw the sharpest increases in energy prices, including Australia, New Zealand, ÔÇőVietnam and Thailand, which together drove a 79% rise in EV registrations outside the three ÔÇîmain Ôüámarkets of China, Europe and North America, Lester added.
EV registrations in China, the world's largest car market, fell by 14% to over 850,000 vehicles sold, slowing a negative trend started in January after the country pulled ÔÇőfunding for auto trade-ins ÔÇőand a tax Ôüáexemption on EV purchases expired.
Lester said Chinese consumers, who had used the incentives to buy smaller EVs, were increasingly ÔÇőopting for larger vehicles.
In North America, EV registrations fell by ÔÇő30% to Ôüá121,500 vehicles sold in the month, the sixth consecutive year-on-year drop after the end of an EV tax credit scheme in the United States and proposals by ÔüáPresident Donald ÔÇőTrump's administration to further cut CO2 emission standards .
"It ÔÇőhas been its highest monthly figure since the tax credit ended, but the reality is the pullbacks ÔÇőhave happened", Lester said.

item.title: US begins Iran port blockade, oil prices ease on hopes for dialogue
item.description:
WASHINGTON/DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. military began a blockade of Iran's ports, angering Tehran and adding uncertainty around the crucial waterway, although hopes for dialogue to end the war provided some relief to ÔÇőoil markets where benchmark prices fell below $100 on Tuesday.
After a breakdown of weekend talks in Islamabad between the two adversaries, a U.S. official said there was continued engagement and ÔÇîforward motion on trying to get to an agreement. Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif also said efforts were still under way to resolve the conflict.
U.S. President Donald Trump said Iran had been in touch on Monday and wanted to make a deal but that he would not sanction any agreement allowing Tehran to have a nuclear weapon.
Since the United States and Israel began the war on February 28, Iran effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz to all vessels except its own, saying passage ÔÇőwould be permitted only under Iranian control and subject to a fee. The fallout has been widespread, since nearly a fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies flowed through the narrow ÔÇőwaterway before the start of the conflict.
Trump has said Washington would block Iranian vessels and any ships that paid such tolls and that any Iranian "fast-attack" ships Ôüáthat went near the blockade would be eliminated. Tehran has threatened to hit naval ships going through the strait and to retaliate against its Gulf neighbours' ports.
Shipping data on LSEG showed Chinese-owned oil-and-chemicals tanker Rich ÔÇőStarry passed through the strait on Tuesday - the first since the U.S. blockade began at 10 a.m. EDT (1400 GMT) on Monday. The vessel, which departed Sharjah anchorage off the coast of Dubai on Monday heading for China, had ÔÇőearlier turned back minutes after approaching the strait.
The U.S.'s blockade has further clouded the outlook for global energy security and the supply of a vast array of goods that relies on petroleum, and has little, if any, international backing.
NATO allies including Britain and France said they would not be drawn into the conflict by taking part in the blockade, stressing instead the need to reopen the waterway.
Despite the breakdown of talks between the U.S. and Iran on Sunday, Vice President JD Vance, who ÔÇőled the U.S. delegation, told Fox News on Monday the U.S. "made a lot of progress" by communicating to Tehran where the U.S. "could make some accommodation" and where it would remain inflexible.
He said Trump was adamant ÔÇőthat any enriched nuclear material must be removed from Iran and a mechanism must be established to verify that Iran is not developing nuclear weapons.
Tehran "moved in our direction, which is why I think we would say that we had ÔÇîsome good Ôüásigns, but they didn't move far enough," Vance said, without disclosing details.
The ceasefire that halted six weeks of U.S.-Israeli airstrikes and retaliatory fire from Iran across the Gulf looked in jeopardy, with only a week left to run.
The U.S. military's Central Command said the blockade would be "enforced impartially against vessels of all nations" entering or leaving Iranian ports in the Gulf and Gulf of Oman. It would not impede neutral transit passage through the Strait of Hormuz to or from non-Iranian destinations, it said in a note to seafarers seen by Reuters.
An Iranian military spokesperson called any U.S. restrictions on international shipping "piracy," warning that if Iranian ports were threatened, ÔÇőno port in the Gulf or Gulf of ÔÇőOman would be secure. Any military vessels approaching Ôüáthe strait would violate the ceasefire, Iran's Revolutionary Guards said.
Trump said Iran's navy had been "completely obliterated" during the war, adding that only a small number of "fast-attack ships" remained.
"Warning: If any of these ships come anywhere close to our BLOCKADE, they will be immediately ELIMINATED, using the same system of kill that we use against ÔÇőthe drug dealers on boats at Sea. It is quick and brutal," Trump said on social media.
He was apparently referring to the U.S. strikes carried ÔÇőout against suspected drug boats Ôüáin the Caribbean and Pacific. The strikes, which began in September, killed more than 160 people. The U.S. military has not provided evidence that the vessels were ferrying drugs.
With the war unpopular at home and rising energy prices causing political blowback, Trump paused the U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign last week after threatening to destroy Iran's "whole civilisation" unless it reopened the strait.
In a letter to the United Nations, Iran's U.N. delegation on Monday asked for reparations from ÔüáSaudi Arabia, ÔÇőthe UAE, Bahrain, Qatar and Jordan, alleging they have allowed their territory to be used in the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Israel has ÔÇőcontinued to bombard Lebanon and on Monday troops launched an attack it said was intended to seize a key south Lebanon town from Iran-backed Hezbollah. Israeli military said on Tuesday that an Israeli soldier was killed and three reservists were wounded during combat in southern Lebanon.
Israel ÔÇőand the U.S. have said the campaign against Hezbollah was not part of the ceasefire, while Iran has insisted it is.

item.title: Leaders of China, Spain pledge closer ties amid 'crumbling' world order
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - China and Spain pledged on Tuesday to deepen ties and safeguard global peace ÔÇőand development amid an international order that President Xi ÔÇîJinping called "crumbling" in remarks during a meeting with Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez in Beijing.
The visit by Sanchez comes as many Western governments are seeking to maintain ties with ÔÇőBeijing despite lingering security and trade tension, as discomfort grows ÔÇőover the policies of key ally, U.S. President Donald ÔüáTrump.
In "today's world, chaos abounds, and the international order is crumbling," Xi ÔÇősaid, adding that deeper ties were in the interests of both ÔÇőChina and Spain.
He urged stronger communication and trust to "uphold the rule of law, jointly defend genuine multilateralism, and safeguard global peace and development".
Sanchez said international law was ÔÇőrepeatedly being undermined, and called for closer ties to promote ÔÇőpeace and prosperity.
"It is more necessary today than ever, and so that together we ÔÇîcan Ôüáestablish an even stronger bond between China and the European Union," Sanchez said.
The latest of his counterparts from Britain, Canada, Finland and Ireland to visit China this year, Sanchez has urged the world's second ÔÇőbiggest economy to ÔÇőtake a larger Ôüárole on issues.
These ranged from climate change to security, defence and the fight against inequality, he said ÔÇőon Monday, calling for Europe to redouble its efforts ÔÇőas Ôüáthe United States has decided to withdraw from many of these fronts.
Spain has been one of Europe's loudest proponents of expanding trade and treating ÔüáChina ÔÇőas a strategic ally, rather than the ÔÇőeconomic and geopolitical rival seen by Trump.

item.title: Russian drones attacked Ukraine's Izmail port, damaged Panama-flagged vessel, deputy PM says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Russian drones attacked Ukraine's ÔÇőIzmail port in ÔÇîthe southern Odesa region overnight, damaging ÔÇőa Panama-flagged ÔÇővessel, Ukraine's Deputy ÔüáPrime Minister Oleksiy ÔÇőKuleba said ÔÇőon Tuesday.
Several strikes have been recorded across ÔÇőthe port ÔÇőarea, Kuleba said on ÔÇîthe ÔüáTelegram messaging app, adding that separate infrastructure elements ÔÇőand ÔÇőequipment Ôüáwere also damaged.
One of ÔÇőthe strikes ÔÇőcaused Ôüáa fire, which was quickly Ôüáextinguished, ÔÇőhe said.

item.title: China's Xi calls for more robust, dynamic ties with UAE
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping on Tuesday called for ÔÇőa more robust and ÔÇődynamic partnership between China and Ôüáthe United Arab Emirates as the world ÔÇőundergoes unprecedented changes.
Greater coordination and ÔÇőcooperation are needed as human society faces the choices of peace and ÔÇőwar, as well as ÔÇőunity and confrontation, Xi told the visiting ÔÇîcrown Ôüáprince of Abu Dhabi, according to a media pool report.
The visit by Sheikh Khaled ÔÇőbin Mohamed ÔÇőbin ÔüáZayed Al Nahyan comes at a time of ÔÇőacute tension in the ÔÇőGulf Ôüáafter weekend talks between Washington and Tehran failed to reach Ôüáa ÔÇődeal to end ÔÇőthe weeks-long war in Iran.

item.title: Weight loss drugs face first economic stress test
item.description:
DUBLIN, April 14 (Reuters Breakingviews) - At Novo Nordisk's (NOVOb.CO) , opens new tab annual shareholder meeting in Copenhagen last month, CEO Maziar Mike Doustdar was keen to focus on the positives. The Danish drugmakerÔÇÖs new Wegovy weight-loss pill is off to a record start, with more than 600,000 prescriptions issued in just two months. Doustdar also managed to get the companyÔÇÖs remedy to market before larger rival Eli Lilly's (LLY.N) , opens new tab oral version, claiming back some ground in a heated rivalry ÔÇőthat has largely favoured the American group of late.
Yet there's an unanswered question hanging over the new generation of obesity drugs. They haven't lived through a serious slowdown in consumer spending since exploding in popularity in recent ÔÇîyears, meaning it's not clear to what extent customers will stick with the treatments when household budgets get tight. At the very least, tougher economic times after the Iran war would intensify an already painful price war for the drugmakers.
Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are the titans of the so-called GLP-1 world, which includes both diabetes and weight-loss drugs. They're on track to generate over $100 billion in revenue this year from treatments in those two categories alone, per analysts' forecasts gathered by Visible Alpha. That number will hit $116 billion by 2030, according to the same estimate, as Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk are expected ÔÇőto launch new forms of the treatments beyond the classic injectable drugs like Wegovy and Zepbound. One key focus is the range of oral pills, which Novo's boss trumpeted last month.
Yet sustaining demand requires that customers keep forking out. It's ÔÇőan unfamiliar problem in the pharmaceutical world, where the buyers are often doctors or hospitals, with the money ultimately provided by insurance companies or health services. Weight-loss drugs, however, have also become a Ôüáretail phenomenon, with customers increasingly paying "out of pocket". The prices can be steep. Out-of-pocket costs for a monthly prescription can consume nearly a fifth of the average gross income in the U.S. and around 10% in the UK, according to Breakingviews calculations.
The upshot is ÔÇőthat demand could conceivably face a hit if unemployment spikes or consumer spending dips. That scenario may now be on the horizon. Rising oil prices will dent household budgets, making them less likely to splash out on other goods and services. Goldman Sachs analysts have slashed ÔÇőtheir forecast for U.S. consumption growth to 1.2% from 2% before the war, referring to the fourth quarter of 2026 compared with a year earlier. And the economy was already weakening. In February, before the Gulf conflict, the U.S. shed 92,000 jobs and unemployment rose to 4.4%. It might be the first real stress test for weight-loss drugs. Back in 2022, when energy prices and interest also rose, the GLP-1 movement was still young, and U.S. consumers had unusually large pent-up savings to draw on as a result of pandemic-era lockdowns and stimulus.
The central question is where weight-loss drugs sit on the list of ÔÇőhousehold priorities: are they like broadband contracts, mortgage payments and medically necessary treatments, which customers tend to keep paying in a recession? Or are they more like discretionary luxuries or brand-name groceries, which usually suffer , opens new tab when times get tough? Put another way: do GLP-1 users ÔÇőprize their new, slimmer figures enough to keep paying up, even if that means cutting spending elsewhere?
The optimistic view, from the perspective of the drugmakers, is that over-the-counter weight-loss customers are relatively affluent on average. Yet there's evidence that cost is already a big challenge for patients and users ÔÇîof GLP-1 drugs. ÔüáSide effects and financial barriers are the most common reasons for discontinuation of treatment. Over half of people who take GLP-1 medications are no longer taking them within a year. Data shows abandonment rises sharply once out-of-pocket costs exceed $500 , opens new tab .
Patients will find it increasingly easy to trade down to cheaper alternatives, too. Instead of splurging over $1,000 , opens new tab a month on a prescription for Eli Lilly's Zepbound they could switch to the cheaper new pill forms, which have similar weight-loss outcomes and can cost as little as $149 , opens new tab a month. Meanwhile, Novo Nordisk has been slashing the cost of its monthly prescriptions in a bid to win back market share. But there's also the riskier option of continuing to buy injectable copycat drugs from online pharmacies. Prices can be less than $130 , opens new tab a month, prompting many patients to try their luck or ÔÇőeven seek out dangerous and often unregulated versions on the internet.
The ÔÇőGLP-1 makers have longer-term problems, too. Eli Lilly and Novo ÔüáNordisk are spending billions of dollars on research and development, trying to improve their current offerings. In NovoÔÇÖs case, this is partly to avert a dramatic loss of revenue once the key ingredient in Ozempic and Wegovy comes off patent in the U.S. in 2031. When a drug's patent protection expires, its price typically drops by around 80%. At that point, a manufacturer would need five times as ÔÇőmany patients to earn the same revenue as before. Apply those numbers to GLP-1 drugs, and the implication is that roughly 75 million Americans would have to take them to keep ÔÇőrevenue steady, which is more than half , opens new tab Ôüáof the U.S. population aged 45 or over. That seems implausible: late last year, only 12% , opens new tab of Americans reported that they were taking the medication.
The drugmakers have not changed their public financial targets amid the conflict in Iran. But investors may be ahead of the issue: Eli Lilly's shares dropped 11% between the day before the first Gulf strikes and last Friday, which compares with a 7% fall for pharmaceutical stocks in the S&P 500 Index. From a shareholder's perspective, it doesn't matter so much whether cash-strapped users stop taking GLP-1s or simply find cheaper alternatives. The Ôüátakeaway in either case ÔÇőis slower growth.
The valuations of both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk have been declining in recent months. Eli Lilly now trades at just 26 times its ÔÇőexpected 12-month forward earnings, down from a peak of nearly 60 times in 2024. Meanwhile, Novo Nordisk is only trading at a multiple of 11, down from a peak of 38 times in 2024. These declines are largely due to the red-hot competition between the two companies and the prospect that other pharmaceutical giants like ÔÇőAstraZeneca (AZN.L) , opens new tab , Pfizer (PFE.N) , opens new tab and Roche (ROPC.S) , opens new tab will soon gatecrash the market. A near-term hit to consumer spending only compounds the problem.
Follow Aimee Donnellan on LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: How the Strait of Hormuz changes economic warfare: podcast
item.description:
Follow on Apple or Spotify . Listen on the Reuters app.
By strangling the waterway, Iran retaliated against the US and eased some financial sanctions. In this episode of The Big View Edward Fishman, senior fellow of the Council on Foreign Relations, tells Peter Thal Larsen how American economic weapons have been turned against it.
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky and LinkedIn .
(The host is a Reuters Breakingviews columnist. The opinions expressed are his own.)
Further Reading
The Big View, March 2025: Why financial warfare could backfire on the US
Edward Fishman: Iran is Using AmericaÔÇÖs Playbook Against Us, New York Times, March 2026

item.title: South Korea's Lee warns Iran war to keep oil price high, orders quick aid rollout
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - South Korean ÔÇîPresident Lee Jae Myung said that rising tensions around the Strait of Hormuz made it hard to be optimistic about the fallout from the Iran war, warning that high oil prices and supply-chain strains were likely to persist for ÔÇősome time.
Lee told a cabinet meeting on Tuesday the government should treat prolonged disruption in global ÔÇőenergy and raw materials markets as a given and reinforce its emergency response Ôüásystem.
"For the time being, difficulties in global energy and raw materials supply chains and high oil prices ÔÇőwill continue," Lee said.
"I ask that we pursue the development of alternative supply chains, medium- to long-term ÔÇőindustrial restructuring, and the transition to a post-plastic economy as top-priority national strategic projects."
Lee also urged ministries to move quickly to deploy a supplementary budget passed in response to the war.
At the meeting, ministers outlined steps to contain the economic shock ÔÇőfrom the conflict, including support for crude imports, controls against hoarding of petrochemical feedstocks and medical supplies, ÔÇőand expanded financial assistance for affected companies.
Industry Minister Kim Jung-kwan said disruptions to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz were ÔÇîstill Ôüáaffecting supplies and that even if the passage normalises, it could take around 20 days for Middle Eastern cargoes to reach South Korea.
The government is prioritising support for the passage of seven South Korea-bound oil tankers stuck in the Gulf area, a document shown during the cabinet meeting said.
Foreign Minister Cho Hyun told ÔÇőthe meeting that the ÔÇőministry had sent officials Ôüáto the Congo, Algeria and Libya in a bid to secure energy supplies, in addition to presidential Chief of Staff Kang Hoon-sik travelling to countries such ÔÇőas Kazakhstan since last week.
"I also urge the parties to this war to take ÔÇőcourageous steps Ôüátoward the peace the world so desperately wants, based on the principles of protecting universal human rights and the lessons of history," Lee said.
South Korea's energy ministry said on Tuesday it would begin rolling out a revised Ôüáseasonal ÔÇőand time-of-use electricity pricing system to shift power demand away ÔÇőfrom evening peak hours toward midday, when solar generation is higher. The new rates taking effect for large industrial users from April ÔÇő16 and weekend discounts for electric-vehicle charging will begin on April 18.

item.title: China's March crude oil imports unscathed by Iran war, gas imports hit a multi-year low
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - China's March crude oil imports fell 2.8% from ÔÇîa year earlier due to a high base, official data showed on Tuesday, while the Iran war curbed refinery runs with Middle East supply disruptions expected to weigh on April imports.
Imports in March were 49.98 million metric tons, or about 11.77 million barrels per day (bpd), ÔÇőthe General Administration of Customs said.
China's March seaborne crude imports were steady at 10.5 million bpd year-on-year, ÔÇőwhile inventories rose by 34 million barrels. Middle East cargoes were loaded in January and ÔüáFebruary so March imports were not yet affected by Strait of Hormuz disruptions, said Emma Li, analyst at ship-tracking firm ÔÇőVortexa.
Chinese refineries' capacity utilisation rate was 68.79% in March, down 0.9 percentage points year-on-year, and down 4.47 percentage points from ÔÇőFebruary, according to Chinese consultancy Oilchem.
Major refiners and independent refiners both lowered operating rates during the month due to factors including crude supply risks, the firm added in a report.
China is likely to face tighter crude supply in April, with imports expected to be about ÔÇő2 million bpd below its average demand from imports, said Ye Lin, vice president at Rystad Energy.
If refiners are ÔÇőto maintain adequate oil product supply, China will probably need to draw on inventories, even with refinery runs expected to fall by about ÔÇî1 million Ôüábpd in April amid weak margins, Ye added.
Customs data also showed that exports of refined oil products, including diesel, gasoline, aviation fuel and marine fuel, dropped 12.2% to 4.6 million tons in March.
China ordered a ban on refined fuel exports last month that halted cargoes that had yet to clear customs as of March 11.
The export ban, which does ÔÇőnot include jet fuel for ÔÇőaviation bunkering, is poised to extend Ôüáinto April, though exemptions could be applied to small volumes bound for countries in the region that have requested help.
Major refineries raised gasoline and diesel yields in March, after export ÔÇőplans for gasoline and diesel were cut, and China's gasoline and diesel consumption remained ÔÇőrelatively low, leaving Ôüásupply ample and domestic inventories higher, Oilchem added.
Natural gas imports in March, including piped gas and liquefied natural gas, were down 10.7% from a year earlier to 8.18 million tons, the lowest since October 2022.
The world's biggest LNG importer reloaded 8 to Ôüá10 ÔÇőcargoes in March, its highest monthly total on record, cashing in on ÔÇősoaring spot prices as China's supply of domestic and pipeline gas was sufficient to meet weaker demand.
Kpler data showed China's March imports at 3.68 million ÔÇőtons, the lowest monthly level since April 2018.

item.title: China said to relax restrictions on some BHP iron ore cargoes, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Officials from China's state ÔÇőiron ore buyer have told ÔÇîlocal plants that they can resume bidding for some BHP (BHP.AX) , opens new tab cargoes denominated in ÔÇőU.S. dollars from Tuesday, Bloomberg ÔÇőNews reported, citing people familiar Ôüáwith the matter.
Last September, China Mineral Resources ÔÇőGroup (CMRG) barred steelmakers and traders from ÔÇőpurchasing BHP's Jimblebar fines, an iron ore product, and has progressively expanded its restrictions ÔÇőas it negotiated the terms ÔÇőof BHP's 2026 supply contract.
The easing of CMRG's ÔÇîrestrictions Ôüácomes after a visit to China by BHP's incoming CEO, Brandon Craig, where he met leaders from ÔÇőChina ÔÇőBaowu Steel ÔüáGroup Corp and CMRG, Bloomberg reported , opens new tab .
Craig, who takes up ÔÇőthe top role at BHP ÔÇőon ÔüáJuly 1, also met the chairman of Chinalco (601600.SS) , opens new tab in Beijing last week and Ôüádiscussed ÔÇőfurther cooperation.
BHP declined ÔÇőto comment.

item.title: US, Iranian teams could return to Islamabad for peace talks this week, four sources say
item.description: ISLAMABAD/DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - Negotiating teams ÔÇőfrom the U.S. ÔÇîand Iran could return ÔÇőto Islamabad ÔÇőthis week, four sources Ôüásaid ÔÇőon Tuesday, days ÔÇőafter the highest-level talks between the ÔÇőtwo ÔÇőcountries since the 1979 ÔÇîIslamic ÔüáRevolution ended in the Pakistani capital ÔÇőwithout ÔÇőa Ôüábreakthrough.

item.title: Exclusive: US officials underwhelmed by French far-right's plans for economy
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. officials who met leaders of France's far-right National Rally were underwhelmed by their economic plans, two diplomatic sources said, in a blow to the party's efforts to present itself as a credible ÔÇősteward of the euro zone's No. 2 economy before next year's election.
The National Rally has become France's largest parliamentary party - and a potential victor in 2027 - ÔÇîby coupling a hardline stance on immigration with populist pledges to defend jobs and purchasing power. But its longstanding rhetoric around state interventionism and protectionist policies worries French blue-chips and investors.
U.S. Ambassador Charles Kushner and his team have met with most of the likely presidential contenders from across France's political spectrum, including National Rally (RN) party chiefs Marine Le Pen and 30-year-old protege Jordan Bardella.
While they were not particularly swayed by any ÔÇőof the candidates they met, the RN's views on how to cut a yawning deficit, win U.S. investment and get the economy moving were a concern, the ÔÇősources said.
Reuters granted the sources anonymity to allow them to speak frankly about private discussions.
Their conclusion echoed concerns among many in France's business elite Ôüáabout whether the RN has the experience or expertise to steer the highly indebted $3.5 trillion economy back to robust growth and steady the country's public finances.
The RN did not respond to a ÔÇőrequest for comment on the U.S. officials' view. A senior aide to Bardella said the party was working to develop its economic programme, including politically sensitive structural reforms to France's costly pension ÔÇősystem.
A State Department spokesperson declined to comment on "private diplomatic exchanges".
Doubts over the RN's economic programme may pose an electoral hurdle in France and shape U.S. thinking on whether to vocally support the RN in 2027, when polls suggest it could win.
U.S. President Donald Trump's administration has backed ideological allies in Europe, but with mixed results. A U.S. push to help Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban win re-election backfired when he lost power ÔÇőafter 16 years on Sunday.
One of the diplomatic sources said there were no signs RN leaders were seeking U.S. support, and European far-right and populist parties that once cheered Trump ÔÇőare increasingly wary of being seen as too close.
France's economy is mired in a morass of feeble growth, high borrowing costs and a debt burden that is one of Europe's heftiest at 115.6% of ÔÇîGDP.
The RN says Ôüáits economic priorities include boosting household purchasing power through tax cuts, reducing public spending and France's EU budget contribution, and restructuring welfare to prioritise French citizens.
But detailed plans have yet to emerge. Critics say the party lacks a coherent economic manifesto.
U.S. officials were concerned by a combination of mixed messaging on the economy, including the RN's desire for a costly rollback of a 2023 pension reform that raised the retirement age, and unclear plans on how to trim the deficit, the sources said.
They were also irked that the RN voted in favour of ÔÇőa budget amendment doubling to 6% a ÔÇődigital services tax that Washington opposes on Ôüáthe grounds it targets U.S. tech giants, the sources added. The amendment never made it to the final 2026 budget.
Business leaders earlier this year told Reuters they were confused by the divergent economic currents within the party leadership, with Le Pen seen ÔÇőas a big-spending populist, and Bardella seeking to chart a more pro-business path.
That ambiguity initially helped the RN broaden its support, ÔÇőbut has become a liability Ôüáas the party seeks to present itself as a credible government-in-waiting, executives say.
Despite making headway with voters, the RN, a party long-shunned by France's political and economic elites, has been struggling to make headway with France Inc .
But in a sign CEOs want to understand the party's economic programme as the April 2027 election edges closer, Le Pen met with the bosses of luxury group LVMH (LVMH.PA) , opens new tab , oil major ÔüáTotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab , insurer ÔÇőAXA and Renault (RENA.PA) , opens new tab among others on April 7, two other officials said.
Fund manager Fran├žois Durvye, whom Bardella brought on ÔÇőas an economic adviser, helped facilitate the meeting, one official said. The same official summarised the meeting as a heated Q&A session.
The second official, a senior figure in the RN, said the meeting was designed to dispel "the caricatures that are often painted ÔÇőof our programme, which is in fact the most pro-growth and pro-business programme across the political spectrum."

item.title: Iran to allocate part of oil revenues for reconstruction after attacks, minister says
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - Iranian oil sales in ÔÇőrecent weeks have ÔÇîbeen favourable and part of the revenue ÔÇőwill be ÔÇőallocated to repairing damage Ôüáto industry caused ÔÇőby wartime attacks, ÔÇőIran's oil minister said on Tuesday.
Mohsen Paknejad said ÔÇőoil workers had ÔÇőmaintained operations across facilities ÔÇîduring Ôüáthe conflict, ensuring oil exports were not halted ÔÇťeven for ÔÇőa single ÔÇőday,ÔÇŁ Ôüáincluding at key export ÔÇőhubs such as ÔÇőKharg ÔüáIsland.
The minister said last month that Ôüáthe ÔÇőselling price ÔÇőof Iranian crude had significantly ÔÇőincreased.

item.title: New trial over soccer legend Maradona's death begins in Argentina
item.description:
BUENOS AIRES, April 14 (Reuters) - A new trial over the death of Argentine soccer legend Diego Maradona will begin on Tuesday, with seven members of his medical team ÔÇőcharged with negligent homicide nearly a year after a previous case collapsed in ÔÇîa mistrial .
An enduring presence in Argentina - from towering murals to tattoos , opens new tab - Maradona died on November 25, 2020, at 60, after a heart attack while he was recuperating from brain surgery to remove a blood clot.
A court in ÔÇőSan Isidro, near Buenos Aires, will hear testimony from just under 100 witnesses ÔÇőas it tries Maradona's medical team over alleged negligence in the death Ôüáof the 1986 World Cup champion.
His medical team has denied wrongdoing. The defendants are ÔÇőpsychiatrist Agustina Cosachov, neurosurgeon Leopoldo Luque, psychologist Carlos Angel Diaz, physician Nancy Edith Forlini, nurse ÔÇőRicardo Almiron, head nurse Mariano Ariel Perroni, and physician Pedro Pablo Di Spagna. An eighth defendant, nurse Dahiana Madrid, will be tried in a separate jury trial, with no date yet set.
Two months into ÔÇőthe first trial , which started last March, a mistrial was declared when one of three ÔÇőjudges, Julieta Makintach, resigned after video surfaced showing her being interviewed by a camera crew in the ÔÇîcorridors Ôüáof the courthouse and in her office as part of a documentary, in breach of judicial rules.
The retrial will require both prosecutors and defense lawyers to reassess their strategies after the first trial aired photographs, videos, audio recordings and forensic evidence. Many witnesses, including Maradona's ÔÇőchildren and his former ÔÇőwife, Claudia Villafane, Ôüáhave already testified.
Prosecutors argued in the initial trial that medical professionals broke treatment protocols and that the home where Maradona was recovering ÔÇőfrom surgery amounted to a "theatre of horror," where necessary care was ÔÇőnot provided.
The Ôüádefense countered that his death was inevitable given his longstanding health problems. Maradona struggled for decades with cocaine and alcohol addiction.
The negligence charges emerged in 2021 after prosecutors appointed a medical board Ôüáto ÔÇőinvestigate Maradona's death. The panel concluded his medical team ÔÇőacted in an "inappropriate, deficient and reckless" manner.
If convicted, the defendants could face prison sentences ranging between eight and 25 ÔÇőyears.

item.title: South Korea to start building Sejong presidential office in August 2027
item.description:
SEOUL, April 14 (Reuters) - South Korea will begin construction of ÔÇîa presidential office in the administrative city of Sejong in August next year, the presidential Blue House said on Tuesday, as President Lee Jae Myung seeks to ensure the complex can be used within his term.
A ÔÇőtender notice for site construction will be issued on Wednesday, Blue House communication ÔÇősecretary Lee Kyu-youn told a press briefing.
The site will cover about 350,000 Ôüásquare metres (3.77 million square feet), with site preparation costs estimated at 9.8 billion won ($6.6 million) ÔÇőand a construction period of about 14 months, he said.
Lee Kyu-youn said the president had expressed ÔÇőhis intention to hold his retirement ceremony in Sejong, which is about 120 kilometres (74.56 miles) south of Seoul, and had instructed officials to ensure the project proceeds without delay.
The Blue House is also holding a ÔÇődesign competition for the new presidential office, with a winning proposal to be selected by ÔÇőthe end of this month, followed by about a year of design work before construction begins in August ÔÇînext Ôüáyear, Lee said.
In December, President Lee moved the presidential office back to the country's traditional Blue House compound in central Seoul, departing from the defence ministry complex where his ousted predecessor Yoon Suk Yeol had set up his office.
Lee had pledged during his June 2025 presidential campaign to ÔÇőbuild a second presidential ÔÇőoffice and a ÔüáNational Assembly building in Sejong and to complete his term in the city.
He said expanding the presidential presence in Sejong would promote balanced ÔÇőregional development, help ease soaring housing prices in Seoul and fulfil a ÔÇőlong-standing pledge Ôüáto complete South Korea's administrative capital.
The Blue House said the goal is for the president to move into the Sejong office by August 2029, adding that whether it would replace the Blue ÔüáHouse as ÔÇőthe main presidential office would require broader public consensus ÔÇőand possibly legislative steps.
Sejong was created in 2012 to relieve congestion in Seoul and already hosts most government ministries, ÔÇőthough the presidency remains based in the capital.
($1 = 1,480.30 won)

item.title: Orban's fall clears another roadblock for European markets
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Hungary's rejection of right-wing nationalist Viktor Orban after 16 years in power is a clear shot in the arm for its domestic markets. It should also lift EU assets more broadly, removing a persistent roadblock for a bloc now forced to ÔÇőgo it alone.
The outgoing prime minister's self-styled "illiberal democracy" not only left the Hungarian economy in a precarious state , opens new tab but clashed with basic EU democratic principles, triggering a series of ÔÇîvetoes on EU-wide policy and some 18 billion euros ($21.12 billion) in frozen EU funds to Budapest.
What's more, Orban's public embrace of Moscow on issues from Ukraine to energy and foreign policy complicated the EU's rapid rearmament to counter the Russian military threat to its East since the Ukraine invasion in 2022 .
His ouster comes despite - and some say partly because of - U.S. President Donald Trump's explicit endorsement, and only ÔÇőunderscores the electorate's decision to turn back toward the EU centre precisely as Transatlantic ties are fraying and the bloc must increasingly fend for itself on defence ÔÇőand trade.
Peter Magyar , whose upstart centre-right Tisza party has won a supermajority giving him power to reverse Orban's constitutional changes, will not Ôüásolve Hungary's problems overnight. Battles lie ahead with Brussels over budgets, frozen funds and the speed of reforms, and relations with Ukraine will need careful handling.
But the sense of relief around EU ÔÇőcapitals - that the multiple frustrations of the Orban period may be passing at last - was clear on Monday.
With traders eyeing a possible Budapest move toward euro membership, Hungary's forint surged to ÔÇőits best levels against the single currency in four years, 10-year Hungarian government borrowing costs fell by half a percentage point to their lowest since 2024, and the stock market (.BUX) , opens new tab gained almost 5%.
The latest twists of the Iran war and energy shock made broader European market moves harder to read, but investors took the vote as reinforcing Europe's continued outperformance.
"It's a great result for Europe," said Lauren ÔÇővan Biljon, senior portfolio manager at Allspring Global Investments. "It sets Europe up for a far more cohesive stance - and that's everything from NATO to kind of everyday ÔÇőEuropean business but then also to Ukraine."
For Morgan Stanley, the domestic Hungarian market implications are obvious - the unfreezing of EU funds alone, which amount to some 8% of Hungary's annual gross domestic ÔÇîproduct (GDP), could Ôüáadd 1-1.5 percentage points to Hungarian growth.
But the read-across to broader European equities is what the bank calls "underappreciated".
It pointed to two specific catalysts: greater EU policy coordination, and the possible release of a 90 billion euro joint loan to Ukraine - agreed in December but vetoed by Hungary - both of which it sees as supportive for EU equity sentiment, particularly in bank and defence stocks.
Morgan Stanley also sees the result supporting a continued narrowing of the valuation gap between European and U.S. equities. The euro zone discount to ÔÇőU.S. equivalents is at its lowest in ÔÇőthree years and roughly half its Ôüápeak just before the 2024 U.S. election.
A full repricing will likely require de-escalation in Iran - on which there is little clarity yet.
The deeper message of the election, though, may matter most to an EU bloc increasingly anxious about changing political winds - internal and global - ÔÇőthreatening its founding principles.
For Laszlo Bruszt - a Central European University professor whose institution was itself driven from Budapest by Orban in ÔÇő2019 - the result carries Ôüáa particular resonance.
"Orban's fall does challenge the sense of inevitability that had surrounded the global drift away from liberal democracy," he wrote in Project Syndicate , opens new tab .
(The opinions expressed here are those of Mike Dolan , a columnist for Reuters.)
Save the date: On April 23 at 1300 GMT/9 a.m. EDT, ROI columnists Mike Dolan and Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "Markets ÔüáUnpacked with ÔÇőReuters Open Interest: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times." Click here to sign up. , opens new tab

($1 = 0.8522 euros)

item.title: How China is plugging energy supply gaps left by US-Iran conflict
item.description:
LITTLETON, Colorado, April 14 (Reuters) - The world's largest energy product importer and consumer is switching up its supplier mix in response to the upheaval in oil, gas and fuel flows from the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel war with Iran kicked off over a month ago.
China secured roughly half of its total imports of crude oil, refined fuels, liquefied natural gas (LNG) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) ÔÇőfrom the Middle East in 2025, data from commodities intelligence firm Kpler shows.
However, the outbreak of U.S. and Israel war against Iran has choked off tanker shipping from the Middle East to other regions, ÔÇîforcing China and other major energy importers to seek alternate sources of energy products.
Here's a breakdown of China's historic reliance on the Middle East for major energy product supplies, and which nations are stepping up shipments to China since the war with Iran has virtually stopped shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz.
China imported roughly 642 million metric tons of crude oil, refined fuels, LNG and LPG in 2025, with 317 million tons or 49.4% coming from suppliers based in the Middle East, Kpler data shows.
No other region comes even close to the ÔÇőMiddle East's share of China's import needs of those energy products, with South America ranking second with a 12% share and East Asia and West Africa both boasting a roughly 8.5% share.
In terms of crude oil, the Middle ÔÇőEast supplied around 52% of China's total oil imports in 2025, or around 1.9 billion of the nearly 3.6 billion barrels of crude the country imported.
Due to the cuts to Ôüáoutbound oil shipments from the Middle East since the war began at the end of February, the Middle East's share of China's total oil imports has plunged, falling to a multi-year low of only 31% in May.
Total crude oil exports from ÔÇőthe Middle East to China were 581 million barrels from January to May, which marked a 28% fall from the same months in 2025.
To make up for the shortfall from the likes of Iran and Saudi Arabia, China has stepped up ÔÇőimports from South America and Eastern Europe, with Brazil and Russia both registering strong year-over-year volumes to China so far in 2026.
That said, total crude oil imports by China through the first five months of 2026 are down roughly 10% from the same months in 2025, indicating China's enduring difficulties in replacing Middle Eastern supplies.
On the fuels front, China's combined imports of gasoline, naphtha, gasoil/diesel and jet fuel/kerosene are down by around 11% during January to May compared to that period in 2025, to around 51 million barrels.
Middle Eastern suppliers accounted for around 41% of ÔÇőChina's total refined product imports in 2025, but supplied less than 1% in May, according to Kpler, following the closure of shipping lanes.
From January to May, China's fuel imports from the Middle East are down by 20% to around 19.2 million ÔÇőbarrels, while imports from all other regions are down by around 4% on the year to around 31.6 million barrels.
The only major region to register a strong increase in fuel flows to China was North Africa, with Algeria and Egypt both recording steep year-over-year rises ÔÇîin fuel exports Ôüáto China so far in 2026.
China imported around 40% of its LNG and LPG supplies from Middle Eastern nations in 2025, so the closure of the outbound traffic from the region has also impacted China's gas markets.
From January through May, total China LNG and LPG imports from the Middle East dropped by 43% to just under 9 million metric tons from over 15 million tons during the same months last year.
Total gas shipments from all other regions have also shrunk so far this year, but by only 12%, revealing that Middle East volumes have fallen much more sharply than those from other suppliers.
In LNG, total exports from the Middle East to China during January to May are estimated around 6 million tons, which is around 2.5 ÔÇőmillion tons or around 30% less than during the same ÔÇőmonths in 2025.
Other major LNG exporting regions - including Australasia - Ôüáhave also posted year-over-year contractions in LNG sales to China, due mainly to enduring weakness across key industrial sectors in China so far in 2026.
Even so, China's total LNG imports have posted only a 15% decline so far this year, meaning that imports from the Middle East have fallen by twice as much as China's total LNG imports.
China's total imports of LPG - ÔÇőused mainly by petrochemical plants and in rural areas for heating and cooking - have dropped by around 25% so far in 2026 compared to a year ago.
However, supplies from ÔÇőthe Middle East have declined by Ôüáonly 16% versus January to May in 2025, meaning that the LPG sector is one of the few energy sectors where the war with Iran has yet to materially impact China's imports from the Middle East.
That said, the enduring weak patch for the country's chemicals sector means that overall demand for LPG remains muted compared to a year ago, while demand for heating in households will have peaked early in the year at the height of winter.
China's LPG import volumes may become more severely impacted by the Middle ÔüáEast conflict if shipments ÔÇőfrom the region remain constrained for several more months and affect restocking trends ahead of next winter.
The opinions expressed here are those of the author , ÔÇőa columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Iran war allows Australia to revive green iron ambitions
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 14 (Reuters) - The conflict in the Middle East has cracked open the door for Australia to speed up the development of a handful of green iron projects as part of efforts to cut emissions from producing steel.
The immediate fallout from the U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran and the resultant retaliation has been surging crude oil and refined product prices ÔÇőamid the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz and damage to the region's energy infrastructure.
But among the second-round effects that are becoming more apparent is the possibility that ambitious plans to ÔÇîturn the Middle East into a major producer of green iron and steel may be delayed, or even curtailed.
The Middle East was shaping up as a major centre for the production of lower carbon iron, which is the process of using hydrogen or natural gas to make direct reduced iron (DRI) from iron ore.
DRI can then be used to make green steel in an electric arc furnace, or it can be converted into hot briquetted iron (HBI), which can be shipped to end users in another country for green steel production.
Brazil's Vale (VALE3.SA) , opens new tab is planning ÔÇőthree major projects in the Middle East, which aim to use iron ore shipped from Brazil and a combination of natural gas and hydrogen to produce DRI for local steelmaking and HBI for export.
The most ÔÇőadvanced of these projects is in Oman and a final investment decision was expected later this year.
Vale has two other projects planned in the Middle East, one in Saudi ÔüáArabia and the other in the United Arab Emirates.
While the Oman project sits outside the Strait of Hormuz, the planned Saudi and UAE plants are located west of the strait and would be dependent on vessels being able to ÔÇőtransit the narrow waterway in order to both deliver iron ore and export HBI.
Vale hasn't made any public comments on the current Middle East conflict or whether this has had any impact on its investment plans in the region.
But ÔÇőit is reasonable to assume that the longer the war continues the more questions companies will be forced to ask about their investment plans, especially if the status of the Strait of Hormuz remains disputed.
But what is an uncomfortable situation for Vale may just be the opportunity Australia's green iron proponents have been looking for.
Australia is the world's largest exporter of iron ore, shipping about 75% of seaborne volumes.
But it has struggled to launch a green iron industry, largely because the high cost of making hydrogen from renewable ÔÇőenergy, coupled with expensive labour and extensive regulatory approvals, has largely rendered projects uneconomic.
There was considerable hype in recent years that if Australia invested billions of dollars in building a green iron value chain, it would reap an even ÔÇőlarger dividend through higher prices for the lower carbon product.
The steel value chain accounts for 7% to 9% of global carbon emissions, the largest single industrial contributor and thus a prime target for the net-zero-by-2050 goals of many countries and companies.
The problem is ÔÇîthat about 80% Ôüáof steel emissions come from a single step in the process, namely turning iron ore into pig, or crude, iron by removing oxygen and other impurities, a process that now involves using vast quantities of coal.
Using hydrogen made from renewables such as solar to replace coal brings the carbon intensity down to around 300 kg (661 lb) per ton of steel, about one-seventh of the current 2.2 tons of emissions.
The problem is always going to be doing this at a price that makes sense.
At last month's Global Iron Ore and Steel Conference in Perth it was clear that the green iron hype has been replaced by the reality that only a small number of projects have the right type ÔÇőof iron ore, access to sufficient and cheap renewable energy ÔÇőand infrastructure solutions to be viable.
The projects vary Ôüáfrom building an integrated iron-ore-mine-to-steel-plant in South Australia to several in Western Australia that aim to produce an HBI product for export markets.
There is likely to be strong demand for green steel in coming years, especially from car makers that have made net-zero emissions commitments.
They are more likely to be willing to pay a premium for green steel of ÔÇőup to several hundred dollars a metric ton as the average car contains only about one ton of steel and thus using green steel doesn't add much to ÔÇőthe cost of a vehicle.
It's still Ôüálikely that the market for green steel in Asia will be small compared to the total steel market, but this may work in favour of Australia's smaller and more nimble projects.
An example is the 2-million-ton-per-annum HBI project being proposed by iron ore miners Fenix Resources (FEX.AX) , opens new tab and Athena Resources (AHN.AX) , opens new tab and energy provider Warradarge Energy in Western Australia.
The project proponents have grasped that producing green iron really means producing renewable energy and tacking iron ore mining on as a steady buyer of the electricity and Ôüáhydrogen being produced.
If ÔÇősmaller projects can get up and running, they are likely to open the door for bigger investments that can leverage economies of scale.
Australia's scaled-back ÔÇőgreen iron ambitions have been given a window of opportunity from the disruption likely to flow as a consequence of the Iran war. As always, seizing the moment will be key.
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI) , your essential new source for global financial commentary. ROI delivers thought-provoking, ÔÇődata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: Equinor sells $169 million stake in solar firm Scatec
item.description:
OSLO, April 14 (Reuters) - Norway's ÔÇîEquinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it has sold 12.9 million ÔÇőshares in solar ÔÇőenergy group Scatec (SCATC.OL) , opens new tab for Ôüáaround 1.6 billion Norwegian ÔÇőcrowns ($169.31 million), completing a transaction ÔÇőfirst flagged on Monday.
The shares were sold at 125 ÔÇőcrowns each, a 7% ÔÇődiscount to Monday's closing price ÔÇîof Ôüá134.4 crowns.
Equinor maintains a stake in Scatec of about 8.05% following the ÔÇőtransaction.
Equinor ÔÇődid Ôüánot provide a reason for its ÔÇőplanned divestment.
Scatec's share ÔÇőprice Ôüárose 26.6% this year before Tuesday's transaction, according Ôüáto ÔÇőLSEG data.
($1 = ÔÇő9.4503 Norwegian crowns)

item.title: Philippines seeks US extension on waiver to buy Russian oil
item.description:
MANILA, April 14 (Reuters) - The Philippines' Secretary of Energy Sharon Garin on ÔÇőTuesday said the country has asked the United States ÔÇîto extend a waiver to purchase Russian oil and petroleum products.
Garin said the government is optimistic about extending the waiver which expired on April 11, but ÔÇőis preparing alternative supply arrangements in case the request is ÔÇődeclined.
"We are awaiting their response, but we are very Ôüápositive on getting this other window," Garin said at a news ÔÇőbriefing.
The Philippines is diversifying its energy sources and supply options are not ÔÇőlimited to Russia, with the government also eyeing producers in South America, including Colombia and Argentina, as well as Canada and the U.S., Garin said.
"We wanted ÔÇőto open the Russian window because we want more options. We ÔÇőneed diversification," she said.
Ambassador to the United States Jose Manuel Romualdez last month ÔÇîsaid ÔüáManila was working with the U.S. to secure waivers and exemptions that will allow the Philippines to obtain oil from U.S.-sanctioned countries.
President Ferdinand Marcos Jr on Monday suspended excise tax on kerosene and liquefied petroleum gas ÔÇőafter Congress granted ÔÇőhim emergency powers Ôüáto adjust fuel tax rates. His team of economic managers have opposed the suspension of excise tax ÔÇőon diesel and gasoline.
Secretary of Finance Frederick Go on ÔÇőTuesday Ôüásaid the Development Budget Coordination Committee composed of those economic managers "has determined that suspending excise taxes on diesel and gasoline would not likely provide Ôüámeaningful ÔÇőrelief, as any reduction in retail pump ÔÇőprices would be marginal and largely offset by prevailing market dynamics."

item.title: HSBC chair says Middle East peace deal needed to restore global energy flows
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - HSBC Holdings (HSBA.L) , opens new tab Chair Brendan Nelson said on Tuesday that a Middle East peace ÔÇődeal was essential to ensure a substantial resumption of global energy ÔÇîflows, with oil-driven inflation looming as a major risk to the world economy.
Nelson, speaking at the HSBC Global Investment Summit in Hong Kong, added that as long as ÔÇőuncertainty persists, energy prices would remain elevated.
Oil prices have surged since ÔÇőthe Iran war began, and prices remain close to $100 a Ôüábarrel as investors position themselves for prolonged tensions over the ÔÇőcrucial Strait of Hormuz, through which a fifth of global oil ÔÇőand gas typically passes.
Nelson warned that current global growth, trade and inflation projections should be "approached with considerable caution" given that the impacts of the ÔÇőIran conflict are yet to be fully understood.
"The longer the ÔÇődisruption continues, the more the indirect effects from higher energy costs will lift ÔÇîinflation Ôüáand depress growth," Nelson said.
With a swift reopening of the strait looking unlikely, Nelson said he expected interest rates to be held steady in the U.S., Europe and Britain this year ÔÇőas a rise ÔÇőin short- and Ôüálong-term market rates had tightened financial conditions.
The U.S. Navy began a blockade of the strait on Monday, ÔÇőfollowing the breakdown of weekend talks to end ÔÇőthe Ôüásix-week-long war.
ANZ analysts estimate about 10 million barrels per day of crude supply have been effectively removed from the market, adding that a Ôüáprolonged ÔÇőU.S. blockade could curb an additional 3 ÔÇőmillion to 4 million barrels per day.

item.title: Philippines, US military drills underscore Washington's defence commitment, US official says
item.description:
MANILA, April 14 (Reuters) - The latest round of military ÔÇîdrills involving the Philippines, the United States and several partner nations will underscore Washington's "ironclad" commitment to its treaty ally and to the region, even as global attention remains fixed on the Middle East, a U.S. military official said ÔÇőon Tuesday.
Running from April 20 to May 8 across multiple locations in the Philippine ÔÇőarchipelago, the annual "Balikatan" or "shoulder-to-shoulder" drills will see more than 17,000 troops participate in Ôüáone of the largest and most complex training programmes yet, expanding beyond bilateral exercises into a ÔÇőbroader multinational effort.
"Balikatan represents an opportunity to showcase our ironclad alliance with the Philippines and demonstrate our ÔÇőcommitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific," Colonel Robert Bunn, U.S. spokesperson for the exercises, said at a press conference in Manila.
Japan's participation in the programme is set to expand this year, with members of its Self-Defense Force taking ÔÇőpart in live-fire drills for the first time, using its Type 88 surface-to-ship missile to help ÔÇősink a decommissioned vessel during a maritime strike exercise.
Tokyo has been strengthening its defence engagement with Manila after ÔÇîthey signed Ôüáa reciprocal access agreement in 2024 , allowing them to deploy their militaries in each other's territory.
The exercises are also set to highlight the Philippines' widening network of security partnerships with Canada , France , New Zealand and Australia, with the countries contributing naval vessels, aircraft and troops.
Bunn said the scale and scope of this year's ÔÇőBalikatan demonstrate the country's sustained ÔÇőcommitment to the ÔüáIndo-Pacific, despite competing global demands.
Thousands of U.S. personnel are deploying to the Philippines for the drills, reinforcing alliance readiness, regional stability, and the shared goal ÔÇőof maintaining a free and open region, U.S. and Philippine military officials ÔÇősaid.
The expanded drills Ôüácome amid rising tensions between the Philippines and China in the South China Sea, where Manila has accused Beijing of increasingly aggressive actions, an accusation China rejects.
Activities will span air, land, sea, and cyber domains, Ôüáincluding maritime ÔÇőoperations, integrated air and missile defence, counter-landing live-fire exercises, ÔÇőand humanitarian missions.
Colonel Dennis Hernandez, Philippine spokesperson for the exercises, said the country has the right to bolster its defence capabilities, ÔÇőadding that the drills were not directed at any country.

item.title: Indonesia's foreign ministry urges caution over U.S. military overflight proposal
item.description:
JAKARTA, April 14 (Reuters) - Indonesia's foreign ministry ÔÇőhas warned the defence ministry in a letter that an American proposal to give its military "blanket" permission to fly over Indonesian territory ÔÇîrisked entangling Jakarta in potential South China Sea conflicts, sources said.
The letter, marked as urgent and confidential, was delivered in early April ahead of a meeting of Defence Minister Sjafrie Sjamsoeddin and his U.S. counterpart Pete Hegseth in Washington on Monday, according to two Indonesian sources with knowledge of the matter.
The letter has not been previously reported and it urged the ÔÇődefence ministry to delay any final agreement with Washington. It is not clear if the overflight proposal was discussed by Hegseth and Sjafrie.
The two ÔÇőIndonesian sources said the proposal had been slated to be signed at Monday's meeting.
A Pentagon statement that followed the meeting said Ôüáthe two countries established a major defence cooperation partnership and listed a number of ways in which the two countries would build defence ties, but did ÔÇőnot refer to overflights.
A U.S. official, speaking on condition of anonymity, said that just because the overflights were not mentioned in the statement did not mean they ÔÇőwere not discussed in private.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
