Indonesia's defence ministry did not respond to a Reuters question on whether the proposal was discussed between the two, but said the U.S. proposal was being carefully reviewed by Jakarta, which had made a number of adjustments keeping in mind the country's sovereignty and national interests.
Indonesia, an ÔÇőarchipelago of more than 17,000 islands, is strategically located on the southern entry point of the South China Sea where it controls the Natuna Islands, and ÔÇőbalances close ties with both China and the United States.
China claims sovereignty over almost all the South China Sea - a waterway carrying more than $3 trillion in annual commerce - despite overlapping ÔÇîclaims by Ôüáthe Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia and Vietnam.
The letter from the foreign ministry said the U.S. proposal should be treated with caution because it would allow Washington to maximise surveillance and reconnaissance using Indonesian waters and territory, and could affect relations with other strategic partners in the region, including China.
It said an agreement with the U.S. would give "the impression that Indonesia is involved in an alliance with the implication of increased national security risks because it places Indonesia as a potential target in ÔÇőa regional conflict situation."
It noted that a ÔÇőnumber of U.S. military aircraft Ôüáhad carried out surveillance operations in South China Sea on 18 occasions between January 2024 to April 2025, which constituted a violation of IndonesiaÔÇÖs territorial waters and airspace.
IndonesiaÔÇÖs protests regarding this operation have never received a proper response from the U.S., ÔÇőit added.
Spokespersons for Indonesia's foreign and defence ministries, as well as the Pentagon did not immediately respond to requests ÔÇőfor comment on Ôüáthe reservations raised by the Indonesian foreign ministry.
Indonesia has a non-aligned foreign policy and President Prabowo Subianto has vowed to befriend all countries.
Indonesia is a key member of President Donald Trump's Board of Peace initiative, and has committed the largest contingent of troops for the body's proposed stabilisation force in Gaza.
He has also remained close to Beijing. His Ôüáfirst foreign ÔÇővisit as president in 2024 was to Beijing and he attended a military parade hosted by President ÔÇőXi Jinping late last year, being photographed with U.S. adversaries Russian President Vladimir Putin and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un.
The U.S. and Australia joined the Philippines for their second joint maritime exercises in the ÔÇőSouth China Sea this year, amid tensions with China in the disputed waterway.

item.title: China Evergrande's billionaire boss falls from circles of power to fraud plea
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 14 (Reuters) - Mingling with power brokers at centenary celebrations of the Chinese Communist Party on July 1, 2021, a beaming Hui Ka Yan betrayed no sign of the pressure mounting on his property company, China Evergrande Group, the world's most indebted.
Hui looked relaxed in navy-blue suit and open-necked ÔÇőshirt as he stood on a podium overlooking the festivities in Tiananmen Square, an invitation many considered an official show of support ÔÇîfor the billionaire businessman.
Flanked by the business elite, the founder of Evergrande, started in 1996 in the southern city of Guangzhou, spoke of his debt deleveraging goals, just a month after having outlined them in a rare meeting with more than 1,000 suppliers.
Now, nearly five years later, with Evergrande in liquidation over total liabilities of more than $300 billion, roughly the equivalent ÔÇőof Finland's gross domestic product, Hui, 67, has pleaded guilty to charges such as fundraising fraud and bribery.
His plea, after three years spent in ÔÇődetention, draws a line under the collapse of an empire that thrust China's property sector into the glare of global Ôüáinvestors.
The liquidators of Evergrande, appointed by a Hong Kong court, declined to comment. Reuters was unable to seek comment from Hui. The municipal government of the ÔÇősouthern hub of Shenzhen did not respond to a Reuters request for comment.
Hui, a former steel technician, raised by his grandmother in a rural village in the ÔÇőcentral province of Henan, built his fortune on the back of low-priced homes.
Under him, the property developer expanded aggressively by raising loans to support its land-buying sprees and selling homes at lower margins for quick turnover.
Evergrande grew to achieve 700 billion yuan ($103 billion) in annual sales by 2020.
In 2017, Hui was Asia's richest man, with a net worth of $45.3 billion, according ÔÇőto Forbes. By 2023, his net worth was estimated at $3 billion.
Hui was known for keeping a low public profile and being a workaholic, who at times ÔÇődemanded that others followed his work style, three employees told Reuters.
He also set ambitious targets; when questioned by investors and reporters in the past decade about his highly leveraged ÔÇîprojects, Hui Ôüásaid Evergrande's high turnover and asset value were sufficient to cover its debts.
Hui did not shy away from new ventures, especially in support of China's larger goals. He dabbled in electric cars and soccer, both passions of Chinese President Xi Jinping.
Outside mainland China, Hui mixed with Hong Kong tycoons, becoming a core member of the "poker club", a tight-knit circle that often did investment deals together, said three people familiar with it.
"He was very composed when he was first brought to ÔÇőthe club; he knowingly lost a lot ÔÇőof money in the games and Ôüágained the fondness of Cheng," said one of the sources, referring to Cheng Yu Tung, the late founder of the New World Development group.
Cheng injected $150 million into Evergrande a year before its Hong Kong IPO in 2009, according to Evergrande's ÔÇőlisting prospectus, helping it through a crunch during the financial crisis in the wake of its aggressive expansion.
Hui's debt-laden ÔÇőbusinesses worried regulators who Ôüáhad warned Evergrande to get its house in order for fear of contagion.
Speaking at the 2018 China Charity Awards as a winner for an eighth consecutive year, Hui said Evergrande had paid tax totalling 185 billion yuan in the past 22 years and donated more than 10 billion yuan.
"Without the country's policy to reform higher education, ÔüáI could ÔÇőnot have left the village. Without the country giving me a scholarship of 14 yuan every ÔÇőmonth, I could not have completed university," Hui said.
"Without the country's good policy to reform and open up, Evergrande would not have become what it has today. Therefore, everything that Evergrande and I ÔÇőhave, they are all given by the Party, by the country, and by society."
($1=6.8171 Chinese yuan)

item.title: School shooting in Turkey leaves 16 wounded, governor says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 14 (Reuters) - A teenager opened fire at ÔÇőa school and ÔÇîwounded at least 16 people, including students and ÔÇőteachers, before ÔÇőkilling himself in Turkey's southeastern Ôüáprovince of Sanliurfa, ÔÇőgovernor Hasan Sildak ÔÇőtold reporters on Tuesday.
The former student, 19, used a ÔÇőrifle during the ÔÇőattack, after which those wounded ÔÇîwere Ôüárushed to the hospital in the Siverek district, Sildak said. ÔÇőTwelve ÔÇőof Ôüáthe wounded were still in ÔÇőthe hospital.
The assailant ÔÇőkilled Ôüáhimself when police tried to capture him Ôüáat ÔÇőthe scene, ÔÇőSildak added.

item.title: Russian Foreign Minister Lavrov arrives in China for talks on Iran and Ukraine
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov arrived in China on Tuesday for talks ÔÇőon the wars in Ukraine and Iran ÔÇîas well as bilateral ties.
Lavrov was shown on Russian state television arriving in Beijing where is ÔÇőexpected to hold talks with Chinese ÔÇőForeign Minister Wang Yi.
"A thorough exchange of Ôüáviews is expected on a number of 'hot ÔÇőtopics' and regional issues, including the Ukrainian ÔÇőcrisis and the situation in the Middle East," Russia's Foreign Ministry said in a statement.
Lavrov and Wang will ÔÇődiscuss bilateral cooperation and cooperation in ÔÇőmultilateral forums such as the United Nations, BRICS, the ÔÇîShanghai ÔüáCooperation Organization, the G20, and the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation (APEC), the ministry said.
China and Russia declared a "no limits" partnership in February 2022 when ÔÇőRussian President ÔÇőVladimir Putin Ôüávisited Beijing, days before he sent tens of thousands of troops ÔÇőinto Ukraine. Putin has sometimes described ÔÇőChina Ôüáas an "ally".
In its annual threat assessment, U.S. intelligence agencies cast China and Russia as the Ôüámain ÔÇőcompetitors for Washington on everything ÔÇőfrom the Arctic and artificial intelligence to space and ÔÇőnuclear weapons.

item.title: Senior Russian commander killed in March air crash, official says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 6 (Reuters) - A senior Russian air force commander was killed when a military transport aircraft crashed in ÔÇőRussian-controlled Crimea last week killing 30 people, according to ÔÇőa senior official cited by Russian media on Monday.
Alexander ÔüáOtroshchenko, commander of the Mixed Air Force Corps of ÔÇőthe Northern Fleet, died in the crash, Andrei Chibis, governor of ÔÇőthe northern Murmansk region where the fleet is based, was quoted as saying.
The An-26 military transport plane crashed into a cliff in Crimea ÔÇőon March 31. Russia's Defence Ministry said shortly afterwards that ÔÇőthe preliminary cause was technical malfunction.
The An-26 has been in service since ÔÇîthe Ôüálate 1960s and has also been used by airlines to carry freight, but the model has been involved in a number of deadly crashes over the last decade.
A Ukrainian An-26 ÔÇőcrashed during a ÔÇőflight in Ukraine's Ôüásoutheastern Zaporizhzhia region in 2022, killing one person. Another aircraft crashed on a training flight ÔÇőin northeastern Ukraine in 2020, killing all but ÔÇőone Ôüáof the 27 people on board.
Eight people, including five Russians, were killed when an An-26 crashed in South Sudan in 2020. ÔüáFour ÔÇőof 10 people on board were ÔÇőkilled when an An-26 crashed on landing in Ivory Coast in West Africa ÔÇőin 2017.

item.title: Iran war upends IEA's global oil market outlook
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - The International Energy Agency ÔÇősharply cut its forecasts for global ÔÇîoil supply and demand growth, saying both are now expected to fall from 2025 levels as war ÔÇőin the Middle East disrupts oil ÔÇőflows and weighs on the global economy.
The ÔüáIEA now sees global oil demand ÔÇőfalling by 80,000 barrels per day in 2026, ÔÇőcompared with a projected year-on-year rise of 640,000 bpd in its previous monthly report.
"Demand destruction will spread ÔÇőas scarcity and higher prices persist," ÔÇőthe IEA said, adding that the deepest cuts in ÔÇîoil Ôüáconsumption have come from the Middle East and Asia-Pacific so far.
The Paris-based watchdog forecasts global oil supply to fall by 1.5 ÔÇőmillion bpd this ÔÇőyear, Ôüácrashing from a 1.1 million bpd projected rise last month.
Attacks on ÔÇőenergy infrastructure in the Middle East ÔÇőand ÔüáIran's effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz have led to the largest oil Ôüásupply disruption ÔÇőin history, the IEA ÔÇősaid, with 10.1 million bpd lost in March.

item.title: Britain will have enough energy this summer despite Iran turmoil, grid operators say
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain ÔÇőwill have enough gas and electricity supply this summer, the countryÔÇÖs network operators said on Tuesday, despite ÔÇîdeclining domestic gas production and fears over energy supplies from the Middle East.
Conflict in the Middle East has disrupted energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz and halted Qatari LNG production, which accounts for around a fifth of global liquefied natural gas supply.
ÔÇťWhile the situation ÔÇőin the Middle East has understandably raised questions about BritainÔÇÖs gas supplies, our forecasts indicate the market ÔÇőhas the capacity to deliver sufficient supply to meet demand this summer,ÔÇŁ Glenn Bryn-Jacobsen, director Ôüáof energy systems and resilience at National Gas, said in a statement with its Gas Summer Outlook for 2026.
Domestic gas supply from the UK Continental Shelf is expected to be 6% lower than last summer, at 13.1 billion ÔÇőcubic metres, reflecting years of declining North Sea output.
Imports of gas from Norway are expected to rise by 24% to 12.2 bcm, while imports of LNG from other countries are expected to soar by 65% to 2.7 bcm, National Gas said.
Wholesale gas prices are up ÔÇő50% since the start of the conflict, forcing Britain to pay more to attract LNG cargoes from alternative sources.
ÔÇťThe ÔÇőduration of these higher prices will depend on how long it takes Qatari production to recover and shipping to resume through the ÔÇîStrait of ÔüáHormuz,ÔÇŁ the outlook said.
Domestic energy consumers will be protected from the higher wholesale prices by regulator OfgemÔÇÖs price cap , with the current level set until the end of July.
Total gas demand is expected to be similar to last summer, around 29.8 bcm.
Gas-fired power plants typically provide around 30% of the countryÔÇÖs electricity but demand is volatile over the summer months, ÔÇőNational Gas said, depending on ÔÇőthe availability of wind Ôüáand solar power.
Strong wind and solar power output and seasonally lower demand mean Britain is likely to see a surplus of electricity supply this summer, the National Energy ÔÇőSystem Operator said in its outlook.
NESO, which is responsible for the country's electricity networks, ÔÇősaid it had Ôüáincreased flexibility, such as measures to encourage customers to shift demand to times when supply is high, with some energy suppliers paying customers, usually via money off their bills, to use extra energy at certain times.
Peak summer electricity demand is forecast Ôüáat 29.7 ÔÇőgigawatts, unchanged from 2025.
The outlooks cover the period from April 1 ÔÇőto September 30, when gas and electricity demand is typically lower.
Winter outlooks covering October to March, to be published later in the year, could reflect ÔÇőgreater impact from the Middle East crisis, the operators said.

item.title: Russia's oil and fuel export revenues rebound in March, IEA says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia's revenues from crude oil and refined products rose in March, rebounding from February when ÔÇőthey fell to their lowest level since the ÔÇîstart of the Ukraine conflict in 2022, as prices surged due to the Iran war , the International Energy Agency said on ÔÇőTuesday.
Russia's commodity revenues are a vital part of ÔÇőthe state budget and are needed to support Ôüárising military spending.
The Paris-based IEA said Russia's crude ÔÇőoil exports rose by 270,000 barrels per day last ÔÇőmonth from February to 4.6 million bpd, mostly driven by higher seaborne shipments as the Druzhba pipeline remained offline.
Flows via the Druzhba ÔÇőpipeline to Hungary and Slovakia across Ukrainian territory ÔÇőhave remained shut following the attacks on the pipeline infrastructure at ÔÇîthe Ôüáend of January.
The IEA also said Russia's oil and oil product export revenues nearly doubled, to $19 billion in March from $9.75 billion in February as global oil ÔÇőprices surged.
It ÔÇősaid that ÔüáRussia's crude production increased last month to 8.96 million bpd from 8.67 million bpd ÔÇőin February.
The agency also said that Russia ÔÇőmay Ôüástruggle to increase oil production above the levels seen in early first quarter in the near term given Ôüádamage ÔÇőto port and energy infrastructure.
Russian ÔÇőBaltic and Black Sea ports and refineries have been repeatedly damaged by ÔÇőUkrainian drone strikes.

item.title: Nornickel seeks new market for palladium in EVs with catalyst for lithiumÔÇĹsulphur batteries
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia's Nornickel said on Tuesday it aims to complete the development of a palladium-based catalyst for lithium-sulphur (Li-S) batteries ÔÇőwithin three years, potentially creating a major new source of ÔÇîdemand for palladium in electric vehicles.
LiÔÇĹS batteries in theory offer higher energy density as well as significantly lower cost and weight than lithium-ion batteries ÔÇőcurrently used in most EVs. But they have so ÔÇőfar failed to reach mass-market production due to their Ôüáextremely limited service life.
The project by Nornickel - the world's ÔÇőlargest producer of palladium, accounting for some 40% of global output - ÔÇőis aimed at sharply extending the lifespan of Li-S batteries to more than 1,000 charging cycles.
"I think this is a three-year horizon for us ÔÇőto further refine the technology so that it can compete ÔÇőwith existing solutions. Overall, lithium-sulphur technologies look quite promising in terms of energy ÔÇîdensity (driving Ôüárange),ÔÇŁ Nornickel Vice President Vitaly Busko said.
If the technology proves successful, it would "open up huge new markets for palladium", Nornickel said, estimating potential demand of at least 1.5 million ounces a ÔÇőyear.
Nornickel this week ÔÇőopened a Ôüápalladium laboratory in Moscow to develop new applications for palladium beyond autocatalysts for non-electric vehicles, which ÔÇőcurrently account for more than 80% of global ÔÇődemand.
The metals Ôüágiant is investing $100 million in a programme aimed at generating around 1.7 million troy ounces of new annual palladium demand by Ôüá2030 ÔÇőto 2035, to offset potential losses from ÔÇőrising EV adoption, and has already identified a nearÔÇĹterm commercial application in fibreglass production in ÔÇőChina.

item.title: ANZ raises oil price forecasts on Middle East supply losses
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - ANZ now expects Brent crude to end the year at $88 a barrel and remain above $90 a barrel for ÔÇőthe rest of 2026, up from its earlier forecast that had ÔÇîassumed prices closer to $80, due to the loss of supply from the Gulf, it said on Tuesday.
The revision reflects export disruptions, logistics constraints and precautionary shut-ins by core Gulf ÔÇőproducers that have sharply reduced supply even where production capacity has ÔÇőnot been physically damaged in the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, ÔüáANZ analysts said in a research note.
The war, which broke out on ÔÇőFebruary 28, has resulted in the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, ÔÇőthrough which about one-fifth of global oil flows.
ANZ estimates that about 10 million barrels per day of crude supply have been effectively removed from the market relative to the ÔÇőbank's January baseline.
"The oil market no longer needs a worst-case escalation to ÔÇőjustify higher pricing levels," the analysts said, adding that tight supply-demand balances alone could sustain ÔÇîBrent Ôüáprices near or above current thresholds, even in the absence of further geopolitical risks.
While some supply could return to the market if security conditions improve, ANZ cautioned that any recovery is likely to be slow and uneven.
The bank ÔÇősaid between 1 million ÔÇőbpd and Ôüá2 million bpd of output could face permanent or semi-permanent disruption due to reservoir damage, deferred maintenance, and financial ÔÇőchallenges.
OECD commercial oil inventories were already near historically low levels ÔÇőbefore the Ôüádisruptions, leaving limited room for stockpile releases to stabilise prices, ANZ said. Without a sharp drop in global demand, the bank said the market may need Ôüáprice-driven ÔÇődemand destruction to rebalance, keeping volatility high into ÔÇő2027.
Benchmark Brent crude futures settled on Monday at $99.36 per barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures ÔÇőfinished at $99.08 per barrel.

item.title: Former NATO chief targets PM Starmer, warning UK's security in 'peril'
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Britain's national security is "in peril" because ÔÇőof political complacency and under-investment in defence, former NATO chief George Robertson will say ÔÇîon Tuesday, in a rare public rebuke of Prime Minister Keir Starmer's handling of military spending.
Robertson, who helped draft a Strategic Defence Review commissioned by Starmer when he came to power in 2024, is expected to say in a lecture that Britain ÔÇőhas become increasingly exposed to external threats.
Robertson, who served in the 1990s as defence secretary ÔÇőfrom Starmer's Labour Party, told the Financial Times there was a gap between the Ôüáprime minister's rhetoric and action on defence, and Starmer was "not willing to make the necessary investment".
In his ÔÇőlecture, to be delivered later on Tuesday in Salisbury, southern England, he is expected to call out finance ÔÇőminister Rachel Reeves for devoting "only 40 words" to defence in a budget speech last autumn and not mentioning it at all in an update last month, the FT said.
"Britain's national security and safety is in peril," he is due to ÔÇősay, according to an excerpt reported by the FT and the BBC. "We are under-prepared. We are under-insured. ÔÇőWe are under attack. We are not safe."
Robertson's office did not immediately respond to a request for comment. There ÔÇîwas no Ôüáimmediate response from Starmer's office.
Starmer has blamed under-investment in the military on 14 years of rule by the rival Conservative Party, and has promised the largest sustained rise in defence spending since the Cold War, to reach 3% of national output in the next parliament.
The government has said it would publish a 10-year ÔÇődefence investment plan soon, ÔÇőaimed at meeting the ambitions Ôüáset out in the 2024 review co-written by Robertson, which called for a shift towards drones, digital warfare and data-driven combat systems reflecting lessons drawn from the war ÔÇőin Ukraine.
Starmer said last week that the war in Iran must be a ÔÇőturning point for ÔüáBritain, pledging to strengthen the economy and military to cope with a more "volatile and dangerous" world.
But Robertson will accuse Britain's political leadership of a "corrosive complacency" towards defence and describe decisions made by "non-military experts in the Treasury" as "vandalism".
"We Ôüácannot ÔÇődefend Britain with an ever-expanding welfare budget," he is expected to ÔÇősay.
He will say the security outlook has deteriorated sharply following Russia's invasion of Ukraine and heightened tensions in the Middle East, calling ÔÇőit one of the most dangerous periods in decades.

item.title: Kremlin says some internet restrictions are necessary for security reasons
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Tuesday that some ÔÇőinternet restrictions which it ÔÇîconceded had caused disruption for many Russians were necessary for ÔÇősecurity reasons and did ÔÇőnot represent a return to Ôüáthe past.
Russia has tightened ÔÇőits control over the internet ÔÇősince the start of its war in Ukraine in February 2022, ÔÇőblocking messenger apps and ÔÇővirtual private networks (VPN) used to bypass online ÔÇîcensorship, Ôüáand shutting down the mobile internet.
Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov suggested that some of ÔÇőthe measures ÔÇőwere Ôüátemporary in nature and would be lifted ÔÇőonce it was safe ÔÇőto Ôüádo so, while others were related to tech companies Ôüánot ÔÇőcomplying with ÔÇőRussian law.

item.title: UK's Reeves 'frustrated and angry' over US Iran war strategy, Mirror reports
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - British finance minister Rachel Reeves said on ÔÇőTuesday she was "very frustrated ÔÇîand angry" over what she said was the United ÔÇőStates' failure to have ÔÇőa clear exit plan or Ôüáobjectives for the war ÔÇőin Iran, according to ÔÇőthe Mirror newspaper.
"This is a war that we did not start. ÔÇőIt was a war ÔÇőthat we did not want. I ÔÇîfeel Ôüávery frustrated and angry that the U.S. went into this war without a ÔÇőclear ÔÇőexit Ôüáplan, without a clear idea of what ÔÇőthey were trying to ÔÇőachieve," ÔüáReeves told the newspaper.
"And as a result the ÔüáStrait ÔÇőof Hormuz is ÔÇőnow blocked," she added.

item.title: Macron, Starmer to chair conference on defensive Hormuz mission on Friday, Elysee says
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - French President Emmanuel Macron ÔÇőand British Prime Minister ÔÇîKeir Starmer will chair a video ÔÇőconference in Paris ÔÇőon Friday for countries Ôüáthat are willing ÔÇőto contribute to ÔÇőthe defensive multilateral mission to restore freedom of navigation ÔÇőin the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz when security ÔÇîconditions Ôüápermit, the French presidency said on Tuesday.
Senior diplomats will ÔÇőhold a ÔÇővideo Ôüácall on Wednesday ahead of ÔÇőthe leaders ÔÇőmeeting, Ôüátwo European diplomats said.

item.title: US truckers' diesel spend hits record high on Middle East conflict
item.description:
NEW YORK/LOS ANGELES, April 14 (Reuters) - The more than 3 million U.S. truckers who move goods for everything from grocery stores to factories to construction sites are grappling with the highest diesel prices in years, compounding pressure on the industry as Iran war-related ÔÇőoil price spikes also threaten to weaken freight demand.
Diesel is the second-largest operating expense for truckers and the national average retail price has jumped $1.89, or 50%, since the start of the ÔÇîIran war effectively halted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a vital artery for global energy flows. Crude oil prices, which underpin diesel and gasoline, also surged, driving up transportation costs and prices for many consumer goods.
Experts warned there was no relief in sight as diesel prices in logistics hubs including California and Texas hit all-time highs and a ceasefire announced last week by the U.S. and Iran looked fragile.
Trucking is a barometer of how the U.S. economy is doing. In 2024, the industry moved 11.3 billion tons of ÔÇőfreight - nearly three-quarters of the national total, including manufactured and retail goods - and generated $906 billion in revenue, according to American Trucking Associations.
As of Monday, U.S. fleets on average spent $5.52 per gallon on diesel, surpassing ÔÇőthe prior all-time high of $5.50 set in June 2022 after Russia invaded Ukraine, according to data from fleet management technology provider Samsara (IOT.N) , opens new tab . The firm's fuel spend data, which Ôüáaccounts for discounts and surcharges, is from more than 5,500 fleets of all sizes across the entire U.S. and represents nearly 1 billion gallons of fuel purchases.
"Not one firm had $5.60 a gallon diesel on their proverbial budget ÔÇőbingo card for 2026," said Jason Miller, a supply chain professor at Michigan State University.
Delivery firm FedEx (FDX.N) , opens new tab , which also operates one of the nation's largest trucking firms, said the fallout from the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran could weigh on ÔÇőfourth-quarter performance if soaring fuel costs prompt customers to pull back.
A March poll from DAT Freight & Analytics shows 18% of over 540 surveyed trucking firms had halted operations due to the spike in fuel prices. About 44% of the firms, which were of various sizes from across the U.S., were being more selective about load weights and about 45% were driving fewer miles.
U.S. trucking is dominated by small businesses. As of June 2025, there were almost 580,000 active U.S. motor carriers registered, with ÔÇő91.5% of those operating 10 or fewer trucks, ATA said, citing Department of Transportation data.
The surge in diesel prices has wiped out profits for most small carriers and owner-operators from December, January and February, DAT's principal analyst Dean ÔÇőCroke said in a market update, adding most other operators are still slightly above breakeven.
Heather Hickson Griffith, a former Marine who has more than a decade of experience behind the wheel of a big rig, is paying up to $8 per gallon ÔÇîin California - a Ôüáprice that is eating through her savings faster than during the 2022 fuel-price spike.
As a result, the Oklahoma-based heavy equipment hauler has stopped eating at restaurants to help save money. Her husband Daniel Griffith is running cargoes on the East Coast, where fuel is cheaper. GKZ Trucking, their small company of 21 owner-operators, does not have the heft of large trucking firms to recoup higher fuel costs - and is more vulnerable to cash flow and profit squeezes when prices soar.
Independent drivers like Hickson Griffith often pay out-of-pocket for fuel and can have trouble convincing customers to reimburse them when prices rise. Large companies, on the other hand, often negotiate volume discounts from fuel sellers and ÔÇőuse surcharges to claw back higher fuel costs from ÔÇőclients.
Without relief, Hickson Griffith said, "By the end of Ôüáthe year I am going to be hurting to the point of no return."
Soaring diesel prices could force thousands of small operators out of business, worsening already tight trucking capacity, said Avery Vise, vice president of trucking at FTR Transportation Intelligence.
Freight rates are expected to rise even more sharply than in 2022, Vise said.
Transportation accounts for a ÔÇősmall portion of the overall cost of goods - but can top 20% for staples like milk, researchers at Texas A&M Transportation Institute found.
So far, inflation is lagging levels ÔÇőseen in the 2022 Russia-Ukraine Ôüáwar energy shock, when pandemic-related supply chain disruptions and massive federal spending contributed to soaring prices.
Nevertheless, high fuel prices have become a political headache for President Donald Trump and his Republican Party as midterm elections near.
States including California, Hawaii, Nevada, North Carolina and Texas have reported diesel prices hitting record highs since the Middle East conflict escalated.
The jump in diesel prices has far outpaced rises in gasoline, said Kelly Soderlund, SamsaraÔÇÖs head of insights, adding the two prices Ôüárose in tandem ÔÇőduring the early days of the Ukraine war. That means today's fuel prices are hitting the trucking industry harder than everyday consumers, ÔÇőshe said.
Motorists should prepare for another round of price surges after ceasefire talks between the U.S. and Iran yielded no agreement over the weekend, sending oil prices sharply higher, said Patrick De Haan, head of petroleum analysis at GasBuddy.
"The move toward a full blockade of the Strait of Hormuz ÔÇőis compounding global supply concerns and risks further disrupting flows," he said.

item.title: Australian state secures own fuel supply
item.description:
PERTH, April 14 (Reuters) - The premier of Western Australia, Australia's mining powerhouse, ÔÇîsaid on Tuesday the state had secured a strategic reserve of 4 million litres of diesel to be held at the Port of Wyndham in the Kimberley region in its far north.
While the north of Western Australia is known ÔÇőfor its heavily diesel-reliant iron ore mining sector dominated by the likes of BHP (BHP.AX) , opens new tab , Rio ÔÇőTinto (RIO.AX) , opens new tab and Fortescue (FMG.AX) , opens new tab , the fuel will be directed to remote, off-grid Aboriginal communities Ôüáand agriculture, Premier Roger Cook said in a statement.
The reserve could rise to 12 million litres, or ÔÇőaround 75,000 barrels of oil, all to be owned by the state.
"Having access to this fuel ÔÇőnow, with the ability to store more in the future, is a crucial step towards shoring up our state's fuel security for the future,ÔÇŁ Cook said.
Remote communities and some agricultural producers are often more reliant on spot market ÔÇődiesel purchases than long term contracts, the state government said in the same statement.
Western Australia Energy and ÔÇőDecarbonisation Minister Amber-Jade Sanderson flagged a domestic fuel supply separate to the federal government's moves to ensure national fuel security ÔÇîon ÔüáSunday.
The state government has made a deal with local independent supplier Cambridge Gulf, which has placed additional orders for diesel from overseas suppliers and will store it free of charge.
ÔÇťThis approach complements the federal government's Minimum Stockholding Obligation (MSO), which requires fuel companies to hold reserves,ÔÇŁ the state government said in its ÔÇőstatement.
Canberra relaxed the MSO ÔÇőon companies last month, Ôüáallowing more fuel to get to market as supplies of petrol and diesel tightened amid panic buying set off by the closure of the ÔÇőStrait of Hormuz, which saw roughly 20% of global oil and natural gas ÔÇőexports before the ÔüáIran war.
The state government earlier introduced emergency powers to ensure fuel suppliers provided ÔÇťdetailed supply chain informationÔÇŁ.
One of the planks of the national plan Canberra announced was coordination between states in addition to a slew of Ôüáother measures ÔÇősuch as cutting excise, allowing higher sulphur levels and securing supply ÔÇőfrom Asian trade partners.
Wyndham port has previously been used as an export point for incremental amounts of conventional onshore oil ÔÇőproduced by Australian junior Buru Energy (BRU.AX) , opens new tab from its now-closed Ungani field.

item.title: UK product testing firm Intertek weighs break up, shares jump
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - British product testing and certifications ÔÇîgroup Intertek (ITRK.L) , opens new tab is considering splitting into separate energy/infrastructure and testing/assurance businesses, it said on Tuesday, sending its shares as much as 14% higher.
The company said the move could help boost ÔÇőgrowth and returns for shareholders and that, if it decided to ÔÇőproceed, the plan could be implemented by mid-2027.
RBC analysts said Ôüáany demerger could lead to higher costs and reduced investments, but added that ÔÇőconsolidation in the sector suggested neither of the two businesses would remain independent ÔÇőfor long.
Shares in London-listed Intertek were up 11% at 0755 GMT, compared with a 0.3% rise in the FTSE 100 (.FTSE) , opens new tab index.
Several British firms have looked to reshape their businesses ÔÇőas tougher market conditions push them to rethink the path to maximum returns.
Outsourcing ÔÇőfirm Capita (CPI.L) , opens new tab sold its private sector contact centre business in March, while engineer Smiths Group (SMIN.L) , opens new tab last year ÔÇîsold Ôüáits baggage-screening unit. Chemicals company Johnson Matthey (JMAT.L) , opens new tab is in the process of selling its catalyst technologies business.
The potential separation of Intertek Energy & Infrastructure and Intertek Testing & Assurance could be via either a sale or a spin-off, the ÔÇőcompany said.
"ÔÇŹWe believe that ÔÇőtwo specialist scale Ôüáglobal ATIC (assurance, testing, inspection, and certification) businesses could be best positioned to accelerate growth and deliver greater value for ÔÇőshareholders," CEO Andr├ę Lacroix said.
Intertek Testing & Assurance reported 5.6% like-for-like ÔÇőrevenue growth Ôüáin 2025 and contributed just over half of the group's revenue, according to the company's website. Intertek Energy & Infrastructure's revenue rose 2% on the same basis.
The Ôüácompany reaffirmed ÔÇőits guidance for mid-single-digit like-for-like revenue growth ÔÇőthis year after a 5.4% increase in the first quarter, which JP Morgan analysts described as "reassuring".
($1 = 0.7400 ÔÇőpounds)

item.title: Shell, in contrast to BP, allows AGM vote on Follow This climate resolution
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab will allow its shareholders to ÔÇîvote on a resolution tabled by climate activist investor group Follow This at its annual general meeting, ÔÇőrecommending a vote against it, according to ÔÇőits notice of meeting.
This stands in contrast Ôüáto BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , whose board decided not to ÔÇőinclude the Follow This resolution on its agenda, ÔÇőtriggering some of its shareholders and influential proxy adviser groups to recommend votes against the board's wishes.
The Follow This ÔÇőresolution calls on Shell to disclose how ÔÇőits strategy would perform under scenarios of declining demand ÔÇîfor Ôüáoil and gas. BP has said the resolution was invalid and would be ineffective if it were to pass at its AGM.
Shell said Follow ÔÇőThis's questions ÔÇőare comprehensively Ôüácovered by Shell's existing disclosures which enable shareholders to model the ÔÇőfinancial resilience of the company in any ÔÇőprice Ôüáscenario of their choosing.
Shell also said that were the Follow This resolution to pass, it would Ôüábe ÔÇőagainst good governance because it ÔÇőwould hold Shell to certain scenarios that are subject to ÔÇőchange.

item.title: Delta Air Lines scales back sustainable fuel and net-zero targets, Bloomberg News Reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Delta Air Lines (DAL.N) , opens new tab quietly removed two key environmental targets from its sustainability webpage late last week, Bloomberg ÔÇőNews reported on Tuesday.
The Atlanta-based carrier deleted its ÔÇîpledge to use sustainable aviation fuel for 10% of its jet fuel by 2030, the report said, adding that the ÔÇőairline has also rephrased its quest to achieve ÔÇőnet-zero emissions by 2050 as an "aspiration," rather than Ôüáa "goal."
SAF, made largely from waste or used cooking oil, ÔÇőcan cut emissions significantly compared with traditional jet fuel. However, ÔÇőit remains two to five times more expensive than conventional fuel.
The Bloomberg report said, quoting a Delta spokesperson, that the airline ÔÇőstill views SAF as one of the most important ÔÇőways to decarbonise flight, but its slow development threatens the industry's ÔÇîclimate Ôüáambitions.
Delta did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
In February, Willie Walsh, director-general of the International Air Transport Association, said that shortages of efficient new ÔÇőaircraft and alternative ÔÇőfuels were Ôüápushing up profits for suppliers and putting at risk the industry's flagship emissions goal.
IATA's ÔÇőroughly 350 airlines adopted the target in ÔÇő2021 to Ôüácurb emissions that represent 2% to 3% of the global total. The effort relies heavily on the use of ÔüáSAF ÔÇőand timely access to new ÔÇőplanes and engines, delayed by supply chain problems.

item.title: Airlines urge EU to step in as Iran war chokes jet fuel supply
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - European airlines have urged the European Union to step in with emergency measures to tackle repercussions from the Iran war, including widespread ÔÇőairspace closures and mounting concerns over jet fuel shortages, a document ÔÇîseen by Reuters showed.
Industry group Airlines for Europe (A4E) has requested that the EU introduce a raft of crisis response measures, including EU-level monitoring of jet fuel supplies, a temporary suspension of ÔÇőthe EU's carbon market for aviation, and scrapping certain aviation taxes, it ÔÇősaid in the document.
The aviation sector has been hit by airspace closures Ôüásince the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran began on February 28, with the European ÔÇőUnion Aviation Safety Agency banning European airlines from operating in the airspace of several Gulf ÔÇőcountries, including the UAE and Qatar until April 24.
The sector is also staring at a jet fuel crunch after the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Last week, industry group Airports Council ÔÇőInternational Europe (ACI) warned that Europe could face a systemic jet fuel shortage in three ÔÇőweeks.
The A4E document urged Brussels to consider joint EU purchasing of kerosene, which is a form ÔÇîof Ôüájet fuel.
The EU introduced joint natural gas buying to attempt to shore up supplies, after Russia slashed gas deliveries to Europe in 2022. However, the model has not been applied to oil or kerosene so far.
A4E, whose members include Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab , Air ÔÇőFrance-KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab and easyJet (EZJ.L) , opens new tab , ÔÇőalso urged the ÔüáEU to amend its legal requirement for countries to maintain 90 days of emergency oil reserves as this currently does not ÔÇőinclude a specific requirement on jet fuel.
The document also asked ÔÇőfor clarification Ôüáon existing legislation, including confirmation that airspace closures due to conflict and resulting operational effects will be considered as justified non-use of slots.
The European Commission has said it Ôüáwill propose a ÔÇőpackage of measures on April 22 to attempt ÔÇőto offset the fallout of the Iran war in energy markets, but has not confirmed if this ÔÇőwill include specific measures on jet fuel.

item.title: Swedish green steel startup Stegra secures $1.7 billion from Wallenberg-led group
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 14 (Reuters) - Swedish ÔÇîgreen steel startup Stegra said on Tuesday it had raised 1.4 billion euros ($1.7 billion) from an investor consortium led by the Wallenberg family, providing a lifeline to complete construction of its greenfield plant.
The financing provides a reprieve for one of ÔÇőthe few remaining large green steel projects in Europe, where high investment costs and ÔÇőthe challenges of scaling green hydrogen have forced several planned projects to be Ôüádelayed, downsized or cancelled.
Stegra, formerly known as H2 Green Steel, has sought for months to raise more cash ÔÇőto finish its flagship hydrogen-based steel plant in northern Sweden.
"This financing reflects the strong conviction in ÔÇőStegra's business model among new and existing investors, as well as lenders," CEO Henrik Henriksson said in a statement.
Henriksson later told a press conference that he would not announce a specific start for production but that there ÔÇőwould be a gradual phased ramp-up of operations.
Start of production has been pushed back several times, ÔÇőwith the latest indications being 2027 prior to this funding news.
Europe has been at the forefrontof efforts ÔÇîto Ôüádecarbonise steelmaking to meet its climate targets, but developers have slowed or halted investments in green hydrogen, raising doubts over how quickly industries can move away from fossil fuels.
The Wallenberg-led consortium also includes Singapore state investor Temasek and IMAS Foundation, an investment arm within the IKEA sphere.
Wallenberg Investments said ÔÇőit had committed to ÔÇőinvesting 250 million euros. ÔüáIts chair, Marcus Wallenberg, said the project had "clear importance to Sweden" and the group saw "a commercially viable way forward".
The Wallenberg family is Sweden's most ÔÇőinfluential business dynasty, controlling firms worth hundreds of billions of dollars, including ÔÇőSEB (SEBa.ST) , opens new tab , Ericsson (ERICb.ST) , opens new tab , ÔüáAtlas Copco (ATCOa.ST) , opens new tab , Electrolux (ELUXb.ST) , opens new tab and Investor AB (INVEb.ST) , opens new tab .
The new financing has been agreed in principle, and is subject to credit approvals from lenders and finalisation of documentation, Stegra said.
The investors intend to nominate Wallenberg-veteran Leif Johansson Ôüáas new ÔÇőboard chair. Johansson has previously held the chairman role ÔÇőat companies such as Ericsson and AstraZeneca, and been CEO of Electrolux and Volvo Group.
($1 = 0.8500 euros)

item.title: Lebanese and Israeli envoys to meet as Israel presses war on Hezbollah
item.description:
JERUSALEM/BEIRUT, April 14 (Reuters) - Israeli and Lebanese envoys will meet in Washington on Tuesday as Israel presses its war on Hezbollah , a diplomatic milestone ÔÇőovershadowed by conflicting agendas with Israel ruling out a ceasefire and demanding Beirut disarm the Iran-backed group.
The meeting comes at a ÔÇîcritical juncture in the crisis in the Middle East , a week into a fragile ceasefire between the United States, Israel and Iran. The parallel war between Israel and Hezbollah in Lebanon has been a complicating factor in Pakistan's mediation to end the wider conflict.
U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio is set to attend the meeting between Israeli ambassador to the U.S., ÔÇőYechiel Leiter, and his Lebanese counterpart, Nada Hamadeh Moawad, at 11 a.m. (1500 GMT), a State Department official said.
It marks a rare encounter between ÔÇőrepresentatives of governments that have remained technically in a state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
The government led by President Joseph Aoun and Prime Minister Nawaf Salam has called for negotiations with Israel despite objections from Hezbollah, reflecting worsening tensions between ÔÇőthe Shi'ite Muslim group and its opponents.
Hezbollah opened fire in support of Tehran on March 2, sparking an Israeli offensive that has killed more than 2,000 ÔÇőpeople and forced 1.2 million from their homes, according to Lebanese authorities.
In Lebanon, the dead include 252 women and 166 children, the health ministry says. Sources familiar with the matter said on March 27 that more than 400 Hezbollah fighters have been killed. Hezbollah attacks have killed 13 Israeli soldiers and two Israeli civilians since March 2, Israel says.
Lebanese ÔÇőofficials have said Moawad only has authority to discuss a ceasefire in Tuesday's meeting.
But Israeli government spokesperson Shosh Bedrosian said Israel would not discuss a ÔÇőceasefire.
"What we're looking for ... is to see that Lebanon is committed to disarming Hezbollah ... demilitarizing southern Lebanon as well, and also to have a peace agreement," she said on ÔÇîMonday.
The Lebanese Ôüástate has been seeking to disarm Hezbollah peacefully since a war between the militia and Israel in 2024. Any move by Lebanon to disarm it by force risks igniting conflict in a country shattered by civil war from 1975 to 1990. Moves against Hezbollah by a Western-backed government in 2008 prompted a short civil war.
The current government banned Hezbollah's military wing after it opened fire on Israel last month.
Israel and the U.S. have ÔÇősaid the campaign against Hezbollah was not ÔÇőpart of the Iran-U.S. ceasefire, Ôüáthough Pakistan's prime minister had said the truce would include Lebanon, as Iran had demanded.
While Israel has pressed attacks in Lebanon, it has launched no airstrikes in Beirut since last Wednesday, when it pounded the capital in a 10-minute ÔÇőbarrage that killed hundreds of people across Lebanon.
The following day, U.S. President Donald Trump, in an interview with ÔÇőNBC News, said Israeli ÔüáPrime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had told him he would "low-key it" in Lebanon.
A U.S. State Department official said that Israel was at war with Hezbollah, not Lebanon, and so there was no reason they should not talk, describing the talks as direct, high-level and the first of their kind since 1993.
The conversation would "scope the ongoing Ôüádialogue about how ÔÇőto ensure the long-term security of Israel's northern border and to support the Government of ÔÇőLebanon's determination to reclaim full sovereignty over its territory and political life".
Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem on Monday called on the government to cancel the meeting, saying Hezbollah would continue to confront Israeli ÔÇőattacks on Lebanon.

item.title: Orban's defeat topples a pillar of Europe's far right, prompts scrutiny of MAGA links
item.description:
BERLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - Europe's far right has lost one of its biggest champions with the defeat of Hungary's nationalist leader Viktor Orban in Sunday's election.
Orban's government provided a blueprint for right-wing populist and illiberal politicians, earning plaudits from the likes ÔÇőof U.S. President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin.
In his 16 years in office, he promoted ethno-nationalism, clamped down on civil society and the media, and fought ÔÇîagainst immigration, LGBTQ rights and liberalism. He was also the European leader with by far the closest ties to Trump's MAGA movement ÔÇô illustrated by Vice President JD Vance's visit to Budapest to endorse him last week.
His loss - blamed on Hungarians' dissatisfaction with the economy, corruption and curbs on democratic freedoms - deprives the growing European far right not only of a model for the nationalist governments they aspire to establish across the region, but a deeply resourced ally who ploughed hundreds ÔÇőof millions of dollars into championing those ideologies.
"Orban has been pretty much the figurehead of the European far right for the last few years and even beyond the European far ÔÇőright," said Gabriela Greilinger, a doctoral researcher based in the United States, focusing on the European far right and democratic erosion.
"He has been the model Ôüábecause he was able to cling to power for so long and really entrench himself and his ideology in the state. And that's something that most other far-right parties have not been able ÔÇőto do so far."
Orban's closeness to the MAGA movement is now seen as a double-edged sword by some far-right politicians, with Trump's threats to seize Greenland and the war with Iran contributing to his deep ÔÇőunpopularity , opens new tab in Europe.
Orban's "ostentatious friendship" with the current U.S. administration "hung like millstones around Orban's neck," wrote Alternative for Germany lawmaker Matthias Moosdorf on X on Monday.
Orban's legacy of regularly wielding Hungary's veto power at the European Union, often to block financing for Ukraine or Russian sanctions, drew praise from other political leaders keen to see the bloc undermined.
"He was a thorn in the side to the EU and that was a good thing," said Ben Habib, the ÔÇőleader of the Advance UK party, an anti-immigration party launched in Britain last year.
In the wake of Orban's defeat by the pro-EU Peter Magyar, Alice Weidel, co-leader of Alternative for Germany, wrote on ÔÇőX: "His achievements for his homeland and his contributions to Europe continue to inspire us to stand up for a continent of sovereign nations."
Much of Orban's influence came not only from his success at home, but his ability to ÔÇîspread his ideas Ôüáand policies.
With government funding and corporate stakes, Orban gave the equivalent of over a billion dollars to the likes of the Mathias Corvinus Collegium (MCC), a private research institute, and the Danube Institute, which have acted as ideological arms of his Fidesz party.
"Budapest became a pilgrimage - people were over there all the time from MAGA world, so it was a successful strategy," said Daniel Fried, a fellow at the Atlantic Council think tank in Washington, D.C.
Political conferences hosted groups from other parts of Europe and the U.S., including some of the most influential organisations in the current Trump administration such as the Heritage Foundation, ÔÇőAmerica First Policy Institute and Alliance Defending Freedom.
The ÔÇőinfluence of the Orban-aligned institutes was also palpable Ôüáin Washington, D.C. as Trump prepared to begin his second term as U.S. president, said Jacob Ross, a research fellow at the German Council on Foreign Relations.
"I was really surprised to see how many Hungarian delegations were there, how active the Hungarian embassy in D.C. was interacting with Heritage," he ÔÇősaid.
Magyar said on Monday the government would no longer use taxpayer money to finance organisations like MCC or political party events.
But the institutes have ÔÇőalready been effective, and are Ôüáunlikely to disappear even with their funding changed, said Greilinger.
"Most of these organisations are also there to ensure that these ideas kind of live on even beyond the rule of the leader, or in this case, beyond the rule of Viktor Orban," she said.
Orban's European allies also said they believed momentum was still behind them, and that after so long in power governments often face growing dissatisfaction.
"We are Ôüágoing to miss ÔÇőthe support of the Hungarian government, but let's see how we move from here," said Ta╠énger Corre╠éa, a member of ÔÇőthe European Parliament in the Patriots for Europe group, which includes Fidesz.
Corre╠éa said polls were promising for France's National Rally ahead of next year's presidential elections, and last year his own Chega party became the second-largest parliamentary force in Portugal.
"It's not ÔÇőpleasant that one of our members lost an election," said Corre╠éa.
"But it's life, we move on."

item.title: Cuba's top destinations deserted, without power or fuel under US sanctions
item.description:
CIENAGA DE ZAPATA, Cuba, April 14 (Reuters) - The sun is setting in P├ílpite, a small town on the edge of Cuba's vast Zapata Swamp, when suddenly the road swarms with activity. But not with the red land crabs that once attracted hundreds of thousands of tourists annually to one of the island's top eco-tourism destinations.
The crowds now are local residents pouring out of their homes in search of a cell ÔÇőphone signal when the lights flicker back on amid a U.S. energy blockade that has condemned a large swath of the island to live without electricity.
"What tourist will want to ÔÇîvisit us under these conditions?" laments Manuela Arencibia B├íez, owner of a rental house - now mostly empty - just down the road in the beachfront Playa Larga, 2 miles away and the region's top overnight destination.
She told Reuters she'd lost count of all the reservations she'd had to cancel, including those from tourists already in the country unable to find a taxi driver with sufficient gas to make the two hour trip south and east from Havana.
Official data underscore Arencibia's concerns. International tourist ÔÇőarrivals plunged 56% in February from one year prior. Both of Playa Larga's hotels are closed. Most of its tourist attractions are shuttered as well.
Cuba has for decades been plagued by shortages, ÔÇőhampered by a state-run economy and a Cold War-era U.S. trade embargo that complicates everything from financial transactions to the import of fuel. But this time Ôüáis different, more than a dozen residents and tourism industry workers of the Zapata region told Reuters.
"We are much worse off even than during the coronavirus pandemic," said Arencibia, rattling off a list of lost ÔÇőreservations from countries like Switzerland, Canada, France and Germany.
Power cuts now run for 22 hours a day, with just a couple hours of light in which residents scramble to call loved ones in Miami, or Havana, ÔÇőor to prepare food before it rots in refrigerators.
Water is running short across many communities. Medical services - always complicated in far-flung corners of the country - are now a remote possibility, hampered by power cuts and lack of communications. Fuel for independent travelers who once flocked to the area in rental cars is largely unavailable.
Trump's fuel blockade coincided with what has historically been peak tourism season for Cuba, all but ensuring a disastrous year, a death knell for an industry already knee-capped by ÔÇőshortages but that in 2024 still accounted for as much as 10% of foreign currency earnings.
Many hotels and services, from Havana east to top beach destinations Varadero, Cayo Santa Mar├şa and Cayo Coco, were forced to close ÔÇőwhen fuel shortages prompted many airlines to cut flights.
"In these months, I am always booked," said Fidel Silvestre Fuentes, a 67-year-old rental home owner who has long provided accommodations to birdwatchers who come from across the world to see ÔÇîthe bee Ôüáhummingbird, the world's smallest, among other endemic species. "Now, we're empty."
The Zapata Swamp region, much of it contained within a pristine national park - nonetheless has more private rental homes open to tourists than even Cuba's top sun and beach destination, Varadero. Now though, it feels like a ghost town.
The potholes have grown deeper along the region's single access road. The coastal route between the white sands of Playa Larga, at the foot of the bay, and Playa Gir├│n, further out, feels abandoned, inhabited mostly by stray crabs, wayward residents on horseback and men and women on bikes.
Even the region's top attractions are closed. The Cueva de los Peces, ÔÇőa transparent seemingly bottomless cave filled with colorful tropical ÔÇőfish, has been shuttered for two months, a Ôüáguard told a reporter.
The impacts are especially dire in regions like Zapata, which offers residents few other options for work beyond tourism.
"If you don't a rental home, you work in a restaurant; or if you don't work in the sector, maybe you are related to someone who does," said Jorge Alberto Brito, a ÔÇőhat and souvenir seller who now lives off a scarce few pesos a day. "Without a doubt we have hit rock bottom."
Fidel Fuentes Ray├│, who rents a ÔÇőhome in Playa Larga, is Ôüáamong the lucky few with money enough to purchase solar panels and a lithium battery for storage, giving his home a leg up on the competition.
It hasn't helped, he says.
"Tourists do not come to Cuba for comfortable accommodation, they come to but to tour the National Park and enjoy bird watching, diving services, boat rides, fly fishing... experiences that are just not available now," he said, citing fuel shortages and transportation challenges.
Still, a scattered few tourists still Ôüároll the ÔÇődice on travel to the region.
Blair Andrews, an American tourist, has returned repeatedly to Cuba, enough so to be on a ÔÇőfirst name basis with the guides at a Bay of Pigs scuba diving operation.
The 51-year-old told Reuters electricity and cell service were the least of her concerns.
"I come back because the Cuban people are good hosts and have a beautiful culture," she said as ÔÇőshe strapped a tank to her back before diving into the clear aqua-green water of the Bay of Pigs. "I'm very sad about what's happening to them."

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Dnipro kills four, officials say
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - A Russian strike ÔÇőon Ukrainian ÔÇîcity of Dnipro ÔÇőkilled ÔÇőfour people and injured Ôüá25, ÔÇőUkrainian officials ÔÇősaid on Tuesday.
Some 21 people ÔÇőhave ÔÇőbeen hospitalised and ÔÇî10 Ôüáare in a serious condition, ÔÇőregional ÔÇőgovernor Ôüásaid on ÔÇőthe Telegram ÔÇőmessaging Ôüáapp.

item.title: Hormuz mission talks to focus on sanctions, seafarer releases, shipping readiness ÔÇô source
item.description:
PARIS, April 14 (Reuters) - Diplomatic meetings led by France and Britain ÔÇőthis week to discuss a ÔÇîpotential future multinational mission will be structured around a series ÔÇőof working groups, ranging ÔÇőfrom possible sanctions on Iran Ôüáto cooperation with industry ÔÇőto ensure maritime transit, a ÔÇőperson familiar with the matter said.
According to the person, talks among ÔÇősenior diplomats ahead of ÔÇőa meeting coÔÇĹchaired by the British and ÔÇîFrench Ôüáleaders on Friday will focus on upholding freedom of navigation and maritime security, ÔÇőpursuing economic ÔÇőmeasures Ôüáagainst Iran if the Strait of Hormuz ÔÇőremains closed, securing the ÔÇőrelease Ôüáof seafarers and stranded vessels, and working with industry Ôüáto ÔÇősupport readiness to ÔÇőresume transit when conditions allow.

item.title: Nigeria police arrest 33-member gang over Kwara church abductions
item.description:
ABUJA, April 14 (Reuters) - Nigerian police have arrested a 33-member gang over a November
attack ÔÇőin which 38 people were abducted from ÔÇîa church in central Kwara state, police said on Tuesday, as part of a nationwide crackdown on ÔÇőviolence.
Federal police spokesperson Anthony Okon Placid ÔÇősaid the gang carried out the November Ôüá18 attack on Christ Apostolic Church ÔÇőin Eruku town and was also responsible for ÔÇőother kidnappings, cattle rustling, and armed robbery across the region.
The 33 suspects were arrested in coordinated raids ÔÇőacross Kwara and neighbouring Kogi state following ÔÇőhuman and technical intelligence, part of a wider operation ÔÇîthat Ôüáhas detained 50 suspects since January, Placid said in a statement.
Nigeria has long grappled with insecurity, from mass kidnappings for ransom ÔÇőto banditry and ÔÇőmilitant Ôüáattacks. The Eruku assault intensified pressure on the government, already under ÔÇőscrutiny from U.S. President Donald Trump, ÔÇőwho Ôüáhad threatened military action over what he calls persecution of Christians.
Placid said the police would charge Ôüáseveral ÔÇősuspects soon, while pursuing fleeing ÔÇőgang members and recovering more weapons.

item.title: Italy suspends defence cooperation deal with Israel
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - Italy's government has decided to ÔÇősuspend the automatic renewal of a defence ÔÇîcooperation deal with Israel, Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said on Tuesday, citing the conflicts in the Middle East .
Meloni's ÔÇőright-wing government has been one of ÔÇőIsrael's closest allies in Europe, but in Ôüárecent weeks it has criticised Israeli attacks on ÔÇőLebanon. Those affected have included Italian troops serving ÔÇőthere under a U.N. mandate.
"In light of the current situation, the government has decided to suspend the automatic ÔÇőrenewal of the defence agreement with Israel," ÔÇőMeloni was quoted as saying in Verona, nothern Italy, ÔÇîby ÔüáItalian news agencies.
A defence ministry source said one of the consequences is that Italy will no longer cooperate with Israel on military training.
Meloni ÔÇőtook the ÔÇődecision on ÔüáMonday with her foreign and defence ministers, Antonio Tajani and Guido ÔÇőCrosetto, as well as Deputy Prime ÔÇőMinister ÔüáMatteo Salvini, the source said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
A spokesperson for Israel's foreign ministry Ôüádid ÔÇőnot immediately respond to a ÔÇőrequest for comment from Reuters.

item.title: No discussions between Qatar and Iran over payment of funds to stop Iranian attacks, Qatari official says
item.description:
DOHA, April 14 (Reuters) - There have been no ÔÇődiscussions between Qatar ÔÇîand Iran over the payment ÔÇőof funds to ÔÇőstop Iranian attacks Ôüáon Qatar and ÔÇőany suggestions that ÔÇősuch discussions took place are untrue, the ÔÇőQatari foreign ÔÇőministry spokesperson said on Tuesday.
"There ÔÇîis Ôüáa high level of coordination with Pakistan and ÔÇőthe ÔÇőUnites ÔüáStates, our demands are ÔÇőbeing raised ÔÇőthrough Ôüáthese channels," Majed Al-Ansari said in Ôüáa ÔÇőmedia ÔÇőbriefing.

item.title: Kremlin says it is glad Hungary's Magyar seems ready for 'pragmatic dialogue' with Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - The Kremlin said on Tuesday that it was pleased that Hungarian Prime Minister-elect Peter ÔÇőMagyar seemed ready for pragmatic dialogue with Russia and ÔÇîwould base its own position on the specific steps taken by his new government
Russian hardliners have mourned the defeat of Viktor Orban, ÔÇőa close partner of Moscow's, who lost a weekend ÔÇőelection to upstart centre-right rival Magyar. But the ÔüáKremlin quickly said it was ready for talks with ÔÇőthe country's new leadership.
"For now, we can note with satisfaction, ÔÇőas far as we understand, his (Magyar's) willingness to engage in pragmatic dialogue," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov told reporters.
"In this instance, there is ÔÇőmutual willingness on our part, and we will then ÔÇőproceed to take our cue from the specific steps taken by the ÔÇînew ÔüáHungarian government."
Russia sells oil and gas to Hungary and is building a new nuclear power plant south of Budapest. Orban was useful to Moscow because he often spoke out ÔÇőagainst EU ÔÇősanctions on Russia Ôüáover its war in Ukraine, opposed Kyiv's bid to join the EU, and had ÔÇőblocked a 90-billion-euro ($105-billion) EU loan to Ukraine.
Magyar ÔÇőhas combined Ôüápro-EU and pro-NATO rhetoric with a public recognition that he will need to hold talks with Russian President Vladimir Putin Ôüáand ÔÇőcontinue to buy Russian oil and ÔÇőgas for now despite talk of diversifying and reviewing contracts.

item.title: Morning Bid: War-weary markets filter out noise
item.description:
April 14 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Markets are clinging to hopes that there will be an eventual breakthrough in U.S.-Iran negotiations, with oil prices retreating and stocks rebounding even as the U.S. blockade ÔÇőof Iranian ports takes effect .
The half-hearted stock selloff early on Monday, which later unwound , indicated that the impact of each twist and turn in the ÔÇîconflict appears to be dissipating, with investors sticking to the view that weÔÇÖll soon see significant de-escalation and a freeing up of oil supplies.
I'll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why Hungary's political shift could have big implications for EU assets.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss the S&P 500's return - ÔÇőjust about - to its pre-war level.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in your calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for ÔÇőa timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up here , opens new tab .
Despite the imposition of Ôüáthe U.S. blockade and hard talk between Washington and Tehran, reports over the past 24 hours have suggested there is still some dialogue between them and that talks could resume ÔÇőthis week .
ThatÔÇÖs been enough to guide Brent and WTI crude back below $100 per barrel. OilÔÇÖs retreat helped spark a rally on Wall Street on Monday, with the S&P 500 ÔÇőending up about 1%, leaving it higher than it was at the outbreak of the war more than six weeks ago.
That momentum has so far carried on into Tuesday, with Asian stock indexes closing in the green, European shares rising , and Wall Street futures ticking up . The dollar index fell to its lowest point in a month-and-a-half on the upturn in risk sentiment.
Turning to corporate news, earnings season ÔÇőis well and truly underway as traders await more updates from the big U.S. banks today. Goldman Sachs, which reported on Monday, stumbled in fixed income and ÔÇőcurrency trading during the quarter, pushing its shares down despite headline results beating estimates.
JPMorgan, Citi, and Wells Fargo are up next in whatÔÇÖs expected to be another impressive corporate scorecard, despite the ÔÇîoil shock Ôüáat the end of the quarter.
The IMF, meantime, will have the unenviable task today of releasing its forecasts for the world economy for this year. Both it and the World Bank have already signaled they will downgrade their global growth projections and raise their inflation predictions because of the war.
On the U.S. macro front, existing home sales fell to a nine-month low in March, pushed down by a lackluster labor market and stumbling household purchasing power and wealth. The outlook for the year appears less than stellar ÔÇőas mortgage rates rise amid the ÔÇőIran war.
Elsewhere, ChinaÔÇÖs exports fell in March Ôüáas the Iran war appeared to hamper AI-driven tech demand. Outbound shipments grew by just 2.5%, a five-month low, and far below the 21.8% surge seen in February. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast growth of 8.3%.
Chart of the day
Goldman Sachs beat expectations ÔÇőfor quarterly profit on Monday, driven by strength in dealmaking and equities trading, but the U.S. investment bank's shares fell ÔÇő2% on weakness in Ôüáfixed income markets, interest rate trading, mortgages and credit. The bank's revenue hit $17.2 billion, however, its highest three-month tally since a record of $17.7 billion in Q1 2021.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March PPI (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* U.S. 12-month bill auction (11:30 a.m. EDT)
* FedÔÇÖs Michael Barr, Boston FedÔÇÖs Susan Collins, Richmond FedÔÇÖs Thomas Barkin, and Philadelphia FedÔÇÖs Anna Paulson all speak
* ÔüáU.S. corporate ÔÇőearnings: JPMorgan, Citigroup, Wells Fargo
* IMF publishes its latest World Economic Outlook (9 a.m. EDT)
Want to receive ÔÇőthe Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They ÔÇődo not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: No shortage of jet fuel in EU, but concerns remain, Commission says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, April 14 (Reuters) - There is no shortage of jet fuel in ÔÇőthe European Union at the ÔÇîmoment, but supply problems could occur and the situation remains a ÔÇőtop concern, the European Commission ÔÇősaid on Tuesday.
European airlines have urged Ôüáthe European Union to step ÔÇőin with emergency measures to ÔÇőtackle repercussions from the Iran war, including widespread airspace closures and mounting concerns ÔÇőover jet fuel shortages, a ÔÇődocument seen by Reuters showed.
"There is no ÔÇîevidence Ôüáfor fuel shortage in the European Union at present, but supply issues could occur in ÔÇőthe near ÔÇőfuture," Ôüáa Commission spokesperson told reporters in Brussels.
"Crude oil ÔÇősupplies to the European ÔÇőrefineries Ôüáremain stable, with no need for additional stock releases at the Ôüápresent. ÔÇőHowever, that remains ÔÇőour primary concern," the spokesperson added.

item.title: Russia imposes temporary export controls on helium
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 14 (Reuters) - Russia has imposed temporary export controls on helium to maintain a stable ÔÇősupply to the domestic market, where the gas ÔÇîis mainly used in the production of fibre optics, the government said in a statement on Tuesday.
The war in the Middle East has tightened ÔÇőthe supply of the gas, which is also used ÔÇőin several key stages of chipmaking, including cooling, leak Ôüádetection and precision manufacturing processes.
The decree added helium to a ÔÇőlist of commodities whose export outside the Eurasian Economic Union (EEU) ÔÇőrequires special permission by top government officials. The new export regime will be in force until the end of 2027.
Russia is the third-largest global ÔÇőproducer of helium, a byproduct of natural gas processing, accounting ÔÇőfor around 8% of the global production, according to Gazprombank's analysts. At ÔÇîhome, Ôüáhelium is important for the production of fibre optics, increasingly used by the army to control drones.
However, Russia trails far behind the United States and Qatar, with the Middle Eastern country ÔÇőproducing over one-third ÔÇőof the Ôüáglobal supply in 2025.
Earlier this month, Russian Prime Minister Mikhail Mishustin said that global supply disruptions ÔÇőcaused by the war in the Middle East ÔÇőhave opened Ôüáup new trade opportunities for Russia, but price stability in the domestic market remains a priority.
He mentioned helium among commodities whose Ôüáexports ÔÇőRussia can increase. Gazprom's (GAZP.MM) , opens new tab Amur Gas ÔÇőProcessing Plant in the Far East is the biggest producer of helium in ÔÇőRussia.

item.title: Yancoal Australia to buy 80% stake in Kestrel coal mine for up to $2.4 billion
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Yancoal Australia (YAL.AX) , opens new tab , said ÔÇîon Tuesday it has agreed to acquire an 80% stake in the Kestrel coking coal mine in Queensland for up to $2.4 billion, a move that strengthens its position as one ÔÇőof the country's largest coal miners.
Yancoal, which has a market capitalisation of about $6.77 ÔÇőbillion, will purchase the majority stake in the Bowen Basin-based mine Ôüáfrom private equity firm EMR Capital and Alamtri Resources Indonesia (ADRO.JK) , opens new tab .
The remaining 20% is ÔÇőheld by Japanese trading house Mitsui & Co (8031.T) , opens new tab , which did not immediately respond to a ÔÇőReuters request for comment.
The transaction comprises an upfront cash payment of $1.85 billion on completion and up to $550 million in additional annual payments over five years, subject to certain conditions.
Yancoal said ÔÇőit intends to fund the acquisition via a combination of available cash and ÔÇőa $1.2 billion five-year syndicated acquisition loan facility, among other options.
The company, controlled by China's Yankuang Energy (600188.SS) , opens new tab , ÔÇîsaid Ôüáthe acquisition would strengthen its position in the Bowen Basin, with Kestrel located near YancoalÔÇÖs existing Middlemount joint venture and Yarrabee operation.
The acquisition was a strategic fit for the company and adds a high-quality mine to its portfolio, CEO Sharif ÔÇőBurra said.
"Kestrel delivers ÔÇőincreased scale and Ôüádiversification to YancoalÔÇÖs portfolio and is expected to contribute premium metallurgical coal into our product mix."
Kestrel, the largest producing underground ÔÇőcoal mine in Australia, recorded saleable production of 5.9 ÔÇőmillion tons Ôüáin 2025, Yancoal said.
The deal is expected to close by the end of the third quarter of 2026.
EMR and Alamtri had bought the 80% stake in the coal Ôüámine ÔÇőfrom mining giant Rio Tinto (RIO.AX) , opens new tab for $2.25 billion in ÔÇő2018.
Yancoal has applied to resume trading on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange on Wednesday, after being halted ahead of ÔÇőthe acquisition announcement.

item.title: Sinopec buys Russian oil to replace Mideast supply after US waiver, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 14 (Reuters) - Chinese state oil giant Sinopec (600028.SS) , opens new tab has bought Russian oil loading in March and April to replace Middle Eastern crude after ÔÇőthe U.S. temporarily waived sanctions to ease tight supplies globally, several trade ÔÇîsources said.
One source pegged Sinopec's purchase volume at 8 to 10 cargoes of ESPO blend crude exported from the eastern port of Kozmino, while another estimated it at about 10 cargoes of ÔÇőESPO.
Each ESPO cargo is 740,000 barrels.
Sinopec bought the cargoes at premiums of $8 ÔÇőto $10 per barrel to ICE Brent, a third person said. Russian Ôüácrude traded at a discount of about $10 per barrel before the Iran conflict.
The sources ÔÇőspoke on condition of anonymity.
The U.S. Treasury Department allowed purchases of Russian oil and products ÔÇőat sea from mid-March with a 30-day waiver that expired on April 11, as part of efforts to control global energy prices during the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran.
The waiver led the trading ÔÇőarms of Sinopec and PetroChina to make inquiries with suppliers for possible purchases. They had ÔÇősuspended seaborne purchases of Russian crude since October due to Western sanctions, Reuters reported previously.
It was ÔÇîunclear Ôüáif PetroChina has bought seaborne cargoes since.
Sinopec did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Sinopec, the world's largest refiner, usually sources roughly half of its crude from the Middle East, leaving it particularly exposed to the near-closure of ÔÇőthe Strait of Hormuz ÔÇőamid the U.S.-Israeli Ôüáwar on Iran.
Last month Sinopec told a results briefing it was cutting runs by 5% in March because of the disruption, while ÔÇőassessing the possibility of purchasing Russian oil under waiver.
China's seaborne Russian ÔÇőcrude imports Ôüáin March stood at 1.82 million bpd, off February's record of 1.92 million bpd, Kpler data showed. April's imports stand at 1.92 million bpd so far.
The U.S. waiver boosted demand Ôüáfrom ÔÇőIndian refiners who snapped up millions of barrels of ÔÇőRussian oil at sea. Market participants expect Washington to extend the waiver , although it has made no announcement.

item.title: BP flags 'exceptional' trading results amid oil price volatility
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - BP (BP.L) , opens new tab said on Tuesday it expects its huge oil trading desk ÔÇîto post "exceptional" results for the first quarter, signalling a windfall from the spike in oil prices triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
This broadly echoed the first-quarter outlook from European rival Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , which also ÔÇőflagged strong results in oil trading , an area where European majors are more active than ÔÇőU.S. competitors, triggering analysts to increase their earnings forecasts.
Citi analysts raised their Ôüáearnings forecast for BP by 20%, expecting first-quarter adjusted net income of $2.6 billion.
BP also said ÔÇőin a quarterly trading statement that its net debt would rise to between $25 billion and $27 ÔÇőbillion from just over $22 billion in the previous quarter because of movements in working capital, an accounting measure of liquidity based on current assets minus liabilities.
Global benchmark Brent crude soared ÔÇőto multi-year highs near $120 a barrel after U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began in late February, ÔÇőfollowed by Tehran's closure of the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping route and attacks on Gulf neighbours.
Brent ÔÇîaveraged Ôüáaround $78 a barrel during the January-to-March quarter, compared with $63 in the fourth quarter and $75 a barrel during the same time last year, according to Reuters calculations.
BP's overall oil and gas production is expected to be broadly flat on the quarter, it said.
Refining margins rose to $16.9 a ÔÇőbarrel in the first ÔÇőquarter from $15.2 in the Ôüáprevious three months, it said, adding this would help boost results in its refined products business by $100 million to $200 million.
Energy companies typically ÔÇődo not reveal full results of their trading divisions.
BP is due to report first-quarter results on April 28.
Meg O'Neill became the company's fifth CEO since 2020 this month, pledging to continue with a one-year-old revamp to redirect billions of spending Ôüáfrom ÔÇőlow-carbon projects into oil and gas to increase profitability.
She ÔÇőwill face shareholders at the company's annual general meeting on April 23 after influential proxy advisers and some shareholders ÔÇőhave supported votes against the board's wishes.

item.title: Tunisian judge orders detention of former head of anti-corruption body
item.description:
TUNIS, April 14 (Reuters) - A Tunisian judge ordered the detention of former anti-corruption chief Chawki Tabib on Tuesday, in the latest incident in what critics describe ÔÇőas an escalating crackdown on high-profile figures in the country.
A judicial ÔÇîofficial said the case against Tabib, who is also a former head of the Tunisian Bar Association, concerned financial and administrative mismanagement during his tenure heading the anti-corruption ÔÇőbody, which was dissolved by President Kais Saied in 2021.
Boubaker Ben ÔÇőThabet, the current head of the Tunisian Bar Association, told ÔüáReuters that Tabib's detention violated legal procedures and amounted to harassment.
Saied dissolved ÔÇőthe elected parliament in 2021 and began ruling by decree, a move the ÔÇőopposition called a coup.
In 2022, he also dissolved the Supreme Judicial Council and sacked dozens of judges. He said the steps were legal and aimed at fighting corruption.
Tabib said ÔÇőon Sunday, before his detention, that the new case was part of ÔÇőa campaign targeting him. He had previously been placed under a travel ban.
His detention ÔÇîcame a Ôüáday after courts sentenced other public figures in separate cases, including El Hiwar TV owner Sami Fehri to five years in prison on financial charges, and media commentator Sonia Dahmani to 18 months over media comments.
Last month, ÔÇőa Tunisian court ÔÇőhanded down a Ôüátwo-year prison sentence to news website editor Ghassen Ben Khelifa , a move the journalists' union described as part of a "systemic" ÔÇőattack on free speech.
The leaders of the main opposition parties ÔÇőin ÔüáTunisia have been jailed within the last three years, along with dozens of politicians, journalists, activists and businessmen on charges of conspiring against state security, money Ôüálaundering and ÔÇőcorruption.
The opposition says Saied is targeting the ÔÇőcountryÔÇÖs elite to silence dissent and strengthen one-man rule. Saied says he is purging corruption and "traitors", ÔÇőinsisting no one is above accountability.

item.title: Pope Leo, after Trump's attack, honours saint in Algeria who decried wars
item.description:
