ANNABA, Algeria, April 14 (Reuters) - Pope Leo paid homage to his spiritual roots on Tuesday, travelling to the ancient birthplace in Algeria of St. ÔÇőAugustine of Hippo, who inspired him to become a priest.
The first ÔÇîAmerican pope has drawn the ire of U.S. President Donald Trump after sharply criticising the Iran war . In honouring Augustine, Leo was highlighting a personal connection to a major figure of the ÔÇőearly Church who first proposed criteria to evaluate whether wars could be ÔÇőconsidered just.
Augustine, who died in the year 430, said wars Ôüáshould only be waged in order to defend against aggression or protect ÔÇőthe innocent, with the intention of restoring a state of peace and never ÔÇőout of a desire for cruelty.
Those criteria, with updates, remain Catholic doctrine. They have been invoked by some critics of the Iran war to argue that the conflict, started by ÔÇősurprise U.S.-Israeli airstrikes against Iran on February 28, is unjust.
Washington Cardinal Robert ÔÇőMcElroy is among those critics. He said on Sunday that the war was "morally illegitimate", citing ÔÇîAugustine's Ôüáprinciples.
Leo, who is on an ambitious, 10-day tour of four African countries, told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, despite Trump's comments.
He did not offer remarks on Tuesday as he visited the remnants of the city ÔÇőof Hippo, now ÔÇőAnnaba, on Algeria's Ôüánortheast coast. Under light rain, he laid a wreath of yellow and white roses and helped several Algerian Muslim ÔÇőScouts in tan uniforms plant a sapling.
At a later meeting ÔÇőon Tuesday Ôüáat a nearby nursing home run by Catholic sisters, the pope, a member of the Augustinian religious order, spoke out again.
"God's heart is torn apart by Ôüáwars, violence, ÔÇőinjustice and lies," Leo told the residents of ÔÇőthe Little Sisters of the Poor home. "Our Father's heart is not with the wicked, the arrogant ÔÇőor the proud."

item.title: Aid groups use land routes to deliver humanitarian relief to Iran
item.description:
GENEVA, April 14 (Reuters) - International humanitarian groups said ÔÇőon Tuesday they had sent emergency relief through overland routes to Iran, some of ÔÇîtheir first deliveries of aid since U.S.-Israeli strikes began in late February.
Aid workers say needs are high in the wake of six weeks of strikes, but stocks of emergency humanitarian supplies became stuck in Dubai warehouses as shipping ÔÇőand air routes were blocked by the expanding conflict.
Iranian authorities say more than 3,000 people ÔÇőhave been killed in Iran during the war and the U.N. refugee agency Ôüásays up to 3.2 million have been displaced.
The International Federation of Red Cross and Red ÔÇőCrescent Societies said it had delivered some 200 trauma kits as well as tents and blankets on ÔÇőtrucks sent along a new route from Turkey.
The trucks crossed the Iranian border on Sunday and are set to arrive in the capital Tehran on Tuesday, said IFRC spokesperson Tommaso Della Longa.
"For us it's very important as ÔÇőit represents a new route for getting aid into Iran and we're very optimistic to scale ÔÇőup," he told a Geneva press briefing.
"Before it was very easy to take a flight or a boat and ÔÇîbring Ôüáaid directly to Iran in a couple of hours."
Separately, the International Committee of the Red Cross said it had made its first shipment to Iran since the conflict began, with five trucks arriving from Jordan on Monday. Another nine are expected later this week.
It also handed supplies to the ÔÇőIranian Red Crescent, the only ÔÇőhumanitarian group working Ôüáacross Iran, with 100,000 responders, four of whom have been killed in the war.
"Needs are high, medical needs in particular, but also the psychological toll ÔÇőon people is immense," Della Longa said in a statement.
"Our partners at ÔÇőthe Iranian ÔüáRed Crescent have been providing psychological care in addition to the work they did throughout, literally digging people out of buildings."
The ICRC trucks contained supplies like buckets, blankets and mattresses to meet the needs Ôüáof some ÔÇő25,000 people, including those displaced by the conflict.
Following the collapse ÔÇőof weekend negotiations to end the war, sources told Reuters that negotiating teams from the U.S. and Iran could return to ÔÇőIslamabad this week to resume talks.

item.title: Malaysia brings home high-value art, including a Picasso, seized in 1MDB probe
item.description:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 14 (Reuters) - Malaysia's anti-graft agency said on Tuesday it had successfully repatriated four high-value pieces of artwork, including a 1961 Pablo Picasso print, recovered in connection with ÔÇőa multibillion-dollar scandal at state fund 1MDB .
Over the past decade, Malaysia has sought ÔÇîto recover more than $4.5 billion siphoned from 1Malaysia Development Berhad between 2009 and 2014 in a complex, globe-spanning scheme.
U.S. investigators have said the funds - allegedly misappropriated by high-level officials of 1MDB and their ÔÇőassociates - were used to buy everything from luxury hotels to jewelry and ÔÇőartwork.
The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission said in a statement the recovered artworks were Ôüávalued at around $198,000 and include Picasso's "LÔÇÖEcuy├Ęre et les clowns" (1961), Joan Mir├│'s "Composition" (1953), Maurice Utrillo's "Maison de ÔÇőRendez-vous de chasse de Henri IV, Rue St. Vincent, Montmartre" (1934) and Balthus' "├ëtude pour femme ÔÇőcouch├ęe" (1948).
"All artworks have been placed in a specially designated location under strict security control and are currently undergoing an environmental stabilisation process to ensure optimal preservation prior to uncrating, authentication, and valuation by the ÔÇőNational Visual Arts Development Board," the agency said.
The MACC said in December it would ÔÇőrepatriate a dozen pieces of art held in the United States, following discussions with officials from ÔÇîthe Federal ÔüáBureau of Investigation and U.S. Justice Department.
The Picasso and Mir├│ pieces were among the 12 artworks that were recovered from a former 1MDB lawyer and then stored at the Christie's and Sotheby's auction houses, the MACC said.
Authorities have said the artwork may be ÔÇőpublicly displayed at Malaysia's ÔÇőnational art gallery Ôüábefore potentially being auctioned for sale.
"Each recovered asset, including these artworks, demonstrates that there is no safe haven for illicit proceeds to ÔÇőbe concealed, even when involving complex cross-border financial networks," the MACC ÔÇősaid on ÔüáTuesday.
To date, Malaysia has recovered about 31.2 billion ringgit ($7.90 billion) in assets, representing nearly 70% of all identified properties linked to 1MDB, it said.
1MDB, co-founded by former prime minister Najib ÔüáRazak, ÔÇőhas been the subject of corruption and money laundering ÔÇőinvestigations in at least six countries. Najib, who has consistently denied wrongdoing, was jailed in 2022 after being convicted ÔÇőof graft linked to the scandal.
($1 = 3.9490 ringgit)

item.title: Greenland appoints former PM Egede as foreign minister
item.description:
COPENHAGEN, April 14 (Reuters) - Greenland's prime minister said on Tuesday he had appointed ÔÇőhis predecessor Mute Egede as foreign ÔÇîminister, handing him the job of steering relations with the United States amid pressure from U.S. President Donald ÔÇőTrump for control over the island.
Prime Minister ÔÇőJens-Frederik Nielsen said Egede's ministerial portfolio will Ôüáalso include those of mineral resources and ÔÇőbusiness policies.
Trump has repeatedly said that the United ÔÇőStates needs Greenland, a part of the Kingdom of Denmark, for security reasons, causing a rift with European NATO ÔÇőallies who reject his claim.
Seeking to salvage ÔÇőrelations, Greenland, Denmark and the U.S. in late January and have said negotiations are still ongoing, with further meetings scheduled to take place.
As prime minister until mid-2025, Egede, the current finance minister, repeatedly ÔÇőemphasised that ÔÇőGreenland is Ôüánot for sale and that its people will determine their own ÔÇőfuture, rejecting Trump's approach.
Greenland's delegation in the ÔÇőinitial ÔüáWashington talks was led by Vivian Motzfeldt, who stepped down from the job of foreign Ôüáminister ÔÇőlast month when her party resigned ÔÇőfrom the coalition government over issues unrelated to the U.S. ÔÇőconflict.

item.title: Former Chinese defence, auto executive Liu Weidong handed 13-year prison sentence over graft
item.description:
BEIJING, April 14 (Reuters) - Liu Weidong, a former deputy general manager of ÔÇőChina South Industries Group Corporation ÔÇîand an auto industry veteran, was sentenced to 13 years in ÔÇőprison on Tuesday for ÔÇőtaking bribes, state-run Xinhua news agency Ôüáreported.
($1 = 6.8158 Chinese yuan renminbi)

item.title: Fujimori heads toward Peru runoff as vote count enters third day
item.description:
LIMA, April 14 (Reuters) - Conservative Keiko Fujimori will advance to a presidential ÔÇőrunoff in Peru as the vote count entered a ÔÇîthird day on Tuesday, with a former Lima mayor and centerÔÇĹleft candidate locked in a tight battle for ÔÇősecond place, according to the official count.
Fujimori, a former ÔÇőcongresswoman and the daughter of former President ÔüáAlberto Fujimori, will advance to a presidential run-off, expected on ÔÇőJune 7, as she held 16.9% of the votes ÔÇőwith 74% of votes counted, the official election tally showed.
Right-wing former Lima mayor, Rafael Lopez Aliaga, and center-left Jorge Nieto ÔÇőare vying for second place with about 13% ÔÇőand 12% of the votes respectively, as the count by Peru's ÔÇîelectoral Ôüábody, ONPE, moved into a third day.
Outsider Ricardo Belmont stood in fourth place securing just below 10% of the votes.
With none of the leading candidates ÔÇőanywhere near ÔÇőthe 50% Ôüárequired to win outright, a runoff will be required, prolonging political uncertainty in ÔÇőthe world's thirdÔÇĹlargest copper producer.
The tally ÔÇőfor Sunday's Ôüáelection has moved into a third day after officials extended voting hours into Monday for more than 50,000 Ôüápeople ÔÇőwho were unable to cast ÔÇőballots as widespread delays and logistical failures hampered voting day.

item.title: Pope Leo's visit revives priest's hopes for peace in anglophone Cameroon
item.description:
DAKAR, April 14 (Reuters) - Armed with pistols and an AK-47 assault rifle, the rebels ambushed Rev. Killian Ndonui Nshamikara in January as he was driving through a village in western ÔÇőCameroon, kidnapping him and demanding a ransom of more than $25,000.
For the 40-year-old Catholic priest, the scene was stressful but familiar: It marked his third time being ÔÇîabducted during the conflict in CameroonÔÇÖs two English-speaking regions, which has killed thousands since erupting nearly a decade ago.
He has managed to negotiate his release each time.
Pope Leo ÔÇÖs trip to anglophone Cameroon - part of a whirlwind 10-day Africa tour - is expected to shine a rare spotlight on the violence there, which is typically overshadowed by unrest elsewhere in the region, from jihadist attacks in the Sahel to rebel advances in mineral-rich eastern Congo.
The ÔÇőpontiff arrives in the Central African nation on Wednesday and is scheduled to spend much of Thursday in Bamenda, Cameroon's biggest English-speaking city, holding Mass and a ÔÇťmeeting for ÔÇőpeaceÔÇŁ in a cathedral.
The Catholic Church is the leading Christian denomination in the English-speaking regions. A separatist alliance Ôüásaid it would observe a three-day ceasefire to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Ndonui is among those hoping that LeoÔÇÖs arrival will revive flagging peace efforts, ÔÇőeven if the odds are stacked against a speedy resolution to a conflict rooted in the country's complex colonial history.
ÔÇťCameroon is a nation in need of healing,ÔÇŁ he said.
Cameroon, a former German ÔÇőcolony, was partitioned by Britain and France after World War One. The French part won independence in 1960 and was joined a year later by the smaller English-speaking British area to the west.
At the time, many in the British territory wanted to form their own state, but they were not given the option under a U.N.-officiated referendum. Secessionist sentiments have simmered ever since.
The current conflict erupted in 2016 following protests by English-speaking ÔÇőlawyers and teachers who complained of marginalisation. Calls to form a new state called Ambazonia gained momentum, and an armed group called the Ambazonia Defence Forces began attacking government soldiers. Dozens ÔÇőof other groups formed to attack troops and civilians.
Nearly a decade later, the fighting has morphed into a brutal standoff. More than 6,500 people have been killed and more than half ÔÇîa million displaced, Ôüáaccording to the International Crisis Group.
The Catholic Church has not been spared. Ndonui said hundreds of priests had been kidnapped and at least five killed, including a Kenyan missionary.
In August 2018, gunmen killed Akiata Gerald, a 22-year-old seminarian who lived with Ndonui, storming the parish and shooting him at close range.
ÔÇťHis life, full of promise and dedication to God, was cut short in a senseless act of violence," Ndonui said.
He blames the rebels for the killing, though he said he did not know which specific group was responsible.
Efforts to broker a ÔÇőpeace deal have so far amounted to little.
The ÔÇőmain separatist leaders were not represented at Ôüáa national dialogue convened by the government in 2019. Mediation attempts involving Switzerland and Canada have also stalled.
The consequences can be seen on the streets of Bamenda, a once-bustling regional capital that has been reshaped by a decade of clashes, with checkpoints and general strikes paralysing daily ÔÇőlife.
Leo will have a tough time inspiring a new, meaningful push for peace on his own, said Enowbachem Agbortanyi, a Yaounde-based political ÔÇőanalyst.
ÔÇťThe pope can propose, but Ôüáhe cannot enforce,ÔÇŁ he said.
ÔÇťThat responsibility lies primarily with the government, which holds legitimate authority.ÔÇŁ
The government has described the fighting in anglophone Cameroon as a "socio-political crisis" that is largely under control. A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment on the possible impact of the pope's visit.
Lucas Asu, spokesperson for the Ambazonia Governing Council, one of the main separatist groups, told Reuters he Ôüásuspected President Paul ÔÇőBiya's government would "try to spin the pope's visit as an indication of the end of the conflict and ÔÇőa return to normalcy".
But while he stressed the visit would have no bearing on how the fighting unfolds, he added that it would be a moment for Catholics in anglophone Cameroon to celebrate.
"The Ambazonia Governing Council calls on the ÔÇőAmbazonian Christians, and especially the Catholics, to turn up in the millions and joyfully welcome their pope," he said.

item.title: US producer prices increase less than expected in March
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. producer prices increased less than expected in March as the cost of services was unchanged, but surging energy prices ÔÇőbecause of the war with Iran were fanning inflation pressures.
The ÔÇîProducer Price Index for final demand rose 0.5% last month after a downwardly revised 0.5% gain in February, the Labor Department's Bureau of Labor Statistics said on ÔÇőTuesday.
A jump in energy prices was partially offset by ÔÇősteady prices for services. March's PPI data likely only Ôüáshowed the initial impact of the Middle East conflict. Economists polled ÔÇőby Reuters had forecast the PPI accelerating 1.1% after a previously reported ÔÇő0.7% gain in February.
In the 12 months through March, the PPI advanced 4.0% after increasing 3.4% in February.
Further increases are likely as oil prices shot ÔÇőup on Monday to more than $100 a barrel after the ÔÇőU.S. military said it would blockade ships leaving Iran's ports.
Oil prices have jumped ÔÇîmore Ôüáthan 35% since the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran started at the end of February.
The BLS reported last week that the Consumer Price Index logged its biggest monthly increase in nearly four years in March ÔÇőamid a record ÔÇőjump in the Ôüácost of gasoline and diesel.
The Federal Reserve tracks the Personal Consumption Expenditures price indexes for its ÔÇő2% inflation target.
Prior to the PPI report, economists estimated ÔÇőthat ÔüáPCE inflation, excluding the volatile food and energy components, increased 0.2% in March after rising 0.4% for two consecutive months. That would translate to Ôüáa ÔÇőyear-on-year increase of 3.1%, up from 3.0% ÔÇőin February. Economists expect the oil price shock will have a moderate impact on ÔÇőthe so-called core inflation.

item.title: Jet fuel shock from Iran war worsens crisis for global airlines
item.description:
BENGALURU/BRUSSELS/LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Air travel's worst crisis in years lurched deeper on Tuesday as Qantas Airways warned ÔÇîof spiralling costs, Lufthansa said it may have to ground planes and Virgin Atlantic flagged a looming supply crunch, with the Iran conflict squeezing fuel supplies.
The war has upended routes between Asia and Europe that relied on Gulf hubs, while a doubling of jet fuel prices and tightening of supplies are hitting airlines hard. Since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began on February 28, carriers have hiked air fares, introduced ÔÇőfuel surcharges and cut routes.
Underscoring efforts to preserve cash, Qantas has delayed a planned share buyback, citing higher and volatile fuel prices, one of the first ÔÇőmajor carriers to stall shareholder returns.
Meanwhile, Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab CEO Carsten Spohr warned that jet fuel supplies will remain constrained, driving up costs.
"Kerosene will remain Ôüáin short supply and therefore more expensive for the rest of the year," Spohr told German newspaper Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung.
Lufthansa has not yet grounded planes due to shortages ÔÇőbut this "may be unavoidable" as kerosene availability is already critical at some airports, particularly in Asia, he said.
In South Korea, low-cost carrier T'way Air (091810.KS) , opens new tab plans to furlough some cabin crew without ÔÇőpay in May and June, a local report said, among the first carriers to reduce staffing.
A two-week ceasefire has provided little relief with the Strait of Hormuz still shut, removing roughly a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas supplies from the market and refineries will take time to repair damage inflicted on them.
"Despite the pause in the conflict we remain concerned about jet kerosene supply and price ÔÇőincrease," UBS analyst Jarrod Castle said in a note on Tuesday, adding that December jet kerosene futures prices are still up more than 50%ÔÇëyear-on-year.
Fuel, typically airlines' second-largest cost after ÔÇőlabour, accounts for about 27% of operating expenses. Prices have more than doubled since the conflict began, far outpacing a roughly 50% rise in crude prices before the ceasefire.
The turmoil may spur ÔÇîconsolidation, with Ôüástronger airlines gaining share from weaker rivals, analysts and executives said.
Reuters reported on Monday that United Airlines (UAL.O) , opens new tab CEO Scott Kirby pitched the potential for merging with American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab days before the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran.
Flight capacity, in particular from the Middle East but also into Europe, has shrunk and is not projected to recover to pre-conflict levels anytime soon, analysts said.
Virgin Atlantic CEO Corneel Koster said in an interview with the Financial Times that the airline has about six weeks of secure jet fuel supplies ÔÇőbefore the outlook gets more uncertain.
And European airlines ÔÇőon Tuesday urged Brussels to step in Ôüáwith emergency measures to cushion the impact, including EU-level kerosene purchasing, a temporary suspension of the bloc's carbon market for aviation and scrapping certain aviation taxes.
Industry group Airports Council International Europe (ACI) warned last week that Europe could face a systemic jet fuel shortage in three ÔÇőweeks.
Several carriers, including SAS, are not hedged, leaving them fully exposed to soaring fuel costs. Delta Air Lines (DAL.N) , opens new tab last week said ÔÇőits jet fuel bill Ôüáthis quarter would be some $2 billion more than last year.
While Qantas has hedged much of its crude exposure, it remains significantly exposed to the spike in jet fuel spreads.
To offset rising costs, the Australian flag carrier is raising fares and shifting capacity toward stronger routes such as Europe, where demand remains firm, while trimming domestic capacity by about 5 percentage points in the June Ôüáquarter.
Lufthansa's Spohr said ÔÇőrecord revenues on Asian routes were also helping offset the impact of rising kerosene costs.
But the airline ÔÇőhas prepared contingency plans, including cutting its capacity by 2.5% or 5% and grounding 20 to 40 older, less fuel-efficient aircraft earmarked for early retirement.

item.title: Exclusive: -TrumpÔÇÖs son Eric to join father's state visit to China
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's son Eric Trump ÔÇőand his wife, Lara, will ÔÇîaccompany the president on his May trip to China, a ÔÇőspokeswoman for Eric Trump ÔÇőtold Reuters on Tuesday.
"Eric and ÔüáLara Trump are proud ÔÇőto accompany the president on ÔÇőhis state visit," said the spokeswoman. "Eric is deeply proud of his ÔÇőfather and the accomplishments ÔÇőof this term, and is attending in ÔÇîa Ôüápersonal capacity as a supportive son.
"He does not have business ventures in China ÔÇőnor plans ÔÇőon Ôüádoing business in China. He will not ÔÇőbe participating in private ÔÇőmeetings, Ôüábut will instead stand alongside the president to mark Ôüáthis ÔÇőhistoric occasion."

item.title: Pope Leo issues warning on democracy after Trump criticism
item.description:
ANNABA, Algeria, April 14 (Reuters) - Pope Leo warned of the risk of democracies sliding into "majoritarian tyranny" on Tuesday, in a ÔÇőletter issued by the Vatican two days after U.S. ÔÇîPresident Donald Trump attacked the pontiff on social media.
The first U.S. pope, writing to participants of a Vatican meeting about the use of power in democratic ÔÇősocieties, said democracies remained healthy only when they were ÔÇőrooted in moral values.
"Lacking this foundation, (democracy) risks becoming either Ôüáa majoritarian tyranny or a mask for the dominance of ÔÇőeconomic and technological elites," said Leo in the letter.
The text, released ÔÇőas the pope was undertaking an ambitious, 10-day tour of four African countries, did not directly address the U.S. or name any specific democracies.
Trump sharply ÔÇőcriticized Leo as " terrible " on Sunday night, after the pope had emerged ÔÇőin recent weeks as a growing critic of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
Leo told Reuters ÔÇîon ÔüáMonday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, despite Trump's comments.
In Tuesday's letter, the pope said the Catholic Church taught that power could not be seen as an end in ÔÇőitself "but as a ÔÇőmeans ordered toward Ôüáthe common good".
"This implies that the legitimacy of authority depends not on the accumulation of economic ÔÇőor technological strength, but on the wisdom and ÔÇővirtue Ôüáwith which it is exercised," said Leo.
The pope also urged leaders in democratic societies to avoid any temptation to hoard power.
"Temperance ... proves Ôüáessential ÔÇőfor the legitimate use of authority, for ÔÇőtrue temperance restrains inordinate self-exaltation and acts as a guardrail against the abuse ÔÇőof power," he said.

item.title: Despite Israeli firepower, Netanyahu struggles for political gains in Iran war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The Iran war was meant to deliver a defining victory over Tehran that would secure Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu's place in history. More than six weeks into the conflict, he has been unable to translate military might into political ÔÇőgain.
Despite Israel's overwhelming firepower, its enemies across every front have been weakened but not neutralised. Even after heavy Israeli-U.S. airstrikes and the loss of senior leaders, Iran remains intact and defiant.
Tehran's nuclear stockpiles endure, its ÔÇîmissile capability is now proven and it holds sway over the Strait of Hormuz , the artery for a fifth of global oil flows.
Palestinian Islamist militant group Hamas has not been disarmed or dismantled in Gaza , and Iran-backed Hezbollah continues to fire rockets at northern Israel from Lebanon .
"Netanyahu is not winning," said Danny Citrinowicz, a senior researcher on Iran at Israel's Institute for National Security Studies. "This war is a strategic failure. There is a gap between what he promised at the start of the campaign and where we ended up."
Netanyahu, 76, is paying a political price ÔÇőfor a military campaign launched with U.S. President Donald Trump that has failed to deliver a decisive outcome, political analysts in the region say.
Netanyahu's approval ratings have slipped and, with legislative elections due by late October, the political ÔÇőrisks he faces are rising.
Netanyahu's office did not respond to a Reuters request for comment for this article. The prime minister has criticised those who he said were diminishing ÔüáIsrael's achievements in Iran, saying Israel has emerged stronger and Iran weaker.
"There are massive achievements here. This is a historic change. We crushed the nuclear program. We crushed the missiles. We crushed the regime," he said in a statement on Saturday.
At ÔÇőthe start of the war, Netanyahu told Iranians they would be "called upon to take to the streets" and topple their clerical rulers. Security officials have since grown increasingly skeptical such an outcome will materialise any time soon, a senior Israeli military ÔÇőofficial said.
Two Israeli officials told Reuters the initial expectation had been for a swift operation to "finish the job" in three weeks. The war has instead expanded into a wider confrontation with regional and global implications.
Aviv Bushinsky, a former Netanyahu adviser, said the war on Iran had initially restored NetanyahuÔÇÖs standing, which had been damaged by the Hamas attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, that led to the Gaza war.
In the early stages of the Iran war , his hardline posture towards Hamas and Hezbollah resonated with parts of the Israeli public, but polls show ÔÇőhis ratings have slipped since then.
An April 11 survey by Hebrew University's Agam Labs found only 10% of Israelis viewed the war as successful, while support for Netanyahu was at 34%, down from 40% at the start of the war. More ÔÇőthan half rated his leadership as poor or very poor.
Political analysts in the region say that although the military campaign built almost entirely on airpower has been tactically impressive and produced operational gains, it has not compounded into a coherent and durable strategic endgame.
"There is ÔÇîthis idea that ÔüáF-15s and F-35s (fighter jets) can shape or remake the Middle East - that if you kill enough Iranian leaders, the regime will fall," said Citrinowicz. "ItÔÇÖs a flawed assumption, and the cost of it keeps getting higher every time."
Bushinsky also questioned the value of IsraelÔÇÖs reliance on targeted assassinations although Iranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei was among those killed in such strikes.
"There is always someone who replaces them," Bushinsky said. "It wakes the bear, it doesnÔÇÖt kill it."
Israeli officials and a Western source said Netanyahu had been informed of a ceasefire plan that was agreed last week only when it was in its final stages. The Western source said Netanyahu had been angered by being left out of the process.
The prime minister has sought since then to counter any ÔÇőperception that he was sidelined in the Pakistan-brokered talks, ÔÇőand on Tuesday issued a statement saying U.S. Vice ÔüáPresident JD Vance had called him from his plane to brief him on the talks.
Netanyahu has also launched a campaign to convince voters the war has been worth the cost.
The war effort has run up about $11.5 billion in budgetary costs, with a large share spent on defence, Israel's finance ministry said on Sunday.
NetanyahuÔÇÖs dilemma, diplomats in the region say, ÔÇőis likely to deepen in the absence of decisive military victories, with security problems remaining for Israel in Gaza and the Israel-occupied West Bank , and the conflict with Lebanon continuing.
Netanyahu could ÔÇőseek to block any immediate diplomatic Ôüábreakthrough in the war with Iran, calculating that a U.S.ÔÇôIran agreement would exacerbate his political troubles, regional diplomats say.
Netanyahu's office did not immediately respond when asked for comment on this assertion.
Israel has said it would accept a deal that curbs IranÔÇÖs missile and nuclear programmes and removes enriched uranium.
The war has crossed a critical threshold for Washington, experts on Iran say, with Iran realising it can survive a conflict with the U.S. and threaten its enemy by attacking Gulf infrastructure and controlling the Strait of Hormuz.
"You can't Ôüáput the genie ÔÇőback in the bottle," Citrinowicz said of Iran's control of the strait. "The Iranians feel strengthened now, they feel emboldened and they want much more ÔÇőthan they were offered in previous talks."
The biggest losers, said Aaron David Miller, a former U.S. Middle East negotiator, are the Gulf Arab states. The region, he said, faces the prospect of dealing with a more bitter, harder-line Iranian leadership.
Abdulaziz Sager, chairman of the SaudiÔÇĹbased Gulf Research Center, said Gulf ÔÇőstates would accept the risks of heightened confrontation with Iran if necessary to keep the strait open, rather than allow Tehran to threaten shipping or Gulf ports.

item.title: Israeli fire kills six in Gaza, including two children, medics say
item.description:
CAIRO, April 14 (Reuters) - Israeli fire killed at least six Palestinians, including two children, in separate incidents across the ÔÇőGaza Strip on Tuesday, local health officials said, in ÔÇîthe latest violence to undermine a U.S.-brokered ceasefire agreement.
Four people, including a young child, were killed in a strike that targeted a police vehicle in Gaza ÔÇőCity, the Hamas-run Interior Ministry said. A police officer was ÔÇőamong the dead, while nine bystanders were wounded, some Ôüácritically, it said.
In the north of the enclave, near Jabalia, Israeli ÔÇőfire killed three-year-old Yahya AlÔÇĹMalahi, health authorities and his family said.
Israel's military ÔÇődid not immediately provide comment on either incident.
In northern Gaza, Israel's military said it killed a man who had approached the armistice line with Hamas, describing ÔÇőhim as an armed militant.
Health authorities confirmed a man had been ÔÇőkilled in the area, without providing details.
The ceasefire that began last October halted ÔÇîtwo Ôüáyears of full-blown war but left Israeli troops in control of a depopulated zone that makes up well over half of Gaza, with Hamas in power in the remaining, narrow coastal strip.
Israel has escalated its ÔÇőattacks on Hamas-led ÔÇőpolice and security Ôüáforces since October, killing dozens, the group's officials in Gaza have told Reuters, accusing Israel of trying ÔÇőto cause chaos and anarchy.
Israel says it aims to ÔÇőthwart Ôüáattacks by Hamas and other militant factions.
More than 750 Palestinians have been killed since the ceasefire deal took effect, while militants have killed four Israeli Ôüásoldiers. ÔÇőIsrael and Hamas have traded blame for ÔÇőceasefire violations.
Palestinians also say Israeli forces have been expanding the zone they occupy. Israel ÔÇődenies this.

item.title: Lula seen ahead of Flavio Bolsonaro in Brazil election second-round, CNT/MDA poll shows
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazil's President Luiz Inacio ÔÇőLula da Silva ÔÇîis seen ahead of Senator Flavio Bolsonaro ÔÇőin a potential ÔÇőrunoff in the October Ôüágeneral elections, a ÔÇőCNT/MDA poll showed ÔÇőon Tuesday.
Lula would receive 44.9% of the votes in ÔÇőa runoff, ÔÇőcompared with 40.2% for Flavio ÔÇîBolsonaro, Ôüáson of former President Jair Bolsonaro, according to the poll.
CNT/MDA ÔÇőinterviewed ÔÇő2,002 Ôüápeople from April 8-12 in ÔÇő140 Brazilian municipalities. ÔÇőThe Ôüásurvey's margin of error is of Ôüá2.2 ÔÇőpercentage points ÔÇőin either direction.

item.title: 'Swift work to be done'┬áafter call with Hungary's Magyar, EU's von der Leyen says
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 14 (Reuters) - European Commission ÔÇőPresident Ursula von der Leyen said on Tuesday that she spoke with Hungary's election winner Peter ÔÇîMagyar and there is swift work to be done to introduce reforms needed for unfreezing EU funding.
"We discussed immediate priorities," von der Leyen said in a post on social media platform X.
"There is swift work to be done to restore, realign and reform. Restore ÔÇőthe rule of law. Realign with our shared European values. And reform, to unlock the opportunities ÔÇőoffered by European investments," she added.
Hungary has a total of about 17 billion euros of ÔüáEU funds frozen over rule of law concerns under the outgoing government of Prime Minister Viktor Orban.
Hungary is ÔÇőalso still waiting for the European Commission to sign off on a 16 billion euro defence investment plan under the ÔÇőbloc's SAFE loans scheme for defence projects.
Magyar, whose Tisza party won a two-thirds supermajority in Hungary's parliament, has pledged to prioritise unlocking the funds and is now under pressure to quickly implement reforms to unlock the money after his government takes office, likely in ÔÇőmid-May.
"The ball is in Magyar's court to prove he's serious about his campaign promises of wanting to mend ties ÔÇőwith Brussels," said one EU diplomat. "Now it's time to show, not tell," they added.
Part of Hungary's EU funding is frozen due ÔÇîto lack Ôüáof compliance with EU standards in areas such as academic freedom and right to asylum. Around 10 billion euros from the bloc's post-pandemic recovery fund is frozen until Hungary fulfills requirements to strengthen judicial independence and accountability, with a deadline of August 31 to implement reforms.
Magyar said in a post on Facebook after the call that von ÔÇőder Leyen told him the ÔÇőEuropean Commission would work Ôüáclosely with the new Hungarian government to make sure results are delivered within the ÔÇťextremely tight deadlineÔÇŁ available to release the funds.
Officials and analysts say introducing the required reforms will ÔÇőnot be an easy feat.
"The most important constraint is the deadline on 31st August ÔÇőfor completing all ÔüáRecovery and Resilience Facility milestones ÔÇô a window that may prove too narrow to unlock the roughly 10 billion euros of frozen EU funds available," said Liam Peach, an economist at Capital Economics.
Credit ratings agency Moody's said on Monday that "Tisza's parliamentary Ôüásupermajority will ÔÇőfacilitate the implementation of these plans". But it added that "officials appointed ÔÇőby Fidesz remain in position across key institutions" and "political differences across the country's institutions can complicate or delay legislative change, limiting the new governmentÔÇÖs ÔÇőability to deliver deep reforms."

item.title: US Treasury's Bessent says China has been 'unreliable partner' by hoarding oil during war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Tuesday ÔÇőthat China had been an ÔÇîunreliable global partner during the Middle East war by hoarding oil ÔÇősupplies and limiting exports of ÔÇőcertain goods, much as they Ôüáhoarded medical supplies during the ÔÇőpandemic.
Bessent told reporters he had ÔÇőspoken with Chinese officials about the issue. He dodged a question about whether ÔÇőthe dispute would derail ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump's plan to visit ÔÇîBeijing Ôüáat the end of the month, but said Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping had ÔÇőa very ÔÇőgood Ôüáworking relationship.
"I think the message for the visit ÔÇőis stability. We've had great ÔÇőstability Ôüáin the relationship since last summer; that emanates from the Ôüátop ÔÇődown," he said. "I ÔÇőthink that communication is the key."

item.title: Trump, India's Modi speak by phone, Indian media says
item.description: April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President ÔÇőDonald ÔÇîTrump and Indian ÔÇőPrime ÔÇőMinister Narendra ÔüáModi ÔÇőspoke by ÔÇőtelephone on Tuesday, ÔÇőIndian ÔÇőmedia reported, adding ÔÇîthat Ôüáthe call between ÔÇőthe ÔÇőtwo Ôüáleaders lasted ÔÇőnearly ÔÇő40 Ôüáminutes.

item.title: Erdogan tells Hungary's Magyar he believes bilateral ties will progress, Turkish presidency says
item.description:
ANKARA, April 14 (Reuters) - Turkish President Tayyip ÔÇőErdogan told ÔÇîHungarian election winner Peter Magyar ÔÇőin a ÔÇőphone call on Tuesday Ôüáthat he ÔÇőbelieved Turkey-Hungary ÔÇőties would progress further as he ÔÇőcongratulated him ÔÇőon his victory, the ÔÇîTurkish Ôüápresidency said.
In a statement, the presidency said ÔÇőErdogan ÔÇőalso Ôüátold Magyar he wanted ÔÇőto ÔÇődeepen Ôüáthe strategic partnership between the ÔüáNATO ÔÇőallies.

item.title: In India, $1 housekeepers spark a consumer, worker frenzy despite safety risks
item.description:
MUMBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - At Indian startup Pronto's training hub, women hone their chopping and mopping skills while learning how to send SOS signals if they feel unsafe inside customers' homes. They are set to join India's newest consumer craze: house help for $1 ÔÇőan hour.
Indu Jaiswar, 35, hopes doing household chores in her first job can help fund her son's dream of becoming a doctor. "This is what we've been doing in our own homes for years. Might as ÔÇîwell get paid for it," said the mother of two.
In a country with an entrenched culture of outsourcing household work, Indian startups Pronto and Snabbit and listed rival Urban Company (URBN.NS) , opens new tab are training thousands of domestic helpers. Urban Company estimates India's rapidly growing cleaning services market is worth an estimated $9 billion and spread across 53 million households.
Like Uber drivers, the helpers receive bookings on their apps, directing them to apartments in assigned neighbourhoods within minutes and press a countdown timer in their apps before starting work. The potential annual earnings from working eight hours a day can be as high as $5,000 - ÔÇőa figure that far surpasses India's per capita income of around $3,000.
The companies are betting big, burning millions of dollars to lure busy professionals in cities like New Delhi and Mumbai with under 99 rupee ($1) offerings that have no ÔÇőglobal parallel. Similar services can cost around $30 an hour in the United States, and around $7 in China.
However, the craze among consumers and workers is tempered by concerns about women's safety in a Ôüácountry with high rates of sexual harassment. Unlike e-commerce couriers who spend just brief moments at doorsteps, housekeepers may spend hours inside private homes, exposing them to greater risks.
Soumya Chauhan, a principal at Dutch e-commerce investor Prosus, which has a stake in ÔÇőUrban Company, said she views worker safety as the fundamental operational challenge to solve.
"The platforms that successfully crack the safety protocols will earn the deepest consumer loyalty and the most sustainable market returns," she said.
Cognisant of the challenges for a business that ÔÇőmainly employs women, Snabbit and Pronto said they have an in-app SOS button that alerts area supervisors in case of distress, while Pronto also offers self-defence training.
"In the offline world, the rate of abuse for a lot of these domestic workers is super high," said Pronto's 23-year-old CEO Anjali Sardana, adding that her company is trying to comfort its workers by assuring legal and medical support when needed.
Urban Company, which also offers services like plumbing, declined to comment for this story. It has previously said it offers a women-only safety helpline and an SOS app feature.
Shabnam Hashmi, a ÔÇőwomen's rights activist, said the companies run extensive background checks on workers before onboarding them but should also check customer credentials. Currently users can simply log in on apps to book home help.
"How is it ever possible for these jobs to be safe ÔÇőfor women - even with an SOS button? Unless they carry cameras, which is of course impossible, there is no way to know what happens behind that door," she said.
Pronto worker Jaiswar has found her own workaround: she always calls a customer before visiting a home and ÔÇîgoes "only if there's Ôüáa woman present".
The companies meanwhile are getting record orders.
Urban Company recorded its highest daily home services bookings of 50,000 in February. Snabbit's have grown to 35,000 orders a day.
Bain Capital-backed Pronto logged a record 22,000 daily bookings in March, up from 2,500 daily orders in October, and raised $25 million in new funding.
Pronto CEO Sardana said she started the business last year after spotting an opportunity to serve three sides: strong demand from customers for reliable maids, workers' need for more stable and safer jobs, and a gap in the market for a scalable service.
"It's possible to build a win-win-win business," she told Reuters.
Fuelling the trend is also India's lack of a do-it-yourself culture, and Indians' love for getting things done cheap.
In Bengaluru, 30-year-old Dhruv, who uses only a first name, ÔÇősaid he spent 100 rupees ($1) per hour for Urban Company's service ÔÇőto help unpack his utensils and hang curtains after Ôüámoving house.
That helped him "save quite a bit of time and effort," but the price does matter: "I wouldn't pay 400 or 500 rupees for it."
Snabbit founder Aayush Agarwal said his service was becoming popular among young couples and singles who want to schedule housekeepers and not hire monthly domestic helpers who are infamous for skipping work.
Pronto is offering some visits for 25 rupees in Facebook ÔÇőads with taglines like "Maid on Leave? Don't grieve", while an Urban Company three-visit pack costs 66 rupees an hour.
Snabbit ads said a customer booked a helper "just to peel 20 potatoes", while ÔÇőanother had lined up a worker Ôüáto "separate LEGO blocks by colour."
Like many startups in their growth phase, the companies are paying their workers out-of-pocket to make the jobs attractive, but also doling out hefty discounts to reel in customers.
In October to December, Urban Company disclosures show it received 1.61 million home-help orders with each incurring a loss of 381 rupees ($4). The company says its "discounts are moderating" but its order values need to almost double to break even.
"Over a period of time, it is safe to say that it will become an earn-as-you-go model," said Rahul ÔüáTaneja, partner at Lightspeed, ÔÇőwhich has backed Snabbit.
At the Pronto centre, where workers get a uniform and are trained to wear polished shoes, posters revealed potential payouts: home helpers ÔÇőcan earn $1.60 per hour for 12 hours of work daily in a month, 48% more than what a new customer pays.
At more than $500 a month, that's a big allure for Nisha Chandaliya, 22, who needs to support her ailing mother and has quit a call-centre job that stretched long hours and paid ÔÇőonly $180 a month.
"It's exhausting to clean six-seven homes, but I need the stability. I can't afford to go back," she said.
($1 = 93.3010 Indian rupees)

item.title: Two arms brokers on trial in UK for missiles and fighter jet deals with Libya and Sudan
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Two arms brokers arranged illegal deals to supply ex-Soviet surface-to-air missile systems to South Sudan and fighter jets to Libya ÔÇőduring its civil war, British prosecutors told a London court ÔÇîon Tuesday.
British national David Greenhalgh, 68, and Greek national Christos Farmakis, 48, are charged with 11 and 12 offences respectively of involvement in the unlicensed supply of arms ÔÇőbetween 2009 and 2016.
They have pleaded not guilty and their trial ÔÇőbegan this week at Southwark Crown Court. Farmakis has decided Ôüánot to attend his trial, which is proceeding in his absence, the ÔÇőjury was told.
Prosecutor Edmund Burge said Greenhalgh and Farmakis had "very close connections ÔÇőto senior figures" in South Sudan, with Farmakis appointed as South Sudan's "honorary consul" to Greece and Cyprus.
The two defendants brokered a deal for a "complete air defence missile system", which ÔÇőwas acquired from Ukraine for nearly $55 million, between 2009 and 2011, the ÔÇőprosecutor said.
South Sudan was still formally part of Sudan until it achieved independence in ÔÇî2011, ÔüáBurge added, meaning the semi-autonomous region was subject to a British arms embargo.
Greenhalgh and Farmakis discussed obtaining a Ugandan end-user certificate as "cover" to obscure the intended destination of the missile system, Burge said.
Burge said Farmakis was arrested ÔÇőin 2016 after using ÔÇőhis email address Ôüáfor his job at the government-funded Greater London Enterprise to privately arrange arms deals.
Farmakis' email account forwarded an ÔÇőemail to his boss, which led to the authorities ÔÇődiscovering documents Ôüárelating to plans to sell fighter jets and other arms to Libya in the wake of the 2011 Arab Spring, Burge added.
The prosecutor said emails and Ôüáother ÔÇődocuments suggested Greenhalgh and Farmakis had arranged to ÔÇősupply weapons to Iran, Iraq and Syria in breach of arms embargoes.
The trial, which is ÔÇődue to conclude in June, continues.

item.title: Drone attacks in Sudan kill two, injure 56, MSF says
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Medecins Sans Frontieres said on Tuesday it has recorded two deaths and treated 56 wounded ÔÇőpeople following five drone attacks carried out ÔÇîby the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) in the Darfur region.
MSF works in nine states across Sudan, responding to emergencies ÔÇőand providing medical care amid a war ÔÇőbetween the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid ÔüáSupport Forces.
"As Sudan enters its fourth year ÔÇőof war, these attacks by the Sudanese Armed ÔÇőForces demonstrate complete disregard for civilian life," the Geneva-based medical charity said in a statement. "We call on the warring parties ÔÇőin Sudan to protect civilians."
The U.N. human ÔÇőrights office has said there has been a sharp rise ÔÇîin Ôüáthe use of drones in Sudan this year with more than 500 civilians killed from such strikes between January and mid-March.
"Children in Sudan continue to ÔÇőbear the ÔÇőheaviest toll, Ôüáwith drones responsible for nearly 80% of all reported child killings and ÔÇőinjuries," Eva Hinds, UNICEF spokesperson in Sudan, ÔÇőtold Ôüáreporters in Geneva. At least 245 children were reportedly killed or injured in the first three months Ôüáof ÔÇő2026, a sharp increase compared ÔÇőto the same period last year, Hinds said.

item.title: Europe could see jet fuel shortage by June, IEA says
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - Europe could start seeing physical shortages of jet fuel by June due to the Iran war if the region is ÔÇőonly able to replace half of the fuel supplies ÔÇîit normally gets from the Middle East, the International Energy Agency said in its monthly report.
Global jet fuel and kerosene demand averaged 7.8 million ÔÇőbarrels per day in 2025, with Gulf exports the largest ÔÇősource to the global market, averaging nearly 400,000 bpd, Ôüáthe report added.
Here are some key details:

item.title: Economic pressure on Russia key for peace, Italy's Meloni says
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - Economic pressure on Russia remains "the most effective weapon" to pressure it to end its conflict with Ukraine, Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said on Tuesday, when ÔÇőasked whether Europe should ease its ban on Moscow's gas imports.
Meloni spoke ÔÇőon the sidelines of a wine fair after the head of energy Ôüágroup Eni (ENI.MI) , opens new tab said that the EU restriction should be reconsidered as the bloc grapples ÔÇőwith the fallout of the Iran conflict on energy supplies.
Energy worries have mounted in ÔÇőItaly and across Europe after the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz , a waterway that normally carries about a fifth of global LNG supplies, to most ÔÇőshipping.
"The economic pressure that we have exerted on Russia over these years is ÔÇőthe most effective weapon to help build peace, and therefore we must be very careful about ÔÇîhow we Ôüámove forward," Meloni told reporters.
Moscow faces a ban on imports of liquefied natural gas on short-term contracts from April 25 and on long-term contracts from January 1, 2027. Eni CEO Claudio Descalzi said it was unclear how to replace 20 billion ÔÇőcubic metres of Russian ÔÇőLNG.
Speaking in the Ôüánorthern Italian city of Verona, Meloni, who is due to meet President Volodymyr Zelenskiy in Rome on Wednesday, said she hoped ÔÇőprogress towards peace could be made before the ban takes ÔÇőeffect.
Her ally, Ôüáthe far-right League party, which has often expressed pro-Russia views, is also urging her to reopen supplies from Moscow.
Party leader Matteo Salvini said on Tuesday he had discussed with ÔüáEconomy Minister ÔÇőGiancarlo Giorgetti an unspecified plan to freeze energy ÔÇőprices at levels seen before the Iran war broke out on February 28, but did not provide details on ÔÇőthe potentially costly move.

item.title: Spain's kerosene, fuels supply guaranteed at the moment, energy minister says
item.description:
MADRID, April 14 (Reuters) - Spain's fuels supply is not at risk at the moment ÔÇőand the country is in ÔÇîa "privileged situation" compared with European peers, Energy Minister Sara Aagesen said on Tuesday.
The U.S.-Israeli ÔÇőwar on Iran and subsequent ÔÇőblockade of the Strait of Hormuz Ôüáhave sparked a supply crisis, ÔÇőwith European airlines calling on the European Union ÔÇőto deploy emergency measures to address growing concerns over jet fuel shortages.
Asked whether the ÔÇőSpanish government can guarantee the supply ÔÇőof kerosene, diesel and gasoline, Aagesen said that "at ÔÇîthis Ôüámoment, I can say yes".
"Our situation is not only robust, it's privileged," she said, thanks to a fleet ÔÇőof eight ÔÇőrefineries Ôüáproducing 80% of the kerosene consumed in Spain.
"This is ÔÇőa very advantageous situation compared ÔÇőto Ôüámany European partners, where the opposite is true: they are dependent on Ôüáimports," ÔÇőshe said, adding that ÔÇőSpanish refiners have not suffered from supply disruptions.

item.title: IMF slashes growth forecast for Middle East as Gulf exporters reel from impact of war
item.description:
DUBAI, April 14 (Reuters) - The Middle East and ÔÇőNorth Africa region is expected to have a sharply slower growth this year as oil-exporting ÔÇîcountries grapple with the fallout from the Iran war , the International Monetary Fund said on Tuesday.
The region's real GDP growth forecast was slashed to 1.1% in the IMF's latest World Economic Outlook, 2.8 percentage points lower than its January projection.
Growth is expected to ÔÇőrebound to 4.8% in 2027. The IMF said, however, that its estimates for 2027 assumed energy ÔÇőproduction and transportation in the region are normalised over the next few months. It Ôüánoted that this assumption may need to be revised if the conflict drags on.
Tehran's attacks on Gulf ÔÇőneighbours, in response to U.S.-Israeli strikes that began in late February, have damaged major energy facilities and disrupted ÔÇőshipping through the Strait of Hormuz , which would normally handle about 20% of global oil and liquefied natural gas flows.
The war has also created inflationary pressures and clouded the global economic outlook.
U.S.-Iran talks to resolve the conflict broke down on the weekend and ÔÇőthe U.S. military has begun a blockade of Iran ports, though efforts to maintain dialogue continue.
The IMF said it revised GDP projections for countries in the region much lower due to diminished production and ÔÇîexports. ÔüáThe degree of revision depended on "damage suffered in energy and transportation infrastructure as well as the dependence on the Strait of Hormuz and availability of alternative export routes," it added.
Saudi Arabia, the world's top oil exporter and the Arab world's biggest economy, is now expected to see growth of 3.1% in 2026, 1.4 percentage ÔÇőpoints lower than a January ÔÇőestimate. The kingdom, however, Ôüáis expected to be less severely affected by the war than its Gulf neighbours.
Iran's economy is forecast to shrink 6.1% in its fiscal year that began on ÔÇőMarch 21, before rebounding to 3.2% growth the year after. Before the war, ÔÇőit was expected Ôüáto expand 1.1% this fiscal year.
Bahrain, Iraq, Kuwait and Qatar are also now expected to see their economies contract this year, the IMF report said, without giving specific estimates. A separate and more comprehensive Middle East regional economic Ôüáoutlook ÔÇőis due to be published on April 16.
GDP growth revisions were ÔÇőmilder for regional oil and gas importers. Growth in Egypt, for example, is projected to slow to 4.2% in 2026 from an earlier estimate ÔÇőof 4.7%, and is forecast to recover to 4.8% in 2027.

item.title: Oil tankers transiting Strait of Hormuz since start of Iran war
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. blockade on vessels entering or leaving Iranian ports adds to uncertainty over shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which has been all-but-shut since the start of the Iran war.
Ordinarily, roughly one fifth of global oil and gas exports transit the Strait of Hormuz.
Here's a look at ÔÇősome of the non-Iranian oil tankers that have passed the strait since the start of the U.S-Israeli war with Iran on February 28, ÔÇîlisted by cargo destination.
Liberia-flagged Very Large Crude Carrier (VLCC) Serifos entered and exited the Hormuz Passage trial anchorage that bypasses Iran's Larak Island on April 10. The tanker, carrying crude loaded from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates in early March, is expected to arrive at Malaysia's Malacca port on April 21.
Ocean Thunder , loaded with Iraqi crude and chartered by a unit of Malaysian state energy ÔÇőfirm Petronas, transited the waterway on April 5 and is expected to discharge its cargo of 1 million barrels of Basrah Heavy crude in Malaysia's ÔÇőPengerang on April 18.
Both tankers are among seven Malaysia-linked vessels cleared by Iran to transit the strait, two people familiar with Ôüáthe matter told Reuters.
China-flagged VLCCs Cospearl Lake and He Rong Hai exited the Strait of Hormuz on April 11. Cospearl Lake, laden with Iraqi oil, is expected to ÔÇőarrive at China's Zhoushan port on May 1. He Rong Hai is heading to Myanmar to discharge its Saudi crude cargo.
Both VLCCs are chartered by Unipec, the trading arm ÔÇőof Chinese energy giant Sinopec (600028.SS) , opens new tab .
VLCC Dhalkut, which passed the strait on April 2, is heading to Myanmar to discharge Saudi crude on April 22, Kpler data showed. Crude discharged at Myanmar typically goes to PetroChina's (601857.SS) , opens new tab Yunnan refinery.
On March 31, a Chinese foreign ministry spokesperson said that three Chinese ships recently sailed through the Strait of Hormuz after coordination with relevant parties.
At least two VLCCs and ÔÇőtwo suezmax tankers exited the Gulf in March and April to offload crude at India.
VLCC Habrut, which crossed the strait on April 2, is heading to Paradip to ÔÇődischarge Abu Dhabi crude for Indian Oil Corp (IOC.NS) , opens new tab on April 15.
Meanwhile, VLCC Marathi discharged Saudi crude at the Sikka port for Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab on March 28, Kpler data showed.
The Liberia-flagged Smyrni ÔÇîsuezmax tanker Ôüáexited the strait on March 12 and discharged 1 million barrels of Saudi crude at Mumbai for state refiner Hindustan Petroleum Corp (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab on March 16, the data showed. Another suezmax Shenlong left the strait on March 6 and discharged the same amount of Saudi crude at Mumbai on March 11.
Gabon-flagged tanker Msg, laden with residue fuel, is heading to India's Pipavav port after passing the strait on April 9, Kpler data showed. Liberia-flagged Navara, which sailed through the strait on March 31, discharged fuel oil at ÔÇőSikka port on April 8, the data ÔÇőshowed.
Two India-bound liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) tankers Ôüácarrying about 94,000 metric tons of the cooking gas have safely transited the Strait of Hormuz and are heading towards India, the government said in late March.
BW Tyr discharged at Mumbai and Pipavav between April 5 and April 7, while BW Elm discharged ÔÇőat three Indian ports between April 6 and April 15, Kpler data showed.
Prior to this, four other Indian-flagged LPG tankers ÔÇőhave moved out of Ôüáthe strait ÔÇö Shivalik, Nanda Devi , Pine Gas, and Jag Vasant .
Two Pakistan-flagged tankers entered the Gulf on April 12.
The Aframax tanker Shalamar is heading to the United Arab Emirates on Monday to load Das crude, the data showed, while the Panamax-sized Khairpur is heading to Kuwait to load refined products, according to the data.
Aframax tanker P. Aliki passed through the strait on March 28 Ôüáand discharged ÔÇőSaudi crude at Karachi on March 31.
A Thai oil tanker owned by Bangchak Corporation (BCP.BK) , opens new tab has safely passed through ÔÇőthe Strait of Hormuz following diplomatic coordination between Thailand and Iran, and was not required to pay to escape the blockade, a Thai official and the oil major that owns the vessel said on ÔÇőMarch 25.
The Suezmax tanker Pola discharged 1 million barrels of Khafji crude at Thailand and Singapore, Kpler data showed.

item.title: Canadian insurers fortify homes, urge Carney to put climate first as wildfire season kicks off
item.description:
TORONTO, April 14 (Reuters) - Worried about wildfires sending claims soaring this year, Canada's property and casualty insurers are pushing owners to flood- and fire-proof ÔÇőhomes and urging the government to take climate issues more seriously despite economic turmoil.
Insurers Intact Financial (IFC.TO) , opens new tab , TD Insurance (TD.TO) , opens new tab , Wawanesa and Definity Financial (DFY.TO) , opens new tab face financial pressure as claims surge and ÔÇîin turn push up home-insurance premiums, which rose about 6% last year in Canada.
They worry Prime Minister Mark CarneyÔÇÖs prioritization of energy and the economy over risks from climate change could contribute to more wildfire- and flood-related damage over time.
Canada, which has nearly 10% of the world's forest, has seen record wildfire and flood damage in recent years. But calamity-prone regions are still insurable in Canada, unlike in some other countries where companies will not insure houses for wildfires.
This year is expected to be ÔÇőamong the hottest years on record, according to Environment and Climate Change Canada, increasing the likelihood of wildfires. Research has linked humanÔÇĹdriven climate change, fueled by rising carbon emissions, to hotter and ÔÇőmore frequent heatwaves, which dry out ecosystems and create conditions that fuel wildfires and intensify floods.
Carney in 2015 as the Bank of England governor delivered Ôüáa historic speech about climate change and its impact on financial stability. But he is now counting on the oil sector to help the Canadian economy weather a hit from U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs and has ÔÇőpledged to support global efforts to stabilize energy prices due to the Iran war, putting some initiatives to combat climate change on the back burner.
"It feels to me like this (climate) has been somewhat deprioritized. ÔÇőAnd that's why we're going to, as an industry, keep it at the top of the table," Rowan Saunders, the CEO of the country's fourth-largest property and casualty insurer Definity, said in an interview.
To be sure, Canada's insurers continue to enjoy strong profits despite the pressure from climate-related disasters.
The industry's major players reported higher profits last year and spent less on paying out claims in 2025 than in 2024, when those hit a record. Net income rose ÔÇő56.7% for the Canadian property and casualty insurance industry last year, according to data from the industry regulator, which requires they hold strong capital levels.
Still, insurers have invested millions to encourage policyholders to ÔÇőrebuild floodÔÇĹ and wildfireÔÇĹresilient homes, folding those upgrades into claim settlements. They have partnered with municipal governments and invested in wildfire risk modeling.
They are also rewarding consumers for weatherproofing their homes, with discounts for completing risk assessments ÔÇîor installing Ôüáfire-resistant roofing and siding.
The insurance industry's proposals to Ottawa, presented to parliament last year, include updating building codes to make new homes resilient to climate change, investing in natural infrastructure and preventing new construction in areas already prone to floods and wildfires.
Canada in 2024 recorded one of its worst fires as flames engulfed nearly a third of the structures in the tourist town of Jasper and forced thousands to evacuate. At the same time, a hailstorm in Calgary and floods in Toronto elevated insurance claims to a record of C$9.4 billion that year.
In the past three years, a total of about 32 million hectares ÔÇőof land has been scorched by wildfires.
Insurers ÔÇőworry in particular that CarneyÔÇÖs plan to Ôüáaddress a chronic housing shortage does not adequately consider severe weather risks.
Ottawa has proposed , opens new tab building 3.9 million homes, of which about 500,000 could be in high-risk flood areas, and over 200,000 in high-risk wildfire areas, said Liam McGuinty, vice-president of federal affairs at the Insurance Bureau of Canada.
"We've got to stop this ÔÇőproblem from getting worse," McGuinty said.
Reuters spoke to insurance companies, reinsurers, climate groups and insurance lobby groups that said climate has taken a backseat ÔÇőas the Carney government Ôüáprioritizes trade and the global energy crisis.
Canada's environment ministry said climate remains a critical issue for the government and fighting climate change continues. Since 2015, the government has invested more than C$6.6 billion to help communities be better prepared and manage wildland fire, among other projects, it said.
In his first budget, Carney eliminated a cap on oil and gas emissions in favor of other climate measures like strengthened industrial carbon pricing and Ôüáthe deployment of ÔÇőcarbon capture and storage technology. But now, the carbon price piece of his plan is in jeopardy, Reuters has reported.
"We're at ÔÇőa point now in Canada where we can have what used to be a year's worth of severe weather losses happening in a single day. And we don't have the level of public investment commensurate to that reality right now," said David ÔÇőLeibl, vice president of sustainability and corporate affairs at Winnipeg-based insurer Wawanesa. "We need to close that gap."

item.title: Trump turns on Meloni, says he is 'shocked' by Italian leader
item.description:
ROME, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told an Italian newspaper on Tuesday he was "shocked" by Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni and had expected her to be more courageous, delivering a ÔÇőblunt public rebuke to one of his closest European allies.
Meloni had been ÔÇîa vociferous supporter of Trump, but she has criticised his decision to go to war with Iran , and on Monday, denounced his weekend criticism of Pope Leo as "unacceptable".
Trump responded in an interview with Corriere della Sera, saying ÔÇőMeloni was "very different from what I thought" and denouncing her for refusing to ÔÇőhelp re-open the Strait of Hormuz, which has been blocked by Iran.
"I'm Ôüáshocked by her. I thought she had courage. I was wrong," he is quoted as ÔÇősaying in the Italian-language article posted online.
The White House declined to comment on the reported ÔÇőquotes. Meloni's office also declined to comment.
The criticism marks a dramatic change in tone toward Meloni, who was the only European leader to attend the president's inauguration in 2025.
Only last month he told Corriere della ÔÇőSera that Meloni was "a great leader", but on Tuesday he accused her of failing ÔÇőto back U.S. efforts over energy security and Iran, and said Italy wanted America "to do the job for ÔÇîher."
Asked Ôüáabout her condemnation of his comments on Pope Leo, he said: "She is the one who is unacceptable, because she does not care whether Iran has a nuclear weapon and would blow Italy up in two minutes if it had the chance."
Meloni had hoped that her ÔÇőclose relationship with the ÔÇőU.S. president would strengthen Ôüáher standing at home and abroad, but instead it risks becoming a political liability.
Some 66% of Italians now hold a negative view ÔÇőof the U.S. leader and pollsters say Meloni's ties to the ÔÇőWhite House may Ôüáhave contributed to her defeat last month in a referendum on judicial reform.
The war in Iran has pushed up energy prices in Italy, which is heavily dependent on oil and gas imports.
"They (Italy) Ôüápay the ÔÇőhighest energy costs in the world and are not ÔÇőeven ready to fight for the Strait of Hormuz... They depend on Donald Trump to keep it open," ÔÇőTrump said.

item.title: Trump says Iran talks could resume over next two days, NY Post reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President ÔÇőDonald Trump said on ÔÇîTuesday that Iran talks could resume in Pakistan ÔÇőover the next ÔÇőtwo days, according to an Ôüáinterview with the ÔÇőNew York Post.
"You should ÔÇőstay there, really, because something could be happening over the ÔÇőnext two days, ÔÇőand we're more inclined to ÔÇîgo Ôüáthere," Trump was quoted as saying.
Trump said Pakistan's Army chief, Field ÔÇőMarshal Asim ÔÇőMunir, Ôüáwas doing a "great job" on ÔÇőthe talks.
"He's fantastic, and ÔÇőtherefore Ôüáit's more likely that we go back Ôüáthere," ÔÇőTrump said.

item.title: Brazil's Lula calls for former spy head return after US detention
item.description:
BRASILIA, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da ÔÇőSilva on Tuesday called ÔÇîon former intelligence chief Alexandre Ramagem, who was detained ÔÇőby ICE in the ÔÇőUnited States the previous Ôüáday, to return to ÔÇőBrazil to serve a ÔÇőcoup-plotting prison sentence.
"I believe Ramagem will come back to Brazil, he ÔÇőhas to come ÔÇőback to serve his sentence," Lula ÔÇîsaid Ôüáin an interview with a local outlet.
The U.S. agency reported on its ÔÇőwebsite on ÔÇőMonday Ôüáthat Ramagem was in its custody, ÔÇőwithout providing details, after ÔÇőhe Ôüáfled Brazil in September following his conviction for Ôüáplotting ÔÇőa coup ÔÇőwith ex-President Jair Bolsonaro.

item.title: Brazil to pursue rare earths policy this year, not state-owned firm
item.description:
BRASILIA, April 14 (Reuters) - Brazil's mining regulator chief said on Tuesday he expected a national policy for critical minerals to be unveiled within the next two to ÔÇőthree months, adding that a proposal to create a state-owned company for rare ÔÇîearths was not a government initiative.
Mauro Sousa, director-general of Brazil's National Mining Agency (ANM), told a press conference that 13 bills currently before Congress addressed rare earths and critical minerals.
In recent days, new bills proposing the creation ÔÇőof a state-backed entity dubbed "Terrabras" have been introduced by lawmakers, prompting mining industry association ÔÇőIbram to push back against the idea.
The group said on Monday Ôüáthat Brazil's weakness in rare earths mining did not stem from a lack of state ÔÇőinvolvement, but rather from limited industrial-scale separation and refining capacity, and other factors such as insufficient ÔÇőfinancing and legal uncertainty that deters investment.
"We have not yet examined these (Terrabras) proposals in detail to assess their content," said Sousa.
He said the government was in talks with congressman Arnaldo Jardim on a bill to create ÔÇőa national policy for critical and strategic minerals - which did not include a provision for ÔÇőa state-run company - and had requested that uranium be included.
The bill sponsored by Jardim was expected to be ÔÇîapproved later Ôüáthis year, as discussions were already at an advanced stage, added Carlos Leonardo Durans, director of the Department of Development of Intermediate Inputs at the Ministry of Development, Industry, Trade and Services.
"We want to facilitate capital inflows with value-added requirements, but so far there has been no ÔÇődiscussion about creating a ÔÇőstate-owned critical minerals company," Ôüáhe said at the same press conference.
President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva has repeatedly said Brazil should not remain merely an exporter of ÔÇőcommodities in the rare earths sector, stressing the need to develop domestic ÔÇőprocessing capacity Ôüáin Latin America's largest economy.
Despite little production, Brazil has vast reserves of these minerals, which are essential for high-tech equipment manufacturing.
At the same event, Julio Nery, mining affairs director at Ibram, said more Ôüáthan 10 "highly ÔÇőpromising" exploration projects were under way in Brazil, particularly ÔÇőtargeting ionic clays, but noted they still depended on geological studies.
"To bring a mine online from scratch, from the first ÔÇődrilling, it usually takes around 10 years," Nery said.

item.title: Libya starts trial run of gas pipeline as NOC pushes export growth
item.description:
CAIRO, April 14 (Reuters) - LibyaÔÇÖs National Oil Corporation (NOC) ÔÇîhas begun trial operation of a long-delayed gas pipeline aimed at easing production bottlenecks and supporting future exports, the company said late on Monday.
The ÔÇőNorth African country, the continent's second-largest oil producer and a ÔÇőmember of OPEC, relies heavily on hydrocarbons, but its Ôüáability to attract foreign investment and expand output has been ÔÇőhampered by years of political and economic turmoil since 2011.
In recent ÔÇőmonths, NOC has sought to reverse that trend, announcing new oil and gas discoveries and awarding exploration blocks in its first licensing round since 2007, signalling a ÔÇőrenewed push to bring in international partners as the nation looks ÔÇőto boost production to 1 billion standard cubic feet per day and raise exports ÔÇîto ÔüáEurope by the early 2030s.
The new 42-inch pipeline links the Intisar A/103 field to the Brega gas distribution network over about 130 km (80 miles). NOC said it would enable the recovery of ÔÇőabout 150 million ÔÇőcubic feet of Ôüágas per day that had previously been flared, boosting supplies to domestic consumers and freeing up ÔÇővolumes for export.
Libya holds an estimated 80 trillion ÔÇőcubic feet Ôüáof gas reserves, but exports via the Greenstream pipeline to Italy remain limited.
NOC said the project, which had been stalled for about Ôüá16 ÔÇőyears, would also eliminate ÔÇťback pressureÔÇŁ that had ÔÇődisrupted operations and forced some fields to shut down, improving overall efficiency across the ÔÇőgas network.

item.title: Zambia forgoes $200 million in revenue with fuel tax suspension
item.description: LUSAKA, April 14 (Reuters) - Zambia will lose about $200 million in revenue by suspending some fuel taxes to ÔÇőcushion the impact of the Iran war ÔÇîon households and businesses, Finance Minister Situmbeko Musokotwane said on Tuesday.

item.title: Canada to temporarily suspend federal fuel tax on diesel and gasoline
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Canada will suspend the ÔÇőfederal fuel excise tax ÔÇîon gasoline and diesel from April 20 to ÔÇőSeptember 7 to help ÔÇőconsumers and businesses deal Ôüáwith rising prices, ÔÇőPrime Minister Mark Carney told ÔÇőreporters on Tuesday.
"(This) is a responsible measure that will ÔÇőreduce operating costs ÔÇőfor truckers and businesses in the ÔÇîfood, Ôüáagriculture, housing, construction, and delivery sectors," he said. The measure is ÔÇőexpected ÔÇőto Ôüáreduce the cost of regular gasoline ÔÇőhy 10 cents ÔÇőa Ôüáliter and diesel by four cents a liter, Ôüáhe ÔÇőadded.
(Reporting by ÔÇőDavid Ljunggren, editing by Maria Cheng)
((Reuters ÔÇőOttawa bureau; david.ljunggren@tr.com , opens new tab ))
Keywords: CANADA POLITICS/TAX

item.title: Shell in talks with UAE's ADNOC for South Africa fuel outlet sale, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab is in advanced talks with Abu ÔÇőDhabi's state oil company ADNOC to sell ÔÇîits retail fuel stations in South Africa in a deal likely to be valued at about $1 billion, Bloomberg News ÔÇőreported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with ÔÇőthe matter.
ADNOC emerged as the preferred bidder after ÔüáShell's negotiations with commodity trader Gunvor Group fell ÔÇőthrough, and an agreement could potentially be reached ÔÇőas early as this quarter, the report said.
The talks come amid volatility in the energy market following the Middle East ÔÇőconflict, prompting the British oil major to trim ÔÇőits first-quarter gas production outlook .
A deal would see the sale of ÔÇîShell's Ôüá600 retail fuel outlets, giving ADNOC about 10% of the market in South Africa, Bloomberg said.
Late in 2024, Shell, which has been in South Africa ÔÇőfor more ÔÇőthan a Ôüácentury, disclosed plans to exit its downstream businesses in the region.
ADNOC, which before ÔÇőthe Iran war produced some 4% of ÔÇőglobal Ôüáoil output, had previously disclosed plans to invest $150 billion between 2026 and 2030 to drive growth and Ôüámeet ÔÇőglobal energy demand.
Shell and ADNOC ÔÇőDistribution did not immediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.

item.title: US solar and wind contract prices rose sharply in first quarter
item.description: April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. solar and wind contract prices rose sharply in the first quarter ÔÇőas project developers faced tariffs , labor shortages and ÔÇîpermitting challenges amid soaring demand for power from data centers, according to a report published ÔÇőon Tuesday.

item.title: French group Veolia aims $1.2 bln in revenue from data centres, chips by 2030
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - French utility Veolia (VIE.PA) , opens new tab aims to almost double its revenue ÔÇőfrom providing energy and water ÔÇîmanagement solutions to data centres and chipmakers by 2030, it said on Tuesday, ÔÇőbetting on strong demand from ÔÇőboth sectors.
The company expects 1 billion Ôüáeuros ($1.18 billion) in annual revenues ÔÇőfrom data centres and the semiconductor ÔÇőindustry, compared to the 560 million euros it made in 2025, as it seeks ÔÇőto expand its presence in ÔÇőthese fast-growing markets.
The tech sector's expansion of ÔÇîdata Ôüácentres, driven by surging demand for AI following the widespread adoption of ChatGPT, has strained power supplies ÔÇőand raised ÔÇőconcerns Ôüáover global grid capacity.
Veolia said it planned to use ÔÇőits proprietary technologies to help ÔÇőmeet Ôüárising demand for integrated solutions in water management, local energy and Ôüáhazardous ÔÇőwaste treatment linked to ÔÇőthe expansion of these industries.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Nigeria wildlife trafficking persists as protection law awaits presidential signing
item.description:
LAGOS, April 14 (Reuters) - NigeriaÔÇÖs customs said on Tuesday its officials had intercepted two hyenas, a porcupine and 24 protected birds, the ÔÇősixth major wildlife seizure since parliament passed a longÔÇĹawaited protection law ÔÇîin October that has not yet come into force.
The bill, intended to overhaul NigeriaÔÇÖs wildlife protection framework and sharply increase penalties, cleared both chambers of the ÔÇőNational Assembly late last year but has yet to receive ÔÇőpresidential assent.
ÔÇťThere was an omission in the schedules where some Ôüáspecies classified as endangered globally are not considered endangered in ÔÇőNigeria,ÔÇŁ said Terseer Ugbor, a lawmaker involved in drafting the bill. ÔÇťWe had ÔÇőto go back and correct that.ÔÇŁ
Ugbor said the revisions have now been completed and the bill was ready for signing.
Conservation groups said they were pushing for the ÔÇőbill to be signed quickly.
ÔÇťSigning the law will significantly empower investigators, ÔÇőprosecutors and the judiciary to curb wildlife trafficking,ÔÇŁ said Linus Unah, West Africa ÔÇîdirector Ôüáat conservation group Wild Africa.
The bill would align domestic law more closely with international conventions, expand protected species lists and introduce tougher sanctions, including longer prison terms and higher fines.
As it awaits the presidential signature, ÔÇőthe wildlife trafficking the ÔÇőlaw is meant Ôüáto curb has continued - as the latest seizures highlight.
Since October, the Nigeria Customs Service has intercepted elephant ÔÇőivory in Abuja, live pangolins in northern and southwestern ÔÇőstates, Ôüáa lion cub and monkeys near the Benin border, and most recently the animals hidden in a vehicle in the northeastern city of Maiduguri, Ôüáaccording ÔÇőto customs statements.
Abdullahi Maiwada, a customs spokesperson, ÔÇősaid the agency could not comment on delays in the lawÔÇÖs enactment but welcomed stronger ÔÇőlegal frameworks to support enforcement.

item.title: Carney vows to tackle cost of living in Canada with new majority government, suspends fuel tax
item.description:
OTTAWA, April 14 (Reuters) - Prime Minister ÔÇőMark Carney said he would prioritize lowering the cost of living in Canada, tackling a ÔÇîhousing shortage, and building major infrastructure projects to help make Canada's economy more independent with his Liberal Party's new parliamentary majority.
After his party swept three special elections on Monday, capping off an extraordinary few months in Canada when several opposition ÔÇőmembers joined him, Carney said he accepts the country's support "with humility, determination and a clear understanding ÔÇőof what this moment demands."
The first Liberal majority government in Canada since 2019 Ôüáshould allow Carney more freedom to pass the legislation he says is needed for the country to ÔÇőthrive in "a more divided and dangerous world," including the uncertainty caused by U.S. President Donald Trump and the ÔÇőongoing war in the Middle East .
Carney has sought to make Canada less dependent on the United States.
"We have to change fundamentally our economy to be stronger, more independent, more prosperous," he said at a Tuesday press briefing to announce a temporary ÔÇősuspension of a fuel tax . He said the measure would reduce Canadians' bills at the gas station by ÔÇő10 cents per litre on regular gasoline and 4 cents on diesel.
"Canada's new government has been relentlessly focused on making ÔÇîlife Ôüámore affordable for Canadians," he said.
Carney's Liberals won three special elections in Ontario and Quebec, giving him a 174 seat majority in the 343-seat House of Commons.
Carney said he had no plans to shuffle his cabinet before Parliament's summer recess or to call an early election, following criticism from political opponents that five recent defections of ÔÇőlegislators to Carney's Liberals ÔÇőundermine the credibility of Ôüáhis majority government.
Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre took to X to express his dissatisfaction at both the process and the result: "The Carney Liberals did not win a ÔÇőmajority government through a general election or today's by-elections. Instead, it was won ÔÇőthrough backroom Ôüádeals with politicians who betrayed the people who voted for them."
