The NAACP, represented by Earthjustice and the Southern Environmental Law Center, sued xAI and subsidiary MZX Tech, charging they violated the federal Clean Air Act ÔÇőby running 27 gas-fired turbines before getting necessary air permits for its ÔÇőmassive data center that powers xAI's Grok chatbot.
Elon Musk's artificial Ôüáintelligence startup xAI has invested more than $20 billion to build the data center ÔÇőin Southaven with the full backing of Mississippi Governor Tate Reeves, but the ÔÇőfacility, as well as Colossus 1 just over the border in Memphis, Tennessee, has met heavy opposition from local communities due to their effect on local air and environmental quality.
"By ÔÇőlooking to evade clean air laws to operate dirty turbines that emit ÔÇőpollution and known carcinogens, these companies are following a shameful, familiar pattern: asking Black and ÔÇîfrontline communities Ôüáto bear the toxic brunt of ÔÇśinnovation,ÔÇÖÔÇŁ said AbreÔÇÖ Conner, director of the Center for Environmental and Climate Justice at the NAACP.
The NAACP announced its intention to sue xAI and MZX in February because the Clean Air Act requires ÔÇő60 days of notice ÔÇőahead of filing Ôüáa lawsuit.
Mississippi regulators held one public hearing that month about permits for those turbines after just a few days ÔÇőof public notice for the hearing, and subsequently approved the ÔÇőpermits.
xAI was Ôüánot immediately available for comment.
Earthjustice said that xAIÔÇÖs Southaven power plant has the potential to emit more than 1,700 tons of smog-causing nitrogen oxides (NOx) each year, a Ôüámajor ÔÇősource of smog in the greater Memphis area. ÔÇőThey are also estimated to emit 180 tons of fine particulate matter, 500 tons of carbon monoxide, ÔÇőand 19 tons of cancer-causing formaldehyde.

item.title: US destroyer interdicts two oil tankers attempting to leave Iran, official says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - A U.S. destroyer interdicted ÔÇőtwo oil tankers attempting to leave Iran on Tuesday, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump's blockade went into ÔÇîeffect, and instructed them to turn around, a U.S. official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The ships had left Chabahar port on the Gulf of Oman and were contacted by the warship via radio communication, the official said. It was unclear whether any further warnings were given.
The disclosure adds further detail to the start ÔÇőof Trump's blockade, which aims to pressure Iran to end its effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a choke ÔÇőpoint for about 20% of the world's oil.
Trump is hoping the blockade will force Iran to accept America's terms Ôüáfor ending a war launched by the U.S. and Israel on February 28, including opening up the Strait of Hormuz. Trump says ÔÇőthat was also a condition of a week-old ceasefire with Iran due to expire next week.
Experts are cautious. Noam Raydan at The Washington Institute ÔÇőfor Near East Policy said tracking data did show one tanker making a U-turn after the start of the blockade but cautioned that a lot of ships working with Iranian oil go dark.
"We just don't know yet how effective it is. We are still in day two," Raydan said.
The U.S. official said the two ÔÇőtankers were among the six merchant vessels the U.S. Central Command said in a statement earlier on Tuesday had followed orders to "turn around to ÔÇőre-enter an Iranian port on the Gulf of Oman."
Central Command said no ships have made it past the blockade since it went into effect on ÔÇîMonday at Ôüá10 a.m. in Washington (1400 GMT).
The blockade is a massive undertaking involving more than 10,000 U.S. forces, over a dozen warships and dozens of aircraft, the U.S. military says.
The U.S. military says it will support freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, as long as they are not going to or from Iran.
Trump announced the blockade following the breakdown of weekend talks to end the ÔÇőwar. Oil prices jumped back above $100 a ÔÇőbarrel before easing on Tuesday Ôüáon hopes of further talks.
If Trump's strategy succeeds, he would eliminate Iran's greatest point of leverage in negotiations with the U.S. and clear the strait again for global trade. But a blockade, experts say, is ÔÇőan act of war that requires an open-ended commitment of a significant number of warships.
It could also ÔÇőtrigger fresh retaliation Ôüáfrom Tehran and put tremendous strain on an already fragile ceasefire.
Iran's threats to shipping have caused global oil prices to skyrocket about 50%. Roughly 5,000 people have died in the hostilities.
Thousands of U.S. military strikes have severely weakened Iran's military. But analysts say Tehran has emerged from the conflict Ôüáas a ÔÇővexing problem for Washington, with a more hard-line leadership and a buried stockpile of ÔÇőhighly enriched uranium.
Raydan said to expect likely Iranian retaliation if the blockade succeeds and lasts for an extended period, noting Iranian threats to strike Gulf states that host ÔÇőU.S. forces and Iran's past attacks on ships.
"We're in the testing period," Raydan said.

item.title: US alleges well-known Mexican human rights activist works for drug cartel
item.description:
MEXICO CITY, April 14 (Reuters) - The U.S. on Tuesday imposed sanctions on a well-known human rights activist in Mexico who has for years levied charges of human ÔÇőrights abuses against the country's Armed Forces, alleging he worked on behalf ÔÇîof a powerful drug cartel.
The U.S. Treasury Department accused Raymundo Ramos, president of the Committee for Human Rights of Nuevo Laredo, of "posing as a 'human rights' activist" for more than a decade in order ÔÇőto fabricate false accusations against the Army and protect members of the ÔÇőpowerful Cartel of the Northeast (CDN).
"On the CDN payroll, Ramos engages in these Ôüáactivities with the goal of boosting the public opinion of CDN and discrediting ÔÇőMexican authoritiesÔÇÖ law enforcement initiatives against the cartel," the Treasury Department said in a statement.
Washington's ÔÇőmove blocks any assets Ramos might have in the United States and bars U.S. persons from working with him.
Ramos did not immediately respond to ReutersÔÇÖ requests for comment about the accusations. Based in ÔÇőNuevo Laredo, an ultra-violent city in the state of Tamaulipas, bordering the U.S., ÔÇőRamos has long been one of the most high-profile human rights activists operating in the area.
In 2023, ÔÇîRamos Ôüáraised the alarm over alleged extrajudicial killings committed by the Armed Forces and publicized video of Army members shooting and killing five young men in a pickup truck, including one with a shot to the back of the neck. A year earlier, he ÔÇőaccused members of ÔÇőthe Navy of forcibly Ôüádisappearing civilians.
In both instances, Mexican authorities detained military officials and carried out investigations. It's unclear whether the investigations resulted in formal ÔÇőcharges or convictions.
The Mexican attorney general's office did not respond to ÔÇőa request Ôüáfor comment about the accusations against Ramos or whether it is independently investigating him.
In 2020, the Mexican government targeted Ramos' phone with the Pegasus spyware program, according to MexicoÔÇÖs ÔüáDigital Rights ÔÇőDefense Network and the Toronto-based digital watchdog Citizen ÔÇőLab. He was among hundreds of Mexican activists and journalists spied on by the government using the spyware, ÔÇőthe groups said.

item.title: Canada, UK and other nations condemn killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - Canada, ÔÇőthe UK and other ÔÇîcountries condemned the killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon in ÔÇőthe conflict between Israel ÔÇőand the Hezbollah and called Ôüáfor an end to ÔÇőhostilities.
ÔÇťAustralia, Brazil, Canada, Colombia, Indonesia, ÔÇőJapan, Jordan, Sierra Leone, Switzerland and the United Kingdom remain deeply ÔÇőconcerned by the worsening ÔÇőhumanitarian situation and displacement crisis in Lebanon," ÔÇîthe Ôüácountries said in a joint statement issued on Tuesday.
The statement comes after the ÔÇődeaths of ÔÇőthree Ôüápeacekeepers last month that the UN said ÔÇőin preliminary findings were due ÔÇőto Ôüáan Israeli tank projectile and an improvised explosive device Ôüámost ÔÇőlikely placed ÔÇőby Hezbollah.

item.title: Oil prices fall for a second day on expectations US-Iran talks may resume
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Oil prices fell for a second day ÔÇîon Wednesday on expectations peace talks between the U.S. and Iran may resume and eventually release supply from the key Middle East producing region trapped by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
Brent crude futures fell 52 cents, or 0.55%, ÔÇőto $94.27 a barrel at 0054 GMT after falling 4.6% in the previous session. U.S. West Texas ÔÇőIntermediate crude was down $1.04, or 1.1%, to $90.24 after dropping 7.9% the session before.
Talks Ôüáto end the war between the U.S. and Israel and Iran could resume in Pakistan over the ÔÇőnext two days, U.S. President Donald Trump said on Tuesday, after the collapse of negotiations over the weekend prompted ÔÇőWashington to impose a blockade on Iranian ports. This has increased optimism talks could eventually settle the conflict and open up crude oil and fuel flows.
The war has shut the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for crude and refined ÔÇőproduct flows out of the Gulf to global buyers, particularly in Asia and Europe.
Despite a two-week ceasefire, ÔÇőtransit through the strait remains uncertain, with traffic at only a fraction of the 130 or so vessels that moved ÔÇîthrough Ôüáthe waterway before the war, sources said on Tuesday.
A U.S. destroyer stopped two oil tankers from leaving Iran on Tuesday, a U.S. official said.
"While diplomatic headlines suggest the possibility of renewed U.S.-Iran talks and even a temporary easing of transit restrictions, the physical reality remains fragmented," the Schork Group said in a note.
"The result is ÔÇőa market that continues to ÔÇőprice optionality around flow Ôüádisruption rather than a return to equilibrium."
The market stands to lose some access to further supply after two U.S. administration officials told Reuters on Tuesday the U.S. ÔÇőwill not renew a 30-day waiver of sanctions on Iranian oil at sea ÔÇőthat expires Ôüáthis week, and quietly let a similar waiver on sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend.
Later in the day, markets will be watching for official U.S. inventory data from the Energy Information Administration due at 10:30 Ôüáa.m. ET (1430 ÔÇőGMT).
U.S. crude oil stockpiles were expected to have risen slightly ÔÇőlast week, while distillate and gasoline inventories likely fell, a Reuters poll showed.
Market sources familiar with American Petroleum Institute figures said on Tuesday U.S. ÔÇőcrude oil inventories jumped for the third straight week.

item.title: Germany must brace for prolonged Iran war energy shock, says finance minister
item.description:
BERLIN, April 14 (Reuters) - Germany must assume that ÔÇőthe energy price shock caused by the Iran war will persist, leaving the ÔÇîeconomic situation extremely fragile, Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil said on Tuesday before travelling to the International Monetary Fund spring meetings .
The IMF cut Germany's growth forecasts for this year and next on Tuesday, projecting ÔÇőgrowth rates of 0.8% in 2026 and 1.2% in 2027, down 0.3 percentage ÔÇőpoints from its previous forecasts for both years.
Klingbeil said he will Ôüádiscuss with finance ministers and international organizations which measures are best suited to ÔÇőstabilize the economy and markets, as well as to provide targeted support to people ÔÇőand businesses that have been hit particularly hard.
"This crisis once again shows that we must become more independent, more crisis-proof and more resilient," he said.
He added that he will use the trip ÔÇőto Washington to present the German government's reform agenda and promote investment in ÔÇőEurope's biggest economy.
Development Minister Reem Alabali Radovan, who will accompany Klingbeil on his visit to Washington, ÔÇîcalled Ôüáfor an end to the war.
"The international community must not hesitate now: The world needs peace, stability and an end to the violence," she said. "Every day counts."
Germany and Ukraine agreed on defence cooperation plans during a visit to Berlin ÔÇőby Ukrainian President Volodymyr ÔÇőZelenskiy and his Ôüádefence minister on Tuesday.
"The fact that the world is now focused on the Middle East must not lead to Ukraine being ÔÇőforgotten," Klingbeil said. "It continues its courageous fight for freedom and ÔÇődemocracy."
He added Ôüáthat he and his Norwegian counterpart, Jens Stoltenberg, have invited the largest donor countries to a meeting in Washington to discuss how to maintain financial support for Ukraine.
Germany is ÔüáEurope's ÔÇőlargest provider of military aid to Kyiv. It ÔÇőhas delivered about 55 billion euros ($64 billion) since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and has set aside ÔÇő11.5 billion euros in the current budget.

item.title: ExxonMobil, Energean to sign contract for first test gas drilling off Greece in years
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. oil major ExxonMobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab , Energean (ENOG.L) , opens new tab and Helleniq Energy (HEPr.AT) , opens new tab will sign ÔÇőa contract on Wednesday with ÔÇîStena Drilling to start exploratory drilling in a western Greek offshore block, Greece's ÔÇőenergy ministry said on Tuesday.
ÔÇőExxonMobil, Energean and Greece's biggest oil Ôüárefiner Helleniq announced an agreement last ÔÇőyear to explore for natural gas ÔÇőin Block 2 in the Ionian Sea.
The consortium will proceed with exploratory drilling in ÔÇőFebruary 2027, a Greek energy ÔÇőministry official said on condition of anonymity, ÔÇîthe Ôüácountry's first offshore exploration drilling in 40 years.
Stena will use a high-performance vessel able to operate in deep-sea ÔÇőwaters and ÔÇődrill Ôüáat a depth of up to 35,000 feet to ÔÇőlook for deposits once considered ÔÇőhard Ôüáto access, the official said.
Europe is attempting to diversify its energy Ôüásources ÔÇőas it seeks to ÔÇőreplace Russian gas following Russia's invasion of Ukraine .

item.title: A shipment of Austrian timber and its tortuous new route to Qatar
item.description:
DOHA, April 15 (Reuters) - Until the Iran war , shipments of Austrian spruce timber to Qatar, where the wood is used to support concrete and make basic frames on construction sites, were a matter of routine.
The standard 2x4, as it is known in the building trade, was typically sourced from Austria ÔÇőin Europe, shipped to DubaiÔÇÖs Jebel Ali port, transferred to a feeder vessel and delivered to QatarÔÇÖs Hamad Port in about 45 days.
It must now be offloaded, trucked overland and reloaded onto ÔÇînew ships, adding thousands of dollars in costs and months to delivery times, as the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has caused unprecedented disruption to oil and other trade.
Anything from medicine to basic foodstuff and the 2x4 beams of spruce, referred to as white wood, are caught in the upheaval.
The two-inch (5 cm) by four-inch beams of white wood that come in various lengths are not a strategic resource, but any shortage would slow activity in the construction sector and drive up costs.
A building materials supplier ÔÇőin Qatar, who shared details with Reuters on condition of anonymity, said that when the U.S.-Israeli airstrikes were launched on Iran on February 28, unleashing a new phase of Middle Eastern conflict, he had 17 containers ÔÇőof white wood on their way.
Each container holds around 2,850 beams of Austrian spruce, worth about 15,000 euros ($17,702).
The cargo left the Croatian port of Rijeka as planned, but instead Ôüáof sailing to Jebel Ali, was diverted to Khor Fakkan on the UAEÔÇÖs east coast, avoiding a transit of the Strait of Hormuz that would have been required to reach Jebel Ali.
After that, the timber was trucked to ÔÇőAbu Dhabi and reloaded onto feeder vessels bound for Doha. The detour added a surcharge of about $3,600 per container - some shippers quoted the supplier surcharges as high as $5,000 per container - more than triple the normal cost of shipping a 40-foot ÔÇőcontainer of timber from Europe to Qatar, the supplier said.
Even after being rerouted, the cargo has yet to arrive in Qatar and delivery is expected to take another one to two months.
At the same time, several containers of plywood that the supplier had ordered were loaded onto feeder vessels at Jebel Ali and spent weeks at sea before being returned to port, underscoring how importers lose control over shipments once they are on the water.
Before the conflict, the supplier said he would sell a standard 2x4 for ÔÇőaround QAR 23ÔÇô25 ($6.30ÔÇô$6.90) per piece. Additional costs caused by rerouting and longer transit have pushed his selling price to QAR 35-37 ($9.60ÔÇô$10.20) per piece.
Future shipments could be more expensive. Routing timber via Saudi ArabiaÔÇÖs Red Sea port of Jeddah, which ÔÇőis a possibility under consideration, would involve higher shipping charges and trucking 1,500 kilometres across the Arabian Peninsula to Qatar, further raising per-piece costs.
Other supply chains are equally disrupted.
Before the Iran war broke out, logistics firm Geodis had planned to fly medicine from ÔÇîthe UK to ÔüáDubai in about four days. Now the journey traversing land and sea is expected to take about 40 days.
For a container of onions travelling from the western shores of India to a warehouse in Dubai, a week-long journey is now a three-week ordeal at twice the cost, according to Ravi Punjabi, Managing Director at Avalon General Land Transport, a UAE-based logistics company.
Dubai and the wider United Arab Emirates, which have built their economies on being a regional hub for tourism and trade, have the advantage of ports - Fujairah and Khor Fakkan - on the Arabian Sea outside the Gulf.
For other countries in the region, the issue is much worse, notably Qatar, Bahrain and Kuwait that depend on ÔÇőthe Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz.
Governments across the Gulf ÔÇőhave sought to coordinate to ease the bottlenecks.
During Ôüáa meeting with his counterparts last month, Saudi ArabiaÔÇÖs Minister of Transport Saleh bin Nasser Al-Jasser announced measures, including allowing empty refrigerated trucks from other Gulf countries to enter the kingdom and create shared storage and redistribution zones at King Abdulaziz Port in Dammam.
Dubai has also activated what it described as a green corridor with Oman, allowing goods diverted to ÔÇőOmani ports to be trucked directly to the UAE with expedited customs clearance and facilitating the export of goods from the UAE to global markets via Omani ports.
The ÔÇősolutions are imperfect, however, and Ôüátransport executives say flows into Dubai and onward to other Gulf capitals are likely to remain slower and more expensive.
Prices for food items, personal care products and industrial supplies have already risen by 5% to 10% in parts of the region since the end of February, with further increases possible if shipping disruptions persist.
Geodis executive Eric Martin-Neuville said items, including some medicines and food that have to be kept cool, were vulnerable.
ÔÇťYou have only so many plugs for electricity, so you can only accommodate so Ôüámany containers in ÔÇőthe port,ÔÇŁ he said.
The uncertainty adds to headaches for businesses that have previously experienced disruption that has endured.
While 45 days to ship white ÔÇőwood from Europe was standard before February, there was a time before the Red Sea Houthi attacks began in 2023 that shipment took only around a month. Even though the attacks have eased, most shippers detour around the Cape of Good Hope.
In Qatar, the building materials supplier said he ÔÇőhas enough white wood stock for several months, but must soon place new orders without knowing which routes will be available or at what cost.
($1 = 0.8474 euros)

item.title: Rattled by Trump, US allies eye Japan's biggest arms opening since WW2
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan's imminent easing of arms export rules has sparked strong interest from Warsaw to Manila, Reuters reporting found, as President Donald Trump wavers on security commitments to allies and the wars in Iran and Ukraine strain U.S. weapons supplies.
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ruling party approved the changes this ÔÇőweek as she tries to invigorate the pacifist country's military industrial base. Her government will formally adopt the new rules as soon as this month, three Japanese government officials told Reuters.
Despite largely isolating itself from global arms markets since World War Two, Japan spends enough ÔÇîon its own military - $60 billion this year - to sustain a sizeable defence industry capable of manufacturing advanced systems like submarines and fighter jets.
Among the potential new customers are the Polish military and the Philippine navy, which are undergoing modernization amid regional security challenges, according to Reuters interviews with Japanese officials and foreign diplomats in Tokyo. Defence contractors Toshiba and Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T) , opens new tab are hiring staff and adding capacity to capitalise on demand, their executives said, providing previously unreported details.
One of the first deals Takaichi's government will likely approve are exports of used frigates to the Philippines, which is locked in maritime confrontation with Beijing in the South China Sea, according to two of the Japanese officials. Reuters is the first to report the timeframe of the likely sale, which ÔÇőmay be followed by missile defence systems, the officials said.
Warsaw and Tokyo can help plug gaps in each other's arsenals, cooperating in areas like anti-drone and electronic warfare systems, said Mariusz Boguszewski, deputy chief of mission at Poland's embassy in Japan.
"There are some bottlenecks that we can overcome having ÔÇőJapan on board," he added, without providing details of specific deals. Poland's WB Group, one of Europe's largest private defence contractors, last year signed a tentative drone deal with Japanese aircraft maker ShinMaywa (7224.T) , opens new tab .
Three other European diplomats said Japan's easing Ôüáprovided a chance to lessen their heavy dependence on U.S. weapons production, which is strained by conflicts . Trump's unpredictability, such as his threats to leave the NATO security alliance and invade Greenland, have also heightened the push to diversify, according to the diplomats, who requested anonymity to discuss sensitive matters.
"Offers are coming from everywhere," ÔÇősaid Masahiko Arai, senior vice president at Mitsubishi Electric's defence unit, which has been adding staff in London and Singapore to facilitate defence exports.
Takaichi's office declined to answer specific questions for this story, instead referring Reuters to a February 20 speech where she said she was reviewing the controls to bolster Japan's defence production ÔÇőand strengthen capabilities of allies.
Tokyo's export overhaul has previously been encouraged by successive U.S. administrations, including Trump's, eager for allies to contribute more to collective defence efforts.
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly did not respond to questions from Reuters about the changes to Japanese policy but said that the two nations were closer than ever under Trump and Takaichi.
China's foreign ministry did not immediately respond to questions about Japanese frigates potentially being sent to the Philippines. Ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning told reporters in April that Beijing was concerned about changes in Tokyo's arms export policy and that it should "act prudently in military and security areas."
The Philippines defence ministry declined to comment.
Japan's first steps to relax the rules began more than a decade ago when Takaichi's ÔÇőmentor, the late premier Shinzo Abe, eased a near-blanket ban on exports to encourage joint arms development with allies that would help counter China's growing military might.
The push largely stalled, however, as many restrictions - including on lethal equipment - remained. Companies continued to shy away from overseas defence sales.
Buoyed by a bumper election win ÔÇőand shorn of the longtime coalition partner that had opposed more radical change, Takaichi hopes the latest easing will nudge arms makers to add the production capacity Japan needs for a major military buildup .
Some Japanese defence firms say they are ready to pivot.
Air defence systems builder Toshiba told Reuters it plans to hire about 500 people over the next three ÔÇîyears and is Ôüáconstructing new testing and manufacturing facilities. It has also established a new department to handle defence exports.
"Reputational risk is not what it used to be," said Kenji Kobayashi, vice president in Toshiba's defence division.
Some big Japanese brands that have sidelines in defence equipment and also make consumer goods have expressed concerns that arms sales will put off their broader range of customers.
"Rather than worrying about that, we focus on fulfilling our role and growing the business," Kobayashi said.
A recruitment listing reviewed by Reuters from Mitsubishi Electric - whose products include fridges and missiles - shows the firm is hiring for an overseas sales role covering fighter aircraft and other military exports.
Demand for finished systems is strongest in Asia, while Europe, Australia and the United States offer markets for components and co-development of new products, said Arai, the Mitsubishi Electric defence executive.
He expects overall sales at his unit, including domestic and international, to increase by 50% to 600 billion yen ($3.8 billion) by 2031.
There remains a gap between the political messaging ÔÇőand the policies of some companies, however, said Latvia's envoy to Japan, Zigmars ÔÇőZilgalvis.
He gave the example of carmaker Toyota (7203.T) , opens new tab , whose subsidiary turned down Ôüáan attempted purchase of engines and related parts by Latvian firm VR Cars for a military utility vehicle in 2023.
The Latvian mission had tried to help broker the failed sale, Zilgalvis said.
Toyota Customising & Development said in response to Reuters questions that it could not accommodate the request for military vehicles "based on our business scope and policy." It declined to comment on the upcoming revisions to Japan's arms export policy.
VR Cars said it respected the decision.
While Tokyo is expected to maintain strict ÔÇőcontrols on sending arms to conflict zones, even Ukraine has sensed an opportunity.
Kyiv's chamber of commerce in Tokyo will soon launch a new industry group of Ukrainian and Japanese drone firms to spur development of new technologies, timed ÔÇőto coincide with the rule changes, its head ÔüáKateryna Yavorska exclusively told Reuters.
The U.S. has long dominated global military supply chains. It accounted for 95% of Japan's defense imports, 85% of Australian and British purchases and 77% of Saudi Arabian buys between 2021-2025, according to a March report by the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI) think-tank.
But Washington's foreign military sales programme, often blamed for late deliveries and rising costs, and its tight control over defence technologies has long been a source of frustration, officials and analysts said.
One objective of Japan's rule changes is to build defence supply chains in Asia that do not rely on the United States, said a ruling party official involved in drafting security policy.
Neighbouring South ÔüáKorea offers something of ÔÇőa blueprint: It has become the largest defence supplier to Poland and the Philippines after steady growth over the last five years, SIPRI data shows.
But the potential for Japan - the world's fourth ÔÇőlargest economy - is greater.
Even with the curbs, Japan's arms industry is on a par with South Korea, Germany, Italy and Israel, and nearly twice the size of India's, according to SIPRI's analysis of leading defence contractor revenues in 2024. The U.S. industry, however, is 25 times bigger.
"Japan has been kind of in the timeout box because of World War Two, frankly. But they were inevitably going ÔÇőto swing closer towards the centre of global politics," said Andrew Koch, founder of Nexus Pacific, a Tokyo-based defence-industry advisory.
($1 = 159.2100 yen)

item.title: Trump reiterates Pope Leo criticism, says it is 'unacceptable' for Iran to have a nuclear bomb
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ÔÇőreiterated his criticisms ÔÇîof Pope Leo on Tuesday and ÔÇősaid it ÔÇőis "unacceptable" for Iran to Ôüáhave a ÔÇőnuclear bomb.
"Will someone ÔÇőplease tell Pope Leo that Iran has ÔÇőkilled at ÔÇőleast 42,000 innocent, completely ÔÇîunarmed, Ôüáprotesters in the last two months, and that ÔÇőfor ÔÇőIran Ôüáto have a Nuclear ÔÇőBomb is ÔÇőabsolutely Ôüáunacceptable," Trump said on Truth ÔüáSocial.

item.title: China calls US claims of military pressure on Taiwan a 'distortion'
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States' claims about ÔÇőChina exerting military pressure on Taiwan are distorted, and demonstrate its "malicious intentions", a government spokesperson ÔÇîin Beijing said on Wednesday.
China has stepped up military activity around democratically governed Taiwan, which it views as its own territory, holding several rounds of war games , most recently with live-fire drills in late December.
"Certain people on the U.S. side are jumping ÔÇőup and down, continuously rehashing the so-called 'mainland threat' or 'military pressure,'" Chen Binhua, a spokesperson for ÔÇőChina's Taiwan Affairs Office, told reporters.
This represented "a complete distortion of the facts and harbours Ôüámalicious intentions," he added, saying Taiwan was an internal affair for China, which would brook no outside ÔÇőinterference.
Chen urged the United States to act with great caution, and handle Taiwan-related matters carefully and prudently.
China has ÔÇőrepeatedly demanded a halt to weapons sales to Taiwan by the United States, its most important international backer, despite a lack of formal diplomatic ties.
Wednesday's remarks came after the U.S. State Department urged China last week to talk to Taiwan and ÔÇőhalt its military and other pressure on the island, after Taiwan opposition leader Cheng Li-wun met President Xi Jinping ÔÇőin Beijing.
China refuses to speak to Taiwan President Lai Ching-te, calling him a "separatist". Lai rejects Beijing's sovereignty claims, saying only ÔÇîTaiwan's Ôüápeople can decide their future.
Cheng said she aimed to foster peace with her visit, when China unveiled measures it said would benefit Taiwan, such as easing controls on exports of food, though it did not cease regular military activities around the island during her trip.
Taiwan's government says it should be leading engagement efforts with China rather ÔÇőthan private party-to-party contacts.
Cheng, whose ÔÇővisit was a month Ôüábefore one planned by U.S. President Donald Trump, hopes China and the United States can reconcile and cooperate.
"We can definitely go down the path of peace," ÔÇőCheng, the chairwoman of Taiwan's largest opposition party, the Kuomintang, told a Taiwan ÔÇőradio station on ÔüáWednesday. "This is the important message I hope to send to Washington."
China has never renounced the use of force to bring Taiwan under its control, but says it prefers "peaceful reunification ", a message it has ramped up in recent weeks.
Chen Ôüáhoped Taiwan's ÔÇőpeople would see the advantages of such a step, from ÔÇőcheaper living costs to sprucing up aged housing.
"In short, national reunification is not only a great moral cause, but also of great benefit," ÔÇőhe added.

item.title: North Korea sharply boosting nuclear weapons capacity, IAEA chief says
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - North Korea has made ÔÇőa very serious ÔÇîincrease in its nuclear weapons production ÔÇőcapabilities, IAEA ÔÇőchief Rafael Grossi said Ôüáon Wednesday.
Speaking ÔÇőin Seoul, the head ÔÇőof the U.N. nuclear watchdog confirmed a ÔÇőrapid rise ÔÇőin activity at THE five-megawatt ÔÇîreactor, Ôüáthe reprocessing unit, a light water reactor and ÔÇőother ÔÇőfacilities Ôüáat North Korea's Yongbyon nuclear ÔÇőcomplex, adding ÔÇőthe Ôüácountry's nuclear programme was estimated Ôüáat ÔÇőa few ÔÇődozen warheads.

item.title: Iran used Chinese spy satellite to target US bases, FT reports
item.description: April 15 (Reuters) - Iran secretly ÔÇőused a ÔÇîChinese spy satellite ÔÇőthat ÔÇőgave the Islamic ÔüáRepublic ÔÇőa new ÔÇőcapability to target U.S. ÔÇőmilitary ÔÇőbases across the ÔÇîMiddle ÔüáEast during the recent ÔÇőwar, ÔÇőthe ÔüáFinancial Times ÔÇőreported on ÔÇőWednesday.

item.title: South Korea says Israel accepts explanation, dispute over President Lee's comments resolved
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - South Korea's Foreign ÔÇőMinister Cho Hyun ÔÇîsaid on Wednesday a high-level Israeli ÔÇőoffical said ÔÇőit accepted Seoul's Ôüáexplanation of President ÔÇőLee Jae ÔÇőMyung's social media remarks on the Holocaust ÔÇőand that ÔÇőthe situation had been ÔÇîresolved.
Lee Ôüásparked a diplomatic row and domestic criticism after comparing ÔÇőIsraeli ÔÇőmilitary Ôüáactions against Palestinians to ÔÇőthe Holocaust ÔÇőin Ôüáa post on X, comments Ôüáthat ÔÇőIsrael strongly ÔÇőrejected.

item.title: Japan's decade-long march towards arms exports
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan has spent the past decade steadily dismantling some of the world's toughest arms export restrictions as it steps back from the postwar pacifism that shaped its ÔÇősecurity policy.
Here are the key events:
2014 - Then-prime minister Shinzo Abe ends a near-blanket ban ÔÇîon arms exports in place since 1976, allowing some transfers for humanitarian assistance and international cooperation seen to enhance Japan's security, as well as joint development programmes.
2016 - The Philippines leases five used TC-90 trainer aircraft for maritime patrols over ÔÇőthe disputed South China Sea, marking the first significant military equipment transfer since the rule ÔÇőchange.
2016 - In an early setback for Tokyo's export ambitions, Australia rejects a Japanese government-backed $40 Ôüábillion bid by Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (MHI) (7011.T) , opens new tab to supply advanced diesel submarines. Canberra picks a rival French ÔÇődesign.
2020 - Mitsubishi Electric (6503.T) , opens new tab becomes the first Japanese company to sell newly manufactured defence equipment overseas with a ÔÇődeal to supply air-surveillance radars to the Philippines.
2022 - Japan joins Britain and Italy in the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) to build an advanced stealth fighter jet by the mid-2030s, marking its first major joint defence project without the United States.
2023 - Tokyo establishes ÔÇőOverseas Security Assistance (OSA), a mechanism to provide military aid to like-minded countries in Southeast Asia and ÔÇőthe Pacific. That programme has delivered patrol boats to Indonesia and Bangladesh, drones to Tonga and Sri Lanka and radar ÔÇîsystems Ôüáto Djibouti and the Philippines.
2023 - Japan loosens its 2014 export rules for the first time to allow equipment built under licence to be sold back to the country of origin. That change enabled MHI to supply Patriot air defence missiles to the United States, indirectly helping Washington arm Ukraine.
2024 - Another tweak is ÔÇőmade a few months ÔÇőlater to allow future Ôüáoverseas sales of the GCAP fighter as long as exports to countries involved in conflicts remain off limits.
2025 - In what will be Japan's biggest-ever military ÔÇőexport, Australia selects an upgraded version of MHI's Mogami-class frigate in a $7 billion, 11-ship ÔÇőorder to Ôüáreplace its ageing Anzac-class warships.
2025 - Japan in December said it will double OSA funding to nations, including some facing maritime pressure from China.
2026 - Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's ruling party in April recommends scrapping limits that confine Ôüáarms sales ÔÇőto five categories: transport, relief and rescue, early warning systems, ÔÇősurveillance and mine clearance. The changes, which her government is expected to approve as soon as this month, maintain a ban ÔÇőon conflict zone exports except in extraordinary cases.

item.title: Britain's Prince Harry speaks of struggles of fatherhood on Australia tour
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 15 (Reuters) - Britain's Prince ÔÇőHarry spoke on Wednesday of the struggles of early fatherhood, as he visited a Melbourne football ÔÇîclub on the second day of his tour of Australia with wife Meghan, Duchess of Sussex.
The couple is on a four-day trip to the country, with engagements covering sport, mental health and veterans' affairs.
At the training ground of the Western Bulldogs Australian rules ÔÇőfootball club, Harry gave a speech in which he spoke about feeling disconnected from his children, and ÔÇőusing therapy to overcome past issues.
"Certainly, I felt a disconnection because my wife was Ôüáthe one creating life, and I was there to witness it," he said at the event, co-hosted by ÔÇőthe Movember mental health charity.
"From a therapy standpoint, you want to be the best version of yourself for your ÔÇőkids. And I knew that I had stuff from the past that I needed to deal with, and therefore prepare myself to basically cleanse myself of the past."
The Duke and Duchess of Sussex have two children, Archie and Lilibet, who are not accompanying them on ÔÇőthe trip.
The couple stepped down as working members of the British royal family and moved to the U.S. ÔÇőin 2020, citing a desire to be financially independent and to escape what they characterised as media intrusion into their private ÔÇîlives.
They last Ôüávisited Australia in 2018 while still working royals, announcing Meghan's first pregnancy hours after arriving in Sydney.
Harry and Meghan have received a mixed reception in Australia, where Britain's King Charles is the head of state, though a sizeable minority supports becoming a republic.
The couple were greeted by hundreds of children and parents at a ÔÇőchildren's hospital in Melbourne on Tuesday, ÔÇőwho said their presence Ôüáhad brought hope to young patients there.
But many other Australians have questioned their reasons for visiting the country.
"This is no royal visit but a continuation of tone-deaf hawking ÔÇőby a couple estranged from reality," an opinion piece in the Sydney Morning Herald ÔÇősaid ahead of Ôüáthe visit.
The couple's travel is being privately funded, though local media reported some policing costs associated with the visit would be paid by Australian taxpayers, sparking a protest petition signed by more than 45,000 people.
In contrast to their previous Ôüávisit, the ÔÇőSussexes will also undertake commercial activities while in Australia, with Meghan ÔÇőremaining in the country to host a wellness retreat at a luxury beachside hotel in Sydney over the weekend.
Tickets for the event, which includes ÔÇőyoga, manifestation and sound healing, start at A$2,699 ($1,912) per person.

item.title: Vedanta plant blast in India's Chhattisgarh kills 14, injures 20
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - At least 14 people ÔÇîlost their lives and 20 others were injured following a boiler explosion at ÔÇőa power plant operated by ÔÇőIndia's Vedanta Ltd (VDAN.NS) , opens new tab in the central Indian state Ôüáof Chhattisgarh, local police confirmed ÔÇőon Wednesday.
The cause of the explosion ÔÇőwas not immediately identified.
The incident took place on Tuesday at Singhitarai, about 230 km away from ÔÇőthe state capital of Raipur.
"The ÔÇődeath toll from the Vedanta power plant blast ÔÇîin ÔüáChhattisgarh has risen to 14, and the number of injured has increased to 20," District Superintendent of ÔÇőPolice PK ÔÇőThakur told ÔüáReuters.
The blast was likely caused by overheating in ÔÇőthe boiler tube, said Thakur.
In ÔÇőa Ôüástatement, Vedanta said an "unfortunate incident" had occurred at its Singhitarai plant and Ôüáthat ÔÇőa thorough investigation is underway to determine ÔÇőthe cause.

item.title: Iran war promises green edge for Asia as plastic packaging runs short
item.description:
SEOUL/TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - An 'eco-friendly' range of paper tubes and pouches touted by Yonwoo, a South Korean maker ÔÇőof packaging for cosmetics, has reaped unexpected benefit from the Iran war, which has disrupted supplies of the plastic needed to turn out single-use ÔÇîwrapping.
While the conflict has sent prices of plastic soaring to roughly four-year highs by choking off flows of the required raw materials of oil and petrochemicals, the company says it has fuelled inquiries three-fold for paper-based options.
"Interest initially came from companies focused on sustainability ... but if the plastics issue gets prolonged we expect demand to further increase," said Kim Min-sang, a senior manager at parent Kolmar Korea (161890.KS) , opens new tab .
The supplier to ÔÇőmajor firms, such as France's L'Oreal (OREP.PA) , opens new tab , has fielded inquiries mainly for paper tubes encasing items such as sunscreen and lotions that use just 20% of the ÔÇőplastic employed by conventional packaging, Kim told Reuters.
Across Asia, home to some of the world's biggest plastic users and polluters, changes that Ôüáenvironmental groups have sought for decades are quickly being adopted, even if they may prove a short-term flip.
Asia is not only heavily reliant on feedstock imported from ÔÇőthe Middle East, but it is hooked on plastic, with China, Japan, South Korea and Southeast Asia together using almost a third of the world's total by 2022, OECD ÔÇődata showed, up 900% since 1990.
The region also accounts for more than a third of all plastic waste leaking into the environment, thanks to poor waste collection methods in low-income Southeast Asian nations.
Japan ranks behind only the United States in terms of plastic production and consumption per head, according to a 2025 study by researchers from Beijing's Tsinghua University published in the science journal Nature.
Wholesalers there have been ÔÇőwarning about possible shortages of plastic trays and bags, said Kensuke Takahashi, product manager for Marutake supermarket in Saitama, adjacent to Tokyo.
"We now have to discuss how to ÔÇősell our products if trays are no longer supplied at all," said Takahashi. "I'm very worried. We really don't know what will happen."
Japanese makers of plastic bags and cling wrap, Mitsubishi Chemical and Sanipak, ÔÇîhave said Ôüáthey will raise prices by about 30% in coming weeks for some products as the conflict drives up costs of raw materials.
Talks for a global treaty to tackle plastic pollution stalled last year after the United States and plastic-producing countries pushed back against a drive to cap plastic production led by the European Union.
It is one of several environmental initiatives to lose steam under U.S. President Donald Trump, who has called climate change a hoax.
Trump's sweeping trade tariffs were also a blow for Taiwan's Lastic, which makes bamboo-based ÔÇőbiodegradable material, said senior development manager Luke ÔÇőAnderson.
American airlines eyeing the material to replace Ôüádisposable plastic cups and cutlery lost interest after Trump imposed the levies on U.S. imports last year, he said.
Now, as prices of plastic rise, several of his U.S. buyers have sought fresh quotes. "It's not that I like to look at the upside of war, ÔÇőbut ... if you can't control it, you've got to find the silver lining," he said.
Some companies are adapting to new alternatives.
In ÔÇőMalaysia, dairy producer Farm ÔüáFresh (FARM.KL) , opens new tab said it has temporarily switched to paper-based milk cartons because of the plastic supply disruptions.
But there is no quick fix for others, such as South Korea's Gaone International, which makes packaging for face masks.
Testing new materials would take time, so it has slashed daily output to between 10% and 20% from the usual 1 million units as it hunts for new Ôüásuppliers.
The 20-year-old factory ÔÇőis now warning clients of a wait of up to eight weeks for orders to be filled, ÔÇőand expects revenue to suffer accordingly, said sales team manager Han Kyung-hun.
"I hope things return to normal as soon as possible," said Han, but cautioned recovery could take a couple of months, even if the war ÔÇőended immediately.

item.title: TSMC confronts new contenders for chipmaking crown
item.description:
HONG KONG, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Powerful monopolies, like absolute monarchs, tend to attract challengers. Taiwan's TSMC (2330.TW) , opens new tab will this week confirm its undisputed dominance of the business of making high-end contract chips for customers from Nvidia (NVDA.O) , opens new tab to Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab . ÔÇőThat is making governments and rival companies even more determined to loosen its grip on the chipmaking crown.
The $1.7 trillion company formally known ÔÇîas Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing is set to deliver first-quarter earnings of roughly $17 billion, a blistering 49% year-on-year increase, per analyst forecasts compiled by Visible Alpha. That's powered by surging sales of its artificial intelligence chips, which companies from OpenAI to Anthropic just cannot get enough of.
Insatiable demand is a boon for TSMC, whose unmatched manufacturing and technological prowess give ÔÇőit an effective monopoly on making cutting-edge chips. Though rival Samsung Electronics (005930.KS) , opens new tab was first to the market with the current generation of advanced ÔÇőprocessors measuring just 3 nanometres, the $880 billion South Korean group has found it hard to secure orders from customers, according Ôüáto domestic media , opens new tab .
The Taiwanese group looks set to extend its dominance over the next generation of 2-nanometre chips. Analysts at Citi expect Nvidia, Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and ÔÇőAmazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab to place significant orders for the tiny processors over the next few years. The bank's researchers forecast TSMC's revenue will top $300 billion by 2030, almost ÔÇőtwice their forecast of $162 billion for this year. Confirming its dominance, analysts expect the company's gross margin to remain above 62%.
But for boss C.C. Wei there is such a thing as being too successful. TSMC's rule comes with rising geopolitical risks: its most advanced research and development and manufacturing operations remain in Taiwan, which Beijing views as its territory.
Japan is ÔÇőstepping up efforts to nurture a homegrown alternative. Last week, the industry ministry announced an additional $4 billion in state funding for Rapidus, taking the total ÔÇőso far to over $16 billion. The startup aims to mass-produce 2-nanometre chips in the next fiscal year. Days earlier, Elon Musk unveiled a tie-up with Intel (INTC.O) , opens new tab as part of his ÔÇîambitious Terafab Ôüáproject, a vertically integrated chipmaking facility that will supply the billionaire's companies SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and xAI.
These challenges remain nascent. TSMC plans as much as $56 billion in capital expenditures for this year, dwarfing Tokyo's support for Rapidus. Meanwhile, the $330 billion Intel is still recovering from a series of setbacks that saw the U.S. government take a 9% stake in the company. Investors are betting on TSMC's enduring advantage: the stock trades on just over 20 times forecast earnings for ÔÇőthe next 12 months, above its 5-year ÔÇőaverage of 18 times, according Ôüáto LSEG.
Others have tried and failed to break TSMC's grip. Yet as long as the company wears its crown it will have to confront new contenders.
Follow Robyn Mak on X , opens new tab .

item.title: EU measures to ease pain of expensive energy must have end date, EU executive says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 14 (Reuters) - European governments' measures to alleviate the impact of high energy prices on businesses and consumers must have a clear end-date and be focused ÔÇőto avoid the mistakes of the energy crisis of 2022, European ÔÇîEconomic Commissioner Valdis Dombrovskis said.
Speaking at the International Monetary Fund, Dombrovskis said any moves to ease the pain of the high prices caused by the closure of the Strait of ÔÇőHormuz as a result of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran should avoid ÔÇőboosting demand for fossil fuels.
"One key lesson from the last energy Ôüácrisis is that measures were often insufficiently targeted and remained in place ÔÇőfor too long, making them much more fiscally costly than necessary. This is something ÔÇőwe want to avoid now," Dombrovskis said in a conversation with IMF European Department head Alfred Kammer.
He said higher debt and deficits as well as higher interest rates than during ÔÇőthe 2022 energy crisis caused by Russia's war on Ukraine made it now ÔÇőmore important for EU governments to avoid costly, lengthy measures to cushion energy price spikes.
"One ÔÇîmessage Ôüáwe are trying to reinforce is that measures should come with clear sunset clauses to ensure they remain time-limited. They should also avoid increasing demand for oil and gas at a time when demand should be declining," he said.
"The problem ÔÇőis that broad ÔÇőmeasures, such as Ôüáacross-the-board tax cuts, are easier to implement politically and administratively. Targeted measures require defining eligibility thresholds, which takes more effort," ÔÇőDombrovskis said.
"Given todayÔÇÖs tighter fiscal space and financial conditions, we ÔÇőneed to Ôüábe much more focused and targeted in our response," he said.
Some EU countries seem to be heeding some of the advice. Germany decided on Monday on fuel price Ôüárelief for ÔÇőconsumers and businesses worth 1.6 billion euros for ÔÇőtwo months, while France said any support measures would be only for sectors most in need ÔÇőand be renewed on a monthly basis.

item.title: Germany to provide further $23.6 million in aid to Sudan this year
item.description:
BERLIN, April 15 (Reuters) - Germany will provide an additional 20 million euros ($23.58 million) to ÔÇőSudan this year, with further funding commitments currently ÔÇîunder review, the development ministry in Berlin said in a statement on Wednesday.
At the end of 2025, the ministry had provided 155.4 million ÔÇőeuros for projects in Sudan and in neighbouring ÔÇőcountries affected by the war in Sudan , which it would Ôüáexpand this year by 20 million euros, it said ÔÇőahead of an international aid conference on Sudan in the ÔÇőGerman capital later on Wednesday.
Sudan's war between the Sudanese army and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces, which enters its third year on Wednesday, ÔÇőhas caused widespread hunger and displaced millions of people amid ÔÇőone of the world's largest humanitarian crises.
Germany aims to gather funding pledges ÔÇîof Ôüáat least 1 billion euros at the conference. "That seems to be working," Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul told radio broadcaster Deutschlandfunk.
Ensuring enough funding for such crises, with wars in Iran ÔÇőand Ukraine also ÔÇőraging and Ôüáthe United States pulling back on aid commitments, is a Sisyphean task, he added.
"We must ÔÇőtry to compensate for what others, including the ÔÇőUnited ÔüáStates, unfortunately fail to do," he said.
It is also in Germany's interest to make money available to ensure people do not Ôüáface ÔÇőhunger, he said, to prevent a ÔÇőrepeat of the large influx of migrants coming from the Middle East in ÔÇő2015/16.
($1 = 0.8483 euros)

item.title: US shuts down Iran's maritime trade despite optimism for more talks
item.description:
DUBAI/WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States said on Wednesday its military had completely halted trade going in and out of Iran by sea, even though President ÔÇőDonald Trump said talks with Tehran on ending the war could resume this week.
Trump said negotiations between U.S. and Iranian officials could resume in Pakistan in the ÔÇînext two days and Vice President JD Vance, who led weekend talks that ended without a breakthrough, said he felt positive about where things stood.
"I think youÔÇÖre going to be watching an amazing two days ahead," Trump told ABC News reporter Jonathan Karl, adding he did not think it would be necessary to extend a two-week ceasefire that ends on April 21.
"It could end either way, but I think a deal is preferable ÔÇőbecause then they can rebuild," Trump said, according to a post by Karl on X. "They really do have a different regime now. No matter what, we took out the ÔÇőradicals."
Officials from Pakistan, Iran and the Gulf also said negotiating teams from the U.S. and Iran could return to Pakistan later this week, although Ôüáone senior Iranian source said no date had been set.
Despite the optimistic note, more vessels were being turned back under the U.S. blockade on Iranian ports, including a U.S.-sanctioned and Chinese-owned tanker ÔÇőRich Starry that was making its way back to the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after exiting the Persian Gulf.
Admiral Brad Cooper, the head of the U.S. Central Command, said American forces ÔÇőhad completely halted economic trade going in and out of Iran by sea, which he said fuels 90% of Iran's economy.
"In less than 36 hours since the blockade was implemented, U.S. forces have completely halted economic trade going into and out of Iran by sea," Cooper said in a post on X.
Earlier the U.S. military said it had intercepted eight Iran-linked oil tankers since the start of the blockade on Monday, according to the ÔÇőWall Street Journal.
Trump, speaking to the New York Post on Tuesday, said his negotiators are likely to be back, thanks largely to the "great job" Pakistan's army chief, Field Marshal ÔÇőAsim Munir, was doing to moderate the talks.
Later on Tuesday, at an event in Georgia, U.S. Vice President JD Vance said Trump wanted to make a "grand bargain" with Iran but there was a lot of ÔÇîmistrust between Ôüáthe two countries.
"You are not going to solve that problem overnight," he said.
The signs of diplomatic engagement to end the conflict that began on February 28 helped calm oil markets, pressing benchmark prices down for a second day on Wednesday. Asian stocks rose while the safe-haven dollar stabilised after falling for a seventh straight session overnight.
The war has prompted Iran to effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a crucial global waterway for crude and gas transport and cut shipments from the Gulf to global buyers, particularly in Asia and Europe.
About 5,000 people have died in the hostilities, including about ÔÇő3,000 in Iran and 2,000 in Lebanon.
Iran's nuclear ambitions were a key sticking Ôüápoint at the weekend talks. The U.S. had proposed a 20-year suspension of all nuclear activity by Iran, while Tehran had suggested a halt of three to five years, according to people familiar with the proposals.
Speaking in Seoul, the head of the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), Rafael Grossi, ÔÇősaid the length of any moratorium on Iranian uranium enrichment was a political decision and it was possible Tehran might accept a ÔÇőcompromise as a confidence-building act.
The ÔüáU.S. has also pressed for any enriched nuclear material to be removed from Iran, while Tehran has demanded that international sanctions against it be removed.
One source involved in the negotiations in Pakistan said back-channel talks since the weekend had produced progress in closing that gap, bringing the two sides closer to a deal that could be put forward at a new round of talks.
However, in a major Ôüácomplication for peace ÔÇőprospects, Israel has continued to attack Lebanon as it targets Hezbollah, an Iran-backed militant group. Israel and the U.S. ÔÇősay that campaign is not covered by the ceasefire, while Iran insists it is.
On Tuesday, the UK, Canada, Japan and seven other countries condemned the killings of UN peacekeepers in Lebanon and called for "an urgent end to hostilities".
The statement comes ÔÇőafter the deaths of three Indonesian peacekeepers last month. The countries welcomed the ceasefire agreed between the U.S., Israel and Iran.

item.title: Exclusive: Russia-linked hackers compromised scores of Ukrainian prosecutorsÔÇÖ email accounts, data shows
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia-linked hackers broke into more than 170 email accounts belonging to prosecutors and investigators across Ukraine during the last several ÔÇőmonths, according to data reviewed by Reuters, a campaign that shows how MoscowÔÇÖs spies are keeping tabs on the Ukrainian officials tasked with rooting out corruption and Russian ÔÇîcollaborators.
The data was inadvertently exposed to the internet by the hackers and discovered by Ctrl-Alt-Intel, a collective of British and American cyber threat researchers. Ctrl-Alt-Intel said data left on the server - including logs of successful hacking operations and thousands of stolen emails - showed that the hackers compromised at least 284 inboxes between September 2024 and March 2026.
Most of the victims were in Ukraine; others are from neighboring NATO countries and the Balkans.
The operation was first described last month in ÔÇőa Ctrl-Alt-Intel blog post. , opens new tab Reuters reviewed the underlying data and is publishing details of the hacks for the first time, including the identities of more than a dozen compromised European agencies ÔÇőand officials.
Ctrl-Alt-Intel said the mistake provided a rare opportunity to examine the workings of a Russian espionage campaign.
The hackers ÔÇťjust made a huge operational blunder,ÔÇŁ Ctrl-Alt-Intel Ôüásaid. ÔÇťThey left their front door wide open.ÔÇŁ
The Russian embassy in Washington did not respond to requests for comment. Moscow has repeatedly denied it engages in hacking operations against other countries.
Ctrl-Alt-Intel ÔÇőattributed the hacking campaign to ÔÇťFancy Bear,ÔÇŁ one of the nicknames assigned to a well-known Russian military hacking squad. Two researchers who independently reviewed Ctrl-Alt-IntelÔÇÖs work - Matthieu Faou, with the cybersecurity company ESET, and Feike ÔÇőHacquebord, with the cybersecurity company TrendAI - agreed the hackers were tied to Moscow. However, Faou said he could not verify Fancy Bear was involved, and Hacquebord disputed Fancy Bear's involvement.
The hackers likely targeted Ukrainian law enforcement either to stay ahead of investigators working to expose MoscowÔÇÖs spies or to gather potentially embarrassing information about top officials in Kyiv, said Keir Giles, an associate fellow at LondonÔÇÖs Chatham House think tank, who reviewed a list of the victims.
The data ÔÇőshowed the hackers broke into accounts managed by the Specialized Prosecutor's Office in the Field of Defense, a wartime body established to fight corruption and unmask spies in the Ukrainian military. They also ÔÇőtargeted UkraineÔÇÖs Asset Recovery and Management Agency (ARMA), which oversees assets seized from criminals and Russian collaborators, and the Kyiv-based Prosecutor's Training Center.
Among the victims were Yaroslava Maksymenko, who was the chief of ARMA at the time, the ÔÇîdata shows. At Ôüáthe Prosecutor's Training Center, the data shows the hackers broke into the mailboxes of 44 employees, including one belonging to the centerÔÇÖs deputy director, Oleg Duka.
The Russians allegedly stole data from at least one senior employee of the Specialized Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office (SAPO), which has investigated some of UkraineÔÇÖs most high-profile corruption scandals, including one that prompted the resignation of President Volodymyr ZelenskiyÔÇÖs chief peace negotiator Andriy Yermak in November.
Maksymenko, Duka, ARMA, SAPO, and the prosecutors did not respond to requests for comment. Ukraine's Computer Emergency Response Team said it was aware of the hack and had already investigated some of the compromises identified by ÔÇőReuters.
The hack uncovered ÔÇőby Ctrl-Alt-Intel represents "a small set of activity Ôüáin regards to the whole Russia-aligned espionage ecosystem,ÔÇŁ said Faou, the ESET researcher.
The data shows the hackers broke into the email inbox of the Central City Hospital in Pokrovsk, a railway hub Russia has been trying to cement its control over , as well as an inbox belonging to the cityÔÇÖs finance committee.
Scores ÔÇőof officials in surrounding NATO countries were also hacked, the data shows.
In Romania, the hackers compromised at least 67 email accounts maintained by the ÔÇőRomanian Air Force, including several Ôüábelonging to NATO airbases and at least one senior military officer. The Romanian Ministry of Defense did not respond to requests for comment.
The data also shows the spies compromised 27 email inboxes managed by Hellenic National Defense General Staff, GreeceÔÇÖs top military body. Among those hacked were Greek defense attaches in India and Bosnia and the public-facing inbox for GreeceÔÇÖs Joint Armed Forces Mental Health Center. The General Staff did not answer Ôüáa detailed list ÔÇőof questions.
In Bulgaria, the hackers broke into at least four inboxes belonging to local officials in Plovdiv province, where Russian interference ÔÇőwas alleged to have disabled satellite navigation services ahead of a visit by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen last year. Bulgarian officials did not respond to comment requests.
The data also shows the spies hacked academics and military officials in Serbia, ÔÇőa traditional Russian ally. SerbiaÔÇÖs Ministry of Defense did not respond to requests for comment.
ÔÇťA supposedly close relationship with Moscow is no insurance against Russian espionage," Giles said.

item.title: Xi assures Russia of China's friendship as ties grow with other nations
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - China and Russia must trust and support each other, deepen ÔÇőcooperation and defend each other's interests, President Xi Jinping said ÔÇîon Wednesday, assuring Moscow of friendship even as the world's No.2 economy expands ties with other neighbours and nations.
In a meeting with Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov at ÔÇőthe Great Hall of the People, Xi said the "stability" and "certainty" of China-Russia ÔÇőrelations were particularly valuable as he told the Russian envoy to convey Ôüáhis sincere regards to President Vladimir Putin, according to the official ÔÇőXinhua news agency.
A handful of Western leaders have paid visits to Beijing ÔÇőthis year, including the prime ministers of Spain, Britain and Canada, seeking to elevate ties with China. On Wednesday, Vietnam's top leader To Lam met Xi in ÔÇőBeijing to cement relations at a time when both work to ÔÇőfend off trade tariff pressure from the United States.
Putin and Xi are expected hold ÔÇîa Ôüásummit sometime this year to affirm Sino-Russian partnership and cooperation as the two neighbours observe anniversaries of their ties, including the 2001 Sino-Russia Treaty of Friendship.
"In the face of a once-in-a-century change, China and Russia ÔÇőmust, through closer ÔÇőand more robust Ôüástrategic cooperation, resolutely defend the legitimate interests of both countries, uphold the unity of the Global South, ÔÇőand demonstrate the sense of responsibility expected of major ÔÇőpowers and Ôüápermanent members of the United Nations Security Council," Xi told Lavrov.
In recent years, China has pushed for the building of what it says should Ôüábe ÔÇőan equal and orderly multipolar world where ÔÇőevery country has a say and major powers shoulder more responsibilities, an alternative to what ÔÇőBeijing describes as U.S. hegemony.

item.title: Pakistan PM Sharif to visit Saudi Arabia, Qatar and Turkey this week
item.description: ISLAMABAD, April 15 (Reuters) - Pakistan Prime ÔÇîMinister ÔÇőShehbaz ÔÇőSharif will Ôüávisit ÔÇőSaudi ÔÇőArabia, Qatar ÔÇőand ÔÇőTurkey from April ÔÇî15 Ôüáto 18, ÔÇőIslamabad ÔÇősaid Ôüáon ÔÇőWednesday.

item.title: Hungary's Magyar says to suspend state media broadcast, pass new media law
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election winner Peter Magyar ÔÇősaid on Wednesday ÔÇîthat his government will suspend the broadcast ÔÇőof state media, ÔÇőpass a new media Ôüálaw, set up ÔÇőa new media authority ÔÇőand ensure press freedom after his cabinet takes ÔÇőpower.
"Every Hungarian deserves ÔÇőa public service media that ÔÇîbroadcasts Ôüáthe truth," Magyar said in an interview on state Kossuth ÔÇőradio. ÔÇőMagyar's ÔüáTISZA (Respect and Freedom) party won a ÔÇőlandslide victory ÔÇőin ÔüáSunday's election, ending Prime Minister Viktor Orban's Ôüá16-year ÔÇőrule.

item.title: Iranian crew members return home from Sri Lanka
item.description:
COLOMBO, April 15 (Reuters) - Over 200 Iranian crew members from two warships who were rescued by Sri Lanka ÔÇőhave been sent back to their homeland, ÔÇîa top official said on Wednesday.
Sri Lanka rescued 32 Iranian crew from the warship IRIS Dena on March 4 after ÔÇőit was hit by a torpedo from a ÔÇőU.S. submarine.
The ship was returning from a naval Ôüáexercise organised by India, amid the U.S.-Israeli war ÔÇőon Iran.
Sri Lanka also rescued 208 crew from a ÔÇősecond vessel, IRIS Booshehr on March 5 that had also requested assistance from Colombo after it ran into engine trouble.
"The crew ÔÇőof both ships were flown out about 11 ÔÇőp.m. on a special flight on Tuesday night," Deputy Defence Minister ÔÇîAruna ÔüáJayasekera told Reuters.
After its crew was offloaded in Colombo, IRIS Booshehr was towed to waters off Sri Lanka's eastern coast of Trincomalee eventually reaching on Tuesday ÔÇőmorning.
"There are ÔÇőabout 8-10 ÔüáIranian crew remaining aboard to assist operations," Jayasekera added.
Sri Lanka granted 30-day entry ÔÇővisas to the crew members and housed ÔÇőthem Ôüáin navy and air force camps before arrangements were made for their return to Iran.
A chartered plane arranged Ôüáby ÔÇőIran took back the bodies of ÔÇő84 crew members from the Dena who were killed in the ÔÇőU.S. attack.

item.title: Putin to visit China in first half of 2026, agencies cite Lavrov
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia is ready to increase energy ÔÇősupplies to China ahead of an expected visit ÔÇîby President Vladimir Putin, Russian news agencies quoted Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov as saying on Wednesday at a news ÔÇőconference in Beijing.
The agencies quoted Lavrov as saying ÔÇőthat the visit would take place in Ôüáthe first half of the year, while the ÔÇőVedomosti newspaper cited sources as saying it would be ÔÇőduring the week beginning May 18.
President Xi Jinping met Lavrov on Wednesday, assuring Moscow of China's friendship and saying that China ÔÇőand Russia must trust and support each other, ÔÇődeepen cooperation, and defend each other's interests.
Lavrov told the news conference ÔÇîthat ÔüáRussia was ready to help China and other countries affected by the Middle East crisis with energy supplies.
"Russia can, of course, make up for the ÔÇőresource shortfall facing ÔÇőboth China Ôüáand other countries that are interested in working with us on an equal ÔÇőand mutually beneficial basis," Lavrov told the ÔÇőnews Ôüáconference in China.
Lavrov also said that Russia and China had all the necessary means to avoid reliance Ôüáon what ÔÇőhe described as U.S. efforts ÔÇőto disrupt global energy markets through conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Trump tells ABC News reporter he was not concerned about Orban's loss in Hungary
item.description:
April 14 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump told ABC News ÔÇőreporter Jonathan Karl on Tuesday ÔÇîhe was not concerned about Viktor Orban's loss in Hungary, and that he likes incoming ÔÇőPrime Minister Peter Magyar.
"I think ÔÇőthe new man's going to do a Ôüágood job ÔÇö he's a good man," ÔÇőTrump told the reporter.
Trump told the ÔÇőreporter he did not know if it would have made a difference if he had gone ÔÇőto Hungary instead of Vice President ÔÇőJD Vance to campaign for Orban.
"He was behind ÔÇîsubstantially," ÔüáTrump said. "I wasnÔÇÖt that involved in this one. ViktorÔÇÖs a good man, though."
Trump had backed Orban leading up to the ÔÇővote, even ÔÇőspeaking briefly Ôüálast week at a campaign rally in Hungary, when Vance telephoned ÔÇőhis boss upon taking the ÔÇőstage.
But ÔüáOrban lost power after 16 years as Hungarians voted in record numbers for Ôüáa ÔÇőpro-EU course spearheaded by ÔÇőcenter-right rival Peter Magyar.

item.title: Kenya raises retail fuel prices as Mideast conflict drives up crude costs
item.description:
NAIROBI, April 15 (Reuters) - Kenya has raised its retail fuel prices by as much as 24.2% amid a spike ÔÇőin crude prices and squeezed petroleum supplies caused by ÔÇîthe Middle East conflict, the East African country's energy regulator said late on Tuesday.
A statement released by the Energy and Petroleum Regulatory Authority (EPRA), ÔÇőwhich sets the maximum allowable retail prices for various ÔÇőproducts monthly, showed a litre of petrol was hiked Ôüáby 16.1% to 206.97 Kenyan shillings ($1.60).
A litre of diesel ÔÇőwas also raised by 24.2% to 206.84 Kenyan shillings, while kerosene ÔÇőwas maintained at 152.78 shillings. The regulator cited the cost of imported products, which they said had risen by as much as 68.7%, ÔÇőto justify the hiking of retail prices.
In March, the EPRA ÔÇőleft prices unchanged, saying the impact of the war had yet to ÔÇîbe Ôüáreflected in retail prices.
Kenya imports nearly all of its fuel products from the Middle East via government-to-government deals with Persian Gulf suppliers, including Saudi Aramco Trading Fujairah, Abu Dhabi's ADNOC ÔÇőGlobal Trading Ltd, ÔÇőand Emirates National ÔüáOil Company Singapore Ltd.
Hours before the new prices took effect late on Tuesday, motorists rushed ÔÇőto pump stations in the capital, Nairobi, to ÔÇőfill Ôüáup their tanks, causing long queues.
The EPRA also said it had reduced value-added tax (VAT) on petrol, diesel, and kerosene from 16% Ôüáto ÔÇő13% to "cushion consumers from the high...cost ÔÇőof petroleum products as a result of the escalated prices in the international ÔÇőmarket."
($1 = 129.2000 Kenyan shillings)

item.title: European oil refining margins turn negative, bucking global trend
item.description:
LONDON, April 14 (Reuters) - European oil refining margins have turned negative, lagging stronger margins ÔÇîin Asia and the U.S., as competition for crude from Asian buyers due to the Iran war drives up costs even as fuel prices hit record highs, according to IEA data and trade sources.
Northwest European light sweet hydroskimming margins dropped to an average of minus $6.45 a barrel ÔÇőin the week beginning April 6, the IEA said in its monthly report on Tuesday based on data ÔÇőfrom Argus Media.
Margins for medium sour cracking were also in negative territory, the data showed. ÔüáLight sweet cracking margins remain positive, though they have also weakened significantly.
The margin squeeze is a consequence of the ÔÇősurge in physical crude prices to record highs as the war in Iran disrupts Middle East flows.
The narrowing European margin effectively ÔÇőshows that these plants would be running at a loss, and is likely to prompt some to process less crude into fuels, analysts said.
Simpler European refineries, which lack upgrading units to extract more higher-value products such as jet fuel, could be forced to trim runs if ÔÇőmargins remain under pressure, though there is no sign of widespread cuts yet, trading sources said.
"As things stand, Europe ÔÇőis going to cut utilisation," Sparta Commodities analyst Neil Crosby said, adding that runs could fall by as much as 500,000 barrels ÔÇîper day.
By contrast, in the U.S. Gulf, heavy sour coking margins strengthened last week compared with the March average, IEA data showed. In Singapore, similarly, medium sour cracking margins were also stronger last week than their March averages.
The squeeze in Europe reflects rising crude costs as Asian refiners compete aggressively for cargoes, several trading sources said, as ÔÇőwell as higher operating costs ÔÇősuch as for electricity Ôüáand natural gas.
"It's typical of these crises," said a trading source at a European refinery. "Fuel cracks rise first, but as crude and other costs adjust, margins get dented." He added ÔÇőthat their margin dropped from about $30 a barrel in the first week of the ÔÇőconflict to just Ôüáover $4 currently.
The squeeze comes after margins globally soared in March, with those in Europe reaching record highs.
In Singapore, the IEA said, margins in March were some 14-fold higher than February levels, while in northwest Europe light sweet hydroskimming margins in March were Ôüámore ÔÇőthan nine times higher than in February at $15.20 a barrel.
Some refiners even ÔÇődelayed planned shutdowns to take advantage of the higher fuel prices.
Italy's 300,000 barrel per day Sarroch refinery, for instance, pushed a maintenance shutdown from late ÔÇőMarch to mid-May, industry monitor IIR said. The refinery's operator, Vitol, declined to comment.

item.title: Russia launches more than 300 drones, missiles at Ukraine overnight
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Russia attacked Ukraine overnight ÔÇőwith hundreds of drones and three ballistic missiles, targeting port ÔÇîinfrastructure in the south and wounding at least seven people, Ukrainian officials said.
Russia launched 324 drones in the period since 6 p.m. (1500 GMT) on Tuesday and ÔÇőthree ballistic missiles, Ukraine's air force said. Air defence units shot ÔÇődown or neutralised 309 drones, but the missiles and Ôüá13 drones hit at nine locations, it said.
Three people were ÔÇőinjured in an overnight Russian drone attack on the southeastern city of ÔÇőDnipro, the regional governor, Oleksandr Ganzha, said on the Telegram messaging app.
Ganzha said the attack also damaged a nine-story apartment building and an administrative building, posting ÔÇőphotos of a building with a gaping hole in one of ÔÇőits sides.
The new attack came after a missile strike on the city killed five ÔÇîand Ôüáinjured close to 30 people on Tuesday.
Four people sought medical attention after an overnight drone attack in the central city of Cherkasy, Ihor Taburets, the regional governor, said.
A woman was killed in the ÔÇőshelling of the ÔÇősoutheastern city of ÔüáZaporizhzhia early on Wednesday, the regional governor, Ivan Fedorov, said.
Fedorov, writing on Telegram, said a 74-year-old ÔÇősaleswoman was killed in a kiosk, and the attack ÔÇőalso damaged Ôüáa car park, business premises, and nearby residential buildings.
Port infrastructure in Ukraine's southern region of Odesa on the Black Sea came under another Ôüádrone ÔÇőattack, the regional governor, Oleh Kiper, said, ÔÇőreporting damage to administrative and warehouse buildings.
Officials in the Kyiv region also reported a ÔÇődrone attack.

item.title: Rohingya survivor recounts ordeal as 250 missing in Andaman sea tragedy
item.description:
DHAKA, April 15 (Reuters) - A Rohingya survivor has recounted a harrowing journey at sea after an overcrowded boat carrying refugees and Bangladeshi nationals capsized in the Andaman Sea, leaving around 250 people missing.
The boat, ÔÇőwhich departed from Teknaf in southern Bangladesh and was bound for Malaysia, sank in the ÔÇîsecond week of April amid rough seas, strong winds and severe overcrowding, the United Nations' refugee and migration agencies said .
Bangladesh hosts around 1.2 million Rohingya refugees in camps in the south, most of whom fled violence in Myanmar in 2017. Many continue ÔÇőto attempt dangerous sea journeys in search of safety and livelihoods abroad.
Survivors said nearly 300 people were ÔÇőpacked onboard, including women, children, crew members and suspected traffickers.
Rafiqul Islam, one of the Ôüásurvivors, said passengers endured four days and nights at sea as conditions rapidly deteriorated. In an attempt ÔÇőto avoid patrols, traffickers forced passengers into cramped storage compartments meant for fish and nets.
ÔÇťThere was hardly any ÔÇőoxygen," he told Reuters, adding that at least 30 people died from suffocation before the boat capsized. "We could not breathe."
When the boat overturned, hundreds were thrown into the sea. Islam estimated that around 240 people were still onboard at the time, including ÔÇőabout 20 women and several children. Only a handful survived.
Islam was among a small group who initially made ÔÇőit out alive. A passing Bangladeshi oil vessel rescued four survivors, who then alerted the crew to others still in ÔÇîthe Ôüáwater.
ÔÇťThey later found five more people,ÔÇŁ he said.
He described a dangerous, multi-stage journey that began on April 4, when passengers first left on a small fishing boat before being transferred to a larger trawler near Myanmar waters. At one point, they were forced to hide in bushes to avoid detection by patrols.
The tragedy highlights ÔÇőthe desperation of Rohingya refugees, ÔÇőmany of whom continue Ôüáto risk perilous sea journeys to countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia and Thailand.
Aid agencies warn the situation is worsening as humanitarian support declines. A recent report by ÔÇőthe International Rescue Committee (IRC), based on a survey of 500 households in CoxÔÇÖs ÔÇőBazar, found Ôüájust 2% of Rohingya parents feel hopeful about their children's future, compared with 84% among host communities.
With food rations reduced to as little as $7 per person per month, many families are resorting to extreme coping measures. Nearly 69% of Ôüárefugee households ÔÇőreport children dropping out of school, while half say their ÔÇőchildren have been forced into labour.
The IRC urged donors and authorities to shift from emergency aid to long-term solutions, warning that without sustained ÔÇősupport, both refugees and host communities face deepening poverty and vulnerability.

item.title: Georgia overhauls higher education as it shifts away from the West
item.description:
TBILISI, April 15 (Reuters) - When anti-government protests began in Georgia in late 2024, Luka Mishveladze started sleeping on the floor at his university building to be closer to student rallies.
Some 18 months later, the 20-year-old student is now a protest ÔÇőorganiser, demonstrating outside the same building in the capital Tbilisi against government reforms of higher education that look set to force his university's closure.
"It was hard ÔÇîfor me to realise that this was happening in reality, that I am losing my university, the place I am used to calling home," Mishveladze told Reuters.
Opponents of the government see the overhaul adopted in February as part of a broader anti-Western shift under the governing Georgian Dream party that began in earnest after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
Once seen in the West as a burgeoning democracy, on the fast track to joining the European Union and ÔÇőescaping Russia's orbit, Georgia is increasingly turning its back on the West and deepening ties with Moscow.
Georgian Dream says the higher education reforms, affecting funding and ÔÇőthe geographical redistribution of faculties, are intended to better reflect the demands of the labour market and promote regional universities.
It says it wants to Ôüáreduce what it calls an excessive concentration of higher education institutions in Tbilisi and stop the "irrational" use of resources.
Detractors say the reforms are further evidence that the government is turning the country ÔÇőof 3.7 million away from the West, more than three decades after Georgia gained independence from the Soviet Union.
Under the reforms, guided by the principle of "one faculty, one city", only one university in ÔÇőa given city will be allowed to offer certain degree programmes.
