The government will decide which academic disciplines can be taught at each of the 19 public universities - attended by over half of Georgia's university students - and redistribute admission quotas.
Tbilisi's Ilia State University (ISU), where Mishveladze and about 17,300 others study, will be hit particularly hard. One of the country's top-ranked research institutions, it is widely seen in Georgia as outspoken and liberal, and has strong ÔÇőties with European partner institutions.
ISU says over 90% of its programmes will be cut and that it will have to wind down over three years. This autumn, ISU will be able to admit ÔÇőonly 335 new undergraduates, down from the 3,770 admitted last year.
"No other sector in Georgia has been so integrated into the European space than higher education. So they're killing it," said Ketevan Darakhvelidze, the chancellor ÔÇîof ISU. "The more Ôüáisolated Georgia will be, the better for the government."
Seven other universities received cuts to their admission quotas although only ISU says it faces closure.
Shalva Tabatadze, who runs an education policy research centre, said state funding had often been used in Georgia to support universities "which have political affiliations", describing this as "problematic".
A March report by a mission from the Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe, a security and rights body, found "marked democratic backsliding" in Georgia and briefly mentioned the higher education reforms. It cited concerns in Georgia that the redistribution of faculties was intended to disperse large numbers of ÔÇőstudent protesters.
The government did not respond to Reuters' ÔÇőrequests for comment for this article. Education ÔüáMinister Givi Mikanadze, a member of a state committee that drew up the reforms, did not respond to Reuters' interview requests.
Georgian Dream, in power since 2012, says its policies are not authoritarian and that it is trying to keep peace in Georgia, which lost a short war to Russia ÔÇőin 2008. It accuses opposition parties of seeking to foment violent coups.
Resistance to the reforms among students and university staff ÔÇőhas injected new fervour into Ôüáthe nightly anti-government rallies that began in late 2024, when the government announced it was suspending talks on joining the EU.
The relatively small rallies do not pose a big threat to the government but have attracted support at universities across Georgia.
"They are going to shut down every free-thinking institution capable of critical reasoning," said ISU sociology professor Nino Rcheulishvili.
Since it was founded in 2006, ISU has forged links Ôüáwith 145 European ÔÇőinstitutions to offer double-degree programmes.
"All those programmes are at risk," said Nino Doborjginidze, ISU's rector, adding that the university ÔÇőcould lose access to most foreign grants under recent legislative changes.
In a small victory for the protesters, the government scrapped a planned merger of two Tbilisi universities in February, but some young people still see little future in Georgia.
Davit Mshvenieradze, 20, ÔÇőis among students who say they may leave, but he wants Georgian Dream ousted first.
"If they are here (in power), I want to stay here and protest against them," he said.

item.title: Ukraine broaches 'stolen' Russian grain cargo on call with Israel
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha held a call with his Israeli counterpart Gideon Sa'ar to discuss ÔÇőa Russian vessel carrying what he described ÔÇîas grain stolen from Ukraine that was allowed to dock in an Israeli port.
Kyiv considers all grain produced in the ÔÇőfour regions Russia claimed as its own ÔÇősince invading Ukraine in 2022, and Crimea, annexed Ôüáby Russia in 2014, to have been stolen ÔÇőby Moscow.
"I stressed that the illegal export of stolen ÔÇőUkrainian agricultural products is part of Russia's broader war effort," Sybiha said late on Tuesday in a post on X. "Such ÔÇőillegal trade with stolen goods must not be ÔÇőallowed."
Russia refers to the four regions as its "new territories", but they ÔÇîare Ôüástill internationally recognised as Ukrainian.
Sybiha said in March that Russia moved more than 2 million tons of stolen Ukrainian grain through the Black Sea last year. ÔÇőUkrainian official ÔÇőestimated in ÔüáAugust that Russia has stolen 15 million tons of Ukrainian grain since the ÔÇőstart of the full-scale war.
The foreign minister ÔÇőadded Ôüáthat the two also talked about security matters and the situation in the Middle East.
"We reaffirmed our mutual Ôüáinterest ÔÇőin advancing the bilateral agenda and ÔÇőmaintaining an active dialogue, including on security-related matters," he said.

item.title: Hungary's Magyar will talk to MOL leadership, focus on fuel supplies
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - HungaryÔÇÖs election-winner Peter Magyar said on Wednesday he would need to hold talks with the leadership of oil company MOL (MOLB.BU) , opens new tab , adding that ensuring ÔÇőfuel security would be the top priority in the coming weeks.
Magyar's centre-right TISZA (Respect and ÔÇîFreedom) party won a landslide victory in Sunday's election, ending nationalist Prime Minister Viktor Orban's 16-year rule.
In early March, Orban imposed a cap on fuel prices as soaring oil prices, fuelled by the war in Iran, ÔÇődrove global diesel and petrol prices higher.
His government also banned the export of crude ÔÇőoil, diesel and 95-octane petrol and said it would release 45 days Ôüáworth of state fuel reserves, as Hungary contended with a halt in supplies through a ÔÇőkey pipeline carrying Russian oil via Ukraine.
Data from the Hungarian Hydrocarbon Stockpiling Association showed that ÔÇőHungaryÔÇÖs strategic oil and oil-product reserves fell to 44 days of net imports by the end of March, from 91 days at the end of February. In a statement on Tuesday, the association said replenishment ÔÇőwas under way and reserves had since risen to 53 days of net imports.
European Union ÔÇőmember states must maintain 90 days of net imports.
"With the strategic oil reserves, the acting government has ÔÇîa Ôüáhuge responsibility in what it does in the next 20-30 days," Magyar said. "Everyone hopes that this Druzhba pipeline can restart by the end of April but even if it does it will take some time for strategic reserves to be replenished."
"The most important is that security ÔÇőof supply must be ÔÇőensured in the next Ôüáfew weeks under the outgoing government and then in the first days of the TISZA government."
MOL said in a response to emailed ÔÇőReuters questions on Tuesday that its crude oil supply was uninterrupted as ÔÇőoil was Ôüácoming via the Adriatic pipeline even though supplies via the Druzhba pipeline were still suspended.
"Following agreements with Libya, Kazakhstan, Norway, and Saudi Arabia, we have also reached agreements on deliveries with companies Ôüáin ÔÇőthe United States. The Danube Refinery is operating at ÔÇőreduced capacity due to a fire that occurred at one of our units in October," MOL said.
"Fuel supply in ÔÇőHungary remains uninterrupted".

item.title: Former military chief to take over as Philippine National Security Adviser
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - Philippine National Security Adviser Eduardo Ano has resigned due to health ÔÇőreasons, a Palace official said on Wednesday, ÔÇîand will be replaced by former military chief Eduardo Oban.
"The President has accepted the resignation of National ÔÇőSecurity Adviser Eduardo Ano. Secretary Ano ÔÇőhas decided to retire from public service Ôüáafter decades of continuous service in government ÔÇőand the military," communications undersecretary Claire Castro told ÔÇőa briefing.

item.title: Back to square one? Markets bet Iran war is over
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Global investors and the International Monetary Fund seem to agree the Iran war is likely over - bar the shouting. The energy market is less sure, and there could be an awful lot of shouting, but many asset prices have returned to square one, and markets now see the conflict's impact only at the margins.
The IMF's unenviable task of forecasting global economic growth during ÔÇőa Middle East war and energy shock left as many questions as answers, and a plethora of different scenarios , opens new tab . Take your pick.
The Fund's central conclusion was probably most powerful in what it didn't ÔÇîdo rather than what it did.
The IMF made no change to its global gross domestic product (GDP) growth forecast for 2027 compared with its last update in January - three months before the war. Yes, it clipped this year's expansion and there's a lower starting point as a result. But next year is essentially expected to develop much like the Fund saw it pre-war in January and even back in October - 3.2% world growth.
Even before those forecasts were released on Tuesday, many in the markets had effectively decided the same .
Wall Street stocks returned to ÔÇőpre-war levels of February 27 on Monday, completing a 565-point, near-10% round trip. The VIX "fear index" of implied volatility (.VIX) , opens new tab has subsided to its lowest since February.
Global stocks captured by the MSCI All Country World Index (.MIWD00000PUS) , opens new tab are not ÔÇőquite back there yet - but they're just 1% from the record highs set two months ago. The pivotal euro/dollar exchange rate is back to February levels too.
Much has been made in recent weeks of how little full-year corporate earnings forecasts have been affected by the oil shock. Flattered by tech and energy firm upgrades, aggregate 2026 earnings growth estimates have actually climbed 2-3 percentage points since ÔÇőthe war started.
Calendar 2027 growth forecasts are still cruising at 18% and 11% for U.S. and European blue chips, respectively.
Real or imagined, 12-month forward price/earnings valuations for the S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab and MSCI all-country cheapened more than 10% in the month ÔÇőthrough early April.
The temptation to screen out the war, bet on eventual de-escalation and focus on the year ahead has been hard to pass up.
That logic drove BlackRock - the world's largest asset manager - to switch back this week to an overweight position in U.S. stocks and emerging markets.
It's not as if nothing has changed, of course.
With global oil and gas still bound up in the ongoing Gulf hiatus, front-month crude futures are still up a third and natural gas and fertilizer prices remain elevated. Airlines fear shortages of jet fuel ahead.
Even ÔÇőthe IMF admits the longer the Middle East energy crunch persists, the more its "adverse scenarios" come into view.
Billionaire investor Ken Griffin warned on Tuesday of a "treacherous moment" and reckons closure of the Strait of Hormuz for 6-12 months will end in global recession.
But ÔÇőthe way oil futures are behaving, that's still not the market's best guess. Brent crude futures through this year and next show some normalization. Even though they are still 10-15% higher than February levels, ready-reckoners - quick, rule-of-thumb models - suggest that would only shave 0.2-0.3 percentage points off ÔÇîglobal GDP Ôüágrowth.
That's simply not enough for portfolios to go to ground - whatever you think of the wider inflationary or political fallout.
The interest rate and bond markets have not returned to pre-war levels - the lingering risk of resurgent inflation and the outside chance of central bank rate rises keep them there.
While that's still a guessing game that hinges on the length of the conflict too, 10-year Treasury borrowing rates are still some 30 basis points above February levels and Federal Reserve futures markets only see about a 30% chance of rate cuts resuming by year-end. Mortgage rates have barreled higher too and corporate bonds have been rattled by jitters in the private credit world.
Global asset managers polled by Bank ÔÇőof America this month clearly scaled back their early-year bullishness, ÔÇőwith sentiment gauges slipping back to last summer's Ôüáfunk. Inflation expectations have climbed too.
But chiming with oil futures, the weighted average of forecasts for year-end crude prices was $84 per barrel and "long oil" along with long global chip stocks were identified as the most crowded trades on the planet.
Less than 10% of funds surveyed expect recession ahead. While average cash levels stayed at 10-month highs last month, they ÔÇőremain well below prior extremes during last year's April tariff shock or the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
That's not exactly complacency - but neither is it a panicked herd.
Second-guessing ÔÇőthe conflict will remain a Ôüádaily obsession, even if an increasingly price-insensitive one. But market de-escalation has already begun. Only time will tell if that's premature.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Mike Dolan , a columnist for Reuters.)
Save the date: On April 23 at 1300 GMT/9 a.m. EDT, ROI columnists Mike Dolan and Jamie McGeever will join LSEG for a webinar discussion, "Markets Unpacked with Reuters Open Interest: Rethinking safe havens in uncertain times." Click here to sign up. , opens new tab

And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , or the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets ÔÇőand finance seven days a week.

item.title: Europe's summer flights at risk as Iran war exposes refining decline
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - European holidaymakers may need to rethink their summer travel plans as airlines brace for possible flight groundings amid a mounting fuel supply crunch triggered by the Iran war . This crisis marks another wake-up call for a region grappling with energy security in an era of heightened geopolitical tensions.
EuropeÔÇÖs oil and gas industry has shrunk dramatically ÔÇőover the past 25 years: North Sea production has dwindled and more than 30 refineries - 16% of refining capacity - have been shuttered across the continent.
The contraction reflects a combination ÔÇîof shrinking domestic demand, rising overseas competition and, increasingly over the past decade, a government-led drive to curb greenhouse gas emissions.
The upshot is that Europe has become heavily reliant on energy imports - a vulnerable underbelly painfully exposed twice in four years.
First came the abrupt loss of Russian natural gas supplies following MoscowÔÇÖs invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Then the closure of the critical Strait of Hormuz during the Iran war - now in its seventh week - has once again laid bare the risks of EuropeÔÇÖs growing exposure to international energy shocks.
Nowhere is that ÔÇővulnerability clearer than in aviation fuel.
Europe is more dependent on jet fuel imports than for any other transport fuel. The region consumed about 1.6 million barrels per day (bpd) last year, ÔÇőof which roughly 500,000 bpd were imported, according to the International Energy Agency. Around 75% of those imports came from the Middle East.
That dependence has left ÔüáEurope acutely exposed to the Gulf supply shock.
Refineries in Asia, which source about 60% of their crude imports from the Middle East, were forced to scale back operations by some 3 million bpd between February ÔÇőand April, according to the IEA.
That pullback triggered acute fuel shortages across Asia, prompting governments to respond with fuel-saving measures, subsidies and, in some cases, restrictions on fuel exports - including in China.
With Asian demand competing ÔÇőfor scarce barrels, benchmark European jet fuel prices surged to an all-time high of $1,800 a ton on March 18, before easing to around $1,450 this week. Jet fuel refining margins have also surged to record highs, exceeding $100 per barrel, more than five times their level a year ago, according to HiLo Analytics.
Europe has struggled to replace the lost Middle Eastern barrels. Jet fuel imports fell to roughly 437,000 bpd in March, down 13% from the 2025 average, and are set to slide further ÔÇőto just 275,000 bpd in April, according to analytics firm Kpler.
Importantly, around 125,000 bpd of AprilÔÇÖs imports are expected to arrive from the U.S. - a shift to long-haul sourcing that underscores just how stretched ÔÇőthe supply chain has become.
Regional stockpiles are also being depleted rapidly. Independently held jet fuel and kerosene stocks in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp refining and storage hub fell nearly 8% last week to 646,000 metric tons, the lowest level since March ÔÇî2023, according to ÔüáDutch consultancy Insights Global.
The last cargoes of jet fuel from the Middle East are now approaching Europe. That means finding replacement barrels will soon prove even more challenging ÔÇô and more expensive.
Industry group Airports Council International Europe (ACI) warned last week that the region could face a systemic jet fuel shortage within just three weeks.
With supplies critically tight, airlines and oil importers face two unpalatable options: bid up prices to compete with Asia for scarce barrels, or cut consumption.
Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab CEO Carsten Spohr warned on Tuesday that grounding aircraft "may be unavoidable" as shortages hit major airports.
Europe's biggest airline has drawn up contingency plans that include cutting capacity by up to 5%, potentially grounding 20-40 older, less fuel-efficient planes earmarked for early ÔÇőretirement.
Spohr said Lufthansa, which hedges fuel up ÔÇőto 24 months ahead and was 85% hedged Ôüáas of December 31, was well placed in the short term.
Not all carriers are so insulated . EasyJet (EZJ.L) , opens new tab has hedged the majority of its fuel needs for the coming months, but those positions begin to unwind by the end of summer, when the British budget carrier has warned ticket prices could rise.
Airlines for Europe (A4E) has requested ÔÇőthat the European Union introduce crisis response measures, including: EU-level monitoring of jet fuel supplies, a temporary suspension of the bloc's carbon market for ÔÇőaviation, and scrapping certain aviation taxes. ÔüáThe industry group's requests were outlined in a document seen by Reuters.
The crisis lays bare the strategic cost of allowing domestic energy infrastructure - from refineries to upstream oil and gas production - to wither without reliable alternatives in place.
Governments' options for short-term relief are limited, and the most obvious ones, such as temporarily easing carbon-related taxes on refiners and airlines, clash directly with climate commitments.
Over the longer term, governments could offer refiners incentives to produce low-carbon Ôüáfuels, expand sustainable ÔÇőaviation fuel production or deploy carbon-capture technologies - policies aimed at reconciling the dual challenge of boosting domestic capacity while cutting emissions.
For ÔÇőnow though, the outlook for European airlines - and summer travellers - is bleak.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Ron Bousso , a columnist for Reuters.)

item.title: Exclusive: China weighs curbs on exports of solar manufacturing equipment to US
item.description:
BEIJING/LOS ANGELES, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese officials have held initial talks with providers of equipment to make solar panels as they consider limiting exports of the most advanced technology to the United States, said five people with knowledge of the consultations.
Such ÔÇőa clampdown would risk investments by U.S. firms and set back a race for space-based computing, as China, estimated to make more than 80% of the world's ÔÇîsolar panel components, is also home to the top 10 suppliers of equipment to make solar cells.
No rule has been finalised, and the talks have not advanced to the stage of canvassing formal feedback from an industry grappling with severe overcapacity after years of aggressive expansion, two of the sources said.
China's commerce ministry and its state council, or cabinet, did not immediately respond to faxed requests for comment from Reuters.
If adopted, such a move could threaten plans by U.S. firms, such as Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab , to build new factories or expand existing ones in efforts to boost local production.
It would ÔÇőalso widen export controls in another area of technology where China has a lead, building on Beijing's move to control rare earth exports a year Ôüáago in response to U.S. tariffs.
The step comes at a time when the rivalry between China and the United States has spilled into the race to produce space-based computing powered by solar ÔÇőpanels, a focus for Tesla CEO Elon Musk.
Other U.S. tech companies such as Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab are investing in ground-based solar and energy storage systems, even as they count on similar orbital data centres ÔÇőto satisfy AI's growing demand for power.
Analysts who track China's solar industry and executives have braced for export controls, in part because concern is growing over efforts by Musk and others to boost solar panel production in the United States, reducing reliance on China.
Musk seeks to exploit China's solar downturn to acquire equipment and talent, Xu Xiaohua, chairman of Anhui Huasun Energy, told the Caijing business magazine ÔÇőthis year.
He called for greater efforts by Chinese companies to retain their lead in technology.
Huasun did not immediately respond to an emailed request for comment.
The prospect of China's curb comes amid preparations for a ÔÇősummit of leaders Xi Jinping and Donald Trump in the Chinese capital next month that both sides view as an opportunity to preserve more stable ties on trade matters.
Reuters reported last month that Tesla was looking ÔÇîto buy $2.9 Ôüábillion of equipment for making solar panels from Chinese suppliers such as Suzhou Maxwell Technologies (300751.SZ) , opens new tab , which was seeking export approval from the commerce ministry.
Musk has said solar power could supply all the electricity needs of the United States and Tesla has pushed a goal of making 100 gigawatts of solar manufacturing on American soil before 2028.
"Tesla succeeding in its solar self-sufficiency push could prove a nightmare for China's world-leading solar manufacturers," research firm Trivium China, focused on Chinese government policy, said in a note this month.
Not only would they lose a major potential customer, but they could ÔÇőface the emergence of a formidable new competitor ÔÇőat a time when they are already Ôüáunder enormous financial pressure, it added.
"Beijing won't sit idly by as its industrial champions inadvertently aid the industrial policies of rival countries."
Suzhou Maxwell Technologies was visited by officials after Reuters reported on Tesla's talks with Chinese suppliers, said three of the sources who sought anonymity because the talks with regulators ÔÇőare confidential.
Talks between regulators and Suzhou Maxwell Technologies focused on possible curbs on shipments to the United States, including high-end equipment needed to produce higher ÔÇőefficiency panels, a technology Ôüáknown as HJT, two of the sources said.
Suzhou Maxwell and Tesla did not immediately respond to emailed requests for comment.
Reuters could not determine how sweeping any curbs would be in terms of other export markets, when any licensing requirement could take effect or what products would be covered.
In 2025, China threatened licensing requirements for exports of a related technology, high-end batteries and materials for the energy storage Ôüásystems demanded ÔÇőby large-scale solar projects, but paused implementation until November this year.
Other Chinese solar producers have continued to negotiate and ship ÔÇősolar manufacturing equipment to the United States, including other firms bidding for the Tesla order, two people said.
HJT or heterojunction solar technology boosts power output by using a wafer of crystalline silicon sandwiched between very thin layers of silicon in ÔÇőthe cell, allowing more of the electrons generated by sunlight to be captured as electricity.

item.title: Russia pledges further oil supplies to Cuba after dispatching crude cargo
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia will continue helping fuel-hungry Cuba with crucial supplies of oil, Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov said on Wednesday, two weeks after Moscow sent a tanker with ÔÇőaround 700,000 barrels of crude to the Caribbean island.
Washington stopped oil ÔÇőexports to Cuba from its main ally Venezuela after capturing ÔüáVenezuelan President Nicolas Maduro on January 3, triggering acute fuel shortages ÔÇőacross the communist-ruled island of almost 11 million people.
President Donald Trump has ÔÇőthreatened punishing tariffs on countries sending crude to Cuba as he seeks to put pressure on the government. The U.S. later allowed the Russian oil delivery to Cuba, this ÔÇőyear's first by Moscow, for humanitarian reasons.
Another major supplier, Mexico, halted ÔÇőits shipments.
Lavrov, on a visit to China, said Russia will provide humanitarian aid to ÔÇîCuba, Ôüáits long-standing ally.
"We have dispatched the first tanker with 100,000 tons (700,000 barrels) of oil for Cuba. Of course, this will probably last for a couple of months - I'm not a specialist," he told a briefing at ÔÇőthe end of the ÔÇőtwo-day visit.
"But ÔüáI have no doubt that we will continue providing such assistance, and that (China) will, of course, continue to ÔÇőtake part in this cooperation as well," added Lavrov, ÔÇőwithout referring Ôüáto the issue of U.S. permission or not for future deliveries.
Cuba produces less than a third of the oil it requires.
Though it cleared the recent ÔüáRussian ÔÇődelivery, the Trump administration said it would ÔÇőreview further oil shipments to Cuba on a "case-by-case" basis.
Lavrov said he hoped the U.S. will not ÔÇőreturn to times of "colonial wars."

item.title: Malaysia's Petronas says fuel supply secured at stations until June-end
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Malaysia's ÔÇőstate oil firm Petronas (IPO-PETO.KL) , opens new tab ÔÇîsaid on Wednesday that fuel supply at all its ÔÇőstations is secured until ÔÇőthe end of June.
The company Ôüásaid it is ÔÇőactively managing its supply chain ÔÇőto ensure sufficient stock across its network amid the global ÔÇőenergy crisis due to ÔÇőthe Middle East war.
Around 50% ÔÇîof ÔüáMalaysia's fuel needs are being supplied by Petronas Dagangan (PETR.KL) , opens new tab , the parent firm ÔÇősaid.
ÔÇőMalaysia has pledged Ôüáto boost the use of biodiesel ÔÇőto deal with ÔÇőthe Ôüásupply constraints as its economy comes under increasing strain Ôüáfrom ÔÇőthe Middle ÔÇőEast crisis.

item.title: Philippines grants banks┬áflexibility to extend loan payments amid energy crisis
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - The Philippines will allow banks to give borrowers more time to settle ÔÇőtheir loans as part of a package ÔÇîof relief measures designed to support consumers and businesses hit by the energy crisis, its central bank said on ÔÇőWednesday.
This includes grace periods of up ÔÇőto six months, and up to one Ôüáyear for agricultural loans, subject to ÔÇőbank assessments, according to a resolution approved last ÔÇőweek by the Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas (BSP).
Banks may also temporarily avoid classifying these loans as past due ÔÇőor non-performing.
"BSP-supervised financial institutions are expected to ÔÇőexercise prudent management in availing these measures and to ÔÇîensure Ôüárelief is only extended to borrowers whose repayment capacity has been materially affected by the energy emergency," the BSP said in ÔÇőa statement.
The ÔÇőBSP also Ôüáurged banks to temporarily suspend fees for online transactions, to ÔÇőease costs for consumers and businesses.
On ÔÇőMonday, ÔüáPhilippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr suspended excise taxes on kerosene and liquefied petroleum gas to help Ôüáthe ÔÇőpublic cope with soaring ÔÇőfuel prices driven by the conflict in the Middle ÔÇőEast.

item.title: Pope Leo, attacked again by Trump, flies to Cameroon on Africa tour
item.description:
ABOARD THE PAPAL FLIGHT, April 15 (Reuters) - Pope Leo set off for Cameroon on Wednesday, ÔÇîwhere he is expected to appeal for peace in the simmering conflict in the country's English-speaking regions on the second leg of an ambitious 10-day tour of four African countries.
Leo, who was attacked again , opens new tab overnight by U.S. President Donald Trump over ÔÇőthe pope's opposition to the Iran war , will arrive from Algeria, where he blasted violations of international ÔÇőlaw by "neocolonial" world powers.
Leo left Algiers on Wednesday morning and was expected to Ôüáland in Yaounde, Cameroon's capital, around 3:20 p.m. (1420 GMT), ahead of a meeting with President ÔÇőPaul Biya and an address to national leaders.
Cameroon is a former German colony that was divided by ÔÇőBritain and France after World War One. Over the last decade, thousands have died in violence between government forces and separatist groups in its two anglophone regions.
A separatist alliance said on Monday it would observe a three-day "safe travel ÔÇőpassage" to allow civilians and visitors to move freely during the pope's visit.
Leo, originally from Chicago, ÔÇőwas chosen to lead the 1.4 billion-member Catholic Church last May after the death of Pope Francis .
He kept a relatively ÔÇîlow Ôüáprofile in his first 10 months, but in recent weeks has become outspoken on a range of issues, and a firm critic of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran.
Trump called the pontiff " terrible " on social media on Sunday, and doubled down on Tuesday night , despite widespread ÔÇőbacklash from U.S. Christians across ÔÇőthe political spectrum.
The Ôüápope told Reuters on Monday that he planned to keep criticizing the war, regardless of Trump's comments.
Leo, aged 70, relatively young for a pope and in ÔÇőgood health, is undertaking one of the most complicated tours arranged for ÔÇőa pontiff Ôüáin decades.
He is traversing nearly 18,000 km (11,185 miles) over 18 flights to 11 cities and towns and will also visit Angola and Equatorial Guinea.
The pope will travel on Thursday to Bamenda, Cameroon's largest English-speaking Ôüácity, holding ÔÇőMass and a "meeting for peace" in a cathedral.
The biggest event ÔÇőof Leo's tour will likely come in Cameroon on Friday, when the Vatican said some 600,000 are expected for a ÔÇőMass in the coastal city of Douala.

item.title: Exclusive: China moves to block entrance to disputed South China Sea shoal, images show
item.description:
HONG KONG/MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - China is employing ships and a barrier to tighten control of the entrance to the Scarborough Shoal in the South China Sea amid roiling ÔÇőtension with the Philippines over the disputed feature, satellite imagery obtained by Reuters shows.
Scarborough is one of Asia's most hotly disputed maritime sites, where ÔÇîsome diplomats and analysts fear long-running frictions and confrontations could degenerate into armed conflict.
The presence of four fishing boats, a Chinese naval or coast guard ship and a new floating barrier comes as the Philippines sends its own coast guard and fisheries vessels to support its fishermen frequently driven away by larger Chinese patrols.
Photographs taken on April 10 and 11 show the fishing boats anchored along ÔÇőthe entrance to the shoal, in addition to a floating barrier stretching across it in the April 11 image.
Satellite image provider Vantor, formerly Maxar Technologies, ÔÇősaid a probable Chinese naval or coast guard patrol vessel can be glimpsed just outside the entrance on April 10.
China's defence ministry Ôüádid not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment on the deployment to the entrance to the shoal or its timing.
The traditionally rich fishing ÔÇőground of the Scarborough Shoal lies entirely within the Philippines' exclusive economic zone, but China also claims it as part of its territory.
Last year, China approved establishment of a ÔÇőnational nature reserve there, alarming Philippine security officials, who called the move a "clear pretext for occupation".
Jay Tarriela, a spokesperson for the Philippine coast guard, told Reuters on Wednesday the Chinese government had installed a 352-m (1,150-ft)floating barrier at the entrance on April 10 and April 11.
"Six Chinese maritime militia vessels were observed within the shoal, while three others were spotted outside, seemingly obstructing the entrance to BDM," ÔÇőhe said.
He was referring to the shoal by its Philippine name of Bajo de Masinloc, while China calls it Huangyan Island.
While the Philippines coast guard has cut barriers ÔÇőin the past, Tarriela said the Chinese side appear to have removed the latest one since the weekend, but the Philippine Navy says its patrols continue.
"According to our assessment in the ÔÇîpast, they Ôüáconsistently exhibit suspicion whenever they monitor a group of Filipino fishing boats," Tarriela added.
Ten Chinese coast guard vessels were sighted at the shoal from April 5 to April 12, Philippine Navy spokesperson Roy Trinidad said on Tuesday.
Despite the competing claims, sovereignty has never been established and the shoal is effectively under Beijing's control even if Philippine boats still try to operate there.
In January, the militaries of the Philippines and the United States sailed together at the shoal in the 11th such drill by the ÔÇőtreaty allies.
Military engagements between them have soared ÔÇőunder Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., Ôüáwho has pivoted closer to Washington in response to China's growing presence in the busy waterway of the South China Sea.
Thousands of troops from both countries are set to begin large-scale exercises across the Philippine archipelago this month, including in Zambales, whose coast is ÔÇőabout 120 nautical miles from the Scarborough Shoal.
Diplomats say the drills and broader tensions are being closely watched amid fears ÔÇőthat China could take advantage Ôüáof perceptions that the U.S. is distracted by the Iran conflict and its effort to re-open the vital Straits of Hormuz waterway.
China has kept a deployment of coast guard and fishing trawlers at the shoal since seizing it in 2012 after a standoff with the Philippines.
Manila has said Chinese maritime militia operate some trawlers at the shoal and other disputed Ôüáareas of ÔÇőthe South China Sea, but Beijing has never acknowledged this.
A landmark 2016 ruling on various South China ÔÇőSea issues by the Permanent Court of Arbitration backed Manila, but establishing sovereignty over Scarborough Shoal was outside its scope.
The court said Beijing's blockade there violated international law as it was a traditional fishing ground ÔÇőfor several countries, including China, the Philippines and Vietnam.

item.title: Hungary election winner Magyar expects to form government by mid-May
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Election winner Peter Magyar said ÔÇőon Wednesday that ÔÇîPresident Tamas Sulyok would ask him at ÔÇőthe inaugural session ÔÇőof parliament to form ÔüáHungary's next government, ÔÇőwhich could be sworn ÔÇőin by the middle of next month or ÔÇősomewhat sooner.
"The President ÔÇőhas informed me that he ÔÇîwill Ôüáask me at the inaugural session of the new parliament ÔÇőto ÔÇőbe ÔüáPrime Minister and form a government, ÔÇőas the ÔÇőleader Ôüáof the party that got the most Ôüávotes," ÔÇőMagyar said ÔÇőafter meeting Sulyok.

item.title: Hungary's election winner Magyar to meet president, calls on him to resign
item.description:
BUDAPEST, April 15 (Reuters) - Hungary's election ÔÇőwinner Peter Magyar, ÔÇîleader of the TISZA ÔÇőparty which ÔÇőwon a landslide Ôüávictory in ÔÇőSunday's election, ÔÇőarrived for a meeting with President ÔÇőTamas Sulyok ÔÇőon Wednesday.
As Magyar ÔÇîwas Ôüágoing into the meeting, speaking to reporters ÔÇőhe ÔÇőreiterated Ôüáthat Sulyok must ÔÇőleave his ÔÇőrole Ôüáafter a TISZA Ôüágovernment ÔÇőtakes office.

item.title: China's Xi calls for strategic clarity, political security with Vietnam
item.description:
BEIJING, April 15 (Reuters) - Chinese President Xi Jinping called for ÔÇőChina and Vietnam to ÔÇîmaintain a high degree of strategic clarity and ÔÇőprioritise safeguarding political security ÔÇőduring a meeting with Ôüáhis Vietnamese counterpart on ÔÇőWednesday.
Vietnam's top leader, To ÔÇőLam, is in China until Friday in his first overseas ÔÇőtrip since being ÔÇőelected state president last week, a ÔÇîsign Ôüáof warming ties between the two Communist neighbours.
The countries have grown closer ÔÇőin ÔÇőrecent Ôüáyears, cooperating in railways, technology and ÔÇőother sectors, even ÔÇőas Ôüáthey remain at odds over maritime boundaries in Ôüáthe ÔÇőSouth China Sea.

item.title: Portugal, unlike Spain, rejects separate European army
item.description:
LISBON, April 15 (Reuters) - Portugal is against the creation of a separate European army and instead backs strengthening and modernising its armed forces within the ÔÇőU.S.-led NATO alliance, the country's defence minister said, contrasting with neighbouring ÔÇîSpain's position.
Nuno Melo said late on Tuesday that Portugal was a founding NATO member which valued the United States, describing Washington as a fundamental transatlantic partner.
"We're not in favour ÔÇőof a single European army," he told a parliamentary committee, adding that ÔÇőPortugal needed to invest in its armed forces to ensure that Ôüáthey were able to fulfil their assigned missions within NATO.
Amid doubts over ÔÇőU.S. President Donald Trump's commitment to Europe's security, Spain has urged the European Union ÔÇőto move towards creating a separate joint army as a deterrent.
NATO chief Mark Rutte has dismissed calls for a European army, arguing it would cost member states far more than the ÔÇő5% of gross domestic product that NATO countries - except Spain - have agreed to ÔÇőspend on defence and related investments by 2035. Madrid said it could meet its commitments ÔÇîby Ôüáspending 2.1% of GDP .
Melo said that Portugal increased its defence spending, under NATO criteria, to 6.12 billion euros ($7.22 billion) - or 2% of GDP - in 2025, four years ahead of the original schedule. In 2024, spending was around 4.5 billion euros, ÔÇőor 1.58% of GDP.
Spain ÔÇőalso reached its Ôüátarget of 2% of GDP last year, spending 33.5 billion euros - a 44.5% increase from 2024.
Portugal has applied for ÔÇő5.8 billion euros in lowÔÇĹcost EU loans to strengthen its ÔÇőarmed forces, ÔüáMelo said. The new equipment including frigates, armoured vehicles, satellites and drones was set to be delivered by 2030, "if all goes well", he added.
The programme, known as ÔüáSecurity ÔÇőAction for Europe (SAFE), is a 150 billion euro ÔÇőEU initiative aimed at reinforcing defence capabilities, closing critical gaps and jointly procuring equipment to counter potential ÔÇőthreats to the bloc.
($1 = 0.8477 euros)

item.title: Japan offers $10 billion support to help Asian neighbours secure oil
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japan said on Wednesday it would establish a financial framework worth about $10 billion to help Asian countries procure energy resources and bolster their stockpiles as Middle East tensions drive prices higher and disrupt supply chains.
The support, aimed at preventing knock-on effects on Japan's ÔÇőown supply chains, would be channelled mainly through state-backed financial institutions such as Japan Bank for International ÔÇőCooperation (JBIC) and Nippon Export and Investment Insurance (NEXI).
Announcing the plan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said Ôüáthe support would be equivalent to as much as 1.2 billion barrels of oil, or about one year's ÔÇőworth of crude oil imports by the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN). She was speaking after a meeting of ÔÇőthe "AZEC Plus" under the Japan-led initiative, Asia Zero-Emission Community (AZEC).
Leaders of the Philippines, Malaysia, Singapore, Thailand and Vietnam attended the meeting, among others.
"We are closely interconnected with Asian countries through supply chains and other channels, and we are mutually dependent,ÔÇŁ Takaichi told reporters after the ÔÇőtalks, citing examples such as dialysis equipment and surgical drains.
"Supporting Asian countries' supply chains would in turn bolster ÔÇőJapanÔÇÖs own economy."
The plan includes credit provision for local companies to procure alternative resources such as U.S. crude oil, as well ÔÇîas financing Ôüáand loans for companies and governments that are part of Japan's supply chain.
Support to help diversify energy sources and boost partner countries' stockpiles through storage tank construction is also included.
Compared with Japan, Southeast Asian countries hold smaller oil stockpiles, leaving supplies of crude and petroleum products such as naphtha ÔÇô a key feedstock for plastics ÔÇô increasingly tight. A ÔÇődisruption in Southeast Asian production ÔÇőhas fuelled anxiety among ÔüáJapanese healthcare providers that rely on Asia for critical supplies such as containers, tubes and gloves.
About 90% of crude oil passing through the Strait of Hormuz is destined ÔÇőfor Asia, according to Japan's Agency for Natural Resources and Energy.
Tokyo has said it ÔÇőhas secured some Ôüáfour months' worth of naphtha for domestic use, but a growing number of manufacturers have flagged a disruption to deliveries in recent days. The government also said on Wednesday it plans to release another 36 million barrels from its national Ôüáoil reserves ÔÇőfrom early May.
Asked whether countries had requested access to Japan's oil ÔÇőreserves following the AZEC Plus meeting, Takaichi declined to comment but stressed the agreement did not involve a release of its stockpile and would ÔÇőnot affect domestic supply.

item.title: Sanctioned China tanker turns back to Strait of Hormuz, day after Gulf exit
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S.-sanctioned tanker Rich Starry made ÔÇőits way back to the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday after exiting the Gulf the day before, shipping data ÔÇîshowed, failing to break through a U.S. blockade on vessels calling at Iranian ports.
U.S. President Donald Trump announced the blockade on Sunday after weekend peace talks in Islamabad between the U.S. and Iran failed to reach a deal .
"During the first 24 hours, no ships made it past the U.S. blockade," the U.S. Central Command ÔÇősaid , opens new tab on X, adding that six vessels complied with directions from U.S. forces to turn around to re-enter an Iranian port.
The ÔÇőChinese-owned tanker was among at least eight ships crossing the waterway on Tuesday, the first day of the ÔüáU.S. blockade.
A U.S. destroyer stopped two oil tankers attempting to leave the Iranian port of Chabahar on the Gulf of Oman on ÔÇőTuesday, a U.S. official said.
The Rich Starry and its owner, Shanghai Xuanrun Shipping Co, were placed under U.S. sanctions for dealing ÔÇőwith Iran. The company could not be reached for a comment.
The Rich Starry is a medium-range tanker carrying about 250,000 barrels of methanol that was loaded at the port of Hamriyah in the United Arab Emirates, Kpler data showed. It is currently anchored off Iran.
The blockade has created even further uncertainty for shippers, ÔÇőoil companies and war risk insurers. Traffic remains at only a fraction of the 130-plus daily crossings before the U.S. and Israel's ÔÇőwar on Iran began on February 28, industry sources said on Tuesday.
There have been no Iranian tankers carrying crude for export and passing through the ÔÇîstrait since Ôüáthe U.S. blockade began, according to Kpler and LSEG data.
The OPEC producer has unused onshore crude storage capacity of about 90 million barrels which can sustain its current output of 3.5 million barrels per day (bpd) for about two months if exports are halted, consultancy FGENexant said in a note.
A production cut of around 500,000 bpd to 3 million bpd would extend this window to about three months, it added.
Another U.S.-sanctioned vessel, the ÔÇőVery Large Crude Carrier Alicia Ôüáthat has a record of carrying Iranian oil since 2023, is entering the Gulf via the strait on Wednesday, Kpler and LSEG data showed. The empty tanker, capable of carrying 2 million barrels of ÔÇőoil, is heading to Iraq to load a cargo on Thursday, Kpler data showed.
Separately, the Malta-flagged ÔÇőVLCC Agios Fanourios I Ôüáentered the Gulf via the strait on Wednesday in a second attempt to transit, according to the data. The tanker was among several vessels that tried to enter the Gulf on Sunday during the U.S.-Iran ceasefire deal.
It is heading to Iraq to load Basra crude for Vietnam's Nghi Son Ôüárefinery, according ÔÇőto the data and trade sources.
Eastern Mediterranean Maritime, which manages the Agios Fanourios I, ÔÇőand Nghi Son Refinery and Petrochemical did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

item.title: Philippines' Marcos urges ASEAN to activate fuel-sharing pact
item.description:
MANILA, April 15 (Reuters) - Philippine President Ferdinand Marcos Jr on Wednesday called for the immediate activation and ÔÇőtesting of the ASEAN Petroleum Security ÔÇîAgreement (APSA), saying it should be implemented while there is an oil crisis due to the Middle East conflict.
Speaking ÔÇőat the Asia Zero Emission Community (AZEC) ÔÇőPlus online summit hosted by Japan, Marcos Ôüásaid the closure of shipping routes had ÔÇőexposed the vulnerability of Asian economies that are ÔÇőheavily dependent on imported petroleum.

item.title: Japan naphtha-dependent firms flag supply issues despite government assurances
item.description:
TOKYO, April 15 (Reuters) - Japanese companies whose output is dependent on naphtha or naphtha-based products are halting orders or slashing production, highlighting ÔÇőa disconnect between official assurances of sufficient supply and realities on the ground.
Over the past week, more than a dozen firms ÔÇîincluding Toto (5332.T) , opens new tab and Asahi Kasei (3407.T) , opens new tab have flagged disruption in delivery or hiked prices, citing concern over the procurement of materials such as adhesives made from naphtha - an oil derivative chiefly used as a solvent or diluent.
Manufacturers of thinner-related products such as Kansai Paint (4613.T) , opens new tab , which by law can only hold limited stock of the hazardous material, have also adjusted delivery arrangements and ÔÇőraised prices, clouding the outlook downstream for goods and services as varied as plastic toys and housing construction.
Just 2.7% of companies are able ÔÇőto obtain thinner as usual, showed a survey conducted last week by the Japan Painting Contractors Association.
The picture amounts Ôüáto a headache for the government, which has sought to prevent disruption by repeating its message that Japan has enough naphtha for the next four months.
The ÔÇőgovernment has said it is working to secure supplies outside the Middle East, from which Japan sourced 40% of its naphtha before the U.S. and Israel attacked ÔÇőIran on February 28. It has also set up consultation services to help businesses with procurement before they opt for changes in operations.
Even so, Toto this week suspended the acceptance of orders for modular bathroom units requiring adhesives that rely on solvents containing naphtha. Rivals Lixil (5938.T) , opens new tab , Panasonic (6752.T) , opens new tab and Cleanup (7955.T) , opens new tab have similarly flagged delivery impact.
Countries including South ÔÇőKorea , Thailand and Bangladesh have responded to disruption in the supply of Middle East oil products by calling on the public to take conservation measures, such as ÔÇőby driving less or even taking shorter showers.
Asked why Japan has not adopted a similar approach, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said she did not want to slow economic activity. She ÔÇîhas tasked Ôüáindustry minister Ryosei Akazawa with ensuring a steady supply of oil-related products.
On Tuesday, Akazawa said the "blockage" was in the middle of the supply chain, with thinner wholesalers, for example, halving supply to customers in April upon being told that shipments would be uncertain in May.
"For Prime Minister Takaichi, the economy comes first. She doesn't say anything that would be difficult for citizens," a government official involved in economic policy told Reuters, declining to be identified because of ÔÇőthe sensitivity of the matter. "This is the ÔÇőadministration's policy."
Another government official said Ôüáthe administration and industry ministry are afraid of consumer panic reminiscent of the hoarding of toilet paper and other goods during the 1970s oil crisis.
"We should really be calling on people to save energy now but the prime minister's ÔÇőoffice has put a stop to that," said the second official on condition of anonymity. "We've already said we ÔÇőhave enough supply so Ôüáif we were to ask for conservation measures, we might invite criticism, and that's the fear."
Shares of naphtha-dependent companies including Toto, Kansai Paint and Mitsubishi Chemical (4188.T) , opens new tab are underperforming Tokyo's benchmark Nikkei share price average (.N225) , opens new tab , which has now recouped most of the loss suffered since the start of Middle East conflict triggered general risk-off sentiment.
"Even Ôüáif producers ÔÇőcould ramp up output regardless of cost ... they risk being stuck with high-priced inventory if ÔÇőprices eventually drop," said Tomoichiro Kubota, senior market analyst at Matsui Securities.
"The Takaichi administration prioritises economic sentiment. While it has announced support for specific items, such an approach is like a game ÔÇőof whack-a-mole."

item.title: South Korea says secures 273 mln barrels of crude via routes outside Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
SEOUL, April 15 (Reuters) - South Korea has secured 273 ÔÇîmillion barrels of crude oil from the Middle East and Kazakhstan through the end of the year, with supplies routed outside the Strait of Hormuz, presidential chief of staff Kang Hoon-sik said on Wednesday.
Asia's fourth-largest economy has also secured 2.1 million metric tons of naphtha over the same ÔÇőperiod, Kang said at a press briefing following his visit as a special presidential envoy to Kazakhstan, Oman, Saudi ÔÇőArabia and Qatar over the past week.
"In particular, the crude oil and naphtha secured this time Ôüáwill be sourced through alternative supply routes unrelated to closure of the Strait of Hormuz, and will therefore make a ÔÇődirect and tangible contribution to stabilising domestic supply," Kang said.
Saudi Arabia had agreed to ship about 50 million barrels of crude oil ÔÇőalready allocated to South Korean companies, using alternative ports near the Red Sea in April and May, Kang said.
Riyadh had also pledged to prioritise South Korean companies in allocating and shipping 200 million barrels of crude oil between June and the end of the year, and promised to ÔÇősupply as much naphtha as possible through year-end, including 500,000 tons requested by South Korea's government, he said.
Kang said Kazakhstan would ÔÇősupply 18 million barrels of crude oil, while Oman has promised 5 million barrels of crude oil and 1.6 million tons of naphtha.
He ÔÇîsaid the Ôüásecured crude oil would be sufficient to power the economy for more than three months under normal conditions based on last yearÔÇÖs usage, while the naphtha volumes were equivalent to about one month of imports.
Kang said the oil and naphtha would be sourced from alternative supply routes not affected by a potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
He described his trip as driven by ÔÇőthe urgent need to secure ÔÇőkey energy supplies amid what Ôüáhe called an economic emergency triggered by the conflict in the Middle East.
South Korea relied on the Strait of Hormuz for 61% of its crude oil imports and 54% of its ÔÇőnaphtha imports last year, Kang said, adding the government could not afford to wait passively ÔÇőfor the regional Ôüásituation to improve.
President Lee Jae Myung conveyed deep concern over the prolonged Middle East conflict in letters sent to the leaders of the countries visited, expressing solidarity and calling for joint efforts to address the energy security crisis, Kang said.
South Korea also held discussions with Ôüáoil producers ÔÇőincluding Saudi Arabia and Oman on cooperation in areas such as constructing ÔÇőbypass pipelines and building oil storage facilities outside the Strait of Hormuz to mitigate risks from a potential blockade.
With additional funding allocated to expand domestic storage ÔÇőfacilities, Kang said joint stockpiling with major oil producers could be expanded, helping secure stable supplies.

item.title: Nigeria orders probe and defends deadly airstrike in insurgent-held Jilli
item.description:
ABUJA, April 15 (Reuters) - Nigeria has ordered ÔÇîa full and independent investigation into a military airstrike on a market that left at least 200 people feared dead , while defending the operation that it said targeted Islamist ÔÇőmilitants based in an enclave controlled by insurgents.
Saturday's airstrike is the ÔÇőlatest to have caused heavy civilian casualties in northeast Nigeria, where Ôüábattlelines are blurred.
As Islamist militants control territories, markets and supply routes, while ÔÇőcivilians move through restricted areas for survival, rights groups have warned strikes ÔÇőon contested spaces carry a high risk of killing bystanders.
Information Minister Mohammed Idris expressed regret for civilian casualties but said the Air Force hit "confirmed terrorist targets" in Jilli village, ÔÇőa high-risk no-go zone in Gubio district that has for years served ÔÇőas a logistics hub for Boko Haram and Islamic State offshoot, ISWAP, in the ÔÇîregion.
"The ÔüáFederal Government reiterates that this was a deliberate, intelligence-led operation, not an indiscriminate attack, conducted in one of the most active insurgent corridors in the northeast," Idris said in a statement late on Tuesday.
He said the investigation ÔÇőwould look into the ÔÇőoperation's planning and Ôüáexecution.
Authorities said Jilli, known locally as the "terrorists' market", and the nearby Gazabure market were shut about five years ÔÇőago after militants took control, with intelligence showing the ÔÇőarea was Ôüáused to collect levies, buy supplies, and plan attacks, including assaults on April 9 in Ngamdu and Benisheikh that killed a general.
Militants have waged a 17-year insurgency Ôüáseeking ÔÇőto carve out an Islamic state in northeast ÔÇőNigeria, killing thousands and displacing at least 2 million people, aid groups say, despite major ÔÇőmilitary campaigns.

item.title: Tanzania investigates death of American social media influencer
item.description:
DAR ES SALAAM, April 15 (Reuters) - Police in Tanzania said they are questioning the fianc├ę of U.S. social ÔÇőmedia influencer Ashly Robinson, who died after ÔÇîan alleged suicide attempt while the couple were on vacation in Zanzibar, a tourist hub off the east ÔÇőAfrican country's mainland.
Police have confiscated the passport of ÔÇőher partner, Joseph Isaac McCann, 45, also Ôüáa U.S. citizen, they said in a statement ÔÇőissued late on Tuesday.
Robinson, 31, who is also ÔÇőknown by her social media name Ashlee Jenae, and McCann arrived in Zanzibar on April 4 and booked into a ÔÇőhotel.
They later moved to a second hotel ÔÇőwhere they "appeared to be having domestic conflicts," police said, citing ÔÇîhotel Ôüástaff. Management subsequently separated the two into different rooms.
"According to reports, she attempted to hang herself using a clothing belt tied inside a wardrobe in ÔÇőRoom 25 ÔÇőof the Ôüáhotel," the police statement said. She died in hospital on April 9 around ÔÇő9 p.m. local time while receiving ÔÇőtreatment.
"Investigations into Ôüáthe matter are ongoing, pending professional medical reports and forensic examination from doctors," police said. McCann Ôüáhas ÔÇőnot been charged. Reuters was not ÔÇőimmediately able to contact his lawyer for comment.

item.title: Britain calls for international effort to stop arms flow to Sudan at Berlin conference
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The international community has failed in Sudan, British ÔÇőForeign Minister Yvette Cooper ÔÇîsaid on the sidelines of a Berlin conference on the ÔÇőwar in the ÔÇőcountry, as she called for Ôüáan international concerted effort ÔÇőto stop the arms flow.
"Countries ÔÇőfrom across the world are coming together here in Berlin to ÔÇődiscuss the way, frankly, ÔÇőthe international community has failed the ÔÇîpeople Ôüáof Sudan," Cooper said on Wednesday.
"We need to ensure that every possible pressure ÔÇőis put ÔÇőon Ôüáthose warring parties to reach the urgent ÔÇőceasefire that we ÔÇődesperately Ôüáneed to see," she said, as she reiterated the Ôüáneed ÔÇőfor humanitarian support.

item.title: Serbia's president tells military to create units armed with attack drones
item.description:
BELGRADE, April 15 (Reuters) - The Serbian army should create units with attack drones, President Aleksandar Vucic said on Wednesday, as the Balkan country seeks to modernise its ÔÇőarmed forces from its obsolete Soviet-era technology.
Belgrade has already bought modern ÔÇîweapons and equipment from Europe, Israel and China and it wants to adapt its military strategy to conform with modern warfare.
Serbia is military neutral and is balancing a partnership with ÔÇőNATO and aspirations to join the European Union with its ties ÔÇőwith Russia and China.
After meeting top military commanders in Belgrade, Vucic, Ôüáthe supreme commander of the army, said he had proposed the formation of ÔÇőunits equipped with long-range attack drones and drones that hover over a strike ÔÇőarea before flying into targets.
"In addition to the increased production of drones, which I expect to explode this year ... we will significantly devote ourselves to the ... digitisation of our army," ÔÇőhe said.
"I think that in this regard we will be one of the ÔÇőarmies with the most pronounced interoperability and the greatest capabilities, not only in our region, ÔÇîbut Ôüáalso wider than that."
Serbia maintains a 22,500-strong army and in 2026 it allocated 3.3% of its GDP for defence expenditure.
Serbia's rearmament has triggered criticism from Croatia, Bosnia and Kosovo, its neighbours and wartime foes from the 1990s, who see it ÔÇőas a threat ÔÇőto regional stability. ÔüáBelgrade denies that.
Vucic's comments come after the announcement of the creation of a joint Serbian-Israeli drone manufacturing plant a day earlier.
In ÔÇőearly 2025, Serbia also bought Israel's PULS artillery systems ÔÇőand Hermes Ôüádrones manufactured by Elbit Systems (ESLT.TA) , opens new tab for $335 million and in August 2025 it also purchased long-range missiles, drones and electronic warfare equipment for around $1.6 billion.
It has bought cargo aircraft Ôüáand ÔÇőhelicopters from Airbus (AIR.PA) , opens new tab and Chinese missiles and drones.
Belgrade ÔÇőalso purchased Rafale jet fighters manufactured by France's Dassault (AM.PA) , opens new tab for 2.7 billion euros ($3.18 billion), to replace ÔÇőits ageing Soviet-made MiG-29 planes.
($1 = 0.8484 euros)

item.title: Trump adviser Sebastian Gorka seeks top counterterrorism job, WaPo reports
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump's security aide ÔÇőSebastian Gorka is ÔÇîseeking to become the next head of the ÔÇőNational Counterterrorism Center, ÔÇőthe Washington Post reported Ôüáon Wednesday, nearly ÔÇőa month after the ÔÇőcenter's previous head quit due to differences over the war ÔÇőin Iran.
Joe Kent, ÔÇőwho previously headed the center, ÔÇîhad said Ôüáwhile resigning last month that Iran posed no imminent threat ÔÇőto ÔÇőthe ÔüáUnited States.
The U.S. National Counterterrorism Center ÔÇőand the White ÔÇőHouse Ôüádid not immediately respond to Reuters' requests Ôüáfor ÔÇőcomment on ÔÇőthe Washington Post report.

item.title: Swedish power plant targeted by pro-Russian group in 2025, government says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, April 15 (Reuters) - A pro-Russian cyber group tried to disrupt operations at a Swedish thermal power plant last year, ÔÇőthe Swedish government said on Wednesday, adding that ÔÇîRussian hybrid attacks had become more frequent and serious.
Carl-Oskar Bohlin, minister for civil defence, said the group had targeted a power ÔÇőplant in western Sweden during spring of 2025, ÔÇőbut that the attack had been unsuccessful. Bohlin Ôüádid not name the plant.
"The Security Police handled ÔÇőthe case and were able to identify the actor behind ÔÇőit, who had connections to Russian intelligence and security services," Bohlin told a news conference.
"Fortunately, no serious consequences occurred due to a ÔÇőbuilt-in protection mechanism," the minister said.
The Russian embassy ÔÇőin Stockholm did not immediately reply to a request for comment but ÔÇîMoscow Ôüáregularly denies responsibility for malicious cyber activity in Europe.
A Security Police spokesperson said the agency no longer had an open investigation into the matter.
Bohlin compared the incident ÔÇőwith similar attacks in ÔÇőPoland on Ôüáenergy infrastructure, which Polish officials have blamed on Russian spy agencies .
He said Russian hybrid attacks ÔÇőhad become more frequent since Russia's invasion ÔÇőof ÔüáUkraine and recently also more dangerous.
"We have seen for some time now that pro-Russian individuals and groups that have Ôüápreviously carried ÔÇőout denial-of-service attacks are attempting ÔÇőto carry out destructive cyberattacks against businesses in Europe, also against Swedish ÔÇőtargets," he said.

item.title: Back to business
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-at-Large, Finance and Markets
Global stock markets are largely back near pre-war levels and Wall Street is once again flirting with record highs, as the mood music from the ÔÇőMiddle East and Washington turns positive on hopes of more peace talks .
Real or imagined, that narrative is capping crude prices, allowing investors to ÔÇîrefocus on earnings and economic fundamentals.
IÔÇÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column , where I dig into markets' apparent belief that the war is likely over, bar the shouting.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast , where I discuss corporate earnings and the IMF's latest global growth forecasts.
Finally, don't forget to mark April 23 in ÔÇőyour calendar, when I'll be joining my ROI colleague Jamie McGeever for a timely webinar discussion on rethinking safe-haven assets in uncertain times. Sign up ÔÇőhere , opens new tab .
Brent and WTI crude were still comfortably below $100 a barrel heading into Wednesday, trading at around $96/bbl and $92/bbl, respectively. That Ôüáfollowed comments from President Trump on Tuesday that talks with Iran could resume in days , even as the U.S. military said it had halted IranÔÇÖs maritime trade as ÔÇőpart of its naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.
This helped fuel another Wall Street rally on Tuesday. The Nasdaq jumped 2%, while the S&P 500 rose 1% ÔÇőto close just shy of its record high. Asian equities followed suit on Wednesday, with JapanÔÇÖs Nikkei up 0.9% and South KoreaÔÇÖs KOSPI surging 3%. European stocks traded flat , as did U.S. futures before the bell.
In a sign that the recovery hasn't only been on Wall Street, the MSCI all-country index, excluding U.S. stocks, hit its best levels since March 2 on Tuesday. ÔÇőElsewhere, the VIX volatility index returned to its February levels and the dollar continued to give back its safe-haven gains, hovering near its lowest point since the ÔÇőwar.
The optimism at large looks to be justified by corporate earnings, which kicked off in earnest this week with mostly bumper results from the U.S. banks. JPMorgan beat first-quarter profit expectations, helped ÔÇîby robust Ôüátrading revenue and dealmaking, while Citi reported its strongest quarterly revenue in a decade, sending its shares to their highest point since 2008. Bank of America and Morgan Stanley will report later today.
ASML, the world's largest chipmaking tool supplier, added to the upbeat tone on Wednesday as it beat earnings expectations and lifted its 2026 revenue outlook on AI-driven demand.
Although the IMF trimmed its global growth forecasts on Tuesday - and even warned the world could be drifting toward an ÔÇťadverse scenarioÔÇŁ amid the war - its reference ÔÇőforecast assumes a short-lived conflict, with ÔÇőits global growth forecast unchanged for Ôüá2027.
Meanwhile, U.S. producer prices jumped in March due to the energy shock, but the increase was almost half what economists had expected - a reassuring sign, particularly as the data covered a period after the start of the Iran conflict.
Chart of the day
As ÔÇőfinancial markets return to pre-war levels, many investors are looking at just why the March oil shock has not ÔÇőshifted the dial much Ôüáon global economic forecasts. One of many reasons is the world economy's dwindling dependency on fossil fuels, with the share of power generated by wind and solar more than trebling over the past decade.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. March import prices (8:30 a.m. EDT)
* FedÔÇÖs Michael Barr and Michelle Bowman speak
* Fed issues the Beige Book
* U.S. Ôüácorporate earnings: Bank ÔÇőof America, Morgan Stanley
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? ÔÇőSign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, ÔÇőwhich, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Hormuz pipeline workaround looks worth the cost
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Middle Eastern oil exporters need a plan B. IranÔÇÖs effective blockade of the Strait of Hormuz means Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and others might end up having to pay tolls to get their exports out to buyers around the world. Unless Tehran sets these charges at a lowball level, there's a major financial incentive for Gulf states to start building alternative pipeline routes in ÔÇőearnest.
There is little recent historical precedent for a nation to tax passage through a natural waterway. Under the 1936 Montreux Convention , opens new tab , Turkey doesnÔÇÖt charge for entry to the Black Sea, while Danish tolls for shipping to ÔÇîpass through the Oresund strait were abolished in 1857 amid international pressure. Egyptian fees for Suez Canal transit, like those levied by the Panama Canal, are different: both are artificial passages that need cash for their upkeep.
Thus far, Iran has merely said that safe passage through Hormuz hinges on coordination with its authorities, stopping short of setting a public and specific toll. Still, there is plenty of scope to charge one. Gulf oil producers stand to lose hundreds of billions of dollars per year if the strait stays shut, which suggests that Tehran has the long-term negotiating power to influence the terms ÔÇőof any re-opening.
Putting a number on the toll takings is tough. In the past month, at least one of the handful of ships that has transited the strait has paid $2 million , opens new tab ÔÇö the same figure the New York Times said , opens new tab Tehran would ÔÇőlook to levy, citing two senior Iranian officials. Trade publication LloydÔÇÖs List says , opens new tab that before the war, up to 50 tankers carrying crude, other oil products and liquefied natural gas typically passed Ôüáthrough each day, or 18,250 a year. On that basis, Tehran could theoretically pocket $37 billion annually if traffic normalises.
Other approaches yield much smaller figures, though. Another toll idea , opens new tab floating around is a $1 per barrel charge. Around 20 million daily barrels flowed through Hormuz before ÔÇőthe war, of which perhaps half could conceivably find another route based on existing infrastructure. Assume the $1 fee applied to 10 million barrels per day, then, and the annual total would be $3.7 billion per year for Tehran. That looks like the low end of ÔÇőreasonable estimates.
Even this relatively small yearly toll would stack up. Apply a 5% discount rate, which is roughly Saudi Arabia's 10-year borrowing cost, to 25 years of payments at $3.7 billion annually. The total sum is $54 billion in today's money. The question is whether that conservative number is high enough to incentivise Iran's Gulf adversaries to construct a workaround to dodge the tolls, in the form of oil pipelines that skirt the strait.
One headache is working out where they should go. Merely replicating SaudiÔÇÖs 1,200-kilometre, 7-million barrel-a-day pipeline to the port of Yanbu on the Red Sea would create new ÔÇőproblems. Gulf states would still have to get their product past the Bab el-Mandeb Strait near Yemen. The nearby territory is controlled by Iran-aligned Houthi rebels, raising the risk of further drone attacks on new and old pipelines.
Gabriel Collins, of Rice UniversityÔÇÖs Baker ÔÇőInstitute for Public Policy, has had a stab at a holistic workaround , opens new tab . He envisages twin pipelines with 56-inch diameters, each carrying 5 million barrels of daily supply, stretching 1,800 kilometres from southern Iraq through Kuwait and then along the coast to pick up oil from Saudi and the ÔÇîUAE. Terminating in Ôüátwo Omani ports, Duqm and Salalah, it would avoid both the Red Sea and Gulf chokepoints, meaning crude could flow freely east into the Indian Ocean to Gulf statesÔÇÖ main Asian customers. But it would take up to seven years to build ÔÇö and cost a lot.
Gulf states might have to fork out $18 billion alone on the construction cost of the pipelines and pumping stations to move the oil along them, Collins reckons. Add a further $12 billion in project development expenses and $7 billion headroom in the plausible event of the project taking longer, or hitting snags. He also assumes nearly $8 billion for new oil loading terminals at Duqm and Salalah, and $10 billion to fortify critical sections with C-RAM and Patriot anti-missile systems.
The overall price tag comes to $55 billion, which is roughly the same ÔÇőas the $54 billion low-end estimate for the present value of ÔÇő25 years of possible Hormuz toll payments. In other words, Ôüáthe pipeline projects could potentially pay for themselves over a generation by allowing the Gulf states to avoid Tehran's strait fees. That's surely worth doing, since the real tolls could be higher, and ending dependence on an adversary has incalculable value.
Even for hugely wealthy states like Saudi and the UAE, these numbers represent an expensive undertaking ÔÇö and an extremely unwelcome one, given all they would ÔÇődo is provide new infrastructure for the same fossil fuels they intend to increasingly pivot away from. Even if they take the plunge, they will still have to pay tolls ÔÇőbefore the pipelines become operational.
Still, it seems Ôüálogical for Saudi and its neighbours to get building. Over 80% of the burden of paying the tolls would likely fall on Gulf states, according to Bruegel research , opens new tab . They could split the cost of new pipelines in step with their intended usage.
Of course, there's another way to conceive of these numbers. Arguably they give Iran a maximum target for how much it could hope to raise. It should rationally set the tolls at a level that would be as high as possible without incentivising Gulf rivals to build the new pipelines.
And plenty could Ôüáchange in the ÔÇőnext seven years. U.S. President Donald Trump is now implementing a blockade of Iran-approved ships that are making it through Hormuz, and an intensified war that ÔÇőultimately unblocks Hormuz is still possible. Yet Iran has proved that it has the ability to wage asymmetric warfare around Hormuz, and the regime seems hard to budge. Even if Saudi and its neighbours pay $55 billion for pipelines that remain as a backup, they might still view that as a sound investment to ÔÇőcreate some geopolitical breathing room.
Follow George Hay on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Carmakers navigating the costly and tricky transition to battery storage systems
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Carmakers and battery companies facing a weak U.S. market for electric vehicles are scrambling to repurpose battery factories to make energy-storage systems to fuel AI's thirst for power instead. But converting plants to new types of batteries wonÔÇÖt be easy, ÔÇőnor will the demand for energy storage materialize fast enough to absorb a glut of unused factory space for EV batteries.
The expected surge in demand for electricity and energy storage comes at ÔÇîan opportune time for automakers like General Motors (GM.N) , opens new tab and Ford Motor (F.N) , opens new tab and their battery suppliers -- Asian manufacturers that include Japan's Panasonic Holdings (6752.T) , opens new tab , and South Korea's Samsung SDI and LG Energy Solution. These companies over the past decade spent or earmarked more than $100 billion toward battery factories to feed a U.S. electric-vehicle market that has been decimated by the Trump administrationÔÇÖs pro-fossil-fuel policies.
Stationary storage systems use lithium-ion battery cells ÔÇô similar to those in EVs ÔÇô that store power often generated by renewable sources like solar and wind, and release it during times of high demand ÔÇőor grid stress. U.S. demand is expected to grow partly to support data centers and cloud computing, which draw gobs of electricity.
But the predicted energy storage boom wouldnÔÇÖt come close to offsetting the bust in demand ÔÇőfor EV batteries, a Reuters review of projected U.S. battery-factory capacity this decade found. U.S. demand for EVs already had been falling short of automakers' original projections even before Ôüáa $7,500 consumer tax credit expired September 30, sending sales down more than 25% over the past six months.
Converting factories to the type of battery chemistry that many energy storage systems use will take time and yet more money, and ÔÇőrequire the companies to delve into a technology that today is dominated by China.
Bob Lee, head of North America for LG Energy Solution (LGES), a battery arm of Seoul-based LG Group, said his company is converting three of its factories in North ÔÇőAmerica to produce batteries for storage systems. He expects the battery business in the United States to continue to struggle with excess capacity, what he calls "fallout" from the EV bust.
ÔÇťLike any other industry that goes through a difficult period like this, I don't think it's going to be all rosy,ÔÇŁ he said.
Ford said it is spending $2 billion on a new battery-storage division over the next two years to "create a new, diversified and profitable revenue stream." GM's joint venture with LGES, called Ultium Cells, said last month it will convert an EV-battery ÔÇőplant in Tennessee to make battery cells for storage.
Demand for stationary batteries in North America will hit 76 gigawatt-hours this year, according to consultancy Benchmark Mineral Intelligence. But the auto industryÔÇÖs investment binge on ÔÇőEV battery capacity has left it with far more factory space than that: roughly 275 GWh. While storage demand is expected to nearly double over the next five years, to 125 GWh, that still wonÔÇÖt be enough to mop up the excess capacity installed ÔÇîfor the auto Ôüáindustry.
For example, LGES, which produces battery cells in North America at its own facilities and in joint ventures with General Motors, Honda and Hyundai, is converting factory space to be able to produce up to 50 GWh of batteries for storage a year by the end of this year. Still, thatÔÇÖs only about a third of the company's overall capacity in the region.
Most energy storage systems use a breed of batteries called lithium iron-phosphate, or LFP, which is cheaper than the nickel-heavy chemistry that is predominantly used for EV batteries in North America. Switching factories to LFP can take as long as 18 months and cost several hundred million dollars, battery executives told Reuters.
For U.S. battery makers, producing LFP is also complicated ÔÇőby ChinaÔÇÖs dominance of that technology and its supply chain. ÔÇőU.S.-based battery makers are working to cut their Ôüáreliance on Chinese materials to qualify for generous federal tax credits for domestic battery production that took effect during the Biden administration and were left in place under Trump, said Iola Hughes, BenchmarkÔÇÖs head of research. Producers must gradually phase out Chinese content in coming years to get the full tax breaks.
Trade barriers are another problem. U.S. tariffs on Chinese-made cathode and ÔÇőanode materials ÔÇô the two electrodes that ping-pong the electric charge back and forth inside a battery ÔÇô stand at 35%, according to Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
The pivot to energy storage Ôüáby U.S. carmakers and battery companies has accelerated in recent months.
In March, the GM-LGES joint venture said it was spending $70 million and retraining workers to make battery cells for storage at its plant south of Nashville. Ford Motor in December disclosed it would repurpose some underused factory space in Kentucky to make storage batteries.
Traditional automakers are playing catch-up to Tesla. Elon Musk's company has spent about a decade developing its energy storage business, which has become one of the EV maker's fastest-growing divisions.
It was also more Ôüáprofitable than its ÔÇőEV business in 2025, with gross margins of about 30% compared to about 15% for its automotive business, excluding earnings from regulatory credits.
Deployments ÔÇőof TeslaÔÇÖs so-called Megapack storage units have surged, including $430 million in revenue last year from sales to MuskÔÇÖs xAI, an example of how AI-driven data-center demand is translating directly into battery orders.
Kurt Kelty, GM's battery chief and a former Tesla executive, told Reuters in January that the company is ÔÇőcommitted to building a battery manufacturing industry and supply chain in the U.S. Whether itÔÇÖs for EVs or storage systems, he said, ÔÇťit really doesnÔÇÖt matter.ÔÇŁ

item.title: Ukraine retakes 50 sq km of territory in March, army chief says
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukraine regained control of nearly 50 sq km (19 sq miles) of ÔÇőits territory from Russia in March, its army chief ÔÇîOleksandr Syrskyi said on Wednesday, building on its gains since the start of the year.
He added that with the change of weather conditions, Moscow's ÔÇőforces have stepped up their offensive operations and were ÔÇőconducting them along almost the entire 1,200-km front line.
Syrskyi Ôüásaid the area around the embattled eastern city of Pokrovsk, ÔÇőwhich Russia has been trying to capture fully since mid-2024, was ÔÇőamong the most intense places of fighting in March.
He also singled out Oleksandrivka, Kostiantynivka, and Lyman directions in the southeast and east as "the hottest" spots ÔÇőduring the month.
Ukrainian counterattacks in the southeast were helping to ÔÇődisrupt Russian efforts around Pokrovsk and the Russian spring offensive overall, military ÔÇîanalysts Ôüáhave said.
Syrskyi said last week that Ukraine has regained control of 480 sq km of territory since late January and President Volodymyr Zelenskiy earlier this month described the frontline situation as the best ÔÇőfor Ukraine since ÔÇőthe middle Ôüáof last year.
