The talks last weekend broke down without an agreement to end the war, which President Donald Trump began alongside Israel on ÔÇőFebruary 28, triggering ÔÇőIranian attacks Ôüáon Iran's Gulf neighbors and reigniting a conflict between Israel and Iran-backed Hezbollah in ÔÇőLebanon.
The war has led Iran to effectively shut ÔÇőthe ÔüáStrait of Hormuz - a vital artery for global crude and gas shipments - to ships other than its own, sharply reducing Ôüáexports ÔÇőfrom the Gulf, particularly to Asia ÔÇőand Europe, and leaving energy importers scrambling for alternative supplies.

item.title: US Senate Republicans block bid to rein in Trump Iran war powers, voting continues
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - A majority of ÔÇőthe U.S. ÔÇîSenate backed President Donald Trump's ÔÇőmilitary campaign ÔÇőagainst Iran on Wednesday, Ôüávoting to ÔÇőblock a ÔÇőDemocratic-led resolution aiming to stop the ÔÇőwar until ÔÇőhostilities are authorized by ÔÇîCongress.
As Ôüávoting continued, the tally was 51-47 to ÔÇőblock ÔÇőthe Ôüáwar powers resolution, as ÔÇőalmost ÔÇőall Ôüáof Trump's fellow Republicans voted Ôüáagainst ÔÇőit.

item.title: 'Unthinkable': African Catholics recoil at Trump's spat with Leo
item.description: 'Unthinkable': African Catholics recoil at Trump's spat with Leo

item.title: Israeli attacks prevent Lebanese from burying their dead in ancestral lands
item.description:
BEIRUT April 15 - Brothers Ali and Wael ÔÇőSabbagh stood facing the remains of the building that contained their family home for more than 50 years.
The ÔÇîsite was pounded into rubble by Israeli strikes last week in an attack that killed their mother, brother, and the family's helper.
Their grief has been compounded by their inability to properly bury their mother and brother ÔÇö a plight shared by other families from south Lebanon. Unable to head to their ancestral lands to ÔÇőhonor their dead, they are forced to bury them temporarily in Beirut until it becomes safe enough to allow their ÔÇőremains to be moved to their final resting place.
"We come from the south of Lebanon, from Nabatieh, and Ôüáthat is being bombarded on a daily basis," Wael said. "We want them to be buried in the family cemetery, but unfortunately we're ÔÇőunable to do that."
Israel has vowed to establish a buffer zone in southern Lebanon and maintain control over territory ÔÇőup to about 30 km (20 miles) north of Israel's border.
Nabatieh, a major city in the south, has been mostly reduced to rubble. The brothers said they cannot risk the journey south, nor can they send someone in their place for fear of them being killed in the strikes.
"Nobody should have to go ÔÇőthrough this. And unfortunately, my brother, sister, and I are not the only ones going through this," Wael said. "Hundreds, if not thousands, of ÔÇőfamilies...are mourning in the same way I am, not understanding what happened."
The Israeli attacks on Beirut last week were some of the most violent strikes on ÔÇîthe Lebanese Ôüácapital in decades. Across the country, more than 350 people were killed, said Lebanese authorities.
More than 2,160 people have been killed in Lebanon by Israeli attacks since the war between Hezbollah and Israel erupted last month. Hezbollah had lobbed rockets at Israel, prompting Israel to retaliate and launch an all-out war on Lebanon. More than 1.2 million people have been displaced in Lebanon since, said Lebanese authorities.
The Israeli military has said its ÔÇőoperations in Lebanon target Hezbollah.
Ali and ÔÇőWael's mother's body was found Ôüáin her home. It took two days for the family to track down their brother, whom they were able to identify by his arm tattoo. The family made arrangements to repatriate the body of ÔÇőthe house helper to Ethiopia.
The brothers spoke to Reuters a day after Lebanese and Israeli officials ÔÇőmet in Washington in Ôüáa rare encounter between representatives of governments that have technically been in a state of war since Israel was established in 1948.
"We're always going to be under the foot of the Israelis," Wael said. The brothers said they will try to seek legal action against the people behind the Ôüákilling of ÔÇőtheir family members.
Ali lamented the cycle of death, destruction and resurgence that has ÔÇőmarked the lives of the Lebanese for most of their country's existence.
Already, behind the Sabbaghs' bombed-out home, construction workers were busy working on a new building.
"Come back in a few years, ÔÇőthere's a new building, new kids, new mothers, new brothers," Ali said. "I will come. I will remember. Nobody else will."

item.title: Russia warns drone plans for Ukraine are pulling Europe deeper into war
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 15 (Reuters) - Russia's defence ministry warned on Wednesday that European ÔÇőplans to step up drone supplies to Ukraine are dragging ÔÇîthose countries deeper into a war with Russia.
The ministry said it believes governments in a number of EU countries have decided to increase ÔÇőthe production and supply of drones to Ukraine, a ÔÇőmove Moscow views as a step that is escalating Ôüáthe conflict.
It published a list of factories and enterprises in ÔÇőseveral European countries it alleges manufacture drones or drone components, ÔÇőand gave their addresses, including sites in Britain, Germany, Spain, Italy, Israel and Poland, among others.
"The European public should not only have a clear ÔÇőunderstanding of the true causes of the threats to their ÔÇősecurity, but also be aware of the addresses and locations of Ukrainian and ÔÇîjoint Ôüáenterprises producing UAVs and components for Ukraine within their own countries," it said.
Former Russian President Dmitry Medvedev, now deputy chair of Russia's Security Council, said in a subsequent post on X ÔÇőthat the list ÔÇőpublished by Ôüáthe military amounted to a list of potential targets for Russia's armed forces.
"When strikes become a ÔÇőreality depends on what comes next. Sleep well, ÔÇőEuropean Ôüápartners!" he said.
Russian officials, including Medvedev, have repeatedly made public statements that have been perceived as threats to European countries over their Ôüásupport for ÔÇőUkraine. However, these have generally taken ÔÇőthe form of warnings and veiled hints rather than outright announcements of imminent ÔÇőstrikes.

item.title: Colombia court orders Petro government to return emergency funds
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 15 (Reuters) - Colombia's Constitutional Court ordered President Gustavo Petro's government on Wednesday to return funds collected during an ÔÇőeconomic emergency that was declared unconstitutional.
The decision is the latest blow ÔÇîto the Petro administration's finances, strained by setbacks in Congress, where several proposals for fiscal reforms have failed to gain support.
The court had struck down the economic emergency declared ÔÇőby Petro's government in January to raise 11 trillion pesos ($3.07 billion) ÔÇőto finance part of this year's budget.
The government had collected 1.67 Ôüátrillion pesos ($467 million) as a result of the decrees enacted during the ÔÇőeconomic emergency, according to a source at the country's National Tax and Customs ÔÇőDirectorate who spoke on condition of anonymity.
The economic emergency is a state of exception that allows the government to make decisions by decree without prior authorization from Congress.
Among the measures ÔÇőthe government had established during the state of emergency were a tax ÔÇőon liquid assets exceeding 2.1 billion pesos, a special income tax for the financial sector, ÔÇîand Ôüáa 19% tax on the sale of alcoholic beverages and gambling, as well as on certain vehicles, motorcycles, and aircraft, among others.
On previous occasions when the court declared other economic emergencies unconstitutional, the ruling was not retroactive; meaning it ÔÇődid not require the ÔÇőgovernment to return Ôüáthe funds already collected.
Latin AmericaÔÇÖs fourth-largest economy is experiencing a deterioration in its finances, which forced the government in ÔÇőJune to suspend a fiscal ruling under which the Ministry ÔÇőof ÔüáFinance raised the fiscal deficit target for 2025 to 7.1% of GDP, from an original 5.1%.
Last week, Finance Minister German Avila announced that he would present a new Ôüátax reform ÔÇőbill to Congress worth 16 trillion pesos ($4.47 ÔÇőbillion) to finance this yearÔÇÖs spending budget, less than three months before the end of the legislative ÔÇőperiod.
($1= 3,578.82 pesos)

item.title: Sovereign borrowers launch platform to amplify voice in debt talks
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - A group of officials from developing economies launched on Wednesday ÔÇîthe Borrowers' Platform, an effort backed by the United Nations to give debt-hit nations a stronger collective voice in dealings with creditors.
United Nations Secretary-General Antonio Guterres said at the launch event that the platform is essential for power relations to change.
"Today, ÔÇőwe launch a breakthrough in global financing, a platform in which borrowing countries sit together, ÔÇőlearn from each other and speak with a collective voice," Guterres said.
He said 3.4 Ôüábillion people live in countries that spend more on debt service than on health or education.
The ÔÇőinitiative is seen partly as a counter for institutions like the Paris Club, where official creditors meet to ÔÇődiscuss negotiation strategies when a common debtor encounters problems with repayment.
This platform aims also to be an experience repository of sorts, where members share their knowledge and new governments, or new countries, avoid arriving at the table with a ÔÇőlack of preparedness.
"What was once a long-standing aspiration of developing countries has now become a concrete and a ÔÇőcollective step forward," said Ahmed Kouchouk, Egypt's finance minister. "Today stands as a strong statement of intent that the voice of borrowing ÔÇînations Ôüáand countries belongs at the very center of the global financial dialogue."
The event, presided over by Egypt as chair of the working group, is due to formally open an interim phase for the platform, establish interim leadership and adopt a work program running through October 2026, according to the launch agenda.
The ÔÇőworking group that developed the ÔÇődraft modalities was Ôüámade up of Egypt, Colombia, Honduras, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan and Zambia. Pakistan served as vice chair.
Under the draft framework, full membership would be voluntary and limited ÔÇőto developing countries that are UN member states, net borrowers and not ÔÇőfull members of Ôüácreditor groupings. The proposed structure includes a Governing Council of finance ministers, central bank governors or equivalent officials, and a Steering Committee of senior technical officials.
"The launch of the Borrowers' Platform is a major milestone Ôüáin rebalancing ÔÇőpower inequalities in global economic governance," said Iolanda Fresnillo, policy and ÔÇőadvocacy manager at the European Network on Debt and Development (Eurodad), in a statement.
"This long-overdue initiative is a first step towards breaking creditor domination over decision-making ÔÇőon sovereign debt issues."

item.title: US targets Iran's oil transportation infrastructure with sanctions
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. Treasury Department said ÔÇőon Wednesday it was targeting Iran's ÔÇîoil transportation infrastructure by imposing sanctions on more than two dozen individuals, companies, and ÔÇővessels.
The sanctions target a network of ÔÇőIranian oil shipping magnate Mohammad Hossein ÔüáShamkhani, the Treasury Department said in ÔÇőa statement. Hossein Shamkhani is the son ÔÇőof Ali Shamkhani, a key figure in Iran's security and nuclear policymaking who was killed in U.S.-Israeli ÔÇőstrikes on Tehran on February 28.
"Treasury ÔÇőis moving aggressively with Economic Fury by targeting ÔÇîregime Ôüáelites like the Shamkhani family that attempt to profit at the expense of the Iranian people," Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent ÔÇősaid in ÔÇőthe Ôüástatement.
The Treasury Department is also sanctioning Iranian national Seyed Naiemaei ÔÇőBadroddin Moosavi, who it alleges to ÔÇőbe Ôüáa financier to Hezbollah, and three companies linked to a money laundering scheme Ôüáinvolving ÔÇőthe sale of Iranian ÔÇőoil in exchange for Venezuelan gold.

item.title: Allbirds lacks footing for dramatic AI pivot
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 15 (Reuters Breakingviews) - After failing to sustain its eco-friendly sneaker empire, Allbirds (BIRD.O) , opens new tab has an even woolier proposition. The company, once valued at ÔÇőmore than $4 billion, sold , opens new tab what remains of its footwear business last month for just $39 million, and is ÔÇînow racing into , opens new tab the world of artificial intelligence. These sorts of bizarre pivots work occasionally, but the odds are long.
Some modern corporate giants have sprouted from highly unusual seeds. South Korean smartphone and chip maker Samsung once exported dried fish while Nokia made the unlikely ÔÇőswitch from rubber-boot manufacturing to wireless telecom. India's Wipro started by selling cooking oil before becoming an ÔÇőIT powerhouse. Shell is so named because of its origins as a collectibles shop where Ôüáfounder Marcus Samuel once sold , opens new tab decorative sea-creature coverings before it later commissioned the first oil tanker to traverse the ÔÇőSuez Canal.
Most such ambitious metamorphoses flop, however. They suffer from a lack of management skill, capital, luck, competition and myriad ÔÇőother factors. After all, defeat is the eventual fate for most ventures, and the bigger the gambit the lower the chance for success.
Jumping onto the latest bandwagon, as Allbirds is doing, also tends to be little more than a gimmick, or worse. The Long ÔÇőIsland Iced Tea Corp, for example, attracted investors in 2021 after it rebranded as Long Blockchain to take advantage ÔÇőof cryptocurrency mania. Its shares were later delisted, and the Securities and Exchange Commission brought insider trading charges , opens new tab .
In addition to raising $50 million ÔÇîin convertible Ôüáfinancing, Allbirds is playing the name game, too. It will now be known as NewBird AI, with a ÔÇťlong term vision to become a fully integrated GPU-as-a-Service and AI-native cloud solutions provider.ÔÇŁ The microscopic stock price jumped 620% on Wednesday.
ItÔÇÖs unclear what the company brings to the crowded and heavily capitalized world of data centers. CoreWeave (CRWV.O) , opens new tab , for one, is ÔÇőworth 400 times as much, ÔÇőhas close ties with ÔüáNvidia and just expanded chunky deals with Jane Street and Anthropic. Despite this progress, it is not clear what advantage it has over deep-pocketed and technologically advantaged rivals such ÔÇőas Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab and Amazon (AMZN.O) , opens new tab .
In this context, NewBird AI looks like a considerable longshot. Its ÔÇőshoes may have Ôüáspread across Silicon Valley, but it will take more than a few dollars and a dream to get off the backfoot.
Follow Robert Cyran on Bluesky , opens new tab .

item.title: Exclusive: European buyers hold talks to ship Canadian LNG via Panama Canal to diversify supply
item.description:
CALGARY, April 15 (Reuters) - European buyers, including Germany's Uniper, are exploring the possibility of purchasing liquefied ÔÇőnatural gas from Canada's Pacific coast and shipping it through the Panama Canal as part of a long-term strategy to diversify supply, ÔÇîan effort made more urgent in the wake of the Iran war, two sources familiar with the matter said.
Three sources said European buyers are among the potential customers that have had commercial talks with Canada's Ksi Lisims LNG , a proposed liquefied natural gas export terminal whose backers have been working to finalize contracts with purchasers before making an expected final investment ÔÇődecision this year.
The interest in Ksi Lisims from potential European customers ÔÇö who include state-owned German energy group Uniper (UN0k.DE) , opens new tab , according to two sources ÔÇö ÔÇőis significant, as Canada's burgeoning LNG export industry is geographically more suited to supplying Asia and tolls and shipping time Ôüáthrough the Panama Canal will increase costs.
All of Canada's existing and emerging LNG export capacity is located on the country's west coast, offering short ÔÇőshipping times to Asian buyers, while its east coast has virtually no infrastructure beyond Repsol's < REP.MC , opens new tab > Saint John terminal.
Lack of infrastructure has long been seen as a ÔÇőbarrier to Canada supplying any meaningful amounts of LNG to Europe. But a source familiar with the Ksi Lisims LNG project said the current Middle East conflict means European buyers are considering accepting the costs and longer transit time of shipping through the Panama Canal in order to diversify their supply sources to a stable, democratic ÔÇőjurisdiction like Canada.
"Since the war in Iran started, there has been especially strong interest in (Ksi Lisims) offtake from LNG buyers all around the world, ÔÇőincluding from Europe," the source said.
Uniper declined to comment. Reuters reported in March that the German company was in talks with Canada at a corporate and political level to ÔÇîexpand its Ôüáliquefied natural gas purchases. The United States accounted for 96% of Germany's LNG imports last year, and two sources said Ksi Lisims is seen having potential when it comes to diversifying that supply.
The Ksi Lisims project is not an immediate solution to Europe's energy needs. Even if its proponents ÔÇö Houston-based Western LNG; a consortium of Canadian natural gas producers called Rockies LNG; and the Nisga'a First Nation, who own the land for the project ÔÇö decide ÔÇőto go ahead, it will be ÔÇőin construction for several years.
However, Ôüáthe project has been referred by the Canadian government to Canada's major projects office for fast-tracking. Prime Minister Mark Carney has sought to speed up natural resource project permitting to boost an economy that has been threatened by U.S. trade ÔÇőpolicy.
< SHEL.L , opens new tab > and < TTEF.PA , opens new tab > have already signed 20-year LNG purchase agreements with Ksi Lisims.
Canada's Energy and Natural Resources Minister said ÔÇőin August German companies are Ôüálooking to buy and swap Canadian LNG cargoes. But now, they are also interested in taking shipments of the physical Canadian product, the sources said.
The Iran war and the resulting Strait of Hormuz closure have made it increasingly likely that Ksi Lisims gets built, said Jamie Heard, vice president, capital markets for Tourmaline ÔüáOil (TOU.TO) , opens new tab , one ÔÇőof the Rockies LNG partners behind the project.
He said Western Canadian natural gas prices continue ÔÇőto lag behind the U.S. benchmark, making a strong business case for Canadian LNG.
"The economic rationale is there, these projects can pay themselves off quite quickly with spreads at that level," ÔÇőHeard said in an interview.

item.title: Bessent says U.S. won't be renewing waivers for Iranian and Russian oil
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The United States will not be renewing ÔÇőthe waivers that allowed purchase of some Iranian and ÔÇîRussian oil without facing U.S. sanctions, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told reporters on Wednesday.
Reuters reported on Tuesday that ÔÇőWashington would not renew a 30-day waiver of ÔÇősanctions on Iranian oil at sea that Ôüáexpires this week, and let a similar waiver ÔÇőon sanctions on Russian oil expire over the weekend.
"We ÔÇőwill not be renewing the general license on Russian oil, and we will not be renewing the general license ÔÇőon Iranian oil. That was oil that ÔÇőwas on the water prior to March 11. So all ÔÇîthat Ôüáhas been used," Bessent said at a White House briefing.
The moves signal an end to the Trump administration's efforts to use the sanctions waivers ÔÇőto free ÔÇőup more Ôüáoil supplies and lower soaring global energy prices.
The Iranian waiver, which the ÔÇőTreasury Department issued on March 20, allowed ÔÇősome Ôüá140 million barrels of oil to reach global markets and helped relieve pressure on energy supply Ôüáduring ÔÇőthe war, Bessent said last ÔÇőmonth. The waiver is set to expire on April 19.

item.title: Higher oil output gives Nigeria fiscal space as reforms continue ÔÇô Edun
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - Nigeria's oil production ÔÇőhas risen to 1.8 million barrels a day, Finance Minister Wale Edun told Reuters, generating fiscal breathing ÔÇîspace that will allow the government to support vulnerable households as it ploughs ahead with reforms.
Speaking in an interview on Wednesday on the sidelines of the International Monetary Fund and World Bank Group spring meetings in Washington, Edun said rising crude production was positive for Nigeria's revenue, ÔÇőfor foreign exchange and for the fiscal situation of the OPEC member.
"It gives us that extra fiscal space ÔÇőwithin which to look at ... helping the vulnerable households at this time."
However, support would be targeted, Ôüásaid Edun, adding "there is no thought of any return or retardation to broad untargeted subsidies."
Edun said the country suffered ÔÇőno fuel shortages thanks to the Dangote refinery.
"We have managed to keep the pumps going and adequate supply of petroleum products, ÔÇőparticularly petrol and diesel and so forth, and even jet fuel."
President Bola Tinubu since 2023 has rolled out Nigeria's most ambitious economic overhaul in decades by ending costly fuel and energy subsidies, devaluing the currency and changing the tax system which - together with rising oil ÔÇőoutput - has supported economic growth of around 4%.
But inflation in Africa's most populous nation is still high, interest rates ÔÇőelevated and fiscal space tight as the government struggles with heavy debt-service costs, weak non-oil revenues and continued reliance on crude exports.
Data out ÔÇîon ÔüáWednesday showed consumer inflation quickened for the first time in a year in March - a headache for Tinubu's government ahead of elections scheduled for January next year.
Asked about the impact on the economy from the Iran war which is expected to crimp growth around the globe, Edun said the outlook was very uncertain.
"The impact on growth can be ... either minimal, but over time ÔÇőit could, if things drag ÔÇőon, it could lead to Ôüáworld recession," he said.
The IMF trimmed its GDP growth forecast for Nigeria to 4.1% for this year from a January prediction of 4.4%, though it expects growth to accelerate ÔÇőto 4.3% in 2027. The Fund said that growth momentum benefited from improved macroeconomic stability ÔÇőand positive terms-of-trade Ôüáeffects, though warned of headwinds from higher goods and transport costs.
Meanwhile the government was committed to continuing its reform programme, said Edun.
"Nigeria is in a position where the resilience that has been built in the economy is actually very obvious for Ôüáall to ÔÇősee," said Edun.
"We are very much focused on maintaining the reform pathway ÔÇőand very much focused on improving the resilience of the Nigerian economy to withstand external shocks."
Asked about its relationship with the Fund, Edun said "Nigeria ÔÇőis not looking for any financial support from the IMF."

item.title: Pakistan weighs spot LNG as Iran war hits supply, ramps up domestic oil and gas
item.description:
ISLAMABAD, April 15 (Reuters) - Pakistan is considering buying ÔÇîliquefied natural gas (LNG) on the spot market to offset supply disruptions caused by the Iran war but would favour government-to-government deals to avoid having to pay steep premiums, its petroleum minister said.
Qatar's force majeure has forced ÔÇőPakistan to make costly spot purchases or find alternative fuels ahead of summer ÔÇődemand.
Spot LNG cargoes have surged to $20 to $30 per mmBtu amid the Middle East Ôüáconflict, Ali Pervaiz Malik, the petroleum minister told Reuters on Wednesday.
Purchases would depend on whether ÔÇőprices are acceptable to the power sector, including under existing government-to-government arrangements with Azerbaijan's SOCAR.
Pakistan has ÔÇőalso been routing some crude supplies via Saudi Arabia's Red Sea port of Yanbu to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, with Malik saying insurance costs on that route were lower than routes crossing or near Hormuz.
Pakistan ÔÇőimports nearly all of its oil, much of it via the Strait of Hormuz, and ÔÇőremains exposed to supply shocks despite cutting its LNG reliance in recent years as gas is still needed ÔÇîto Ôüámeet the country's peak summer power demand.
It has begun commercial output from its highest-ever producing oil and gas well, as it shores up domestic supply.
"We have arrangements in place to meet domestic and industrial requirements," Malik said, adding that gas disruptions have not led to major curbs, ÔÇőwith eight of 10 ÔÇőfertiliser plants operating.
Officials are Ôüáalso considering the use of costlier fuels such as furnace oil to limit load shedding, although at the expense of higher tariffs. Malik ÔÇőwarned that prolonged shortages could threaten food security.
Pakistan's Baragzai X-01 well in ÔÇőnorthwestern Khyber ÔüáPakhtunkhwa is producing about 15,000 barrels of oil per day and 45 million cubic feet of gas, with output expected to rise further, its state-run operator Oil and Gas Development Company Ltd (OGDC) (OGDC.PSX) , opens new tab Ôüásaid.
The well ÔÇőcould reach up to 25,000 bpd and 60 mmcfd ÔÇőof gas, making it Pakistan's highest-producing well, and may contribute around 10% of crude output while cutting the country's import ÔÇőbill by about $329 million annually, OGDC said.

item.title: US may impose secondary sanctions on Iran oil purchases
item.description: WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - U.S. ÔÇîTreasury ÔÇőSecretary ÔÇőScott ÔüáBessent ÔÇőon ÔÇőWednesday said ÔÇőthe ÔÇőUnited States ÔÇîcould Ôüáimpose ÔÇősecondary ÔÇősanctions Ôüáon ÔÇőcountries ÔÇőthat Ôüápurchase ÔüáIranian ÔÇőoil.

item.title: White House offers no hint of Iran war cost as it seeks military funding surge
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - White House budget director Russell Vought said on Wednesday he could not estimate the cost of the Iran war, as he defended President ÔÇőDonald Trump's request for a massive $1.5 trillion annual military budget against bipartisan criticism from U.S. lawmakers who cited the Pentagon's historic lack ÔÇîof financial accountability.
"We're not ready to come to you with a request. We're still working on it. We're working through to figure out what's needed," Vought told a hearing of the House of Representatives Budget Committee. "I don't have a ballpark."
The cost of the war with Iran, which Trump began alongside Israel on February 28, has remained an open question on Capitol ÔÇőHill. An initial $200 billion request for additional funding for the war met with stiff opposition in Congress last month.
Vought appeared before the panel to ÔÇődiscuss Trump's proposed budget for fiscal year 2027, with its $500 billion increase in military spending and 10% reduction for non-defense programs.
The Ôüárequest is intended to reflect Republican priorities heading into the November midterm elections, in which Trump's Republicans hope to retain control over the House of Representatives ÔÇőand the Senate but face growing public concern about the cost of living, energy prices and the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran .
Democrats took issue with Vought's ÔÇőassertions that healthcare, education and low-income energy assistance programs were marred by fraud.
"I'm so glad you asked about fraud, because you are coming back to ask for a $1.5 trillion budget for the Department of Defense," Democratic Representative Pramila Jayapal of Washington state told the budget director. "The Department of Defense is the only federal agency that has never passed an audit ... But ÔÇőyou're not going after any of that."
Vought said the administration is pursuing "inefficiencies" at the Pentagon.
"I don't think you're doing enough," said Republican Representative Glenn Grothman, who ÔÇőcalled for a Pentagon audit to be completed before Congress votes on defense spending.
"There is so much arrogance in that agency," added Grothman, of Wisconsin. "They just say we don't have ÔÇîto do Ôüáit on audit. We're so damn important. We don't care what Congress thinks."
Vought promoted Trump's budget proposal for the fiscal year beginning October 1 as aimed at reducing spending. He promoted Trump's 2025 tax-cut-and-spending package known as the "One Big Beautiful Bill Act" as an initiative that achieved $2 trillion in mandatory savings through cuts to Medicaid health coverage and food assistance to low-income families.
That bill, which extended 2017 tax cuts, will add $4.7 trillion to U.S. deficits over the next decade, while reduced ÔÇőimmigration will add another $500 billion, according to ÔÇőthe nonpartisan Congressional Budget Office.
Representative Brendan Boyle of Pennsylvania, the budget panel's top Democrat, pointed to forecasts saying the legislation's healthcare cuts would mean the loss of health coverage for more than 15 million people. Vought said they were able-bodied adults, people in the country ÔÇőillegally or ineligible for benefits.
"You're going to sit here with a straight face and say they're all illegals? They ÔÇőwere all defrauding Ôüáthe system? That's actually your position?" Boyle asked.
"Yes," Vought replied.
Democratic Representative Scott Peters of California pointed out to Vought that the watchdog Government Accountability Office has found the administration illegally withheld billions of dollars allocated for National Institutes of Health grants, public schools and Head Start early education programs nationwide.
"Do you dispute GAO's findings?" Peters asked.
"Yes. GAO Ôüáis typically ÔÇőwrong. They're very partisan," Vought replied.
To become law, Trump's proposed budget needs approval from Congress ÔÇőat a time when Republicans are trying to overcome Democratic opposition to funding for Trump's immigration crackdown , just months after the longest government shutdown in U.S. history . Democrats have already declared the budget proposal dead ÔÇőon arrival, leaving government funding to closed-door negotiations between appropriators.

item.title: Ukraine says it is employing new integrated drone-infantry warfare system
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Ukraine's Defence Ministry said on Wednesday it was introducing a ÔÇőnew model of combat operations integrating ÔÇîdrone warfare with infantry and pointed to successes it said had been ÔÇőachieved in retaking territory from ÔÇőRussian forces in the south of Ôüáthe country.
"A new model of warfare ÔÇőis being introduced, drone-assault units that ÔÇőcombine aerial and ground unmanned systems with infantry into a single integrated system," the ÔÇőministry said in a statement ÔÇőon Telegram.
"This approach has already produced results ÔÇîin Ôüáthe south, where since February a large area of territory has been liberated, precisely thanks to the use ÔÇőof ÔÇőthese advanced Ôüáunits."
Ukraine's top military commander, Oleksandr Syrskyi, said on Wednesday ÔÇőthat Kyiv's forces had regained ÔÇőcontrol Ôüáof nearly 50 sq km (19 sq miles) of its territory from Russia Ôüáin ÔÇőMarch, building on its ÔÇőgains since the start of the year.

item.title: US envoy Waltz appears to torpedo Bachelet as next UN leader
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. envoy to the United Nations, Mike Waltz, appeared to torpedo the ÔÇőcandidacy of former Chilean President Michelle Bachelet to be the next leader of the world body on ÔÇîWednesday by saying he shared a U.S. senator's concerns about her fitness for the job.
At a U.S. Senate committee hearing, Republican Senator Pete Ricketts of Nebraska charged that Bachelet had pulled punches as U.N. human rights chief in a 2022 report in failing to label China's ÔÇőactions against Uyghur Muslims as genocide, and has also promoted abortion as a fundamental human right.
Waltz said he ÔÇőwas not currently in a position to say whom the United States would support to Ôüáreplace U.N. Secretary-General Antonio Guterres, and whom it would oppose. But he said in response to Ricketts, "I share your concerns."
He ÔÇőadded that he was sure that U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio also shared the concerns.
A new United Nations secretary-general will ÔÇőbe elected this year for a five-year term starting on January 1, 2027.
Support of the five permanent veto-wielding members of the U.N. Security Council - the United States, Russia, China, Britain and France - is vital for a candidate to succeed.
Bachelet also fell foul of China over the ÔÇőUyghur report, which said the detention of Uyghurs and other Muslims in the country's Xinjiang province may constitute crimes ÔÇőagainst humanity.
A representative for Bachelet's campaign did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Waltz said the conventional wisdom and in New York, where ÔÇîthe ÔüáUnited Nations has its headquarters, was that because there had never been a female secretary general, or one from Latin America, that the future U.N. leader should therefore be a Latin American woman.
"We have taken the position of we just need the best," he said. "And this institution desperately needs strong, effective leadership."
Waltz said, "reform, reform, reform ... and getting back to basics ÔÇőon peace and security will be ÔÇőat the top of Ôüáour criteria."
So far four candidates have been nominated, including Bachelet, Chile's first female head of state, who twice served as president of the South American nation.
In March, Chile withdrew its ÔÇőbacking for her candidacy after a change in leadership in the country and a ÔÇősharp shift to Ôüáthe right, but she said she would press ahead with support from Brazil and Mexico.
Bachelet was U.N. high commissioner for human rights from 2018-22 and executive director of U.N. Women from 2010-13.
The other declared candidates are Rafael Grossi, a veteran Argentine diplomat Ôüáwho is ÔÇőcurrently director-general of the International Atomic Energy Agency, Rebeca Grynspan, a former vice ÔÇőpresident of Costa Rica, and Macky Sall, the former president of Senegal.
The candidates are due to take part in interactive dialogues , opens new tab next week at the United ÔÇőNations that will be broadcast live, starting with Bachelet on Tuesday.

item.title: Edison says Qatar may extend gas force majeure, sees US LNG filling gap
item.description:
MILAN, April 15 (Reuters) - Qatar may extend its force majeure on gas supplies beyond mid-June, Italian importer Edison ÔÇősaid on Wednesday, while predicting the gap will be plugged by ÔÇîU.S. supply instead of Russian gas.
Speaking in a media briefing, Edison (EDNn.MI) , opens new tab CEO Nicola Monti and its head of gas portfolio management Fabio Dubini said the Italian group bought seven cargoes of liquefied ÔÇőnatural gas from the United States after 10 were cancelled by QatarEnergy ÔÇőbetween April and mid-June due to the Middle East war.
Edison has a ÔüálongÔÇĹterm contract with QatarEnergy to receive 6.4 billion cubic metres of LNG per ÔÇőyear, around 10% of Italy's annual consumption.
The utility, Italy's second-biggest gas importer, last month ÔÇőreceived notification from its Gulf supplier that it would not deliver 10 cargoes - equal to 1.4 billion cubic metres of gas - from April to mid-June. Attacks by Iran knocked out 17% of QatarEnergy's ÔÇőLNG export capacity last month, according to its CEO.
QatarEnergy could not be immediately reached ÔÇőoutside normal business hours.
The deliveries to Edison's final customers in Italy are not at risk, Edison ÔÇîsaid, Ôüáeven if Dubini said it was reasonable to expect an extension of the disruption of QatarEnergy's supply.
"I think the market at the moment, while tight, offers sufficient flexibility options to allow us to continue getting supplies from other areas," Monti said, when ÔÇőasked whether Europe should ÔÇőreconsider its planned Ôüában on Russian LNG imports, as Italian energy group Eni has said.
Monti cited the startup this year of two U.S. LNG ÔÇőplants ÔÇö Venture Global's Plaquemines and Golden Pass, which is partly ÔÇőowned by ÔüáQatarEnergy and Exxon Mobil ÔÇö as a reason for confidence in supply. Other plants are also under construction, he said.
"Over the next 18 months, the market will return to being structurally Ôüábalanced," Monti ÔÇősaid.
A previously expected LNG supply glut around 2027 ÔÇőto 2028 is likely to shift to 2029 to 2030 due to the temporary shortfall of gas from ÔÇőthe Middle East, Monti said.

item.title: 'Significant' fire breaks out at Viva Energy's refinery in Australia
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Firefighters have ÔÇőarrived on the scene to respond to a ÔÇťsignificantÔÇŁ ÔÇîfire at Viva Energy Group's Geelong refinery in southern Australia, according to local fire authorities.
The extent of the damage was not immediately clear. ÔÇőThe blaze was reported at around 11 p.m. ÔÇőlocal time on Wednesday, Fire Rescue Victoria said in Ôüáa statement, adding it was not yet under control ÔÇőbut all staff had been accounted for.
The dispatch to the ÔÇőrefinery in Corio in the state of Victoria came after multiple calls reporting explosions and flames, it said.
The refinery can process up to ÔÇő120,000 barrels of oil per day, according to the company's ÔÇőwebsite.
It is one of two refineries remaining in Australia and employs over ÔÇî1,100 Ôüápeople, supplying over 50 percent of VictoriaÔÇÖs and 10 percent of AustraliaÔÇÖs fuel, the website said.
Viva Energy Group did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
Australia has faced ÔÇőfuel security ÔÇőconcerns since Iran Ôüáall but closed the Strait of Hormuz after being attacked by the U.S. and ÔÇőIsrael.
Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said last month ÔÇőthe Ôüágovernment would halve the excise on fuel and diesel and remove the heavy road user charge for three months to Ôüáhelp ÔÇőhouseholds cope with a surge in ÔÇőcosts driven by the Iran war.

item.title: World Bank launches 'Water Forward' programme to tackle global water stress
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - The World Bank and other top development lenders launched a new global initiative dubbed Water Forward on Wednesday, aimed at improving secure water ÔÇőaccess for a billion people within the next four years.
The programme seeks ÔÇîto boost investment in water management while encouraging governments to treat water as a strategic economic resource rather than a low-cost public utility. It will focus on mobilising private capital ÔÇőand philanthropic money alongside public funding, the World Bank said.
"Water is foundational ÔÇőto how economies function," the head of the World Bank, ÔüáAjay Banga, said in a statement, adding that the task was now to "deliver ÔÇőreliable water services at scale."
Global demand for freshwater is expected to outstrip supply by ÔÇőup to 40% by the end of the decade, the World Bank estimates, with water-related shocks already costing some countries several percentage points of annual economic growth.
Climate change is intensifying ÔÇőboth droughts and floods, placing pressure on public finances and vulnerable communities, particularly ÔÇőin fast-growing cities. A report last year estimated that over 2.1 billion people lack safe drinking ÔÇîwater, Ôüáand more than 3.4 billion live without adequate sanitation.
Water Forward will initially focus on 14 countries in water-stressed regions in Africa, the Middle East and South Asia and prioritise projects that reduce leakage in urban areas, modernise irrigation, improve wastewater ÔÇőreuse and expand ÔÇődata-driven planning.
Other development Ôüábanks involved include the European Investment Bank, Asian Development Bank, the Inter-American Development Bank and the New Development Bank. The ÔÇőlatter institution was established by BRICS nations Brazil, Russia, India, ÔÇőChina and ÔüáSouth Africa.
The World Bank said it estimated that 4 billion people experience water scarcity due to a mix of unclear government policies, weak regulations and financially unsustainable utilities.
Its Ôüácommitment ÔÇőwas to deliver water security to 400 million ÔÇőpeople by 2030, it added, while "additional partner commitments" would take the Water Forward programme's total reach to ÔÇőmore than 1 billion people.

item.title: Trading Day: S&P 500, Nasdaq nab all-time closing highs, buoyed by Middle East optimism
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 15 (Reuters) - The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq both logged record closing highs on Wednesday as investors assessed developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict and turned their focus to corporate earnings.
And before you read on, don't forget to sign up , opens new tab for Jamie McGeever's upcoming LSEG webinar ÔÇőon April 23, where he'll talk about safe havens in uncertain times with his ROI colleague Mike Dolan.
Here are a ÔÇîfew articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* S&P 500, Nasdaq reached their first record closing highs since the U.S.-Iran conflict began
Hopes of de-escalation and robust earnings expectations pushed ÔÇîthe indexes Ôüáto all-time closing highs, reassuring investors that a bull market remains intact.
Reaching those records during an active geopolitical crisis signified a shift in market positioning as traders appeared more willing to price in less severe escalation risks in the near term.
* Shares of Allbirds soared 582.3% after announcing a move from footwear to AI computing infrastructure
In an unusual pivot, the San Francisco-based company said it would execute a $50 million convertible financing agreement with an ÔÇőinstitutional investor, the proceeds of which ÔÇőwill be used to acquire Ôüágraphics processing units (GPUs).
* Trump threatens to fire Powell if he doesn't quit Fed board
Trump has threatened to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell from his separate seat on the central bank's Board of Governors if he does ÔÇőnot relinquish the post when his term as Fed chief ends on May 15.
The administration's threats against ÔÇőPowell, including an ongoing Ôüácriminal investigation, could delay Senate confirmation of Kevin Warsh, Trump's pick to succeed Powell.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do ÔÇőnot reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Trafigura signs $1 billion oil prepayment deal with Gabon
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Commodities trader Trafigura said on Wednesday it had signed a $1 billion prepayment agreement ÔÇőwith Gabon, under which it will receive crude oil deliveries ÔÇîover a sevenÔÇĹyear period.
The Central African country seeks to strengthen cash flow and boost foreignÔÇĹexchange reserves amid high global oil prices, Gabon's economy and ÔÇőfinance ministry said.
Under the agreement, Trafigura will provide upfront ÔÇőfinancing to the African state in exchange for future Ôüácrude oil deliveries and will act as the exclusive offtaker ÔÇőof Gabon's profit oil for the duration of the deal, the ÔÇőcompany said in a statement. Profit oil refers to the governmentÔÇÖs share of oil production after companies have recovered their costs.
The government will receive the $1 ÔÇőbillion in preÔÇĹfinancing with a maturity of seven years, the ÔÇőministry said in a separate statement, adding that no guarantees or collateral were ÔÇîpledged Ôüáagainst oil cargoes in the deal advised by Algest Consulting.
These resources are exclusively earmarked for funding investment programs and addressing the social needs of the population, it said.
The oil underpinning the ÔÇőprepayment will be ÔÇősourced from production Ôüáacross multiple petroleum-sharing contracts and from a range of producing assets and operators, Trafigura said.
"We are pleased to ÔÇőhave signed this agreement with the Republic of ÔÇőGabon, continuing Ôüáour long-standing trading relationship and contributing to the country's development agenda," Dave Gallagher, Trafigura's global head of structured finance, said in the Ôüástatement.
Trafigura ÔÇőhas also begun syndicating part of ÔÇőthe exposure to international financial institutions.

item.title: Iran can go up to two months without oil exports before cutting output, analysts say
item.description:
LONDON, April 15 (Reuters) - Iran can withstand a complete halt ÔÇőin oil exports of up to two months before being forced to curb production, ÔÇîanalysts said, after the U.S. began blocking shipping in and out of the country's ports on April 13.
The blockade could prevent roughly 2 million barrels per day (bpd) of Iranian crude from reaching its main buyer China.
Any Iranian production shutdowns would add ÔÇőto more than 12 million bpd of supply already disrupted by the regional war, tightening markets ÔÇőfurther and lifting oil prices.
With its exports blocked, Iran faces having to divert crude Ôüáinto onshore storage tanks. Once those tanks are filled, the OPEC member would be required to curb ÔÇőupstream output.
Consultancy FGE NextantECA estimates Iran has about 90 million barrels of available onshore crude storage capacity, ÔÇőout of total capacity of roughly 122 million barrels.
"Iran can sustain current production of around 3.5 million bpd for roughly two months without exports, extendable to around three months with a modest 500,000 bpd production cut," FGE NextantECA said in ÔÇőa note.
Iranian domestic refineries process about 2 million bpd of oil, they added.
The relevant Iranian authorities were ÔÇőnot immediately available for comment.
Energy Aspects assumes significantly lower available onshore storage of about 30 million barrels, based on ÔÇîdata from ÔüáKayrros.
Under that scenario, Iran could maintain current export levels for about 16 days before storage capacity runs out, based on export levels of 1.8 million bpd.
"The blockade may not have a significant impact on Iranian production in April, but if it continues into May then output would need to be reduced substantially," ÔÇősaid Richard Bronze, co-founder ÔÇőof Energy Aspects.
He said Ôüáthe consultancy assumes Iran cannot utilise its full nameplate storage capacity, adding that historic data show stocks peaked at 92 million barrels in May 2020, which ÔÇőlikely marks a realistic ceiling.
Bronze also said Iran will likely deploy available oil ÔÇőtankers in Ôüáports as floating storage, delaying production cuts.
The U.S. military said more vessels were being turned back under the blockade, including the Chinese-owned tanker Rich Starry, which is under U.S. sanctions and which was seen heading back through the ÔüáStrait ÔÇőof Hormuz on Wednesday.
Eight Iran-linked oil tankers have been intercepted since ÔÇőthe blockade began on Monday, the Wall Street Journal reported. A U.S. destroyer stopped two tankers attempting to leave Iran's Chabahar ÔÇőport on the Gulf of Oman on Tuesday, a U.S. official said.

item.title: IMF chief says 12 or more countries seeking loans to cope with Middle East war energy shock
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The International Monetary Fund expects at least a dozen countries to seek new loan programs to cope with surging energy prices and ÔÇősupply chain disruptions caused by the Middle East war , with several sub-Saharan African nations seeking help, the head of the global crisis lender said on Wednesday.
IMF Managing Director Kristalina ÔÇîGeorgieva also warned of deepening supply disruptions from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz even if the conflict ends quickly and urged countries to take measures to reduce their fuel usage.
Speaking at a press conference during the IMF and World Bank spring meetings in Washington, Georgieva repeated her estimate that disruptions from the war could trigger new demand for $20 billion to $50 billion in financial support that could include new loans and augmentation of some of the global lender's ÔÇő39 existing country financing programs.
She did not name specific countries that have requested aid, although she said the IMF was not currently discussing an augmentation of Egypt's $8 billion loan program despite ÔÇőthe war's impact on its economy.
IMF strategy chief Christian Mummsen said the needs estimate was developed before the start of the meetings and could Ôüábe expanded after bilateral meetings with finance officials from the IMF's member countries.
"This is preliminary. We're still taking stock," Mummsen said, adding that the list of countries seeking help would likely expand beyond ÔÇőa dozen.
Georgieva said she was concerned about the physical breakdown of supply chains, especially for Asian countries dependent on oil, natural gas, naphtha, helium, fertilizer and other inputs from Gulf countries.
She said ÔÇősuch disruptions are "not going to evaporate overnight, even if the war ends tomorrow. Why? Because a tanker is a slow-moving vessel, it would take 40 days to get all the way to Fiji. So we need to be prepared that the impact of the supply disruptions in the weeks ahead is going to be deeper."
The IMF already has said global economic conditions are worsening beyond those that informed the relatively mild cut in growth it projected on Tuesday in ÔÇőits updated World Economic Outlook. The global lender's 3.1% forecast for 2026 was based on a swift end to the conflict and drop in oil prices.
Instead, the IMF's chief economist, Pierre-Olivier Gourinchas, said ÔÇőthe global economy was now "drifting" past that forecast towards a more adverse scenario in the IMF's World Economic Outlook, with 2026 growth falling to 2.5% and oil prices averaging about $100 a barrel for the year.
In its worst-case, "severe scenario" of ÔÇîa deeper Ôüáand longer conflict, global growth falls to 2% to the brink of global recession.
With more shortages looming, Georgieva said countries should take measures to conserve energy use and create incentives to reduce the oil intensity of their economies, such as temporarily making public transport free.
She repeated the IMF's warnings against countries taking untargeted actions such as broad energy subsidies to offset the impact of higher prices, saying they would only "prolong the pain of high prices."
The IMF's Fiscal Monitor , released on Tuesday, also urged countries to refrain from subsidies and, instead, aid their citizens with targeted, temporary cash transfers for the most vulnerable that do not obscure ÔÇőhigher fuel prices and do not stoke demand.
IMF ÔÇőFiscal Affairs Director Rodrigo Valdes said broad fuel Ôüásubsidies would do that and shift supplies away from poorer countries, telling a press conference: "If you try to undo a supply shock by trying to prop up demand, you will end up with more inflation."
To prevent the war-driven energy shock from morphing into a 1970s-style runaway inflation problem, ÔÇőthe IMF has been urging central banks this week to stay vigilant for signs of wage-price spirals but not move right away to tighten ÔÇőmonetary policy and cool demand.
"What Ôüáwe tell central banks is, if you have high credibility, signal that your objective is to protect price stability, but don't rush," Georgieva said. "Wait to see how conditions would evolve."
Central banks with less credibility on controlling inflation may need to take stronger measures, she added, without naming specific countries.
Mummsen said financial markets had remained orderly, but some tightening had occurred for emerging markets and developing countries, where borrowing costs are already high.
"In a Ôüáworld where uncertainty ÔÇőis high, and shocks keep coming, our message is basically A, economic fundamentals matter and B, policy agility is key," ÔÇőhe said.
Mummsen said delays or cancellation of fertilizer shipments were hitting developing countries particularly hard, with some estimates predicting that 45 million more people will now face food insecurity. He said higher food costs hit low-income countries harder since they spent ÔÇőabout 36% of their consumption on food, while the rate was about 20% for emerging markets and just 9% for advanced economies.

item.title: Former Brazil spy chief freed in the US after ICE detention, Bolsonaro ally says
item.description:
SAO PAULO, April 15 (Reuters) - Former Brazilian intelligence chief ÔÇőAlexandre Ramagem has ÔÇîbeen freed in the United States, said Paulo ÔÇőFigueiredo, an ally ÔÇőof former President Jair ÔüáBolsonaro who lives ÔÇőin the U.S., on ÔÇőWednesday.
Ramagem was also no longer listed on the website ÔÇőof U.S. Immigration ÔÇőand Customs Enforcement among people ÔÇîdetained Ôüáby the entity. Reuters was not immediately able to determine his ÔÇőimmigration ÔÇőstatus Ôüáin the country.
Ramagem, who fled Brazil ÔÇőin September following his ÔÇőconviction Ôüáfor plotting a coup with Bolsonaro, had been Ôüádetained ÔÇőby ICE ÔÇőon Monday.

item.title: Australia's Viva Energy says refinery units operating at minimum rates after fire
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 16 (Reuters) - Australia's Viva Energy (VEA.AX) , opens new tab said on ÔÇőThursday that most ÔÇîunits at its Geelong refinery were still ÔÇőoperating and processing ÔÇőfuel though at minimum Ôüárates to maintain ÔÇősafety across the site ÔÇőafter a fire overnight.
"We'll only start increasing production ÔÇőagain once we're ÔÇőconfident that we can do ÔÇîthat Ôüásafely," CEO Scott Wyatt told reporters.
Firefighters were battling a ÔÇőfire at ÔÇőViva's Ôüámotor gasoline or MOGAS production ÔÇőunit at the ÔÇőGeelong Ôüárefinery in Victoria state, which processes Ôüácrude ÔÇőinto petrol.

item.title: Colombia announces renewable energy auction
item.description:
BOGOTA, April 15 (Reuters) - Colombian Energy ÔÇőMinister Edwin Palma on Wednesday announced an upcoming renewable ÔÇîenergy auction, saying that he would travel to the United States next week to promote it.
ÔÇťWe will be ÔÇőin New York on Monday and Tuesday ÔÇődiscussing energy and promoting the renewable energy Ôüáauction, which we hope will be on the ÔÇőmarket soon,ÔÇŁ Palma said during a conference in ÔÇőthe city of Cartagena, without providing further details.
Palma also proposed an open-season auction for regasification projects, which the ministry ÔÇősaid would be a transparent and competitive ÔÇőmechanism to secure firm gas-sector capacity, support new infrastructure and ÔÇîstrengthen Ôüáthe reliability of the energy system.
Open-season auctions are used in the energy and infrastructure sectors to measure demand before projects are developed, helping ensure ÔÇőfinancial viability ÔÇőand efficient Ôüácapacity allocation, especially in natural gas transportation and regasification.
ÔÇťThis will allow us ÔÇőto ensure sufficient backup for the system ÔÇőand Ôüáfacilitate the integration of clean energy,ÔÇŁ Palma said.
Colombia is facing a gas production deficit, as production Ôüáis ÔÇőinsufficient to meet domestic demand, ÔÇőforcing the South American country to increase fuel imports.

item.title: Permian-focused EagleRock plans rare US oil and gas IPO, sources say
item.description:
NEW YORK, April 15 (Reuters) - EagleRock, a company that collects royalties and fees from oil and gas ÔÇîproduction on the land it controls in the Permian Basin, is exploring a U.S. initial public offering that could value it at up to $2 billion, people familiar with the matter said.
The Houston-based firm has hired Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab on the potential rare U.S. oil and gas listing, four of ÔÇőthe sources said, with two adding it could come as soon as the second quarter.
The listing plan comes as ÔÇőcrude prices have soared due to the conflict in the Middle East . The turmoil has triggered one Ôüáof the greatest-ever global energy shocks and boosted the appeal of U.S. assets, which have continued to move oil and gas unencumbered ÔÇőby the war.
EagleRock owns and controls land in the Delaware and Midland portions of the Permian, the heart of the U.S. ÔÇőshale industry in Texas and New Mexico, as well as infrastructure to handle water used in the hydraulic fracturing process, according to its website.
By holding surface rights, it collects royalties from energy companies operating on its land, allowing EagleRock to earn revenue without producing.
Volatile energy and stock markets mean ÔÇőthe IPO's timing and the value EagleRock achieves could fluctuate significantly, with the sources forecasting a valuation of between $1 billion ÔÇőand $2 billion. They cautioned that plans remain subject to change, and spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss confidential deliberations.
EagleRock did not respond to ÔÇîa Ôüácomment request. Goldman declined to comment.
Should EagleRock's listing proceed, it would mark a rare U.S. oil and gas IPO. For many years, investors' prioritization of environmental concerns limited appetite for new listings, while private owners found buyers among listed competitors eager for additional resources. While the sale option remains, demand for natural gas to support rising power generation, especially for artificial intelligence ÔÇőinfrastructure, is drawing many investors back ÔÇőto oil and gas.
Logical Ôüápublic comparisons for EagleRock would be Permian-focused LandBridge (LB.N) , opens new tab , and its water infrastructure affiliate WaterBridge (WBI.N) , opens new tab . LandBridge shares have gained almost 300% since its June 2024 IPO, while WaterBridge is up 26% since listing in ÔÇőSeptember.
EagleRock's website does not disclose its ownership. Sources said the company's portfolio combines oil and ÔÇőgas assets from Ôüáa handful of entrepreneurs. They include Greg Mabee, part of the famed Texas oil family that owns one of the largest ranches within the Permian, according to one of the sources. Mabee did not respond to a comment request.
Among the management listed on EagleRock's website Ôüáare CEO ÔÇőGreg Pipkin Jr., who previously led corporate strategy at Infinity Natural Resources (INR.N) , opens new tab , ÔÇőwhich itself listed in January last year, and Chief Financial Officer Neal Shah, who held the same role at Pioneer Natural Resources when it was bought ÔÇőfor $60 billion by Exxon Mobil in 2024.

item.title: Nuclear reactor developer XÔÇĹEnergy targets $7.5 billion valuation in US IPO
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Amazon-backed (AMZN.O) , opens new tab X-Energy is targeting a valuation of up to $7.51 billion in its initial public offering ÔÇőin the United States, the nuclear reactor developer said on Wednesday.
The ÔÇîMaryland-based company is seeking up to $814.3 million by offering roughly 42.9 million shares priced between $16 and $19 apiece.
The IPO roadshow launch coincides with the S&P 500 reaching record closing highs, driven by ÔÇőstrong corporate earnings and optimism in U.S.-Iran negotiations.
"We've seen the IPO ÔÇőwindow slam shut before, so when companies see an opportunity, they Ôüátake it," said Matt Kennedy, senior ÔÇŹstrategist at Renaissance Capital, a provider of IPO-focused ÔÇőresearch and ETFs.
A choppy market in February and March, weighed down by AI ÔÇődisruption worries and geopolitical risks in the Middle East, slowed new listings.
"This is not yet a broad reopening, which is obvious from the glaring lack of tech issues on the ÔÇőcalendar. Still, it's a step in that direction," Kennedy added.
Nuclear energy is experiencing ÔÇőa resurgence, driven by surging electricity demand from hyperscalers that operate massive cloud-computing infrastructure to ÔÇîmanage Ôüárising AI processing.
Morgan Stanley reports global electricity demand is projected to rise more than 1 trillion kWh annually through 2030, with data centers anticipated to contribute nearly 20% of this growth.
Founded in 2009, X-Energy designs nuclear reactor technology known as ÔÇősmall modular reactors (SMR) ÔÇőand manufactures nuclear Ôüáfuels.
SMRs are more compact and cost-effective compared to full-sized reactors that often take decades to build.
Cathie Wood's ARK Investment Management ÔÇőand its affiliated entities have indicated an interest in ÔÇőpurchasing up Ôüáto $105 million of shares from X-Energy's offering.
XÔÇĹEnergy had previously planned to go public via an Ares Management (ARES.N) , opens new tab -backed blank-check firm in 2023, but later decided not to proceed, citing Ôüátough ÔÇőmarket conditions.
J.P. Morgan, Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, and Moelis ÔÇőare acting as the lead joint book-running managers. XÔÇĹEnergy will list on the Nasdaq under the ÔÇősymbol "XE".

item.title: Two children dead,┬áRussia's Tuapse port area hit in Ukrainian drone attacks, governor says
item.description:
MOSCOW, April 16 - Two children aged ÔÇő5 and 14 ÔÇîhave been killed and a ÔÇőnumber of ÔÇőenterprises in the area Ôüáof Russia's ÔÇőBlack Sea port ÔÇőof Tuapse were hit in Ukrainian drone ÔÇőattacks, local ÔÇőgovernor Veniamin Kondratiev said ÔÇîon ÔüáThursday.
In his post on the Telegram messaging ÔÇőapp, ÔÇőhe Ôüáalso said several residential ÔÇőhouses were ÔÇődamaged Ôüáin the massive drone attacks Ôüáon ÔÇőthe region.

item.title: Hopes for Middle East peace grow as Israel discusses Lebanon ceasefire
item.description:
WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, April 16 (Reuters) - Optimism grew on Thursday that the war in the Middle East may be near an end, with a key Pakistani mediator in Tehran and the administration of President Donald Trump talking up hopes for a deal that would open the ÔÇőcrucial Strait of Hormuz.
Israel's cabinet met on Wednesday to discuss a possible ceasefire in neighbouring Lebanon, a senior Israeli official said, more than six weeks into its war with Iran-backed Hezbollah. ÔÇîAccording to Lebanese officials, a ceasefire could be announced soon, the Financial Times reported.
Ending the fighting in Lebanon was a key sticking point in earlier peace talks, along with how to deal with Tehran's nuclear ambitions.
"We feel good about the prospects of a deal," White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said at a news conference on Wednesday, calling conversations mediated by Pakistan "productive and ongoing." She denied reports ÔÇőthat the U.S. had formally requested an extension of a two-week ceasefire agreed by the two sides on April 8.
More in-person talks had not yet been Ôüáconfirmed but would likely take place in Pakistan again, Leavitt said.
Pakistan's military confirmed Munir had arrived in Tehran. A senior Iranian source told Reuters that Munir, who had mediated the last round of talks, would seek "to ÔÇőnarrow gaps" between the two sides. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi posted on X welcoming Munir and said Tehran was committed to "promoting peace and stability in the region."
The talks last weekend broke down without an agreement to ÔÇőend the war, which Trump began alongside Israel on February 28, triggering Iranian attacks on Iran's Gulf neighbours as well as reigniting the Israel-Hezbollah conflict.
Stock markets have rallied strongly in recent days on expectations of a swift resolution to the fighting, with indexes on Wall Street hitting record highs on Wednesday as crude oil prices steadied.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent predicted that China's purchase of Iranian oil would "pause" given the U.S. blockade on vessels calling at Iranian ports. He said the U.S. could ÔÇőimpose secondary sanctions on countries that purchase Iranian crude.
The U.S. Treasury had warned two Chinese banks not to process Iranian money or face sanctions, he said, without naming the banks. China previously bought more than 80% ÔÇőof Iran's shipped oil.
Trump said on Wednesday he had told Chinese President Xi Jinping not to give Iran weapons , which Xi had said he wasn't. Trump also said that China was very happy he was "permanently opening" the Strait of Hormuz.
"I am ÔÇîdoing it for Ôüáthem, also - And the World," he wrote on social media, adding: "President Xi will give me a big, fat, hug when I get there in a few weeks."
The war has led Iran to effectively shut the Strait - a vital artery for global crude and gas shipments - to ships other than its own, sharply reducing exports from the Gulf and leaving energy importers scrambling for alternative supplies.
During the first 48 hours of the U.S. blockade on ships entering and exiting Iranian ports, no vessels have made it past U.S. forces, the U.S. military said. Additionally, nine vessels complied with direction from U.S. forces to turn around and return toward an Iranian port ÔÇőor coastal area.
However, Iran's Fars News agency said an ÔÇőIranian supertanker subject to U.S. sanctions crossed the Ôüástrait towards Iran's Imam Khomeini port despite the blockade. Fars did not identify the tanker or give further details of its voyage.
Iran's joint military command warned it would halt trade flows in the Gulf, the Sea of Oman and the Red Sea - which connects to the Suez Canal - if the U.S. blockade continued.
Iran could consider allowing ÔÇőships to sail freely through the Omani side of the strait without risk of attack as part of proposals it has offered in negotiations with the ÔÇőU.S., providing a deal is Ôüáclinched to prevent renewed conflict, a source briefed by Tehran said.
Trump has also threatened to escalate if the war resumes.
"We could take out every one of their bridges in one hour. We could take out every one of their power plants, electric power plants, in one hour. We don't want to do that...so we'll see what happens," he told Fox Business Network.
Iran's nuclear ambitions were a key sticking point at last weekend's talks. The ÔüáU.S. proposed a ÔÇő20-year suspension of all nuclear activity by Iran - an apparent concession from longstanding demands for a permanent ban - while Tehran suggested a ÔÇőhalt of three to five years, according to people familiar with the proposals.
Washington has also pressed for any enriched nuclear material to be removed from Iran, while Tehran has demanded that international sanctions against it be lifted.
One source involved in the talks said back-channel discussions ÔÇőhad made progress in narrowing gaps, bringing the two sides closer to a deal that could be presented at a new round of talks.

item.title: Russian missiles hit Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, three dead
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Russian forces attacked the Ukrainian capital Kyiv and other cities early on Thursday, killing three people, including ÔÇőa 12-year-old child, injuring more than 20 and badly damaging ÔÇîbuildings, officials said.
In Kyiv, Mayor Vitali Klitschko said two people, including the child, had died. In the southeastern city of Dnipro, where Russian attacks set ÔÇőresidential buildings ablaze, the regional governor said one person was killed.
"At ÔÇőresult of the enemy attack on the capital, two Ôüápeople have been killed, a 12-year-old boy and a 35-year-old woman," Klitschko ÔÇőwrote on Telegram. "Ten residents have been injured. Six are being treated in ÔÇőhospital."
Tymur Tkachenko, head of the capital's military administration put the injury toll in the city at 18, including a child.
An air raid alert remained in ÔÇőeffect in both Kyiv and Dnipro more than two hours after ÔÇőit had been imposed in the capital.
Photos posted on line showed fires burning ÔÇîout Ôüáof control and smoke billowing skyward.
Klitschko said rescue teams had rescued a mother and child from a building in a central district where the ground floor was badly damaged.
He also said a missile had hit the ÔÇősixth floor of ÔÇőan apartment building Ôüáin the central Podil district.
Klitschko said a large fire had broken out in a building in a ÔÇődistrict in the north of the capital and four ÔÇőemergency medical Ôüáworkers were injured, while debris had fallen in several locations.
In Dnipro, Regional Governor Oleksandr Ganzha wrote on Telegram that one resident had died. He listed 10 people as Ôüáinjured and ÔÇőposted pictures showing residential buildings ablaze.
In Kharkiv, ÔÇőUkraine's second largest city in the northeast, officials said two people had been injured in ÔÇődrone strikes.
(This story has been refiled to add the dropped word 'and' in the headline)

item.title: US Senate Republicans back Trump military sales to Israel
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate on Wednesday blocked two resolutions that would have stopped the sale ÔÇîof some $450 million in bombs and bulldozers to Israel, as President Donald Trump's fellow Republicans rallied behind his support for the Jewish state.
But support for the resolutions from a large majority of the 47-member Senate Democratic caucus underscored growing frustration within that party about the effect on civilians ÔÇőfrom Israeli strikes on Gaza, Lebanon and Iran .
A decades-long tradition of strong bipartisan support for Israel in the ÔÇőU.S. Congress means resolutions to stop weapons sales are unlikely to pass, but backers Ôüáhope raising the issue will encourage Israel's government and U.S. administrations to do more to protect civilians.
Supporters of ÔÇőthe sales say Israel is an important ally to whom the United States should sell military equipment.
Senator Bernie Sanders ÔÇőof Vermont, an independent who caucuses with Democrats, forced votes on the resolutions, saying the sales violate criteria for foreign assistance in the Foreign Assistance Act and the Arms Export Control Act.
The first resolution would have prohibited the $295 million sale of D9R ÔÇőand D9T Caterpillar bulldozers, parts and other support. The vote was 59 to 40 against advancing the measure.
Seven ÔÇőDemocrats voted with every Republican against advancing the resolution of disapproval of the bulldozer sale. Republican Senator Cynthia Lummis of Wyoming ÔÇîdid not Ôüávote.
The second would have prohibited the $151.8 million sale of 12,000 BLU-110A/B general purpose 1,000-pound "dumb" bombs and related logistics and technical support services.
Eleven Democrats joined every Republican to block the measure by 63 to 36. Republican Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina did not vote.
Israel uses the bombs in attacks on Gaza and Lebanon and uses the bulldozers to ÔÇődemolish homes in Gaza, Lebanon ÔÇőand the West Bank, ÔüáSanders said.
"The United States must use the leverage we have - tens of billions in arms and military aid - to demand that Israel ends these atrocities," he said, urging ÔÇősupport for the resolutions.
Israel says it does not intentionally target civilians, and that its ÔÇőstrikes are Ôüáintended to neutralize militants and military infrastructure.
Wednesday's vote showed an uptick in support for efforts to limit military sales to Israel. In July, two resolutions that would have blocked arms sales in response to civilian casualties in Gaza were blocked Ôüáin the ÔÇőSenate.
Also introduced by Sanders, they failed by 73 to 24 and 70 ÔÇőto 27 in the 100-member chamber.
The Trump administration bypassed the normal congressional review of military sales early in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, saying there ÔÇőwas an emergency that made it necessary to immediately transfer the weapons.

item.title: US-sanctioned supertankers enter Gulf despite blockade
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - A second U.S.-sanctioned supertanker ÔÇőhas entered the Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz, shipping data showed, despite ÔÇîa U.S. blockade on vessels visiting Iranian ports.
U.S. President Donald Trump announced the blockade on Sunday after weekend peace talks in Islamabad between the U.S. and Iran failed to reach a deal .
The U.S. Central Command said on X that 10 ÔÇővessels have been turned around and no ships have broken through since the start of ÔÇőthe blockade on Monday.
Still, Iran's Fars News Agency said on Wednesday that an Iranian Ôüásupertanker subject to U.S. sanctions crossed the strait towards Iran's Imam Khomeini port despite the blockade. ÔÇőFars did not identify the tanker or give further details of its voyage.
The empty Very Large Crude ÔÇőCarrier (VLCC) RHN entered the Gulf on Wednesday, data from LSEG and Kpler showed. It was not immediately clear where the VLCC, which is capable of carrying 2 million barrels of oil, is heading.
The tanker's entry into the Gulf ÔÇőcomes a day after U.S.-sanctioned VLCC Alicia passed through the Strait of Hormuz. The Alicia is ÔÇőheading to Iraq, Kpler data showed.
Both tankers have records of carrying Iranian oil in the past few years, according ÔÇîto ÔüáKpler data.
Vessels that have been forced to turn back include the U.S.-sanctioned tanker Rich Starry , which returned to the Gulf on Wednesday, a day after exiting.
The U.S. has warned it could add secondary sanctions on buyers of Iranian oil in an apparent effort to gain leverage ahead of more negotiations, just weeks ÔÇőafter Washington loosened the ÔÇőenforcement of some Iran Ôüáenergy sanctions.
Iran could consider allowing ships to sail freely through the Omani side of the Strait of Hormuz without risk of attack as part of ÔÇőproposals it has offered in negotiations with the U.S., providing a deal is ÔÇőclinched to Ôüáprevent renewed conflict, a source briefed by Tehran said.
The U.S. blockade is expected to reduce Iran's crude exports, although the OPEC producer could sustain its current production at 3.5 million barrels per day (bpd) for weeks Ôüáby ÔÇőstoring oil in onshore tanks, analysts say.
Iran exported 1.84 million ÔÇőbpd of crude in March and has shipped 1.71 million bpd thus far in April, compared with an average of 1.68 ÔÇőmillion bpd in 2025, according to Kpler data.

item.title: Former US Marine pilot loses appeal against┬áextradition from Australia
item.description:
SYDNEY, April 16 (Reuters) - A former ÔÇőU.S. Marine Corps pilot on Thursday lost an appeal against his extradition from Australia ÔÇîto the United States on charges of violating U.S. arms control laws while training Chinese pilots.
Daniel Duggan, a naturalised Australian citizen, was arrested in the state of New South Wales in October 2022 by Australian Federal Police on a ÔÇőU.S. request, shortly after returning from China, where he had lived since 2014.
In December ÔÇő2024, Australia's then attorney-general Mark Dreyfus approved a U.S. extradition request for him.
Duggan challenged the extradition Ôüádecision, arguing it was invalid because the attorney-general made legal errors by failing to properly apply the ÔÇőcountry's Extradition Act in light of the extradition treaty between Australia and the United States.
But Federal Court of ÔÇőAustralia Judge James Stellios dismissed his appeal on Thursday, finding no errors in the decision.
"I am not persuaded that the impugned decisions were infected by jurisdictional error. Therefore, the application must be dismissed," he said.
A spokesperson for Attorney-General Michelle Rowland said ÔÇőthe government noted the Federal Court's ruling.
"Mr Duggan will remain in extradition custody in Australia until his surrender ÔÇőto the United States of America," they said in a statement.
Duggan, who has six children in Australia, has been ÔÇîin custody Ôüásince his arrest.
His wife, Saffrine, told public broadcaster ABC that the family was disappointed by the ruling but would consider its options. "Make no mistake: we will not give up," she said. An appeal can be lodged within 28 days.
The U.S. sought Duggan's extradition for four offences linked to the ÔÇőtraining of Chinese pilots ÔÇőin South Africa between Ôüá2009 and 2012.
The charges include one count of conspiring to violate U.S. arms export laws and defraud the U.S., which carries a five-year jail term, two arms ÔÇőexport control violations each carrying potential 20ÔÇĹyear prison terms, and one count ÔÇőof conspiracy to Ôüácommit money laundering, also punishable by up to 20 years in jail.
Duggan's legal team had argued that his conduct was not an offence in Australia at the time or when the U.S. requested extradition and so Ôüádid not ÔÇőmeet the requirement for "dual criminality" in Australia's extradition treaty with ÔÇőthe United States.
The court said the treaty did not modify the Extradition Act to require "dual criminality", and therefore did not require ÔÇőAustralia to refuse the extradition request from the U.S.

item.title: Chile's Kast unveils slew of reforms including corporate tax rate cut
item.description:
SANTIAGO, April 15 (Reuters) - Chile's new president, Jose Antonio Kast, ÔÇîunveiled on Wednesday details of a long-awaited reform package that includes cuts to the corporate tax rate, as his government seeks to revive growth and promote job stability.
Some 40 measures were outlined in the package, with Kast ÔÇősaying that it had five main aims: to make Chile more tax competitive, strengthen formal ÔÇőemployment, streamline regulations, provide more legal and regulatory certainty and exercise restraint in Ôüápublic spending.
"This bill is not an ideological agenda. It is a concrete response to ... real emergencies," ÔÇőKast said in his first address to the nation since taking office last month, urging members ÔÇőof Congress to pass the measures quickly.
The right-wing leader has painted Chile, the world's largest copper producer, as a country riddled with organized crime and weak finances.
His government has said it wants to lift Chile's economic annual growth ÔÇőto around 4% from last year's 2.5%, though analysts have expressed skepticism about whether the goal ÔÇőis realistic.
Kast also lacks a congressional majority, which could hamper his ability to deliver his agenda. Allied right-wing ÔÇîblocs hold Ôüájust 76 of 155 lower-house seats and 25 of 50 Senate seats.
The centerpiece of the bill is proposed gradual cuts in the corporate tax rate to 23% from 27%, which his government has previously said would be over four years. Some opposition members have, however, argued that the real ÔÇőimpact of a corporate ÔÇőtax cut remains unclear.
Other Ôüátax measures include the creation of a tax credit for wage payments, designed to encourage many smaller companies to pay employees on the books instead ÔÇőof under the table.
"This injects $1.4 billion annually into the productive sector, benefits ÔÇő235,000 SMEs (representing Ôüá86% of the credit's recipients), and protects more than 4 million workers. Formal employment will no longer be a penalty but an advantage," he said.
Other reforms include steps to speed up the issuing of Ôüáenvironmental permits ÔÇőfor projects such as mines, a temporary VAT exemption on ÔÇősales of new homes, 400 billion pesos ($450 million) in funds for fire-hit regions and an exemption for homeowners over 65 ÔÇőfrom property taxes on their primary residence.
($1 = 884.0000 Chilean pesos)

item.title: US military says its strike in Eastern Pacific killed 3
item.description:
WASHINGTON, April 15 (Reuters) - The U.S. military ÔÇősaid on Wednesday ÔÇîit conducted a strike on ÔÇőa vessel ÔÇőin the Eastern ÔüáPacific that ÔÇőkilled 3 people.
The ÔÇőU.S. Southern Command alleged that the ÔÇővessel was ÔÇőoperated by "Designated Terrorist Organizations" ÔÇîthat Ôüáit did not identify.
It added that no ÔÇőU.S. ÔÇőmilitary Ôüáforces were harmed and ÔÇődescribed those ÔÇőkilled Ôüáas "male narco-terrorists," without offering details.

item.title: South Korea, Vietnam leaders to meet in Hanoi next week, sources say
item.description:
HANOI, April 16 (Reuters) - Vietnam's president To Lam is set to meet his South Korean counterpart Lee Jae Myung in Hanoi next week, five people familiar with the matter said.
