Lee's visit would ÔÇőbe the first by a foreign leader to Vietnam since Lam was elected ÔÇîpresident last week, a move that allows him to combine the post with the leadership of the Communist Party, the most powerful job in the one-party state.
It would not be however Lam's first top-level meeting in his ÔÇőnew position, as he met China's President Xi Jinping in Beijing on Wednesday.
The meeting ÔÇőis currently scheduled for April 23, the sources said.
Government cooperation agreements are Ôüálikely to be signed. Seoul is also interested in some of Vietnam's largest infrastructure projects, ÔÇősuch as railways and nuclear power plants, but no major decisions are expected in those areas, ÔÇőthey said.
South Korea's foreign ministry declined to comment. Vietnam's foreign ministry did not respond to a request for comment.
South Korea is the largest investor in the Southeast Asian nation in terms of accumulated capital, with output from Samsung ÔÇőfactories alone accounting for more than one-tenth of the country's exports. Yet Korean investment pledges fell ÔÇőabout 25% last year from 2024, according to Vietnam's government data, amid trade uncertainties and concerns over Vietnam's regulations .
The ÔÇîmeeting would Ôüábe the leaders' second in about eight months, after Lam visited Seoul in August , the first foreign leader hosted by Lee after he was elected president in Seoul.
Lee will be accompanied by a large business delegation including officials from Samsung, with multiple corporate agreements expected to be signed, ÔÇőpossibly on April 23, ÔÇőofficials have said without Ôüáelaborating on the possible deals.
Samsung's talks with Vietnamese authorities to build a back-end semiconductor factory have made progress after years of negotiations, but it ÔÇőremains unclear when any announcement could be made, three people familiar ÔÇőwith the matter Ôüásaid.
Reports of a planned $4 billion investment in a semiconductor testing, and possibly packaging, facility were removed from Vietnamese media in recent days.
The three people said Samsung's potential outlay had not been finalised. The total Ôüáinitial ÔÇőinvestment would be smaller and could expand over time, potentially ÔÇőincluding a cluster of suppliers, one of them said.
Samsung declined to comment.

item.title: Nepal panel to probe property, assets of politicians and officials
item.description:
KATHMANDU, April 16 (Reuters) - Nepal's new government, led by rapper-turned politician Balendra Shah, has set up a panel ÔÇőto investigate the property and assets of past and ÔÇîpresent politicians and officials, a move aimed at controlling corruption in the Himalayan nation.
Shah, 35, became prime minister after his Rastriya Swatantra Party (RSP) ÔÇőscored a sweeping victory in the March 5 ÔÇőparliamentary election ÔÇô the country's first vote after the Ôüáanti-graft ÔÇśGen ZÔÇŁ protests last September.
During his three-year stint ÔÇőas mayor of Kathmandu, NepalÔÇÖs capital city, Shah gained popularity ÔÇőfor his fight against corruption and reformist credentials.
Sasmit Pokhrel, a cabinet spokesperson, said the five-member panel would be headed by Rajendra Kumar Bhandari, ÔÇőa retired Supreme Court judge.
ÔÇťAn impartial investigation will be ÔÇőcarried out on the basis of evidence based on legal standards ÔÇŽ Its ÔÇîreport Ôüáand recommendations will be implemented by concerned agencies of the government,ÔÇŁ Pokhrel told reporters after a cabinet meeting late on Wednesday without mentioning the time frame given ÔÇőto the panel ÔÇőto complete Ôüáwork.
The probe is expected to cover hundreds of politicians and officials who held public ÔÇőoffices after the popular movement that led ÔÇőto Ôüáthe abolition of the 239-year-old monarchy in 2008, analysts said.
The three-year-old RSP had made corruption control one of its Ôüámajor promises ÔÇőduring the election and scored ÔÇőa comfortable victory over the parties that had dominated politics in the ÔÇőcountry for decadessuffered.

item.title: Ukraine says it downs 31 missiles, 636 drones in Russian attacks over past 24 hours
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Ukraine's air force downed or ÔÇőneutralised 31 Russian ÔÇîmissiles and 636 drones in ÔÇőattacks at the ÔÇőcountry over the Ôüápast 24 hours, ÔÇőit said on ÔÇőThursday.
"During this period, the enemy launched two ÔÇőwaves of ÔÇőcombined attacks on Ukrainian territory, ÔÇîusing Ôüáground-based and air-launched missiles, as well as attack ÔÇődrones," ÔÇőthe Ôüáair force said in ÔÇőa statement ÔÇőon ÔüáTelegram.
Overall, it detected 703 Russian aerial Ôüátargets, ÔÇőit ÔÇőadded.

item.title: Can Iran legally impose tolls on the Strait of Hormuz?
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Tehran has sought to tighten its grip over the Strait of Hormuz by charging tolls on vessels to ensure safe passage, in conjunction with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.
The following explains law governing toll collections and ÔÇőactions that countries opposed to tolls might take.
The Strait of Hormuz is ÔÇîa waterway connecting the Gulf with the Gulf of Oman, and located within Iran's and Oman's territorial waters. It is perhaps the world's most important energy shipping lane. About 20% of the world's oil passes through it.
The waterway is about 104 miles (167 km) long. Its width varies, ÔÇőand at its narrowest point provides 2-mile channels for inbound and outbound shipping, separated by a 2-mile buffer ÔÇőzone.
Iran effectively closed the strait following U.S.-Israeli strikes on the country, and has demanded a right Ôüáto collect tolls as a precondition to ending the war. The status of any toll collections so far could not ÔÇőimmediately be confirmed.
The UN Convention on the Law of the Sea , opens new tab , sometimes known as UNCLOS, was ÔÇőadopted in 1982 and has been in force since 1994.
Article 38 provides vessels a right of unimpeded "transit passage" through more than 100 straits worldwide, including the Strait of Hormuz.
The treaty allows a country bordering a strait to regulate passage within its "territorial sea," up to 12 nautical ÔÇőmiles from its border, but shall permit "innocent passage."
Passage is innocent if it is not prejudicial to a country's peace, good ÔÇőorder and security. Military action, serious pollution, spying and fishing are not permitted. The concept of innocent passage was key to a 1949 International Court ÔÇîof ÔüáJustice case concerning the Corfu Channel, along the coasts of Albania and Greece.
Approximately 170 countries and the European Union have ratified UNCLOS. Iran and the United States have not. This raises the question of whether the treaty's rules affording freedom of maritime navigation have become part of customary international law, or bind only ratifying countries.
Experts say UNCLOS has become or is generally ÔÇőviewed as customary international law. ÔÇőSome non-ratifying countries may Ôüáargue that they need not follow the treaty because they persistently and consistently object. Iran has argued that it has made such objections. The United States disputes Iran's authority to charge ÔÇőtolls.
There is no formal mechanism to enforce UNCLOS. The International Tribunal for ÔÇőthe Law Ôüáof the Sea in Hamburg, Germany, which the treaty established, and the International Court of Justice in The Hague, Netherlands could issue rulings but cannot enforce them.
Countries and businesses have other potential means to counteract tolls.
A willing state or coalition of states could Ôüátry to ÔÇőenforce the treaty. The UN Security Council could pass a resolution opposing ÔÇőtolls.
Companies could redirect shipments away from the Strait of Hormuz, and have begun doing so. Countries could expand sanctions targeting financial transactions believed to benefit ÔÇőIran's government, by sanctioning companies willing to pay tolls.

item.title: Malaysia's Petronas to supply excess fuel to Australia as leaders vow closer energy ties
item.description:
SYDNEY/KUALA LUMPUR, April 16 (Reuters) - Malaysia Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said on Thursday that state energy firm ÔÇőPetronas (IPO-PETO.KL) , opens new tab would give priority to providing Australia with its excess fuel supplies, ÔÇîas the two countries sought to bolster energy security following the disruptions caused by the Middle East conflict .
Anwar said he had obtained an assurance from Petronas on the matter following discussions with his ÔÇőAustralian counterpart Anthony Albanese on boosting agricultural and fuel trade.
"The priority ÔÇőis for domestic requirements... but here is where negotiations among friends Ôüáare critical," Anwar said at a press conference after the meeting ÔÇőat his office in Malaysia's administrative capital Putrajaya.
Malaysia also requires mineral phosphates from ÔÇőAustralia, which it could offer in exchange for urea supplied to Canberra, Anwar said.
Malaysia is Albanese's third stop in Southeast Asia in the past week, following trips to Singapore and Brunei, as Canberra looks ÔÇőto shore up food and energy supplies.
Albanese said his government had secured ÔÇőan additional 100 million litres of diesel from two shipments, one from Brunei and ÔÇîone Ôüáfrom South Korea.
"This is the first of many expected shipments secured under the government's new strategic reserve powers with the support of Export Finance Australia," he said.
Australia also pledged to honour existing contracts with Malaysia, despite disruptions, Albanese ÔÇősaid.
Australia is a ÔÇőmajor supplier of Ôüánatural gas to Malaysia, which imports about 20% of its domestic supplies, as well as a provider of wheat, ÔÇőlamb and beef products.
Anwar and Albanese also witnessed the ÔÇősigning of Ôüáa memorandum of understanding between the two countries on halal meat trade, a deal that the Australian leader said would boost the country's red meat exports Ôüáand ÔÇősupport Malaysia's food security.
Majority-Muslim Malaysia is widely ÔÇőconsidered as a global leader in halal processing due to its established certification standards.

item.title: Aluminium in crisis: war, tariffs and a market running on empty
item.description:
LONDON, April 16 (Reuters) - The Iran war is triggering an unprecedented crisis in the global aluminium market with potentially devastating knock-on effects across sectors as diverse as construction, packaging, transport and green energy.
Even if the war were to end tomorrow, it could take up to a year for Emirates Global Aluminium to recover from ÔÇőthe damage inflicted by a missile strike on its Al Taweelah smelter last month.
Aluminium Bahrain (ALBH.BH) , opens new tab , the largest single-site production plant outside of China, has also been hit, although the extent ÔÇîof the damage is currently unknown. Alba had already reduced output prior to the attack, as had Qatar Aluminium, due to a shortage of power.
With shipping through the Strait of Hormuz severely constrained, the loss of production could rise further as smelters run through their stocks of raw materials.
The global market is looking at a supply deficit of up to 4 million metric tons this year, according to consultancy Wood Mackenzie.
Western buyers will bear the brunt of that massive ÔÇősupply hit and policymakers will face some hard choices in the weeks ahead if they want to cushion the impact.
In times gone by the market could have ÔÇőturned to the London Metal Exchange (LME) for extra metal. Registered inventory exceeded 5 million tons in the first part of the last decade.
LME stocks have Ôüásince shrunk to under 400,000 tons with another 100,000 tons sitting in the off-warrant category.
CME warehouses have also been raided. Total deliverable stocks have slumped by 70% since the start of the year ÔÇőand now total just 1,864 tons.
Even those figures flatter to deceive. Russian metal, which many Western users can't use due to sanctions imposed after the invasion of Ukraine , accounted for 270,000 tons of registered LME ÔÇőinventory at the end of March.
Traders have been tussling over the non-Russian component. Someone cancelled 98,000 tons of LME-registered Indian aluminium in the first week of March, only to re-warrant most of it last week as time-spreads exploded.
The benchmark cash-to-three-months spread flexed out to a backwardation of $95.50 per ton, the tightest the market's been since 2007.
There is idled smelter capacity, particularly in the U.S. and Europe, that could in theory be reactivated to help ÔÇőalleviate the squeeze on physical metal supplies.
However, most of this capacity was taken offline during previous energy crises. Smelters produce metal by electrolysis, and a typical one can use as much power as ÔÇőa city the size of Boston.
Given the impact of the Iran war on energy prices, it seems highly unlikely much, if any, of the mothballed capacity will return.
Indeed, the global shortage of affordable power was already ÔÇîforcing more closures Ôüáeven before the start of hostilities in the Gulf.
The Mozal aluminium smelter in Mozambique, majority owned and operated by Australia's South32 (S32.AX) , opens new tab , was placed on care and maintenance in March after the company failed to get an economically viable power supply contract.
Even allowing for greater recycled production and softer demand due to the energy hit on manufacturing activity, "there is no escaping a large deficit in the global aluminium market over the next 18 months," according to Wood Mackenzie.
That deficit is going to be felt most acutely in the West, which is going to force governments to make some unpalatable choices.
Two countries ÔÇőcould help alleviate the shortfall.
The first is China, ÔÇőthe world's largest aluminium producer. The problem Ôüáis that China tends to process most of its metal into semi-manufactured products such as rod, plate and wire.
The rest of the world has spent the last decade erecting trade barriers against the Chinese export flood, accusing Beijing of undermining competitors.
Western aluminium users need primary metal and alloy, not ÔÇőmore cheap Chinese product exports.
That leaves Russia, which produces both primary metal and the array of value-added alloys produced in the Gulf.
Japanese manufacturers are already ÔÇőshowing signs of returning to Russian Ôüásupply, having self-sanctioned after the 2022 invasion.
U.S. and European buyers would need government sanction waivers to follow suit.
The situation in the United States is compounded by President Donald Trump's decision to hike import aluminium tariffs to 50%.
That has sent the cost of imported ingot to over $2,500 per ton above the LME price , which is itself hovering at four-year highs of $3,580 per ton.
For now, this remains a price and cost equation. The longer the ÔüáGulf disruption runs, the ÔÇőfaster stocks deplete.
At some stage it may stop being a question of price altogether and become a question of simply having enough ÔÇőmetal to meet manufacturing orders.
(The opinions expressed here are those of Andy Home , a columnist for Reuters.)
Enjoying this column? Check out Reuters Open Interest (ROI), , opens new tab your essential new source for global financial commentary. Follow ROI on LinkedIn, , opens new tab and X. , opens new tab
And listen to the Morning Bid daily podcast on Apple , opens new tab , Spotify , opens new tab , ÔÇőor the Reuters app , opens new tab . Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.

item.title: China added more crude to its massive stockpile in March, but outlook shifts
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, April 16 (Reuters) - China continued to build the world's largest stockpile of crude oil in March even as the rest of the world started to draw on inventories to compensate for the loss of millions of barrels from the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz.
China, ÔÇőthe world's biggest crude importer, had surplus oil of 1.74 million barrels per day (bpd) in March, according to calculations based on official data.
For the first quarter ÔÇîChina's excess crude was 1.41 million bpd, down from the record high of 2.67 million bpd in December but up from the average of 1.13 million bpd for the whole of 2025.
China does not disclose the volumes of crude flowing into or out of its strategic and commercial stockpiles, but an estimate can be made by deducting the amount of oil processed from the total crude available from imports and domestic output.
It ÔÇőis worth noting that not all the surplus crude was likely to have been added to storage, with some being processed in plants not captured by the official ÔÇődata.
But even allowing for those gaps, it is clear that from March 2025 onwards, China has been importing crude at a far higher rate Ôüáthan necessary to meet domestic fuel demand.
China's imports of crude oil were 11.77 million bpd in March, while domestic output was 4.49 million bpd.
Refinery processing was 14.52 million bpd, leaving a surplus ÔÇőof 1.74 million bpd available for storage.
The continuing building of China's crude stockpiles in March came as imports were largely unaffected by the conflict in the Middle East, which started on February ÔÇő28 when the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
The oil delivered to China in March would have exited the Strait of Hormuz prior to the conflict's start, but imports from April onwards are likely to be affected.
This raises the question as to how China responds to lower crude imports from April onwards, which may be further constrained if the U.S. military is successful in executing President Donald Trump's ÔÇőorder to blockade vessels from Iranian ports.
China's seaborne crude imports in April are estimated by commodity analysts Kpler at 8.7 million bpd, and while this figure is likely to increase as ÔÇőmore cargoes are assessed as arriving prior to month end, it's still likely that arrivals will be the lowest since the 7.97 million bpd in August 2022.
China can tap some of its vast stockpile ÔÇîof crude oil Ôüáif it wants to keep domestic refinery processing around current rates.
The exact amount in both commercial and strategic inventories is not disclosed by Beijing, but analyst estimates are that it is at least 1.2 billion barrels.
If China did release up to 2 million bpd from reserves in order to maintain refinery throughput, it would take almost two years for the stockpile to deplete.
This is obviously an extreme situation and the reality is that crude oil flows from the Middle East will at some point return to levels close to what they were prior to the ÔÇőstart of the conflict.
This means China could easily ÔÇőmaintain refinery processing, and even increase it Ôüáto allow for exports of fuels, something Beijing has placed informal restrictions on since the start of the conflict.
China exported 1.19 million bpd of refined fuels in March, according to calculations based on official customs data, but this is likely to drop sharply in April, with Kpler ÔÇőtracking shipments of about 524,000 bpd for the month.
The loss of refined fuel exports from China has tightened an already stressed market ÔÇőfor products in Asia, with Ôüájet fuel and diesel both hitting record high prices during March.
It's likely that Beijing is expecting the Iran conflict to end in the coming weeks and that the flow of crude and products from the Middle East will resume.
However, if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for an extended period the risks mount for China.
While it can maintain domestic fuel supplies for an extended period because of Ôüáits huge crude ÔÇőreserves, if Asian neighbours experience shortages and their economies start to shut down it won't take long for ÔÇőthis to blow back on China's export-focused industries.

item.title: Spain's Repsol wins back control of Venezuelan oil operations, FT says
item.description:
April 16 (Reuters) - Spanish energy group Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab is ÔÇőpoised to take back operational control of its ÔÇîVenezuelan oil assets and boost production following a deal signed with the South American government, the Financial Times reported on ÔÇőThursday.
Repsol is expected to announce the agreement as ÔÇőearly as Thursday, FT added, citing a Ôüáperson familiar with the matter.
The agreement will include ÔÇőplans to triple production from its Venezuelan oil operations within ÔÇőthree years and establish a "guaranteed" payment system that will avoid previous pitfalls under which the capital city of Caracas failed ÔÇőto pay up, according to the report.
Reuters could ÔÇőnot immediately verify the report. Repsol did not immediately respond to ÔÇîReuters' Ôüárequest for a comment.
Venezuela holds one of the largest oil reserves in the world but has dilapidated energy infrastructure.
In 2023, Repsol reached an agreement with ÔÇőVenezuela to continue ÔÇőoperating its Ôüáfacilities there. The deal later lapsed after U.S. President Donald Trump revoked licences granted ÔÇőto Repsol and other Western companies to ÔÇőoperate Ôüáin the country.
After the U.S. captured President Nicolas Maduro in January, Washington eased sanctions on Venezuela's energy sector, Ôüáissuing general ÔÇőlicences that allow global energy ÔÇőcompanies to operate oil and gas projects in the OPEC member.

item.title: Thailand's PTT offers more fuel oil for May loading, sources say
item.description:
SINGAPORE, April 16 (Reuters) - Thai state-owned energy company PTT has offered a ÔÇőlarger volume of high-sulphur ÔÇîfuel oil (HSFO) for loading in May than in previous months, according ÔÇőto market sources.
PTT offered four ÔÇőcargoes of HSFO in a Ôüátender that closes on ÔÇőThursday, sources said. The cargoes will ÔÇőload from the port of Sriracha.
One of the cargoes consist of 18,000 ÔÇőtons of fuel oil ÔÇőscheduled to load between May 5 and ÔÇî7, Ôüáthe sources said, while the other three cargoes each consist of 25,000 tons, scheduled to ÔÇőload ÔÇőbetween May Ôüá15-17, May 23-25, and May 29-31, respectively.
The ÔÇőrefiner typically offers one ÔÇőto Ôüátwo HSFO cargoes for loading per month via Sriracha, based Ôüáon ÔÇőpast records.
Companies typically do ÔÇőnot comment on commercial tenders.

item.title: Duke Energy seeks price increase in North Carolina to recoup winter power costs
item.description:
April 15 (Reuters) - Duke Energy (DUK.N) , opens new tab has sought approval from North Carolina regulators to raise its prices and ÔÇőrecover more than $800 million from customers for ÔÇîhigher fuel and power purchase costs incurred during an extreme winter cold snap.
It was seeking to recover winter ÔÇőfuel and power costs totaling about $500 million ÔÇőat Duke Energy Carolinas and $309 million at ÔüáDuke Energy Progress, it said on Wednesday, ÔÇőwhich would on approval raise monthly bills by ÔÇőabout $6.90 and $7.88, respectively, starting June 1.
Duke Energy Carolinas serves about 2.3 million customers in central and western North Carolina, ÔÇőwhile Duke Energy Progress supplies about 1.6 ÔÇőmillion customers in the central and eastern parts of the ÔÇîstate.
The Ôüáextreme cold in late January and early February drove demand beyond the existing generation and storage capacity, forcing it to buy additional power from ÔÇőneighboring utilities ÔÇőat high Ôüámarket prices, the company said.
Duke Energy has proposed spreading the recovery over ÔÇő19 months, instead of the typical ÔÇő12-month Ôüáperiod, to limit the immediate impact on customers.
It said electricity demand hit a new winter high Ôüáof ÔÇő37,308 megawatt-hours on January 27, ÔÇőthe highest level ever recorded across Duke Energy's Carolinas system.
